topics.title: Fluid Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Pressure (P):** The normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a real or imaginary surface. Mathematically expressed as the limit of the normal compressive force $\Delta F$ acting on an infinitesimal area $\Delta A$ as the area approaches zero:
>
> $$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

The **SI Unit** of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, which equals **N/m²** (one Newton per square meter). The **CGS Unit** is **dyne/cm²**, while in engineering practice, the **bar**, **atm**, and **kgf/cm²** are commonly used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Fluid pressure is a **scalar quantity** (even though force is a vector) because at a point in a fluid, the pressure acts equally in all directions (Pascal's Principle). It has **magnitude only**, not direction.

### Classification of Pressure
Pressure at any point in a static fluid is measured in three reference frames:

1. **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** The total pressure measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (zero reference). It is **always positive**.
2. **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** The pressure measured **above local atmospheric pressure**. $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$.
3. **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** The pressure **below local atmospheric pressure**. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$.

| Reference State | Formula | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Absolute Vacuum | $P_{abs}$ | Thermodynamics, Gas laws |
| Local Atmosphere | $P_{gauge} / P_{vac}$ | Most pressure gauges, Hydraulic systems |
| Standard Atmosphere (101.325 kPa) | $P_{std}$ | Aviation, Standardized testing |

> [!TIP]
> **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$)** at sea level under standard conditions equals **101.325 kPa**, **1.01325 bar**, or **10.33 m of water column**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are underwater in a swimming pool. The deeper you dive, the more your ears hurt. **Why?** Because the weight of all the water *above* you is pressing down on your eardrum. This is **fluid pressure** in action.

**Real-world analogy — The Stack of Books:**
Think of fluid pressure like a stack of books on a table. The book at the **bottom** experiences pressure from **all** the books above it, while the book at the **top** experiences almost no pressure. Similarly, in a fluid:
- The pressure at the **bottom** of a tank = pressure due to the entire column of fluid above it + atmospheric pressure pushing down on the top surface.
- The pressure at the **top surface** = just atmospheric pressure (assuming an open tank).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Point:** Fluid pressure depends **only on depth (height)**, **density of the fluid**, and **gravity** — **NOT on the shape or volume of the container!** This counterintuitive fact is known as the **hydrostatic paradox**. A narrow tube and a wide tank of the same fluid depth will exert the **same pressure** at the bottom.

---

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

This topic directly maps to:
- **CO1:** Understand the fundamental properties of fluids and the principles governing fluid statics.
- **Module Learning Outcome:** Apply hydrostatic principles to solve manometry and pressure measurement problems.
- **Bloom's Level Covered:** Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pascal's Law (Pressure Transmission Law)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** *An external pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, producing equal pressure changes.*

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why it works:** Fluids (especially liquids) are nearly incompressible. Molecules transfer the force through collision without losing energy.
- **Real-world utility:** Foundation of all **hydraulic lifts, hydraulic brakes, hydraulic jacks**, and **hydraulic presses** used in heavy machinery.

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$\Delta P_{applied} = \Delta P_{at\ every\ point}$$

## 2.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Equation (Fundamental Law)

The pressure at any depth $h$ below the free surface of a static, incompressible fluid is given by:

$$P = P_0 + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = Pressure at the free surface (typically $P_{atm}$ for open tanks, in Pa)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (in kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = Vertical depth below the free surface (in m)

**Key Deductions:**
1. Pressure varies **linearly** with depth $h$.
2. Pressure is **independent** of the cross-sectional area or shape of the container.
3. For fluids of **different densities** in a connected vessel, pressure depends on the **weighted column** of each fluid.

## 2.3 Manometry — Pressure Measurement Using Fluid Columns

Manometers are devices that use the **hydrostatic principle** to measure pressure differences by balancing columns of manometric fluids.

### 2.3.1 Simple Manometer (Piezometer)
A vertical open tube attached to a pipe/vessel containing the fluid. The height $h$ of the fluid column directly gives the gauge pressure:

$$P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Cannot measure gas pressure (gas has negligible density) or high pressures (requires impractical tube lengths).

### 2.3.2 U-Tube Manometer
A U-shaped tube containing a heavier **manometric fluid** (typically mercury, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600\ kg/m^3$) connected between two points A and B.

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where $h$ is the differential height of the manometric fluid and $\rho_m$, $\rho_f$ are the densities of the manometric and working fluids, respectively.

### 2.3.3 Inverted U-Tube Manometer
Uses a **lighter** fluid (like oil or air) trapped between two heavier working fluids. Used to measure pressure differences in **water pipelines** without contaminating them.

$$P_A - P_B = h \cdot g \cdot \left( \rho_f - \rho_{air} \right) \approx h \cdot g \cdot \rho_f$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{dF}{dA}$ | Basic pressure definition | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 3 | $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$ | Gauge pressure below free surface | Pa |
| 4 | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Differential U-tube manometer | Pa |
| 5 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute to gauge conversion | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | Vacuum pressure relation | Pa |
| 7 | $P = \gamma h$ | Pressure in terms of **specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$ | Pa |
| 8 | $1\ atm = 101.325\ kPa$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | kPa |
| 9 | $1\ bar = 10^5\ Pa = 100\ kPa$ | Metric bar | kPa |
| 10 | $1\ m\ H_2O = 9.81\ kPa$ | Equivalent water column | kPa |
| 11 | $1\ m\ Hg = 13.6\ m\ H_2O$ | Mercury-water height ratio | — |
| 12 | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2$ | **Hydrostatic Law** (Total head constant) | m |

> [!TIP]
> **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$. For water, $\gamma_w = 9810\ N/m^3$. Using $\gamma$ simplifies many problems because the $g$ factor is absorbed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application | Principle Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Civil Engineering | Dams, Retaining walls, Water supply | Hydrostatic pressure on surfaces |
| Mechanical Engineering | Hydraulic lifts, Hydraulic brakes | Pascal's Law |
| Chemical Engineering | Storage tanks, Pressure vessels | Manometry & Pressure gauges |
| Aerospace | Altimeters, Cabin pressurization | Atmospheric pressure variation |
| Marine Engineering | Submarines, Diving bells | Hydrostatic pressure with depth |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Pressure Equation

**Statement to Prove:** Pressure at a point in a static fluid varies linearly with depth, as $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.

### Setup
Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dh$, located at depth $h$ below the free surface of a static liquid of density $\rho$.

**Free Body Diagram of the element:**
- **Top face:** Force $F_1 = P \cdot dA$ acting **downward** (pressure from fluid above).
- **Bottom face:** Force $F_2 = (P + dP) \cdot dA$ acting **upward** (pressure from fluid below).
- **Weight of element:** $W = (\text{Volume}) \times (\text{specific weight}) = (dA \cdot dh) \cdot \rho g$ acting **downward**.

### Equilibrium Condition (Newton's First Law)
Since the fluid is **static**, the net vertical force must be zero ($\sum F_y = 0$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 - F_1 - W &= 0 \\
(P + dP) \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh &= 0 \\
dP \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh &= 0 \\
dP \cdot dA &= \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh \\
dP &= \rho g \cdot dh
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of algebraic transitions:**
- **Line 1 → 2:** Substituting expressions for the forces acting on the top, bottom, and gravity.
- **Line 2 → 3:** The $P \cdot dA$ terms cancel (they are equal and opposite on the two faces minus the weight).
- **Line 3 → 4:** Divide both sides by $dA$ (non-zero).
- **Line 4 → 5:** Simplify to get the fundamental differential equation of fluid statics.

### Integration from Free Surface to Depth $h$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{P_0}^{P} dP &= \rho g \int_{0}^{h} dh \\
P - P_0 &= \rho g (h - 0) \\
P &= P_0 + \rho g h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence Proved.** The pressure at any depth $h$ in a static, incompressible fluid is **$P_0 + \rho g h$**, confirming linear variation with depth.

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation assumed: (i) static condition, (ii) incompressible fluid ($\rho = \text{constant}$), and (iii) constant gravity ($g = 9.81\ m/s^2$).

## 3.2 Derivation of U-Tube Manometer Equation

**Setup:** A U-tube containing manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ is connected between two pipes A and B containing working fluid of density $\rho_f$. Let the manometric fluid rise to a differential height $h$ between the two limbs.

### Step-by-Step Pressure Analysis

Starting at point A, travel through the connecting tube to the manometer, through the heavier fluid, and out to point B. The pressure must be the same at any horizontal level within the connected manometric fluid.

**Pressure at the lower meniscus (left side):**
$$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$
where $h_1$ is the height of the working fluid column above the left meniscus.

**Pressure at the lower meniscus (right side):**
$$P_B + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 - h) + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$
where $h_1 - h$ is the height of the working fluid above the right meniscus (one limb is higher by $h$).

### Equating the Pressures at the Same Horizontal Level

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A + \rho_f g h_1 &= P_B + \rho_f g (h_1 - h) + \rho_m g h \\
P_A + \rho_f g h_1 &= P_B + \rho_f g h_1 - \rho_f g h + \rho_m g h \\
P_A - P_B &= \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h \\
P_A - P_B &= g h (\rho_m - \rho_f) \\
\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**When $\rho_f \ll \rho_m$ (e.g., measuring water pressure with mercury manometer):**
$$P_A - P_B \approx \rho_m g h$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pressure Head Conversions

This matrix shows the **equivalent head** of common fluids producing **1 atm** of pressure.

| Fluid | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Head to produce 1 atm (m) | Use case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Water | 1000 | 10.33 | Water manometers |
| Mercury | 13600 | 0.76 | Standard barometer |
| Sea Water | 1025 | 10.08 | Marine applications |
| Glycerin | 1260 | 8.20 | Heavy manometric fluid |
| Air (STP) | 1.225 | 8432 | Gas manometry |

**Conversion Identity:**
$$h_{water} \cdot \rho_{water} = h_{mercury} \cdot \rho_{mercury}$$
$$h_{water} = 13.6 \cdot h_{mercury}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Concepts Architecture

```mermaid

    A["FLUID PRESSURE"] --> B["Pressure at a Point"]
    A --> C["Hydrostatic Law"]
    A --> D["Manometry"]

    B --> B2["Gauge Pressure P_gauge"]
    B --> B3["Vacuum Pressure P_vac"]

    B1 --> B1a["Reference Vacuum Zero"]
    B2 --> B2a["Reference Local Atmosphere"]
    B3 --> B3a["Below Atmospheric"]

    C --> C1["P = P0 + rho g h"]
    C --> C2["Linear with depth"]
    C --> C3["Pascal Law Transmission"]

    D --> D1["Piezometer"]
    D --> D2["U-Tube Manometer"]
    D --> D3["Inverted U-Tube"]
    D --> D4["Differential Manometer"]

    C1 --> APP["Application Hydraulic Jack"]
    C3 --> APP2["Application Hydraulic Brake"]
    D2 --> APP3["Application Pressure Measurement"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — U-Tube Manometer Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LeftLimb["Left Limb"]
        A1["Point A Pressure P_A"]
        F1["Working Fluid rho_f"]
        M1["Manometric Fluid rho_m"]
    end

    subgraph RightLimb["Right Limb"]
        A2["Point B Pressure P_B"]
        F2["Working Fluid rho_f"]
        M2["Manometric Fluid rho_m"]
    end

    A1 --> F1
    F1 --> M1
    M1 -. Differential Height h .-> M2
    M2 --> F2
    F2 --> A2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Reference Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VAC["Perfect Vacuum 0 Pa"] --> ABS["Absolute Pressure P_abs"]
    ATM["Atmospheric Pressure P_atm = 101.325 kPa"] --> GAUGE["Gauge Pressure Region"]
    ATM --> VAC2["Vacuum Pressure Region"]
    GAUGE --> PG["P_gauge = P_abs - P_atm greater than 0"]
    VAC2 --> PV["P_vac = P_atm - P_abs greater than 0"]
    ABS --> REL["P_abs = P_atm + P_gauge OR P_atm - P_vac"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution

For a vertical tank filled with a liquid of density $\rho$:

- **Top (free surface):** $P = P_{atm}$ (if open) or $P = P_0$ (if closed & pressurized).
- **At depth $h$:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.
- **Pressure increases linearly** with depth — this can be represented as a **triangular pressure prism** for a vertical wall or a **trapezoidal prism** for surfaces where $P_0 \neq 0$.

> [!TIP]
> For a horizontal surface submerged at depth $h$, the pressure is **uniform** across the entire surface. For a vertical or inclined surface, the pressure varies with depth, requiring integration to find **Total Force** and **Center of Pressure**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Fluid Pressure and State its SI Unit
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom L1: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**
Fluid pressure is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a surface (real or imaginary) in contact with it. Mathematically:

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

The **SI unit** of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, where $1\ Pa = 1\ N/m^2$. Other commonly used units include bar, atm, psi, and kgf/cm². Pressure is a scalar quantity because at any point in a static fluid, the magnitude of pressure is the same in all directions.

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Absolute Pressure and Gauge Pressure
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom L2: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference | Perfect vacuum (zero) | Local atmospheric pressure |
| Value | Always **positive** | Can be **positive, zero, or negative** |
| Formula | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| Use | Thermodynamics, Gas laws | Engineering instruments, Hydraulics |
| Device | Barometer, Manometer (absolute type) | Bourdon gauge, Pressure transducer |

**Note:** A **vacuum gauge** reads the difference between atmospheric and absolute pressure when the latter is below atmospheric. The relation is $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions have an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each long question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Manometry and Hydrostatic Pressure

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom L3: Apply]**

**Question:**
**(a)** A closed tank contains oil of specific gravity 0.85 above a layer of water of depth 1.5 m. If the depth of oil is 3 m, determine the **gauge pressure** at the bottom of the tank. Also, find the **absolute pressure** at the bottom. Take $P_{atm} = 101.325\ kPa$.

**(b)** A differential U-tube mercury manometer is connected between two pipes A and B carrying water. The deflection of mercury in the manometer is **200 mm**. Determine the **pressure difference** between points A and B in kPa.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Pressure at the Bottom of a Two-Layer Tank

**Given Data:**
- Specific gravity of oil, $S_{oil} = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho_{oil} = 850\ kg/m^3$
- Depth of water layer, $h_w = 1.5\ m \Rightarrow \rho_w = 1000\ kg/m^3$
- Depth of oil layer, $h_{oil} = 3\ m$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325\ kPa$
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Step 1: Identify the reference and compute pressure contributions.**

The tank is closed, but since no air pressure is mentioned, we assume the space above oil is at $P_{atm}$ (vented tank). Hence, the pressure at the top of the oil = $P_{atm}$.

**Step 2: Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation layer by layer.**

At the **oil-water interface** (depth = 3 m):
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_{atm} + \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_{oil} \\
P_1 &= 101325 + 850 \times 9.81 \times 3 \\
P_1 &= 101325 + 25015.5 \\
P_1 &= 126340.5\ Pa = 126.34\ kPa
\end{aligned}
$$

At the **bottom of the tank** (depth = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 m):
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= P_1 + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_w \\
P_2 &= 126340.5 + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \\
P_2 &= 126340.5 + 14715 \\
P_2 &= 141055.5\ Pa = 141.06\ kPa
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final Answers.**
- **Gauge pressure at bottom:** $P_{gauge} = P_2 - P_{atm} = 141.06 - 101.325 = \mathbf{39.73\ kPa}$
- **Absolute pressure at bottom:** $P_{abs} = \mathbf{141.06\ kPa}$

#### Part (b): Differential U-Tube Manometer

**Given Data:**
- Differential manometer reading, $h = 200\ mm = 0.2\ m$
- Working fluid: water ($\rho_f = 1000\ kg/m^3$)
- Manometric fluid: mercury ($\rho_m = 13600\ kg/m^3$)
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Apply the U-tube manometer formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A - P_B &= (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h \\
P_A - P_B &= (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \\
P_A - P_B &= 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \\
P_A - P_B &= 12600 \times 1.962 \\
P_A - P_B &= 24721.2\ Pa \\
\boxed{P_A - P_B &\approx 24.72\ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Direction:** If $P_A > P_B$, the mercury level on side A is **pushed down** and rises on side B. The **higher mercury level indicates the side with lower pressure**. The given formula assumes standard orientation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Hydrostatic Law and Total Pressure on Submerged Surface

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom L3: Apply]**

**Question:**
**(a)** State and prove the **hydrostatic law** (or Pascal's law). Mention **two practical applications** of Pascal's law.

**(b)** A vertical rectangular gate, 2 m wide and 3 m high, is installed in a vertical wall retaining water on one side. The top edge of the gate is 2 m below the free water surface. Determine:
(i) The **total hydrostatic force** on the gate.
(ii) The location of the **center of pressure** measured from the free surface.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Statement and Proof of Pascal's Law

**Statement:** Pascal's Law states that *"The pressure applied at any point to a confined static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and this pressure acts perpendicular to the surfaces with which the fluid is in contact."*

**Proof (using element analysis):**

Consider a small tetrahedral fluid element of infinitesimal size, with three mutually perpendicular faces of area $dA_x$, $dA_y$, $dA_z$ and an inclined face of area $dA_n$. Let the pressure on the three orthogonal faces be $P_x$, $P_y$, $P_z$ and on the inclined face be $P_n$.

For static equilibrium, sum of forces along each axis = 0. Considering forces along the x-axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_x \cdot dA_x &= P_n \cdot dA_n \cdot \cos\theta_x \\
\text{Since } dA_n \cdot \cos\theta_x &= dA_x \text{ (projection relation)} \\
P_x &= P_n
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, $P_y = P_n$ and $P_z = P_n$. Hence, $P_x = P_y = P_z = P_n$, proving that **pressure at a point is equal in all directions**.

**Two Practical Applications:**
1. **Hydraulic Lift/Jack:** A small force on a small piston creates a large force on a larger piston, used to lift heavy vehicles in garages.
2. **Hydraulic Brakes:** Used in automobiles — foot pressure on the brake pedal is transmitted through brake fluid to engage wheel cylinders with amplified force.

#### Part (b): Total Force and Center of Pressure on a Vertical Gate

**Given Data:**
- Width of gate, $b = 2\ m$
- Height of gate, $h_g = 3\ m$
- Depth of top edge from free surface, $\bar{h} = 2\ m$
- Fluid: water ($\rho = 1000\ kg/m^3$, $\gamma = 9810\ N/m^3$)
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Step 1: Find the depth of the centroid ($\bar{h}$).**

The centroid of the rectangular gate is at its geometric center:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{h} &= 2 + \frac{h_g}{2} = 2 + \frac{3}{2} = 2 + 1.5 \\
\bar{h} &= 3.5\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the area of the gate.**

$$
A = b \times h_g = 2 \times 3 = 6\ m^2
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total hydrostatic force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho \cdot g \cdot \bar{h} \cdot A \\
F &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times 6 \\
F &= 206010\ N \\
\boxed{F &\approx 206.01\ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the location of the center of pressure ($h^*$).**

For a vertical rectangular surface, the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis (horizontal) is:
$$I_{xx} = \frac{b \cdot h_g^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5\ m^4$$

The depth of center of pressure from the free surface is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h^* &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx}}{A \cdot \bar{h}} \\
h^* &= 3.5 + \frac{4.5}{6 \times 3.5} \\
h^* &= 3.5 + \frac{4.5}{21} \\
h^* &= 3.5 + 0.2143 \\
\boxed{h^* &\approx 3.714\ m\ \text{below the free surface}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Express center of pressure measured from the top of the gate.**

$$h^*_{from\ top} = 3.714 - 2 = 1.714\ m\ below\ the\ top\ edge\ of\ the\ gate$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to add $\bar{h}$ in the center of pressure formula, writing only $\dfrac{I_{xx}}{A \bar{h}}$ as the answer. The **complete** formula is $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xx}}{A \bar{h}}$.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $\bar{h}$ (depth of centroid from free surface) with $h_g$ (height of the gate itself). Always re-read the problem statement for what depth is given.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Using $g = 10\ m/s^2$ instead of the standard $9.81\ m/s^2$. The KTU key uses **$g = 9.81$** unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not converting mm to m when manometer readings are given. Always check unit consistency.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** In manometry, **forgetting the sign convention** for the working fluid when it sits above the manometric fluid. Draw a clear FBD and trace the pressure changes step by step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fluid Pressure** is a **scalar** quantity, defined as normal force per unit area: $P = dF/dA$.
- [x] **SI Unit = Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**. **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**. **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**.
- [x] **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted **equally in all directions**.
- [x] **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — pressure depends **only on depth**, not container shape.
- [x] **Specific Weight** $\gamma = \rho g$ — useful shortcut in pressure calculations ($\gamma_{water} = 9810\ N/m^3$).
- [x] **Absolute Pressure = Atmospheric + Gauge**; **Vacuum Pressure = Atmospheric - Absolute**.
- [x] **Piezometer** measures only positive gauge pressures; **U-tube manometer** is the most versatile.
- [x] **Differential Manometer Formula:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$.
- [x] **Mercury height for 1 atm = 760 mm = 0.76 m**; **Water column for 1 atm = 10.33 m**.
- [x] **Total Hydrostatic Force** on a submerged plane surface: $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$.
- [x] **Center of Pressure** depth: $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xx}}{A \bar{h}}$ — **always below the centroid**.
- [x] For **vertical surfaces**, $I_{xx} = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ (rectangle) or $\dfrac{\pi r^4}{4}$ (circle).
- [x] **Atmospheric pressure** decreases with altitude; **water pressure** increases with depth.
- [x] Remember the **3-equation mantra**: (i) $P = \rho g h$, (ii) $\Delta P = \Delta \rho g h$ (manometer), (iii) $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ (force).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pascal's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pascal's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pascal's Law** (also called the **Principle of Transmission of Fluid Pressure**) is a fundamental principle in fluid statics formulated by the French mathematician, physicist, and philosopher **Blaise Pascal (1623 – 1662)**. The law, as stated in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 1 of *Fluid Mechanics*, is:

> *The pressure applied at any point to a confined fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid, and the pressure acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel.*

In rigorous vector calculus terms, the law is a direct consequence of the **gradient of the pressure field** in a static (hydrostatic) fluid being purely a function of the body force (gravity). Since fluid at rest has no shear stress, the only internal force is the normal pressure, which is a **scalar quantity** that acts equally in every direction at a given point.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Squeezed Toothpaste Tube"

Imagine a completely sealed toothpaste tube filled with fluid, fitted with a piston on top and connected to several thin outlet tubes of varying shapes radiating outward in every direction.

- If you press down on the main piston with your thumb, the toothpaste (fluid) is forced out **simultaneously** from every single outlet.
- The pressure you apply travels through the fluid and pushes outwards on every surface that confines it.
- The walls of the tube do not "absorb" or "filter" the pressure — it arrives at every wall at the exact same intensity.

Now, replace the toothpaste tube with a **hydraulic car jack**: when you pump a small piston, the pressure travels through the oil and lifts a heavy car. The fluid acts as a "pressure courier," delivering your input force to a different, often much larger, surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Pascal's Law is the foundational principle behind *hydraulic machines* and *manometry*. It is the prerequisite for understanding the **hydraulic press**, **hydraulic lift**, and **hydraulic brake**, which are assessed almost every semester in KTU University Examinations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity ($g$):** $9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ (standard) or $9.80665 \ \text{m/s}^2$ (exact, as per **BIPM**).
- **Specific weight of water ($\gamma_w$):** $9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$ at standard temperature ($4^\circ \text{C}$).
- **Density of water ($\rho_w$):** **$1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$** at standard temperature.
- **Atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm}$):** $101.325 \ \text{kPa}$ or $1.01325 \ \text{bar}$ or $760 \ \text{mm of Hg}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — "Confined Fluid"**
> The word *confined* is critical. Pascal's Law strictly applies to fluids that are **enclosed within rigid boundaries** so that any change in pressure at one point must be transmitted (not absorbed) by the entire fluid mass. It does **not** apply to an open, free-flowing river or unconfined atmospheric air, where pressure can dissipate into the surroundings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Pascal's Law — Step-by-Step Breakdown

To truly understand *why* Pascal's Law works, we must examine the microscopic force balance on a tiny fluid element.

- **Step 1 — Fluid at Rest:**
  A fluid element in static equilibrium has zero net force. The only forces acting on it are the **pressure forces** on its six faces and the **body force** (gravity) acting through its mass.

- **Step 2 — Pressure is a Scalar:**
  Unlike stress in a solid (which is a tensor), pressure in a static fluid is a **scalar**. At any point inside the fluid, the magnitude of the pressure is the same in every direction. This is the **Pascal's Principle of Isotropy of Pressure**.

- **Step 3 — No Shear in Static Fluid:**
  A fluid at rest cannot sustain shear stress. If it could, the fluid layers would slide past each other until equilibrium is reached. Therefore, the only contact force between adjacent fluid layers is the **normal pressure**.

- **Step 4 — Conservation of Energy / Equal Transmission:**
  When an external force is applied on a piston pushing against the confined fluid, the work done on the fluid is transmitted as a pressure pulse. Since the fluid is incompressible (for liquids like water, oil, hydraulic fluid), the volume change is uniform, and the pressure rise ($\Delta P$) is identical at every point in the fluid.

- **Step 5 — Action on Containing Surfaces:**
  This uniform $\Delta P$ then pushes outward on every square millimetre of the container walls and on every piston, ram, or membrane exposed to the fluid.

## 2.2 Mathematical Statement

For two points $1$ and $2$ within a confined, incompressible fluid at rest, under only the influence of gravity:

$$P_1 = P_2$$

More generally, if an external pressure $P_{ext}$ is applied at one point, the pressure at every other point in the fluid becomes:

$$P_{final} = P_{initial} + P_{ext}$$

The pressure rise is transmitted *undiminished*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Fundamental definition of pressure. **Units:** $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$. Used as the starting point for every hydraulic machine problem. |
| 2 | $P_1 = P_2$ | Pascal's Law for transmission between two points in a confined static fluid. |
| 3 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Direct consequence of Pascal's Law, the working equation of a **hydraulic press / lift / jack**. |
| 4 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | **Force amplification** equation. The output force is multiplied by the area ratio. |
| 5 | $V_1 = V_2 \Rightarrow A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2$ | **Incompressibility condition** (conservation of volume). Used to find the displacement of the larger piston. |
| 6 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Relationship between absolute and gauge pressure, vital for boundary conditions. |
| 7 | $P = \rho g h$ | **Hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$ below a free surface. Pairs with Pascal's Law for any manometer / hydraulic reservoir problem. |
| 8 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{F_2 d_2}{F_1 d_1}$ | Mechanical efficiency of a hydraulic machine (always $\leq 1$). KTU frequently tests this. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> Pascal's Law is the operating principle behind nearly every **heavy-lifting hydraulic device** in the modern world: car jacks, forklift trucks, hydraulic presses for forging and stamping, aircraft landing gear, hydraulic brakes in automobiles, hydraulic cranes, and even the **dead-weight pressure tester** used in calibration laboratories.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pascal's Law from a Fluid Element

Consider a small triangular fluid element in static equilibrium inside a confined body of fluid. The element has unit length (perpendicular to the page) and is bounded by surfaces of areas $dy$, $dx$, and $ds$ (where $ds = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2}$).

The pressure $P$ varies only in the $x$ and $y$ directions due to the weight of the fluid. The body force per unit volume is $\rho g$ acting downward (along $-y$).

**Force balance in the $x$-direction:**

$$P \cdot dy - (P + dP_x) \cdot dy = 0$$

The two pressure forces on the vertical faces act in opposite directions. The body force has no $x$-component, so:

$$\frac{dP_x}{dx} = 0$$

This means pressure does not vary in the $x$-direction at a constant elevation.

**Force balance in the $y$-direction:**

$$P \cdot dx - (P + dP_y) \cdot dy \cdot \frac{dx}{ds} - \rho g \cdot \frac{1}{2} (dx \cdot dy) = 0$$

Recognising that the inclined face has a vertical projection of $dy \cdot (dx/ds)$, and the body force acts on the element's area $(1/2 \cdot dx \cdot dy)$, simplifying using the geometry $\frac{dx}{ds} = \sin\theta$ and $\frac{dy}{ds} = \cos\theta$:

$$\frac{dP_y}{dy} = -\rho g$$

**Combining the two equations**, since the pressure must be the same in all directions at a point (a direct consequence of zero shear stress), we get the **general hydrostatic equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dy} = -\rho g}$$

**Integration** from point 1 to point 2, where point 1 is at elevation $z_1$ and point 2 is at elevation $z_2$:

$$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$

Since $z$ is measured vertically upwards and $h = z_1 - z_2$ is the depth difference, this yields the famous **hydrostatic pressure equation**:

$$P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$$

**Interpretation:** This is the **exact mathematical equivalent of Pascal's Law** for a fluid in a gravity field. It shows that any change in pressure at point 1 ($\Delta P_1$) is transmitted to point 2 with the *same* magnitude $\Delta P_1$ (the $\rho g h$ term is constant for a given geometry). Hence, $\Delta P_1 = \Delta P_2$ — the **transmission is undiminished**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hydraulic Press Equation

Consider a simple hydraulic press consisting of two cylinders of cross-sectional areas $A_1$ (small) and $A_2$ (large), connected at the bottom and filled with an incompressible hydraulic fluid. A force $F_1$ is applied to the small piston.

**Step 1 — Apply Pascal's Law:**

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the output force:**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 3 — Volume conservation (incompressibility):**

When the small piston moves down by $d_1$, the volume of fluid displaced is $V = A_1 d_1$. This volume pushes the large piston up by $d_2$:

$$A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2$$

$$d_2 = d_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Advantage (ideal):**

$$\text{MA}_{ideal} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{d_1}{d_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight:** The force is multiplied by the area ratio, but the distance is divided by the same ratio. This is a direct application of the **Principle of Conservation of Energy**: $F_1 \cdot d_1 = F_2 \cdot d_2$ (in the ideal, frictionless case). KTU examiners love to test this trade-off.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $15 \ \text{mm}$ and a large piston of diameter $150 \ \text{mm}$. A force of $200 \ \text{N}$ is applied on the small piston. Find:
(a) The pressure generated in the fluid.
(b) The force exerted by the large piston.
(c) The distance the large piston moves when the small piston moves $50 \ \text{mm}$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Pressure:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.015)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.132 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa} = 1.132 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Output force:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = (1.132 \times 10^6) \times (1.767 \times 10^{-2}) = 20{,}000 \ \text{N} = 20 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (c) — Displacement:**

$$A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2 \implies d_2 = d_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 50 \times \left(\frac{15}{150}\right)^2 = 50 \times \frac{1}{100} = 0.5 \ \text{mm}$$

> The force was multiplied by **100**, but the displacement was divided by **100** — a perfect illustration of energy conservation in a hydraulic system.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic Press

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the functional flow of force, pressure, and energy in a typical hydraulic press, mapping how an input force is amplified via Pascal's Law.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Input Force F1] --> B[Small Piston - Area A1]
    B --> C[Pressure Generation - P equals F1 over A1]
    C --> D[Confined Hydraulic Fluid - Incompressible Oil]
    D --> E[Pressure Transmitted Undiminished to All Points]
    E --> F[Large Piston - Area A2]
    F --> G[Output Force F2 equals F1 times A2 over A1]
    G --> H[Load Lifted - Mechanical Work Output]
    D --> I[Fluid Reservoir and Check Valves]
    I --> B
    H --> J[Waste Heat and Friction Losses]
    J --> K[Actual Efficiency eta less than 100 percent]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Pascal's Law Pressure Transmission

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Point 1 - Pressure P1] -- Equal Pressure --> P2[Point 2 - Pressure P2]
    P2 -- Equal Pressure --> P3[Point 3 - Pressure P3]
    P3 -- Equal Pressure --> P4[Point 4 - Pressure P4]
    P1 -- Equal Pressure --> P4
    P1 -- Equal Pressure --> P3
    P2 -- Equal Pressure --> P4
    subgraph SF[Sealed Fluid Container]
        P1
        P2
        P3
        P4
    end
    EXT[External Force] --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every node (`P1`, `P2`, `P3`, `P4`) represents a distinct point inside the sealed fluid. The double-headed arrows labelled "Equal Pressure" emphasise that **Pascal's Law is isotropic** — pressure at *every* point in the fluid is identical at any given instant, regardless of its spatial position.

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Practical Engineering Applications

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Pascal Law Foundation] --> A1[Hydraulic Press]
    L1 --> A2[Hydraulic Jack]
    L1 --> A3[Hydraulic Brake]
    L1 --> A4[Hydraulic Lift]
    L1 --> A5[Manometer Theory]
    L1 --> A6[Dead Weight Pressure Gauge]
    A1 --> U1[Forging and Stamping]
    A2 --> U2[Vehicle Lifting]
    A3 --> U3[Automobile Safety Systems]
    A4 --> U4[Building Elevators]
    A5 --> U5[Pressure Measurement]
    A6 --> U6[Calibration Standards]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**State and explain Pascal's Law. Mention two practical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** Pascal's Law states that the pressure applied to a confined fluid at rest is transmitted **undiminished and equally in all directions** throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel.

**Explanation (1 Mark):** This means that if an external pressure $P$ is applied at any point in a confined, incompressible fluid, the pressure at every other point in the fluid increases by the same amount $P$. Mathematically, $P_1 = P_2$ for any two points in the fluid, leading to $\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$ for a hydraulic system.

**Applications (1 Mark):**
1. **Hydraulic press / jack** — used in automobile workshops and forging industries for force multiplication.
2. **Hydraulic brake** — used in automobiles and trucks for the transmission of braking force from the pedal to the wheel cylinders.
3. *(Any other valid one, e.g., hydraulic lift, dead-weight pressure gauge.)*

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Sample Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between Pascal's Law and Hydrostatic Pressure Law. Write the equation relating pressure and depth in a static fluid.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Difference (2 Marks):**
| Basis | Pascal's Law | Hydrostatic Pressure Law |
|-------|-------------|-------------------------|
| Scope | Applies to *confined* fluids only, where pressure is transmitted undiminished. | Applies to *all static fluids* (both open and confined), governing the variation of pressure with depth. |
| Physical meaning | Describes how an *external pressure* is communicated throughout the fluid. | Describes the *internal pressure variation* due to the weight of the fluid itself. |
| Equation | $\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$ | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ |

**Equation (1 Mark):** The pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a static fluid of density $\rho$ is given by:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where $P_0$ is the pressure at the free surface.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** State Pascal's Law and derive the relationship between the forces and areas of a hydraulic press. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a ram diameter of $30 \ \text{cm}$ and a plunger diameter of $5 \ \text{cm}$. It is used to lift a load of $50 \ \text{kN}$. Find:
- (i) The force required at the plunger.
- (ii) The pressure developed in the fluid.
- (iii) The distance moved by the ram when the plunger moves $100 \ \text{mm}$. **7 Marks**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):** Pascal's Law: *The pressure applied at any point to a confined fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid, and the pressure acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel.*

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Consider a hydraulic press consisting of two connected cylinders filled with an incompressible hydraulic fluid, with cross-sectional areas $A_1$ (small plunger) and $A_2$ (large ram).

Let a force $F_1$ be applied on the small plunger, generating pressure $P_1$ in the fluid.

$$P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

By Pascal's Law, this pressure is transmitted undiminished to the large ram, so $P_2 = P_1$:

$$P_2 = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Equating the two pressures:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Therefore, the force exerted on the ram is:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

This is the **force amplification** relationship of the hydraulic press. **Mechanical Advantage** is $\frac{A_2}{A_1}$.

**Volume Conservation (1 Mark):** Since the fluid is incompressible, the volume displaced by the plunger equals the volume gained by the ram:

$$A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2 \implies d_2 = d_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

This confirms that the force gain is exactly compensated by a distance loss — consistent with the conservation of energy.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Ram diameter $D_2 = 30 \ \text{cm} = 0.30 \ \text{m}$
- Plunger diameter $D_1 = 5 \ \text{cm} = 0.05 \ \text{m}$
- Load $F_2 = 50 \ \text{kN} = 50{,}000 \ \text{N}$
- Plunger displacement $d_1 = 100 \ \text{mm} = 0.1 \ \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Find pressure developed (ii):**

$$P = \frac{F_2}{A_2} = \frac{50{,}000}{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}} = 7.074 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa} \approx 707.4 \ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Find force at the plunger (i):**

$$F_1 = P \cdot A_1 = (7.074 \times 10^5) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 1389 \ \text{N} \approx 1.389 \ \text{kN}$$

*(Alternative check using the area ratio: $F_1 = F_2 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 50{,}000 \times \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}} = 1389 \ \text{N}$ ✓)*

**Step 4 — Find ram displacement (iii):**

$$d_2 = d_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 100 \times \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}} = 100 \times 0.02778 = 2.778 \ \text{mm}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $F_1 \approx 1.389 \ \text{kN}$, (ii) $P \approx 707.4 \ \text{kPa}$, (iii) $d_2 \approx 2.78 \ \text{mm}$.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **hydraulic brake** system. Derive the relationship between the forces applied at the master cylinder and the wheel cylinder using Pascal's Law. **7 Marks**

**(b)** The master cylinder of a hydraulic brake system has a diameter of $20 \ \text{mm}$, and each of the four wheel cylinders has a diameter of $40 \ \text{mm}$. If the driver applies a force of $500 \ \text{N}$ on the brake pedal (assuming a mechanical advantage of $5$ in the pedal linkage), calculate:
- (i) The pressure developed in the fluid.
- (ii) The total braking force generated at all four wheels. **7 Marks**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Working of Hydraulic Brake (7 Marks):**

**Description (3 Marks):** A hydraulic brake system transmits the driver's foot force to all four wheel brake shoes/drums using a **hydraulic fluid** (usually brake oil) and Pascal's Law. The main components are:
1. **Brake pedal** with a mechanical lever linkage.
2. **Master cylinder** (small piston) connected to the pedal.
3. **Wheel cylinders** (larger pistons), one at each wheel.
4. **Connecting pipes/tubing** filled with incompressible brake fluid.

When the driver presses the brake pedal, the linkage pushes the master cylinder piston inward, generating high pressure in the fluid. This pressure is transmitted instantaneously and equally (Pascal's Law) to all four wheel cylinders, which push the brake shoes outward against the rotating drums/discs, creating friction and decelerating the vehicle.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Let $F_m$ be the force on the master cylinder, $A_m$ its area. Pressure generated:

$$P = \frac{F_m}{A_m}$$

This pressure acts on each wheel cylinder of area $A_w$:

$$P = \frac{F_w}{A_w}$$

Equating the two:

$$\frac{F_m}{A_m} = \frac{F_w}{A_w}$$

$$F_w = F_m \cdot \frac{A_w}{A_m}$$

The total braking force from $n$ wheel cylinders is $F_{total} = n \cdot F_w$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_m = 20 \ \text{mm} = 0.020 \ \text{m}$, $D_w = 40 \ \text{mm} = 0.040 \ \text{m}$
- Pedal force $F_{pedal} = 500 \ \text{N}$
- Pedal mechanical advantage (MA) $= 5$
- Number of wheel cylinders $n = 4$

**Step 1 — Force on master cylinder:**

$$F_m = F_{pedal} \times \text{MA} = 500 \times 5 = 2500 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute areas:**

$$A_m = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.040)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Pressure developed (i):**

$$P = \frac{F_m}{A_m} = \frac{2500}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.958 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa} \approx 7.96 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Force at one wheel cylinder:**

$$F_w = P \cdot A_w = (7.958 \times 10^6) \times (1.2566 \times 10^{-3}) = 10{,}000 \ \text{N} = 10 \ \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Total braking force (ii):**

$$F_{total} = n \cdot F_w = 4 \times 10{,}000 = 40{,}000 \ \text{N} = 40 \ \text{kN}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $P \approx 7.96 \ \text{MPa}$, (ii) $F_{total} = 40 \ \text{kN}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Mixing diameter and radius:** A frequent blunder is to use the diameter instead of radius in the area formula. KTU examiners *specifically* deduct 1 full mark for using $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ as $A = \pi D^2$ or similar.
> 2. **Forgetting the mechanical advantage in brake problems:** Students often take pedal force directly as the master cylinder force. Remember the pedal acts as a lever — its MA must be multiplied.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by the number of wheel cylinders:** Braking force per wheel ≠ total braking force. Always multiply by $n$ for total system output.
> 4. **Unit errors in final answer:** KTU strictly expects SI units (Pa, N, m). Marks are docked for using $\text{kgf/cm}^2$ or $\text{bar}$ without conversion.
> 5. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** The pressure generated inside a hydraulic system is *gauge* pressure unless the problem explicitly says otherwise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Pascal's Law — pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted **undiminished and equally** in all directions.
- **Pre-condition:** Fluid must be *confined* and *incompressible*; the law holds only for *static* fluid.
- **Master Equation:** $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$.
- **Force Amplification:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$.
- **Displacement Trade-off:** $d_2 = d_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$.
- **Conservation of Energy:** $F_1 \cdot d_1 = F_2 \cdot d_2$ in the ideal (frictionless) case.
- **Mechanical Advantage:** $\text{MA} = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$ (since $A \propto D^2$).
- **Key Applications:** Hydraulic press, hydraulic jack, hydraulic lift, hydraulic brake, dead-weight pressure tester, hydraulic crane.
- **Hydrostatic Companion Equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (often appears in conjunction with Pascal's Law).
- **Efficiency Rule:** Real machines have $\eta < 1$ due to friction and leakage; never assume 100% efficiency unless the problem says "ideal".
- **Diameter vs. Radius:** Always square the *diameter* directly in $\frac{\pi}{4}D^2$ — do not double-compute the radius.
- **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level:** This topic is typically assessed at *Understand (L2)* for Part A and *Apply / Analyse (L3–L4)* for Part B, with emphasis on numerical problem solving.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrostatic Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydrostatic Law # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Hydrostatic Law** (also called the **Fundamental Equation of Fluid Statics**) states that in a fluid at rest, the **rate of change of pressure in the vertical direction is equal to the specific weight of the fluid**, and pressure acts **equally in all directions** at a point. Mathematically expressed in its differential form: $$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g = -\gamma$$ Where: - $P$ = Hydrostatic pressure (Pa or N/m²) - $z$ = Vertical depth measured from a reference datum (m) - $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $\gamma = \rho g$ = Specific weight of the fluid (N/m³) For **water** at standard conditions, $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The hydrostatic law is the **foundation stone** of Module 1. Almost every subsequent topic in fluid statics — manometry, buoyancy, floatation, and pressure measurement — is a direct consequence of this single differential equation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are standing in a **swimming pool** with water up to your neck. Do you feel more pressure on your ears at the surface or at the bottom? Of course, at the **bottom**! Why? Because the **entire column of water above you is pushing down** due to gravity, and this weight accumulates as you go deeper. A simpler analogy: think of a **stack of books** on a weighing scale. The bottom book feels the weight of all the books above it, while the top book feels almost no weight. Fluid pressure behaves in the **exact same way** — the deeper you go, the heavier the "stack" of fluid above you. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Hydrostatic pressure depends only on **depth**, NOT on the shape or volume of the container. A narrow tube and a wide lake will have the same pressure at 10 m depth (assuming the same fluid). This is known as the **hydrostatic paradox** and is a frequent KTU viva question. ## 1.3 Types of Pressure in KTU Context | Type | Definition | Reference | |------|------------|-----------| | **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** | Pressure measured from perfect vacuum (zero) | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | | **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$)** | Pressure exerted by Earth's atmosphere | $\approx 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$ | | **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$)** | Pressure measured relative to atmosphere | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | | **Vacuum Pressure** | Pressure below atmospheric | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | > [!NOTE] > KTU problems typically use **gauge pressure** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Atmospheric pressure in SI units is $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Logic — Why Does Pressure Increase with Depth? The phenomenon is rooted in three physical truths: 1. **Gravity acts on every fluid particle** — each particle has weight ($W = mg = \rho g V$). 2. **Fluids cannot sustain shear stress at rest** — this means pressure forces must balance gravity in all directions. 3. **Fluid is continuous** — the weight of the upper layer is transmitted downward through molecular collisions (Pascal's principle). For a **static fluid**, every tiny fluid element must be in equilibrium. If the pressure on the bottom were equal to the pressure on the top, the fluid element would accelerate downward under gravity — violating the static condition. Hence, the **bottom face must support more pressure** to compensate for the weight of the element itself. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Buildup - **Step 1:** Consider a stationary fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$. - **Step 2:** Apply Newton's first law (sum of forces = 0) in the vertical direction. - **Step 3:** Pressure acts perpendicular to every face. Horizontal pressures cancel out due to symmetry. - **Step 4:** Vertical equilibrium gives a simple balance: **(Pressure on bottom × area) − (Pressure on top × area) = Weight of element**. - **Step 5:** Simplify the equation to obtain $dP/dz = \rho g$. - **Step 6:** Integrate for an **incompressible fluid** (constant $\rho$) to get $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) | |---|---------|-------------|------------| | 1 | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ | Differential form of Hydrostatic Law | Pa/m | | 2 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure at depth $h$ below free surface | Pa | | 3 | $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$ | Gauge pressure at depth $h$ (when surface is open) | Pa | | 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure below an open liquid surface | Pa | | 5 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight relationship | N/m³ | | 6 | $h = \dfrac{P}{\gamma}$ | Pressure head (height of fluid column) | m | | 7 | $\Delta P = \gamma \cdot \Delta h$ | Pressure difference between two points | Pa | | 8 | $P_1 + \gamma z_1 = P_2 + \gamma z_2$ | Bernoulli's static form (constant along horizontal) | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to find pressure at a point, always start from a **known pressure surface** (free surface with $P = P_{atm}$, or a closed surface with given $P$) and **add** $\rho g h$ as you go **down**, or **subtract** as you go **up**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Dam Design:** The horizontal thrust on a dam wall is $\dfrac{1}{2} \rho g h^2$ per unit width — derived directly from hydrostatic law. 2. **Submarine Hulls:** Must withstand pressure $\rho g h$ at ocean depths (e.g., at 1000 m, $P \approx 10$ MPa). 3. **Storage Tanks & Silos:** Wall thickness design uses hydrostatic pressure distribution. 4. **Barometers & Manometers:** Direct application — column height is converted to pressure via $P = \rho g h$. 5. **Hydraulic Press (Pascal's Machine):** Multiplies force using incompressible fluid transmission. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Law (Full KTU Board Style) ### Assumptions - Fluid is **static** (no motion, no shear stress). - Fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$). - Acceleration due to gravity $g$ is **constant**. ### Setup Consider a small **rectangular fluid element** of dimensions $dx$ (x-direction), $dy$ (y-direction), and $dz$ (z-direction) immersed in a static fluid. Let the **z-axis point upward**. ### Free Body Diagram (Verbal Description) - **Top face** (area = $dx \cdot dy$): Pressure $P$ acts **downward** (pushing into the element). - **Bottom face** (area = $dx \cdot dy$): Pressure $(P + dP)$ acts **upward** (pushing into the element). - **Side faces:** Pressures cancel in pairs (Newton's 3rd law) by symmetry. - **Body force:** Weight $W$ acts **downward**, $W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot V \cdot g = \rho \cdot (dx \cdot dy \cdot dz) \cdot g$. ### Equilibrium Equation (Sum of vertical forces = 0) Taking **upward as positive**: $$\sum F_z = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow (P + dP)(dx \cdot dy) - P(dx \cdot dy) - \rho g (dx \cdot dy \cdot dz) = 0$$ ### Step-by-Step Simplification **Step 1:** Expand the first term: $$P \cdot dx \cdot dy + dP \cdot dx \cdot dy - P \cdot dx \cdot dy - \rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0$$ **Step 2:** The $+P \cdot dx \cdot dy$ and $-P \cdot dx \cdot dy$ **cancel out**: $$dP \cdot dx \cdot dy - \rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0$$ **Step 3:** Factor out the common term $dx \cdot dy$ (since $dx \neq 0$ and $dy \neq 0$): $$dx \cdot dy \left[ dP - \rho g \cdot dz \right] = 0$$ **Step 4:** Divide both sides by $dx \cdot dy$: $$dP - \rho g \cdot dz = 0$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the **differential form of the hydrostatic law**: $$\frac{dP}{dz} = \rho g$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Note:** If $z$ is measured **upward**, then $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ (pressure decreases upward). If $z$ is measured **downward** (depth $h$), then $\dfrac{dP}{dh} = +\rho g$ (pressure increases downward). KTU problems almost always use the **depth form** with the positive sign. ### Step 6: Integration for Incompressible Fluid For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ = constant. Integrate from the free surface (where $P = P_0$ at $h = 0$) to a point at depth $h$ (where $P = P$): $$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \rho g \int_{0}^{h} dh$$ **Result:** $$P - P_0 = \rho g h$$ $$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Horizontal Pressure Equivalence (Pascal's Component) Consider the same element but apply equilibrium in the **x-direction**: - Left face pressure: $P$ (acting in $+x$ direction) - Right face pressure: $(P + dP)$ (acting in $-x$ direction) - No body force in $x$-direction (gravity is vertical) $$\sum F_x = 0 \Rightarrow P \cdot dy \cdot dz - (P + dP) \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow dP \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0 \Rightarrow dP = 0$$ **Conclusion:** Pressure does not vary in the **horizontal direction** — it is the **same at all points on the same horizontal level** within a connected static fluid. This is a **direct KTU exam question** every year. # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fluid Element Under Hydrostatic Equilibrium ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fluid Element at Rest
Dimensions dx, dy, dz] --> B[Forces on Top Face
Pressure P acting DOWN] A --> C[Forces on Bottom Face
Pressure P plus dP acting UP] A --> D[Side Faces
Pressures cancel by symmetry] A --> E[Body Force
Weight W equals rho g dx dy dz DOWN] B --> F[Sum of Vertical Forces equals 0] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[Equation: P dA plus dP dA minus P dA minus rho g dV equals 0] G --> H[Simplify: dP equals rho g dz] H --> I[Integrate: P equals P zero plus rho g h] I --> J[HYDROSTATIC LAW established] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure Variation in a Static Tank ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Free Surface
Pressure equals P_atm or P_zero] --> Q1[Move DOWN by depth h1] Q1 --> R1[Pressure equals P_zero plus rho g h1] Q1 --> Q2[Move DOWN by depth h2] Q2 --> R2[Pressure equals P_zero plus rho g h2] Q2 --> Q3[Move DOWN by depth h3] Q3 --> R3[Pressure equals P_zero plus rho g h3] R1 --> S[Horizontal Line Check
Same depth equals Same Pressure] R2 --> S R3 --> S S --> T[Application: Dam walls, Manometers, Hydraulic press] ``` ## 4.3 Concept Map — Inter-relations within Fluid Statics ```mermaid flowchart TD H[Hydrostatic Law
dP over dz equals rho g] --> A[Pressure at Depth
P equals P0 plus rho g h] H --> B[Pressure is Same Horizontally
Pascal Theorem] A --> C[Manometry
U tube and Differential] A --> D[Buoyancy and Floatation
Archimedes Principle] A --> E[Pressure Measurement
Barometer and Piezometer] B --> F[Hydraulic Press
Force Multiplication] C --> G[Module 1 Applications] D --> G E --> G F --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Diagram Convention:** Always draw the fluid element with **clear dimension labels** ($dx$, $dy$, $dz$), show **arrows for pressure forces** (perpendicular to faces) and **downward arrow for weight**. The derivation is incomplete without this diagram in the exam. # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State and explain the Hydrostatic Law. Mention the assumptions involved.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** The rate of increase of pressure in a static fluid with depth is equal to the specific weight of the fluid. In differential form: $$\frac{dP}{dz} = \rho g$$ where $z$ is the depth, $\rho$ is density, and $g$ is gravity. **Explanation:** In a static fluid, consider a small fluid element. The vertical equilibrium of the element gives the pressure variation equation. On integration (for incompressible fluid), $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Fluid is **continuous and at rest**. 2. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$). 3. $g$ is **constant**. 4. **No shear stresses** exist in the static fluid. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define gauge pressure, absolute pressure, and vacuum pressure. How are they related?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Absolute Pressure:** Pressure measured above absolute zero (perfect vacuum). $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ - **Gauge Pressure:** Pressure measured above atmospheric pressure. $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ - **Vacuum Pressure:** Pressure below atmospheric pressure. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ **Relation diagram:** $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$ $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$ (Any valid numerical example = full 3 marks; e.g., $P_{abs} = 200$ kPa, $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa → $P_{gauge} = 98.7$ kPa). --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Derive the hydrostatic law in differential form. Starting from a free surface with pressure $P_0$, obtain an expression for pressure at a depth $h$ in a static incompressible fluid. A tank contains oil (specific gravity 0.85) up to a depth of 6 m. Find the gauge pressure and absolute pressure at the bottom of the tank. Take atmospheric pressure = 101.3 kPa.** #### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ at rest. **Step 2:** Vertical equilibrium: $$(P + dP)(dx \, dy) - P(dx \, dy) - \rho g (dx \, dy \, dz) = 0$$ **Step 3:** Simplify: $$dP = \rho g \, dz$$ **Step 4:** Integrate from free surface ($P = P_0$ at $z = 0$) to depth $h$: $$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \rho g \int_{0}^{h} dz$$ $$P - P_0 = \rho g h$$ $$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$ **Marks Distribution:** - Free body diagram: 2 marks - Equilibrium equation: 2 marks - Simplification: 1 mark - Integration and final result: 2 marks #### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - Specific gravity of oil, $S = 0.85$ - Depth, $h = 6$ m - $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa - Density of water, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ **Solution:** **Step 1:** Density of oil: $$\rho_{oil} = S \times \rho_w = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2:** Specific weight of oil: $$\gamma_{oil} = \rho_{oil} \times g = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 8.34 \text{ kN/m}^3$$ **Step 3:** Gauge pressure at bottom: $$P_{gauge} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 6$$ $$P_{gauge} = 50{,}031 \text{ Pa} \approx 50.03 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 4:** Absolute pressure at bottom: $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 101.3 + 50.03$$ $$\boxed{P_{abs} = 151.33 \text{ kPa}}$$ --- ### **🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Explain with a neat sketch the concept of pressure variation in a static fluid. A U-tube manometer is connected to a pipe carrying water under pressure. The differential mercury level is 20 cm. Find the pressure of water in the pipe. Take specific gravity of mercury = 13.6.** #### **Part (a) — Pressure Variation Explanation (7 marks)** **Sketch (3 marks):** Draw a vertical tank with a free surface at the top and several horizontal lines marked at depths $h_1$, $h_2$, $h_3$. Show pressure arrows perpendicular to the walls indicating increasing magnitude with depth. Label: $P_1$ at depth $h_1$, $P_2$ at $h_2$, $P_3$ at $h_3$, with $P_1 < P_2 < P_3$. **Explanation (4 marks):** - Pressure at any depth $h$: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. - The variation is **linear with depth** (straight line on a P vs. h graph). - Pressure is **constant along any horizontal plane** within a connected static fluid. - Pressure at a point acts **equally in all directions** (Pascal's principle). #### **Part (b) — U-Tube Manometer Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - Differential mercury level, $h = 20$ cm = 0.2 m - Specific gravity of mercury, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$ - Manometer fluid: mercury; pipe fluid: water **Manometer Equation:** Starting from the pipe centerline (point A) and moving through the manometer to the open end: $$P_A + \rho_w g \cdot h_w - \rho_{Hg} g \cdot h - \rho_w g \cdot h_{above} = P_{atm}$$ For a simple U-tube with the open end exposed to atmosphere: $$P_A = \rho_{Hg} g \cdot h$$ **Substituting values:** $$P_A = (13.6 \times 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.2$$ $$P_A = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.2$$ $$P_A = 26{,}683.2 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{P_A \approx 26.68 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert cm to m** — A classic 1-mark loss. Always use SI units. > 2. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — Read the question carefully. "Find the pressure" without "absolute" usually means **gauge**. > 3. **Missing the FBD in derivation** — The fluid element diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. 2 marks are reserved for the sketch. > 4. **Wrong sign convention** — Always state clearly whether $z$ is measured upward or downward. > 5. **Manometer direction error** — Always start from the **unknown pressure point** and move toward the **known pressure** (usually $P_{atm}$), adding pressure when going down and subtracting when going up. > 6. **Using $\rho_w = 1000$ instead of $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$** — The manometer equation uses the **heavier fluid** (mercury) for the differential column. --- ## 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Hydrostatic Law:** $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \rho g$ (or $-\rho g$ if $z$ is upward). - ✅ **Integrated form:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (for incompressible static fluid). - ✅ **Pressure is same at all points on the same horizontal level** in a connected static fluid. - ✅ **Pressure at a point acts equally in all directions** (Pascal's Law). - ✅ **Standard values:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$. - ✅ **Specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$ is the direct proportionality constant. - ✅ **Pressure head** $h = P/\gamma$ converts pressure to an equivalent fluid column height. - ✅ **Horizontal pressure thrust on a submerged surface** $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ (used in dam design). - ✅ **Depth is measured from the free surface**, not from the tank bottom. - ✅ **For gases:** $\rho$ varies with height → use the **compressible form** $dP = -\rho g \, dz$ (not in Module 1). - ✅ **Always draw the FBD** of the fluid element in derivation questions. - ✅ **Gauge pressure** is what most pressure gauges read; **absolute** includes $P_{atm}$.

topics.title: Measurement of Fluid Pressure Using Manometers: Simple Manometers (Piezometer and U-tube Manometers) and Differential Manometers (U-tube Differential Manometer and Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Fluid Pressure Using Manometers: Simple Manometers (Piezometer and U-tube Manometers) and Differential Manometers (U-tube Differential Manometer and Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Foundational Concept: Fluid Pressure **Pressure (P)** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. $$P = \frac{dF}{dA}$$ The standard SI unit is **N/m²** (also called **Pascal, Pa**). In engineering practice, multiples like **kN/m² (kPa)**, **bar**, and head units like **m of water** or **mm of Hg** are routinely used. > [!NOTE] > **Classification of Pressure** > - **Absolute Pressure (P_abs):** Measured relative to a perfect vacuum (true zero). > - **Gauge Pressure (P_gauge):** Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$. > - **Vacuum Pressure (P_vac):** When absolute pressure is below atmospheric. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$. ### 1.2 What is a Manometer? A **Manometer** is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing the column of fluid against the weight of a known liquid column (the manometric fluid). It operates on the principle of **hydrostatic equilibrium**, where the pressure at any horizontal level within a continuous, static fluid is constant. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balance the Column" Idea Imagine a child's seesaw. If you put a heavy box on one side, the only way to balance it is to push down with a known weight on the other side. A manometer does the same thing with fluids: the unknown pressure on one side is "balanced" by a column of a known, heavier (or lighter) liquid on the other side. By measuring how tall that balancing column is, we can mathematically deduce the unknown pressure. The manometer is essentially a *fluid seesaw*. ### 1.4 Classification of Manometers (KTU Syllabus Scope) | Category | Sub-Types | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Simple Manometers** | 1. Piezometer
2. U-tube Manometer | Measure pressure at a single point | | **Differential Manometers** | 1. U-tube Differential Manometer
2. Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer | Measure pressure *difference* between two points | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The questions from this topic usually carry 7 or 14 marks. Be thorough with all four types — derivations of pressure head are the most frequently asked. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Type 1 — Piezometer A **piezometer** is the simplest form of manometer. It is essentially an open vertical glass tube connected to a vessel/pipeline in which pressure is to be measured. **Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):** - The fluid under measurement rises up the tube until its weight balances the pressure at the base. - The height of the fluid column above the point of measurement is called the **"pressure head"**. - It works **only for liquids** (since gases would simply escape) and is limited to **moderate pressures** (long tubes become impractical). **Derivation Logic:** Consider a point A at depth $h$ below the free surface of the liquid in the piezometer. The pressure at point A must equal the weight of the liquid column above it. $$P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ Where: - $\rho$ = density of the liquid - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) - $h$ = height of the liquid column (m) This is the **gauge pressure** because the top of the column is exposed to the atmosphere. **Real-world utility:** Piezometers are widely used in **dam monitoring**, **groundwater wells**, and **tank level measurement**. > [!WARNING] > **Piezometer Limitation:** Cannot be used for gases, highly corrosive fluids, or very high pressures (over ~3 m of water head becomes structurally impractical). ### 2.2 Type 2 — U-Tube Manometer When the fluid whose pressure is to be measured is a gas, or is highly corrosive, or is under high pressure, a piezometer is unsuitable. In such cases, a **U-tube manometer** is used. **Construction:** A U-shaped glass tube containing a **manometric fluid** (denser than the working fluid — commonly mercury, $SG = 13.6$). **Operating Logic:** - One limb is connected to the point where pressure is to be measured. - The other limb is open to the atmosphere. - The manometric fluid is pushed down on the high-pressure side and rises on the open side. - The difference in heights of the manometric fluid in the two limbs is the **manometer deflection (h)**. ### 2.3 Type 3 — U-Tube Differential Manometer Used to measure the **pressure difference** between two points (A and B) in a pipe or system. **Setup:** The U-tube connects two pressure tapping points. The manometric fluid (heavier than the working fluid) sits at the bottom of the U. ### 2.4 Type 4 — Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer Used when the **manometric fluid is lighter than the working fluid** (typically air or oil for water flow measurement). The U-tube is inverted — the manometric fluid sits at the top, trapped in the middle. --- ### 📘 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The table below contains every key equation you need. **Memorize these** — they are the core of all numericals. | # | Manometer Type | Governing Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Piezometer** | $P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $h$ in m | | 2 | **U-tube Manometer (heavy manometric fluid)** | $P_A = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$ | $\rho_m$ = manometric fluid density, $\rho_f$ = fluid above mercury | | 3 | **U-tube Manometer (above point of measurement)** | $P_A = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$ | When fluid column is present above manometric fluid in measuring limb | | 4 | **U-tube Differential Manometer (heavy manometric fluid)** | $P_A - P_B = h \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g$ | $h$ = differential deflection, $\rho_m > \rho_f$ | | 5 | **U-tube Differential Manometer (general case)** | $P_A - P_B = h \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - x \cdot \rho_f \cdot g - y \cdot \rho_{f2} \cdot g$ | General form with various fluid columns $x, y$ | | 6 | **Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer** | $P_A - P_B = h \cdot \left( \rho_f - \rho_m \right) \cdot g$ | $\rho_m < \rho_f$ (e.g., air/oil in water pipe) | | 7 | **Pressure Head** | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Head in metres of fluid | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units & Conversions to Memorize:** > - **1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ N/m²** > - **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water = 760 mm of Hg** > - **Specific Gravity of Mercury (Hg) = 13.6** ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: U-Tube Manometer (Heavy Manometric Fluid) **Setup:** A pipe contains a fluid of density $\rho_f$ at point A. A U-tube manometer with manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ is connected. The manometric fluid rises by a height $h$ on the open side. The fluid above mercury on the pipe side extends a height $h_1$ above the lower meniscus. **Reference points:** Let the lower mercury meniscus (on the pipe side) be point **X** and the upper mercury meniscus (on the open side) be point **Y**. **Step 1 — Pressure at point X (pipe side):** The pressure at X is the sum of pressure at A and the weight of fluid column between A and X. $$P_X = P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$ *Logic:* Hydrostatic principle — adding fluid column increases pressure by $\rho g h$. **Step 2 — Pressure at point Y (open side):** The pressure at Y is the sum of atmospheric pressure and the weight of the open manometric fluid column. $$P_Y = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ *Logic:* Open tube is at atmospheric pressure at the top, plus the mercury column of height $h$ pushes down. **Step 3 — Apply the fundamental principle of manometry:** At the same horizontal level within a connected static fluid (mercury here), pressures are equal. The horizontal level we choose passes through the lower meniscus, but the equality holds if we go from X up through mercury to the same level as Y. The pressure at the level of Y on the *left* side equals $P_X$ (since we are in the same connected mercury, assuming X and Y are at the same horizontal level after tracing through the mercury). Actually, the standard KTU derivation is: $$P_X = P_Y \text{ (at the level of lower meniscus, in the same fluid — mercury)}$$ But X and Y are at different levels within mercury. Going from X to Y through mercury, the pressure changes by $\rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$. So: $$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $P_A$ (gauge pressure form):** $$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$ **Step 5 — If we want gauge pressure (subtract $P_{atm}$):** $$P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$ $$\boxed{P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \left( \rho_m \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot h_1 \right) \cdot g}$$ **Special Case:** If the fluid above mercury is negligible (i.e., the piezometer tap is at the level of the lower mercury meniscus), then $h_1 = 0$: $$P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: U-Tube Differential Manometer **Setup:** Two points A and B in a pipe carry the same working fluid of density $\rho_f$. The U-tube connects them, with manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ ($\rho_m > \rho_f$) at the bottom.
