Let the lower meniscus be on the higher-pressure side (say, side A). - Deflection of manometric fluid = $h$ - Distance from A to lower meniscus = $x$ (in working fluid) - Distance from B to upper meniscus = $y$ (in working fluid) **Step 1 — Pressure at the lower meniscus (level of A's meniscus, let's call it point 1):** Going down from A through working fluid: $$P_1 = P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot x$$ **Step 2 — Pressure at the upper meniscus (level of B's meniscus, point 2):** Going up from B through working fluid: $$P_2 = P_B - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot y$$ *(Note: Pressure decreases as we go up, hence the negative sign.)* **Step 3 — Connect points 1 and 2 through the manometric fluid:** The pressure difference between points 1 and 2 is the manometric fluid column of height $h$: $$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3:** $$(P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot x) - (P_B - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot y) = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 5 — Expand and simplify:** $$P_A - P_B + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot x + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot y = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ $$P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot x - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot y$$ **Step 6 — General working fluid density simplification:** If the fluid columns are similar and we can group terms: $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (x + y)$$ **Step 7 — Special Case (most common in KTU):** If the points A and B are at the **same elevation** (i.e., $x = y$ or the geometry cancels out, and only the differential manometer fluid column matters), and the working fluid columns are equal on both sides: $$\boxed{P_A - P_B = h \cdot \left( \rho_m - \rho_f \right) \cdot g}$$ --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer **Setup:** Two points A and B in a water pipe ($\rho_f$). An *inverted* U-tube connects them. The manometric fluid is *lighter* than water (e.g., oil with $SG < 1$, or air with $\rho_m \ll \rho_f$). The manometric fluid sits at the top of the inverted U, with the lighter fluid column of height $h$ being the differential. **Step 1 — Pressure at the interface on side A (call it point 1, at top of water column on A's side):** Going *up* from A through water by a height $h_1$: $$P_1 = P_A - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$ **Step 2 — Pressure at the interface on side B (point 2):** Going *up* from B through water by a height $h_2$: $$P_2 = P_B - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2$$ **Step 3 — Connect points 1 and 2 through the manometric fluid (air/oil):** The pressure at point 1 *plus* the weight of the manometric fluid column *equals* the pressure at point 2 (the air column adds to the pressure as we go from 1 down to the manometric fluid level... actually, let us reason carefully). Going across the top from 1 to 2, the path goes *down* through the manometric fluid (oil/air). Pressure at the bottom of the manometric fluid column on side A = $P_1 + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$ (where $h$ is the vertical difference between the two oil-air interfaces). This must equal the pressure at the bottom of the manometric fluid column on side B, which is at a different level. A cleaner approach: at the same horizontal level in a connected fluid, pressure is the same. The inverted U has the manometric fluid (lighter) trapped at top. The differential head reading is $h$. For the standard KTU formula derivation: $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot \left( \rho_f - \rho_m \right) \cdot g$$ **Reasoning:** If A is at higher pressure, water is pushed *up* higher on side A inside the inverted U. The difference in water column heights is $h$. The manometric fluid (lighter) is at the top. The manometric balance equation: $$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h = P_B + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2$$ where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are water column heights and $h$ is the differential in the manometric fluid region. If we set $h_1 - h_2 = h$ (the differential reading), and isolate: $$P_A - P_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_2 - h_1) + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ $$P_A - P_B = -\rho_f \cdot g \cdot h + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ $$\boxed{P_A - P_B = h \cdot g \cdot \left( \rho_m - \rho_f \right)}$$ If $\rho_m < \rho_f$ (e.g., air in water), then this becomes **negative**, indicating that the direction of higher pressure reverses. The conventional form (taking magnitudes): $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot \left( \rho_f - \rho_m \right) \cdot g \quad \text{(when } \rho_f > \rho_m \text{)}$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A pipe carrying oil ($SG = 0.8$) has a U-tube manometer connected. The manometric fluid is mercury ($SG = 13.6$). The mercury level on the open side is 25 cm above the level on the pipe side. The oil-mercury interface on the pipe side is 40 cm below the centerline of the pipe. Find the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline. **Given:** - $h = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ (mercury deflection) - $h_1 = 40 \text{ cm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$ (oil column above the lower meniscus) - $\rho_{oil} = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 1000 = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Solution using the formula:** $$P_A = \left( \rho_m \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot h_1 \right) \cdot g$$ $$P_A = \left( 13600 \times 0.25 - 800 \times 0.40 \right) \times 9.81$$ $$P_A = \left( 3400 - 320 \right) \times 9.81$$ $$P_A = 3080 \times 9.81$$ $$\boxed{P_A = 30,214.8 \text{ Pa} = 30.21 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$ **Pressure head in terms of oil:** $$h_{oil} = \frac{P_A}{\rho_{oil} \cdot g} = \frac{30214.8}{800 \times 9.81} = 3.85 \text{ m of oil}$$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Manometer System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pipe or Vessel at Unknown Pressure] --> B[Connection Tap Point] B --> C{Manometer Type Selection} C -->|Direct Liquid Column| D[Piezometer] C -->|Heavy Manometric Fluid| E[U-tube Manometer] C -->|Pressure Difference Heavy| F[U-tube Differential Manometer] C -->|Pressure Difference Light| G[Inverted U-tube Differential] D --> D1[Liquid rises to height h: P = rho x g x h] E --> E1[Mercury column h, fluid column h1 above: P_A = rho_m x g x h - rho_f x g x h1] F --> F1[Differential h between points A and B: P_A - P_B = h x rho_m - rho_f x g] G --> G1[Light fluid trapped on top: P_A - P_B = h x rho_f - rho_m x g] D1 --> H[Read Pressure Head] E1 --> H F1 --> H G1 --> H H --> I[Convert to Required Units kPa, bar, N per m squared] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic Flow: U-Tube Differential Manometer Reading Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Identify Two Pressure Points A and B] --> B[Locate Manometric Fluid Interface] B --> C[Measure Differential Deflection h] C --> D[Note Distances x and y in Working Fluid] D --> E[Substitute into Formula P_A minus P_B] E --> F[Apply Density Values rho_f and rho_m] F --> G[Compute Pressure Difference in Pascals] G --> H[Convert to kPa or bar if Required] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture of the Four Manometers ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SimpleManometers[Simple Manometers] P1[Piezometer]:::simple U1[U-tube Manometer]:::simple end subgraph DifferentialManometers[Differential Manometers] U2[U-tube Differential]:::diff IU[Inverted U-tube Differential]:::diff end SimpleManometers -->|Single Point| OUT1[Pressure Reading] DifferentialManometers -->|Two Point Comparison| OUT2[Pressure Difference] classDef simple fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000 classDef diff fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#bf6b04,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Functional Cross-Section: Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Point A in Water Pipe] --> A1[Water column h1 rises in left limb] B[Point B in Water Pipe] --> B1[Water column h2 rises in right limb] A1 --> X[Top of Inverted U tube] B1 --> X X --> M[Manometric Fluid lighter than water trapped at top] M --> H[Deflection h equals h1 minus h2] H --> EQ[Apply P_A minus P_B equals h x rho_f minus rho_m x g] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a manometer. Differentiate between a simple manometer and a differential manometer.** **Model Answer:** A **manometer** is a device used to measure the pressure of a fluid (liquid or gas) by balancing it against the hydrostatic pressure of a column of a known fluid (manometric fluid). It is based on the principle that at the same horizontal level in a continuous, static, homogeneous fluid, the pressure is constant. | Feature | Simple Manometer | Differential Manometer | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Measures pressure at a *single point* | Measures *pressure difference* between two points | | **Examples** | Piezometer, U-tube manometer | U-tube differential, Inverted U-tube differential | | **Output** | Absolute or gauge pressure at one location | Difference $(P_A - P_B)$ | | **Application** | Tank pressure, gas pressure | Pipe flow losses, orifice meter, venturi meter | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the limitations of a piezometer. Why is mercury commonly used as a manometric fluid?** **Model Answer:** **Limitations of Piezometer:** 1. It can only be used for **liquids** — gases escape from the open tube. 2. It is unsuitable for **high pressures**, as the required tube becomes impractically long (e.g., 1 MPa ≈ 102 m of water column). 3. It cannot be used for **corrosive, hot, or toxic fluids**. 4. It must be installed **vertically**, which limits placement flexibility. **Why Mercury is Preferred:** 1. **High density** (SG = 13.6) → a small height $h$ balances a large pressure, making the instrument compact. 2. **Immiscible** with most working fluids (water, oils) → forms a clear, stable meniscus. 3. **Low vapour pressure** → minimal evaporation, stable readings. 4. **Visible meniscus** in glass → easy, accurate deflection measurement. 5. **Chemically stable** under normal conditions. --- ### 📘 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024] **(a) Derive an expression for the pressure at point A in a pipe containing a fluid of specific gravity $S_f$, when a U-tube manometer using mercury (specific gravity 13.6) is connected. The mercury deflection is $h$, and the fluid column above mercury in the connecting tube is $h_1$.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Two pressure taps in a horizontal water pipe are connected to an inverted U-tube differential manometer using oil of specific gravity 0.7 as the manometric fluid. If the differential manometer reading is 20 cm, calculate the pressure difference between the two taps. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.** **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Given:** Working fluid specific gravity $S_f$, manometric fluid (mercury) specific gravity 13.6, manometer deflection $h$, fluid column $h_1$ above the mercury on the pipe side. **Setup:** Refer to derivation in Section 3.1. **Step 1 — Pressure at lower meniscus (X) on pipe side:** $$P_X = P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$ **Step 2 — Pressure at upper meniscus (Y) on open side:** $$P_Y = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 3 — Apply manometric principle (same level in same fluid):** At the level of X, going through mercury, the pressure on the open side equals $P_X$. The mercury column on the open side is $h$ higher than X, so: $$P_X = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 4 — Equate and solve:** $$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ $$\boxed{P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1}$$ **Gauge pressure form:** $$P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$ **Substituting densities in terms of specific gravity:** $$P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \left( 13.6 \cdot \rho_w \cdot h - S_f \cdot \rho_w \cdot h_1 \right) \cdot g$$ $$P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \left( 13.6 \cdot h - S_f \cdot h_1 \right)$$ --- ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Working fluid: water → $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Manometric fluid: oil ($SG = 0.7$) → $\rho_m = 700 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Differential reading: $h = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - Points A and B are at the **same elevation** (horizontal pipe). **Formula (Inverted U-tube differential, lighter manometric fluid):** $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot \left( \rho_f - \rho_m \right) \cdot g$$ **Step 1 — Calculate density difference:** $$\rho_f - \rho_m = 1000 - 700 = 300 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Substitute all values:** $$P_A - P_B = 0.20 \times 300 \times 9.81$$ $$P_A - P_B = 60 \times 9.81$$ $$\boxed{P_A - P_B = 588.6 \text{ Pa} = 0.5886 \text{ kPa}}$$ **Pressure head equivalent (in terms of water):** $$h_{water} = \frac{P_A - P_B}{\rho_f \cdot g} = \frac{588.6}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.06 \text{ m of water} = 6 \text{ cm of water}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Piezometer. What are its limitations?** **(7 marks)** **(b) A differential U-tube manometer is connected to two points A and B in a pipe carrying oil of specific gravity 0.85. The manometric fluid is mercury. The deflection of mercury is 12 cm. The centerline of the pipe is at the same level as the lower meniscus. Determine the pressure difference between A and B in terms of head of oil. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.** **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Piezometer Diagram Description:** A piezometer is a simple, straight, vertical glass tube open at the top, connected at the bottom to a vessel or pipe whose pressure is to be measured. The fluid from the pipe rises into the tube until the hydrostatic pressure of the rising column equals the pressure at the connection point. ``` | | <- open to atmosphere | | <- fluid rises to height h | ______|_________ | | | VESSEL / | <- pipe at pressure P_A | PIPE | |______________| ``` **Working Principle:** The pressure at the base of the fluid column (point A) is balanced by the weight of the column above it: $$P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ This is the **gauge pressure** at point A. **Limitations:** 1. Suitable **only for liquids** — gases would escape into the atmosphere. 2. For high pressures, the **column height becomes impractically large** (e.g., 1 bar ≈ 10.2 m of water). 3. Cannot be used for **hot, toxic, or corrosive fluids** that would damage the open tube. 4. Installation must be **vertical** with sufficient clearance above the pipe. --- ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Working fluid: oil ($SG = 0.85$) → $\rho_f = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Manometric fluid: mercury ($SG = 13.6$) → $\rho_m = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Deflection: $h = 12 \text{ cm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$ - Pipe centerline at the level of lower meniscus (so $h_1 = 0$ — no oil column correction needed). - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Formula (since centerline = lower meniscus, simplified case):** $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot \left( \rho_m - \rho_f \right) \cdot g$$ **Step 1 — Compute density difference:** $$\rho_m - \rho_f = 13600 - 850 = 12750 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute pressure difference:** $$P_A - P_B = 0.12 \times 12750 \times 9.81$$ $$P_A - P_B = 1530 \times 9.81$$ $$P_A - P_B = 15{,}009.3 \text{ Pa} \approx 15.01 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3 — Express in head of oil (as asked):** $$h_{oil} = \frac{P_A - P_B}{\rho_f \cdot g} = \frac{15009.3}{850 \times 9.81}$$ $$\boxed{h_{oil} = 1.80 \text{ m of oil}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Sign Error:** Many students forget the minus sign in $P_A - P_B = h(\rho_m - \rho_f)g$. The density of the manometric fluid is *always* larger, so the formula works as written. For inverted U-tube, the order flips. > 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Always convert $h$ from cm to m, and $\rho$ to kg/m³, before substituting. Mixing units loses 2–3 marks instantly. > 3. **Forgetting Atmospheric Pressure:** If the problem asks for *gauge* pressure, subtract $P_{atm}$. If it asks for *absolute*, add it. The wording is critical. > 4. **Reading the Manometer Wrong:** The "deflection $h$" is the *difference* in heights of the manometric fluid in the two limbs — not the height of one limb. > 5. **Inverted vs. U-tube Confusion:** The inverted U-tube has the **lighter** manometric fluid at the top. Using the standard U-tube formula on an inverted setup is a common error. --- ### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - [ ] **Pressure** is normal force per unit area: $P = F/A$, SI unit is **Pa (N/m²)**. - [ ] **Absolute pressure** = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure. **Vacuum pressure** = atmospheric − absolute. - [ ] **Piezometer** → open vertical tube for liquids, $P = \rho g h$, limited to low/moderate pressures. - [ ] **U-tube manometer** → uses a heavy manometric fluid (mercury, $SG = 13.6$); can measure gas or liquid pressure. - [ ] **U-tube manometer formula:** $P_A = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1$ (gauge form). - [ ] **U-tube differential manometer:** measures $P_A - P_B = h(\rho_m - \rho_f)g$ when pipe is horizontal and same fluid. - [ ] **Inverted U-tube differential:** uses a *lighter* manometric fluid; formula $P_A - P_B = h(\rho_f - \rho_m)g$. - [ ] **Specific gravity of mercury** = 13.6 — always required in numericals. - [ ] **Atmospheric pressure** = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water = 760 mm of Hg. - [ ] **Head conversion:** $h = P / (\rho g)$ — pressure expressed in metres of any fluid. - [ ] **Manometric principle:** At the same horizontal level in a connected static fluid, pressure is the same — this is the foundation of every manometer derivation. - [ ] **Key constants:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. - [ ] **Always read the deflection** as the differential height between the two limbs of the manometric fluid. - [ ] **Sketch the manometer** before substituting values — examiners reward clear, labeled diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions.

topics.title: Mechanical Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Mechanical Gauges?

A **mechanical gauge** in fluid mechanics is a self-contained, purely mechanical instrument used to measure the **static pressure** of a fluid (liquid or gas) at a point in a system, without requiring any external electrical power source. The measurement is achieved through the elastic deformation of a sensing element, which is then mechanically magnified and displayed on a calibrated dial or scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCET302 – Module 1):**
> Mechanical gauges are devices that measure fluid pressure by balancing the unknown pressure against the elastic deformation of a solid element (spring, tube, diaphragm, or bellows) or against a known column of fluid (manometers). The output is read directly on a graduated scale.

### 1.2 Classification of Mechanical Gauges

Mechanical pressure-measuring devices are broadly classified into two families:

| Family | Working Principle | Typical Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Manometers (Fluid Columns)** | Pressure is balanced against the **weight** of a fluid column. | U-Tube Manometer, Inclined Manometer, Well-Type (Single-Leg) Manometer, Micromanometer |
| **Elastic-Element Gauges** | Pressure is balanced against the **elastic deformation** of a solid element. | Bourdon Tube Gauge, Diaphragm Gauge, Bellows Gauge, Dead-Weight Gauge (Tester) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, the primary focus lies in **manometric devices** and the **Bourdon tube gauge**, as these appear most frequently in End-Semester Examinations (ESE) and university question papers.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeeze-and-Show" Idea 💡

Imagine you are squeezing a **balloon** with your hand:
- The harder you squeeze (more pressure), the more the balloon's shape changes (deformation).
- Your eyes act like the "indicator" — they see the deformation and your brain converts it to a "squeeze level."

A mechanical gauge works **exactly the same way**:
- The unknown fluid pressure **pushes** on a flexible element (tube, diaphragm, or fluid column).
- The element **deforms** proportionally to the pressure.
- A **linkage** converts this small deformation into a large pointer movement on a **calibrated dial**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy 1 (Manometer):** Think of a **U-shaped transparent straw** in a cold drink. If you blow harder from one side, the liquid level drops on one side and rises on the other. The **difference in heights** tells you how hard you blew.
>
> **Real-World Analogy 2 (Bourdon Tube):** Think of a **crumpled paper straw** — when you try to blow air into it, it tries to straighten out. The more pressure inside, the more it uncurls. A Bourdon tube behaves identically with fluid pressure.

### 1.4 Fundamental Terminology (Must-Know for KTU)

- **Static Pressure ($P$):** The pressure exerted by a fluid that is **not moving** relative to the measurement point. Measured perpendicular to the surface. Units: $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$.
- **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$):** The pressure exerted by the Earth's atmosphere. Standard value: **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$**.
- **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Pressure measured **relative to atmospheric pressure** (zero is set at $P_{atm}$).
- **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Pressure measured **relative to a perfect vacuum** (true zero).
- **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$; defined as $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Relationship for KTU:**
> $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$
> Always remember this conversion. A common error is using gauge pressure where absolute is required (e.g., ideal gas law problems).

### 1.5 Standard Units of Pressure (Conversion Master Table)

| Unit Name | Symbol | Equivalent in Pascal ($\text{Pa}$) | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pascal | $\text{Pa}$ | $1$ | SI Base Unit |
| Kilopascal | $\text{kPa}$ | $10^3$ | General Engineering |
| Bar | $\text{bar}$ | $10^5$ | Industrial Gauges |
| Atmosphere | $\text{atm}$ | $101325$ | Meteorology / Standard |
| Metres of Water | $\text{m H}_2\text{O}$ | $9806.65$ | Hydraulics |
| Millimetres of Mercury | $\text{mm Hg}$ | $133.322$ | Barometers / Medical |
| PSI | $\text{lbf/in}^2$ | $6894.76$ | Imperial / US Systems |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Manometer Principle — Hydrostatic Equilibrium

A manometer is essentially a **U-tube (or modified U-tube)** filled with one or more liquids, used to measure pressure by observing the **height difference** of the manometric fluid. The governing principle is **hydrostatic equilibrium**: in a static fluid, pressure at any horizontal level within a continuous fluid is the same.

### 2.1.1 The Core Pressure-Height Equation

The fundamental relationship for any manometer calculation is:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pressure difference between two points ($\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$)
- $\rho$ = Density of the manometric fluid ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- $h$ = Vertical height difference of the fluid column ($\text{m}$)

> [!NOTE]
> The **manometric fluid** must always be **heavier (denser) than the fluid** whose pressure is being measured, and must be **immiscible** (non-mixing) with it. For water systems, **mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$)** is the standard choice.

### 2.1.2 General Manometer Rule (KTU Favourite)

When tracing pressure through a manometer, apply the following:

| Direction of Travel | Effect on Pressure |
| :--- | :--- |
| Moving **downward** through a fluid | Pressure **increases** by $\rho g h$ |
| Moving **upward** through a fluid | Pressure **decreases** by $\rho g h$ |
| Moving **horizontally** | Pressure remains **unchanged** |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award a full 7 marks only if you clearly state the **starting pressure**, identify the **manometric fluid**, and show the **direction of travel** through each leg of the manometer. Always start your solution with: *"Starting at point A and moving toward the open end..."*

---

## 2.2 Type 1: Simple U-Tube Manometer

A **U-tube manometer** consists of a glass tube bent into a U-shape, partially filled with a manometric fluid. One end is connected to the point where pressure is to be measured; the other end may be open to the atmosphere or connected to another pressure point.

### Working Principle

If pressure $P_A$ is applied at end A (denser than the manometric fluid) and end B is open:

$$P_A = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

(If the fluid above the manometric fluid is air, $\rho_f$ is negligible and the second term is dropped.)

A more general form using the U-tube connecting two points A and B:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P_A, P_B$ = Pressures at points A and B
- $\rho_m$ = Density of manometric fluid
- $\rho_f$ = Density of fluid in the pipe
- $h$ = Differential height of manometric fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **gases** above the manometric fluid, $\rho_f \approx 0$, so the equation simplifies to: $P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$

### Advantages & Limitations

| ✅ Advantages | ❌ Limitations |
| :--- | :--- |
| Simple construction, low cost | Requires large height for low pressures |
| High accuracy for high pressures | Not portable |
| No moving parts (high reliability) | Limited readability (only $\pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$) |

---

## 2.3 Type 2: Inclined Manometer (Draft Gauge)

An **inclined manometer** is a modification of the U-tube where one limb is **inclined at an angle $\theta$** to the horizontal. This provides a **magnified reading** for measuring small pressure differences (e.g., in ventilation ducts, airflow studies).

### Reading Magnification

If the inclined limb makes angle $\theta$ with horizontal, and the fluid moves a length $L$ along the inclined limb:

$$h = L \cdot \sin\theta$$

So the equivalent vertical height $h$ that would be read on a U-tube is now spread over a **larger length $L$**. The **magnification factor** is:

$$\text{Magnification} = \frac{L}{h} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta}$$

> [!EXAMPLE]
> For $\theta = 30°$: Magnification = $1/\sin(30°) = 2$. A 10 mm vertical column becomes a **20 mm** travel along the inclined tube. For $\theta = 10°$, magnification ≈ **5.76×**.

---

## 2.4 Type 3: Well-Type (Single-Leg / Cistern) Manometer

The **well-type manometer** replaces one limb of the U-tube with a **large reservoir (well)** of cross-sectional area $A_w$, while the other limb remains a narrow tube of area $A_t$.

### Key Equation

When pressure is applied, the fluid level **drops in the well** by $h_1$ and **rises in the tube** by $h_2$. Since the volume displaced from the well equals the volume gained in the tube:

$$A_w \cdot h_1 = A_t \cdot h_2$$

The **true height difference** is:

$$h = h_1 + h_2 = h_1 \left(1 + \frac{A_w}{A_t}\right)$$

If $A_w \gg A_t$ (typical: $A_w/A_t > 100$), then $h \approx h_2$, and the well-level drop is **negligible**. We can directly read $h = h_2$ with very small error.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** When $A_w \geq 100 \cdot A_t$, the manometer is called a **"well-type"** and the reading is taken as the **rise in the narrow tube only** — no need to add the well-drop correction.

---

## 2.5 Type 4: Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge (Elastic Element)

The **Bourdon tube gauge** is the **most widely used industrial pressure gauge** in the world. It uses a specially shaped metallic tube (typically C-shaped, helical, or spiral) that tends to **straighten** when internal pressure increases.

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. Fluid pressure enters the **fixed end** of the Bourdon tube.
2. The cross-section of the tube **deforms** from oval to circular (because circular cross-section has a larger area, and the tube wants to maximize volume).
3. This deformation causes the **free end** of the tube to **move outward** (straightening).
4. The free-end movement is transmitted through a **linkage** to a **pinion gear**.
5. The pinion rotates a **pointer** over a **calibrated dial** showing pressure in $\text{kPa}$, $\text{bar}$, or $\text{psi}$.

### Bourdon Tube Shape Comparison

| Shape | Pressure Range | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **C-Type** | Medium to high ($> 1 \text{ bar}$) | General industry |
| **Helical** | High pressure | Steam boilers |
| **Spiral / Twisted** | Low pressure (high sensitivity) | Gas systems |

> [!NOTE]
> The deflection $x$ of the free end of a C-type Bourdon tube is **approximately proportional** to the applied pressure $P$, provided the elastic limit of the material is not exceeded.

---

## 2.6 Type 5: Diaphragm & Bellows Gauges (Brief)

- **Diaphragm Gauge:** Uses a thin circular disc (metallic or non-metallic) clamped at its edges. Pressure causes the disc to deflect. Used for **low pressures** and **corrosive fluids**.
- **Bellows Gauge:** Uses a corrugated metallic cylinder. Larger deflection per unit pressure than diaphragms, used in **low-pressure measurement** (e.g., 0–50 kPa).

---

## 2.7 Type 6: Dead-Weight Pressure Gauge (Pressure Tester)

The **dead-weight tester** is a **primary standard** used to **calibrate** other pressure gauges. It works on **Pascal's principle**: a known weight is placed on a piston of known area, and the fluid pressure needed to **just lift** the piston equals the weight divided by the area.

$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Weight placed on piston ($\text{N}$)
- $m$ = Mass of the weights ($\text{kg}$)
- $A$ = Effective area of the piston ($\text{m}^2$)
- $g$ = $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dead-weight testers are **not for routine measurement** — they are **laboratory standards** with accuracy of $\pm 0.1\%$ or better.

---

## 2.8 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure of a fluid column | Basic manometer |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute ↔ Gauge conversion | All pressure problems |
| 3 | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Differential U-tube manometer | U-tube problems |
| 4 | $h = L \sin\theta$ | Inclined manometer vertical equivalent | Draft / low ΔP |
| 5 | $h = h_1 + h_2$, $A_w h_1 = A_t h_2$ | Well-type manometer correction | Single-leg manometer |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{m g}{A}$ | Dead-weight gauge pressure | Calibration / lab |
| 7 | $P = \rho_m g h$ (gas system) | Simplified (gas density ≈ 0) | Gas pipelines |
| 8 | $P_v = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure | Sub-atmospheric |
| 9 | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$ | Standard atmospheric conversions | Unit conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always include $\rho$ in the manometric equation. A common mistake is to compute $g \cdot h$ only, forgetting to multiply by the fluid density $\rho$ — this leads to a **3-mark deduction**.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Applications of Mechanical Gauges

- **Bourdon Tube Gauges:** Found on every compressed air tank, LPG cylinder regulator, boiler, and hydraulic press.
- **U-tube Manometers:** Used in laboratory calibration, HVAC balancing, blood pressure measurement (sphygmomanometer).
- **Inclined Manometers:** Used in mine ventilation surveys, duct airflow measurement.
- **Dead-Weight Testers:** Used by national metrology institutes (NPL, NIST) as **primary standards**.
- **Diaphragm Gauges:** Used for slurries, viscous fluids, and sanitary applications (food/pharma industry).
- **Bellows Gauges:** Used in aircraft altimeters and oxygen systems.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General U-Tube Manometer Equation (Two Fluids)

**Setup:** Two pipes A and B contain fluids of densities $\rho_A$ and $\rho_B$. A U-tube manometer with a manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ is connected between them.

```
   ┌────────┐                                 ┌────────┐
   │ Pipe A │                                 │ Pipe B │
   │ ρ_A    │                                 │ ρ_B    │
   │  P_A   │                                 │  P_B   │
   └────┬───┘                                 └───┬────┘
        │                                         │
        │   h_1 (in fluid A)                      │   h_2 (in fluid B)
        │                                         │
        ├──────────┬──────────────────────────────┤
        │          │                              │
        │          │  ρ_m                         │
        │       ┌──┴──────────────────────────┐   │
        └───────┤ ←──── h (manometric) ────→  ├───┘
                │   mercury column          │
                └────────────────────────────┘
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start at point A with pressure $P_A$.**
Move downward through fluid A by height $h_1$. Pressure increases:
$$P_1 = P_A + \rho_A \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

**Step 2: Reach the manometric fluid (mercury) interface on side A.**
This is the **left mercury level**. We are now in the manometric fluid.

**Step 3: Move horizontally across the mercury to the right mercury level.**
In a continuous static fluid, horizontal movement does **not change pressure**:
$$P_2 = P_1 \quad \text{(same horizontal level in mercury)}$$

**Step 4: Move upward through fluid B by height $h_2$ to reach point B.**
Pressure decreases as we move up:
$$P_B = P_2 - \rho_B \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

**Step 5: Combine all three equations.**

Substituting back:
$$P_B = (P_A + \rho_A g h_1) - \rho_B g h_2$$

Rearranging:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_B g h_2 - \rho_A g h_1$$

For a **symmetric** setup where $h_1 = h_2 = 0$ (both fluids start at the mercury interface), this reduces to:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(when both pipes carry gases)}}$$

And in the **general case** with fluids of differing densities and unequal heights:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h}$$

where $h$ is the manometer deflection and $\rho_f$ is the density of the lighter fluid (e.g., water or oil) lying above the manometric fluid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Well-Type Manometer Correction

**Setup:** A well-type manometer has a well of cross-sectional area $A_w$ and a tube of cross-sectional area $A_t$. The fluid rises in the tube by $h_2$ and drops in the well by $h_1$.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Volume conservation.**
The volume of fluid leaving the well equals the volume entering the tube (assuming an incompressible manometric fluid):
$$V_{well} = V_{tube}$$
$$A_w \cdot h_1 = A_t \cdot h_2$$

**Step 2: Solve for $h_1$ in terms of $h_2$:**
$$h_1 = h_2 \cdot \frac{A_t}{A_w}$$

**Step 3: Total true deflection:**
The true vertical height difference between the two free surfaces is:
$$h = h_1 + h_2$$

**Step 4: Substitute $h_1$:**
$$h = h_2 \cdot \frac{A_t}{A_w} + h_2 = h_2 \left(\frac{A_t}{A_w} + 1\right)$$

**Step 5: Final Form (with area ratio):**
$$\boxed{h = h_2 \left(1 + \frac{A_t}{A_w}\right)}$$

**Step 6: Pressure equation:**
$$P = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h_2 \left(1 + \frac{A_t}{A_w}\right)$$

**Step 7: Limiting case** — if $A_w \to \infty$ (ideal well):
$$h \to h_2 \quad \text{(well-level drop is negligible)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example:** A well manometer has $A_w = 100 \text{ cm}^2$ and $A_t = 1 \text{ cm}^2$. The tube reading is $h_2 = 25 \text{ cm}$. The **true** height is:
> $$h = 25 \times (1 + 1/100) = 25.25 \text{ cm}$$
> The error if you ignore the correction: **0.25 cm** which equals 1% — significant in precision work.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inclined Manometer Equation

**Setup:** One limb of the U-tube is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The fluid travels a length $L$ along the inclined limb.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Resolve the inclined length $L$ into vertical and horizontal components.**

The vertical rise (which is the *true hydrostatic height*) is:
$$h = L \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 2: The pressure difference is given by the standard manometric equation:**
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3: Substitute $h$ in terms of $L$:**
$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot L \sin\theta}$$

**Step 4: Magnification factor** (compared to a U-tube reading $h$):
$$M = \frac{L}{h} = \frac{L}{L \sin\theta} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta} = \csc\theta$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A draft gauge uses an inclined manometer at $\theta = 20°$ with water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The fluid travels $L = 15 \text{ cm}$ along the inclined tube. Find the pressure difference.

**Step 1: Vertical equivalent.**
$$h = L \sin\theta = 0.15 \times \sin(20°) = 0.15 \times 0.342 = 0.0513 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Pressure difference.**
$$P = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0513 = 503.2 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.503 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Magnification check.**
$$M = 1/\sin(20°) = 1/0.342 = 2.92 \times$$
A 5.13 cm vertical column has been spread over 15 cm on the scale — **nearly 3× easier to read**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dead-Weight Gauge Pressure

**Setup:** A piston of effective area $A$ carries a known total mass $m$ (piston + weights). Oil beneath the piston is pressurised until the piston is just **lifted** and floats freely.

### Derivation

**Step 1: The piston is in equilibrium when floating.**
The downward force on the piston is its weight:
$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2: The upward force** is the pressure of the oil acting on the piston area:
$$F_{up} = P \cdot A$$

**Step 3: For equilibrium** (piston on the verge of lifting):
$$P \cdot A = m \cdot g$$

**Step 4: Solve for $P$:**
$$\boxed{P = \frac{m \cdot g}{A}}$$

### Numerical Example

A dead-weight tester has a piston area $A = 1/100 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$. The total mass on the piston is $m = 5 \text{ kg}$.

**Step 1: Apply the formula.**
$$P = \frac{m \cdot g}{A} = \frac{5 \times 9.81}{10^{-6}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate.**
$$P = 49.05 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 49.05 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Convert to bar.**
$$P = 49.05 \times 10^6 / 10^5 = 490.5 \text{ bar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> By changing the weights (and therefore $m$), the same dead-weight tester can generate **any desired pressure** — making it a universal calibration standard.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Gauge

| Property | U-Tube | Inclined | Well-Type | Bourdon | Dead-Weight |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Pressure Range** | Medium | Very Low | Low–Medium | Wide | Any (Lab) |
| **Accuracy** | High | High | Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Portability** | No | Limited | No | Yes | No (Lab) |
| **Best For** | Lab | Draft | Field | Industry | Calibration |
| **KTU Exam Focus** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐ |

---

## 3.6 Sensitivity Analysis: Why Inclined Manometers Excel at Low Pressure

A standard U-tube reads a height $h$ in cm. If we are measuring very low pressures (e.g., 10 Pa), the deflection may be only **1 mm** — difficult to read accurately.

By inclining the tube at angle $\theta$, the **same vertical $h$** is now spread over a **length $L = h/\sin\theta$**, which can be 5–10 times larger. Reading errors of $\pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ in $L$ translate to a much smaller error in $h$:

$$\Delta h = \Delta L \cdot \sin\theta$$

For $\theta = 10°$, $\Delta h = 0.5 \times 0.174 = 0.087 \text{ mm}$ — a **5.7× improvement** in resolution.
