# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### ❓ Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a manometer. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a U-tube manometer.**

**Model Answer:**

A **manometer** is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing the unknown pressure against the **weight of a column of liquid** (manometric fluid). It operates on the principle of **hydrostatic equilibrium**, which states that in a static fluid, the pressure at any horizontal level within a continuous body of fluid is the same.

**Working of U-Tube Manometer:**

A U-tube manometer consists of a **glass tube bent into a U-shape** and partially filled with a manometric fluid (commonly mercury for high pressure, or water/oil for low pressure). One limb is connected to the point where pressure $P_A$ is to be measured, and the other limb may either be open to the atmosphere or connected to a second point of pressure $P_B$.

When pressure $P_A$ is applied, it pushes the manometric fluid down on the left side and up on the right side. At equilibrium, the difference in the levels of the manometric fluid in the two limbs is $h$. Applying the principle of hydrostatic equilibrium, the pressure difference is:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $\rho_m$ is the density of the manometric fluid. The reading $h$ is then converted to the required pressure unit using the above equation.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **3-mark question**, the examiner expects: (1) A clear definition, (2) A labelled sketch, and (3) The governing equation $P = \rho g h$. Skipping the sketch costs **1 mark**.

---

### ❓ Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bourdon tube pressure gauge** is a mechanical pressure measuring instrument that uses the elastic deformation of a curved metallic tube to indicate pressure.

**Construction:** It consists of a **C-shaped (or helical/spiral) metallic tube** of oval cross-section, closed at one end (free end) and open at the other (fixed end). The free end is connected through a mechanical **linkage** to a **pinion gear**, which rotates a **pointer** over a **calibrated dial**. The fixed end is connected to the fluid system whose pressure is to be measured.

**Working:** When the fluid enters the Bourdon tube, internal pressure tends to **straighten the tube** (because the oval cross-section tends to become more circular, increasing the cross-sectional area). The free end of the tube moves outward proportionally to the applied pressure. This small displacement is magnified by the linkage and transmitted to the pointer, which deflects over the calibrated dial to indicate the pressure.

The gauge reads **gauge pressure** (i.e., pressure above atmospheric). The dial is calibrated in suitable units (bar, kPa, or psi).

> [!TIP]
> A labelled sketch earns 1 mark. The cross-section note (oval → circular) is the **key conceptual point** worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

---

### ✍️ Question Choice A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the equation for the pressure difference measured by a **differential U-tube manometer** connecting two points A and B containing fluids of different densities. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two pressure taps are connected by a differential U-tube manometer using mercury. Pipe A carries water (density $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and Pipe B carries oil (density $850 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The mercury level in the limb connected to A is $0.4 \text{ m}$ below the pipe centreline, and the level in the limb connected to B is $0.55 \text{ m}$ above the pipe centreline. Calculate the pressure difference $(P_A - P_B)$ in $\text{kPa}$. Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): State the Principle.**
A differential U-tube manometer works on the principle of **hydrostatic equilibrium**. In a continuous static fluid, pressure at the same horizontal level is identical.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Set Up the Geometry.**
Consider two pipes A and B. Pipe A contains a fluid of density $\rho_A$, and Pipe B contains a fluid of density $\rho_B$. A U-tube containing a manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ (where $\rho_m > \rho_A, \rho_B$) is connected between them.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Trace Pressure from A to B.**
- Starting at A: $P = P_A$
- Move down through fluid A by height $h_1$: $P = P_A + \rho_A g h_1$
- Cross the manometric fluid (pressure unchanged horizontally at the lower interface)
- Move up through fluid B by height $h_2$ to reach B: $P_B = P_A + \rho_A g h_1 - \rho_m g h - \rho_B g h_2$

where $h$ is the differential height of the manometric fluid.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Simplify and Arrive at the Final Equation.**
Rearranging:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - \rho_A g h_1 - \rho_B g h_2$$

**Assumptions:**
- Fluids are **incompressible** and **static**.
- Manometric fluid is **immiscible** with the working fluids.
- Temperature is **uniform** (no density variation along the column).
- The tubes are **vertical** (no inclination correction needed).

**Step 5 (1 mark): Special Case.**
If both A and B carry gases, then $\rho_A = \rho_B \approx 0$, and the equation reduces to $P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h$.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\rho_A = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (water in pipe A)
- $\rho_B = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (oil in pipe B)
- $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (mercury)
- Height of mercury below centreline on A side: $h_1 = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- Height of mercury above centreline on B side: $h_2 = 0.55 \text{ m}$
- Differential mercury height: $h = h_1 + h_2 = 0.4 + 0.55 = 0.95 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 (1 mark): Identify the Manometric Equation.**
Since the mercury column straddles the centreline unequally, we must use the **general form**:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - \rho_A g h_1 - \rho_B g h_2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute Each Term.**

Term 1: Mercury column contribution
$$\rho_m g h = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.95 = 126{,}745.2 \text{ Pa}$$

Term 2: Water column on side A
$$\rho_A g h_1 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 = 3924 \text{ Pa}$$

Term 3: Oil column on side B
$$\rho_B g h_2 = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.55 = 4585.4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Combine.**
$$P_A - P_B = 126{,}745.2 - 3924 - 4585.4 = 118{,}235.8 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Final Answer.**
$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = 118.24 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Verification.**
- Sign check: $P_A > P_B$ (mercury is pushed more towards B, so A is at higher pressure). ✓
- Magnitude check: For 0.95 m of mercury, full pressure would be $\approx 127$ kPa. The correction reduces it by ~8 kPa due to water and oil columns above. Reasonable. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mistakes (Lose 2–3 Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the fluid columns above mercury** — using $P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h$ only. This over-estimates the answer by ~8.5 kPa. Cost: **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Subtracting $h_1$ and $h_2$ from $h$** — The differential height $h$ is the **sum**, not the difference. Many students write $h = 0.55 - 0.4 = 0.15$ m. This under-estimates by ~110 kPa. Cost: **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention** — if Pipe A mercury is **below** the centreline and Pipe B is **above**, then **mercury is being pushed from A towards B** → $P_A > P_B$.

---

### ✍️ Question Choice B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge**. What are the different shapes of Bourdon tubes used in practice? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dead-weight pressure tester has a piston of effective diameter $10 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the pressure generated when a total mass of $8 \text{ kg}$ is placed on the piston. If the same pressure is to be measured using a U-tube manometer with mercury, what will be the height of the mercury column? Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (a) — Construction & Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A **C-shaped (or helical/spiral) metallic tube** of oval cross-section is the heart of the gauge.
- One end (the **fixed end**) is open and connected to the fluid system.
- The other end (the **free end**) is sealed.
- A **mechanical linkage** connects the free end to a **pinion gear** at the centre of a calibrated dial.
- A **pointer** is mounted on the pinion, moving over the dial which is graduated in pressure units.
- The whole assembly is enclosed in a **circular casing** with a glass cover for dial visibility.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Fluid under pressure enters through the fixed end.
- Pressure acts on the **inner wall** of the tube. Since the tube has an oval cross-section, internal pressure tends to **make the cross-section more circular** (a circle has a larger area for the same perimeter).
- This causes the tube to **straighten** — the free end moves outward.
- The displacement of the free end is **directly proportional** to the applied pressure (within elastic limits).
- The linkage converts this small linear motion (~2–4 mm) into a **larger angular rotation** of the pointer.
- The pointer indicates the pressure on the calibrated dial.

**Different Shapes of Bourdon Tubes (1 mark):**

| Shape | Description | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **C-type** | Single curve, 250° | General purpose, medium pressure |
| **Helical** | Multi-turn helix | High pressure, large deflection |
| **Spiral** | Flat spiral, multiple turns | Low pressure, high sensitivity |

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (b) — Dead-Weight & Manometer Equivalence (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Piston diameter $d = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$
- Total mass $m = 8 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute Piston Area.**
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.01)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute Pressure from Dead-Weight.**
$$P = \frac{m g}{A} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$P = \frac{78.48}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = 999{,}236 \text{ Pa} \approx 999.24 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Equate to Manometric Equation.**
If this same pressure is balanced by a mercury column of height $h$:
$$P = \rho_{Hg} g h$$
$$h = \frac{P}{\rho_{Hg} g}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Compute Mercury Column Height.**
$$h = \frac{999236}{13600 \times 9.81} = \frac{999236}{133416}$$
$$h = 7.49 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Final Answer.**
$$\boxed{P = 999.24 \text{ kPa}, \quad h = 7.49 \text{ m of mercury}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mistakes (Lose 2–3 Marks):**
> 1. **Using radius instead of diameter** in $A = \pi d^2/4$ — gives a pressure that is **4× too small**. Cost: **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the piston mass** — In a real dead-weight tester, the piston itself has mass. The problem states "total mass of 8 kg", which is **inclusive** of the piston. Don't add a piston mass separately. Cost: **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** — area will be wrong by a factor of $10^4$, giving nonsensical answers. Always convert SI units first.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Manometer Principle:** $P = \rho g h$ — pressure equals weight of fluid column per unit area.
- ✅ **Golden Conversion:** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$. Use **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**.
- ✅ **Manometer Rule:** Pressure **increases** as you go **down** through a fluid; **decreases** as you go **up**; **unchanged** horizontally.
- ✅ **U-Tube Manometer:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$. For gases, $\rho_f \approx 0$.
- ✅ **Inclined Manometer:** $h = L \sin\theta$; **magnification** $= 1/\sin\theta$. Used for **very low pressures**.
- ✅ **Well-Type Manometer:** Volume conservation gives $A_w h_1 = A_t h_2$; if $A_w \gg A_t$, $h \approx h_2$.
- ✅ **Bourdon Tube:** Elastic straightening of a curved tube; oval → circular cross-section; deflection $\propto$ pressure.
- ✅ **Dead-Weight Gauge:** **Primary standard** for calibration; $P = mg/A$; **not for routine measurement**.
- ✅ **Mercury density:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (use this standard value unless told otherwise).
- ✅ **Water density:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 4°C).
- ✅ **Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (always use this unless $g = 10$ is explicitly allowed).

### ⚠️ Top 5 KTU Pitfalls (Valuation Losers)

1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m³ in calculations — always convert to SI units **first**.
2. **Forgetting $\rho$:** Writing $P = g h$ instead of $P = \rho g h$ — **loses 2–3 marks**.
3. **Wrong Manometric Fluid Direction:** Confusing which leg the manometric fluid rises/falls — sketch the system first.
4. **Ignoring Fluid Columns Above Mercury:** Using simplified $P = \rho_m g h$ when oil/water is above mercury.
5. **Sign Convention Errors:** Not clearly indicating whether $P_A > P_B$ or vice-versa. Always state the direction.

### 🧠 Mnemonics for the Exam

- **"Down = Up the Pressure"** — moving down through a fluid, pressure goes **up**.
- **"Mercury is Heavy, Water is Light"** — for water systems, use mercury as the manometric fluid.
- **"Bourdon Bends Back"** — internal pressure **straightens** the curved Bourdon tube (it "bends back" to its original shape attempt).
- **"Dead Weight = Direct Truth"** — dead-weight tester is a primary standard with **no calibration needed**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Total Pressure and Centre of Pressure on Surfaces: Vertical, Horizontal, Inclined, and Curved Plane Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determination of Total Pressure and Centre of Pressure on Surfaces: Vertical, Horizontal, Inclined, and Curved Plane Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Total Pressure (Hydrostatic Thrust)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Total Pressure (P)** on an immersed plane surface is defined as the resultant of all the hydrostatic pressure forces acting perpendicularly over the entire wetted area of that surface, due to a static column of fluid.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$P = \int_A p \, dA = \rho g \bar{h} A$$

where:
- $p$ = hydrostatic pressure at any elemental depth (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\rho$ = mass density of the liquid (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\bar{h}$ = vertical depth of the **centroid (G)** of the immersed area from the free liquid surface (in **m**)
- $A$ = total wetted area of the surface (in **m²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Total pressure always acts **normal (perpendicular)** to the immersed surface, regardless of its orientation (vertical, horizontal, or inclined).

## 1.2 Centre of Pressure

**Formal Definition:**
The **Centre of Pressure ($h^*$ or $P$)** is the point on the immersed surface where the entire hydrostatic thrust (Total Pressure) is assumed to act. It is essentially the line of action of the resultant pressure force.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pressing a rectangular sponge flat against the inside of a water tank wall. The water pushes on the sponge, but not uniformly — it pushes **harder at the bottom** and **less at the top** (because pressure increases with depth). If you wanted to replace all those uneven pushes with **one single equivalent push** acting at a single point, that point would be the **Centre of Pressure**. For a vertical rectangular wall, this point is always **below the centroid** (closer to the bottom) because pressure piles up downward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The Centre of Pressure **always lies below the centroid** of the immersed area (when the surface has a free liquid surface above it), because the lower regions experience greater pressure.

## 1.3 Surface Orientations — A Quick Overview

| Surface Type | Pressure Distribution | Total Pressure Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal** (flat, submerged) | Uniform | $P = \rho g h A$ (constant over area) |
| **Vertical** (wall) | Linearly increasing (triangular) | $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ |
| **Inclined** (sloped) | Varies with slant depth | $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ |
| **Curved** (arc, gate) | Resolved into H & V components | $F_H = \rho g \bar{h}_v A_v$, $F_V = W_{\text{liquid}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure — The Foundation

According to the fundamental equation of hydrostatics, the pressure at a point submerged at a vertical depth $h$ below the free liquid surface is:

$$p = \rho g h$$

This pressure acts **equally in all directions** at that point (Pascal's Law), but on a surface, the **normal component** of pressure contributes to the thrust.

## 2.2 Logical Steps to Determine Total Pressure

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometry:**
- Locate the centroid (G) of the immersed surface.
- Determine the vertical depth $\bar{h}$ of G below the free surface.

**Step 2 — Compute the Resultant Thrust:**
- Apply the general formula $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$.

**Step 3 — Locate the Centre of Pressure:**
- Use the relation $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$
- This gives the vertical depth of the centre of pressure below the free liquid surface.

**Step 4 — For Curved Surfaces, Resolve Components:**
- Horizontal force ($F_H$) = pressure force on the vertical projection.
- Vertical force ($F_V$) = weight of the liquid (real or imaginary) vertically above the curved surface.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Centre of Pressure

The total pressure $P$ acts at the centre of pressure, **not** at the centroid, because pressure is **not uniform** — it grows linearly with depth. Mathematically, the moment of the pressure distribution about the free surface intersection equals the second moment of area (moment of inertia), which shifts the resultant deeper than the centroid.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade** and aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme question patterns.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Surface |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure at depth $h$ | $p = \rho g h$ | Pa (N/m²) | All surfaces |
| 2 | Total Pressure (general) | $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | N | Vertical / Horizontal / Inclined |
| 3 | Centre of Pressure — Vertical | $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}$ | m | Vertical ($\theta = 90°$) |
| 4 | Centre of Pressure — Inclined | $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ | m | Inclined (angle $\theta$ to horizontal) |
| 5 | Centre of Pressure — Horizontal | $h^* = \bar{h}$ (top) or $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}$ (bottom) | m | Horizontal (top or bottom of tank) |
| 6 | Horizontal Force on Curved Surface | $F_H = \rho g \bar{h}_v A_v$ | N | Curved |
| 7 | Vertical Force on Curved Surface | $F_V = \rho g \times \text{Volume above curve}$ | N | Curved |
| 8 | Resultant Force on Curved Surface | $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ | N | Curved |
| 9 | Direction of Resultant | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{F_V}{F_H}$ | — | Curved |
| 10 | Moment of Inertia — Rectangle ($b \times d$) | $I_G = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | m⁴ | Plane surface |
| 11 | Moment of Inertia — Circle (radius $R$) | $I_G = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$ | m⁴ | Plane surface |
| 12 | Moment of Inertia — Triangle (base $b$, height $d$) | $I_G = \dfrac{b d^3}{36}$ | m⁴ | Plane surface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** For an **inclined surface**, the distance $\bar{h}$ in the formula is the **vertical depth** of the centroid (not the slant distance). Always project perpendicularly down to the free surface!

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Surface Type | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Dam Design** | Vertical / Inclined | Total pressure determines base sliding and overturning stability |
| **Submarine Hulls** | Curved | Resolved forces dictate hull thickness and material |
| **Tainter Gates** (hydro dams) | Curved | Vertical force = self-weight; Horizontal force = water thrust |
| **Storage Tank Walls** | Vertical | Determines wall thickness, reinforcement, and anchorage |
| **Lock Gates** | Vertical / Curved | Net forces dictate gate-opening mechanism design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Total Pressure on an Inclined Plane Surface

**Setup:**
Consider an inclined plane surface submerged in a static liquid, making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free liquid surface.

- Let the surface have total area $A$.
- Let the centroid $G$ be at slant distance $\bar{y}$ from the line of intersection of the inclined plane with the free surface.
- The vertical depth of $G$ is $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$.

**Step 1:** Consider a small elemental strip of area $dA$ at slant distance $y$ from the free-surface intersection (parallel to it).

The vertical depth of this strip is $y \sin\theta$.

**Step 2:** The hydrostatic pressure on this strip is:

$$p = \rho g \cdot (y \sin\theta)$$

**Step 3:** The elemental hydrostatic thrust on $dA$ is:

$$dP = p \cdot dA = \rho g y \sin\theta \cdot dA$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the entire immersed area to obtain the total pressure:

$$P = \int_A \rho g y \sin\theta \cdot dA$$

**Step 5:** Pull constants outside the integral:

$$P = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A y \, dA$$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $\int_A y \, dA = A \bar{y}$ (definition of centroid — first moment of area):

$$P = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot A \bar{y}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $\bar{y} = \bar{h} / \sin\theta$:

$$P = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot A \cdot \frac{\bar{h}}{\sin\theta}$$

$$\boxed{P = \rho g \bar{h} A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The total pressure formula is **universal** — it does not depend on the surface's inclination angle $\theta$. The angle $\theta$ cancels out during substitution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Centre of Pressure on an Inclined Plane Surface

**Step 1:** The centre of pressure is the point where the resultant thrust $P$ acts. Take moments of all elemental thrusts about the intersection line (free surface).

$$P \cdot y^* = \int_A y \cdot dP$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $dP = \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA$:

$$P \cdot y^* = \int_A y \cdot (\rho g y \sin\theta) \, dA$$

$$P \cdot y^* = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $\int_A y^2 \, dA = \bar{I}$ = second moment of area about the free-surface intersection line (parallel axis):

$$P \cdot y^* = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{I}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **Parallel Axis Theorem**: $\bar{I} = I_G + A \bar{y}^2$, where $I_G$ is the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis parallel to the free surface.

$$P \cdot y^* = \rho g \sin\theta \, (I_G + A \bar{y}^2)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $y^*$ (slant distance from free surface to centre of pressure):

$$y^* = \frac{\rho g \sin\theta \, (I_G + A \bar{y}^2)}{P} = \frac{\rho g \sin\theta \, (I_G + A \bar{y}^2)}{\rho g \sin\theta \cdot A \bar{y}}$$

$$y^* = \frac{I_G + A \bar{y}^2}{A \bar{y}}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify into the standard form:

$$y^* = \frac{I_G}{A \bar{y}} + \bar{y}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to vertical depth $h^* = y^* \sin\theta$. Substitute $\bar{y} = \bar{h}/\sin\theta$:

$$h^* = \left(\frac{I_G}{A \bar{y}} + \bar{y}\right) \sin\theta = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{A \bar{y}} + \bar{y}\sin\theta$$

Since $\bar{y}\sin\theta = \bar{h}$ and $\frac{\sin\theta}{\bar{y}} = \frac{\sin^2\theta}{\bar{h}}$:

$$\boxed{h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case (Vertical Surface):** When $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$, giving $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}$, the classic vertical-wall formula.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Vertical Rectangular Gate

**Problem:** A vertical rectangular gate **1.5 m wide** and **2 m high** is submerged in water with its **top edge 1 m below the free water surface**. Find the (a) total pressure on the gate and (b) position of the centre of pressure.

**Given:**
- Width $b = 1.5$ m, Height $d = 2$ m
- Depth of top edge from free surface = 1 m
- $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total Pressure:**

Step 1: Area of the gate:

$$A = b \times d = 1.5 \times 2 = 3 \, \text{m}^2$$

Step 2: Vertical depth of the centroid from the free surface:

$$\bar{h} = 1 + \frac{2}{2} = 2 \, \text{m}$$

Step 3: Apply the total pressure formula:

$$P = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 3$$

$$\boxed{P = 58860 \, \text{N} = 58.86 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Part (b) — Centre of Pressure:**

Step 1: Moment of inertia of the rectangle about its centroidal axis (horizontal):

$$I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 2^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 8}{12} = 1.0 \, \text{m}^4$$

Step 2: Apply the vertical-surface centre of pressure formula:

$$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}} = 2 + \frac{1.0}{3 \times 2} = 2 + 0.1667$$

$$\boxed{h^* = 2.1667 \, \text{m below the free surface}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The centre of pressure is **0.1667 m below the centroid** — confirming that pressure piles up at greater depths.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Inclined Circular Plate

**Problem:** A circular plate of **diameter 1.5 m** is placed inclined at **60° to the horizontal** in water. The centroid of the plate is at a vertical depth of **2.5 m** below the water surface. Find the total pressure and centre of pressure.

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 1.5 \, \text{m}$, Radius $R = 0.75 \, \text{m}$
- Inclination $\theta = 60°$
- Vertical depth of centroid $\bar{h} = 2.5 \, \text{m}$

**Solution:**

**Total Pressure:**

Step 1: Area of the circular plate:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5^2}{4} = 1.767 \, \text{m}^2$$

Step 2: Apply the formula:

$$P = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 1.767$$

$$\boxed{P = 43327.7 \, \text{N} \approx 43.33 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Centre of Pressure:**

Step 1: Moment of inertia of the circle:

$$I_G = \frac{\pi R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.75^4}{4} = 0.2485 \, \text{m}^4$$

Step 2: Apply the inclined-surface formula:

$$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} = 2.5 + \frac{0.2485 \times \sin^2 60°}{1.767 \times 2.5}$$

$$h^* = 2.5 + \frac{0.2485 \times 0.75}{4.4175} = 2.5 + 0.0422$$

$$\boxed{h^* = 2.5422 \, \text{m below the free surface}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Different Surface Types

| Surface | Pressure Variation | Total Pressure | Centre of Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal (top of tank) | Uniform $p = \rho g h$ | $P = \rho g h A$ | At the centroid (uniform) |
| Horizontal (bottom) | Uniform $p = \rho g h$ | $P = \rho g h A$ | At the centroid (uniform) |
| Vertical wall | Triangular (linearly increasing) | $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | $h^* = \bar{h} + I_G / (A \bar{h})$ |
| Inclined | Trapezoidal variation along slant | $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | $h^* = \bar{h} + I_G \sin^2\theta / (A \bar{h})$ |
| Curved | Complex — resolved into H & V | $F_H$ and $F_V$ separately | Locate by component intersection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Total Pressure & Centre of Pressure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Immersed Surface in Static Fluid] --> B{Surface Orientation?}
    B -- Horizontal --> C[Uniform Pressure Distribution]
    B -- Vertical --> D[Triangular Pressure Distribution]
    B -- Inclined --> E[Trapezoidal Pressure on Slant]
    B -- Curved --> F[Resolve into H and V Components]
    C --> G[Centroid Depth h-bar equals Centre of Pressure]
    D --> H[h-star equals h-bar plus I_G over A h-bar]
    E --> I[h-star equals h-bar plus I_G sin-square theta over A h-bar]
    F --> J[F_H equals rho g h-bar v A_v]
    F --> K[F_V equals weight of liquid above curve]
    J --> L[F_R equals sqrt F_H square plus F_V square]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Tan alpha equals F_V over F_H gives direction]
    G --> N[Final Resultant Thrust and Location]
    H --> N
    I --> N
    M --> N
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style N fill:#d4edda
    style F fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Curved Surface Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Curved Surface Geometry] --> B1[Identify Vertical Projection Area A_v]
    A1 --> C1[Identify Liquid Volume Above Curve]
    B1 --> D1[Compute F_H using rho g h-bar v A_v]
    C1 --> E1[Compute F_V using rho g times Volume]
    D1 --> F1[Resolve Vector Components]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Compute Resultant F_R]
    F1 --> H1[Compute Angle alpha using tan inverse F_V over F_H]
    G1 --> I1[Locate Centre of Pressure by Intersecting F_H and F_V lines]
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Final Design Parameters for Gate or Dam]
    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style J1 fill:#d4edda
    style F1 fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.3 Pressure Distribution Comparison Across Surface Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Liquid Surface Reference Line] --> B[Depth Measurement Starts Here]
    B --> C1[Horizontal Top: Constant Pressure Line]
    B --> C2[Horizontal Bottom: Constant Pressure Line at Higher Value]
    B --> C3[Vertical: Triangular Wedge Increasing with Depth]
    B --> C4[Inclined: Linear Variation along Slant Direction]
    B --> C5[Curved: Resolved into H Component and V Component]
    C1 --> D[Centroid of Pressure equals Centroid of Area]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> E[Centroid of Pressure below Geometric Centroid]
    C4 --> E
    C5 --> F[Two Component Forces with Independent Centres]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#d4edda
    style E fill:#fff3cd
    style F fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.4 Derivation Logic Tree — Centre of Pressure Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Submerged Plane Surface] --> S2[Choose Elemental Strip dA at Slant Distance y]
    S2 --> S3[Pressure on Strip: p equals rho g y sin theta]
    S3 --> S4[Elemental Thrust: dP equals rho g y sin theta dA]
    S4 --> S5[Integrate for Total P: P equals rho g h-bar A]
    S4 --> S6[Take Moment about Free Surface: P y-star equals rho g sin theta I-bar]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Parallel Axis Theorem: I-bar equals I_G plus A y-bar square]
    S7 --> S8[Solve for y-star: y-star equals I_G over A y-bar plus y-bar]
    S8 --> S9[Convert to Vertical Depth h-star using sin theta]
    S9 --> S10[Final: h-star equals h-bar plus I_G sin-square theta over A h-bar]
    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style S5 fill:#d4edda
    style S10 fill:#d4edda
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Conceptual Definition** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define (a) Total Pressure and (b) Centre of Pressure for a submerged surface. Why does the centre of pressure lie below the centroid for a vertical surface?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Total Pressure:** The total pressure on an immersed surface is the **resultant of all hydrostatic pressure forces** acting normally over the entire wetted area. It is given by $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$, where $\bar{h}$ is the vertical depth of the centroid and $A$ is the surface area.

**(b) Centre of Pressure:** The centre of pressure is the point on the immersed surface through which the **resultant total pressure** is assumed to act.

**Reason for lying below the centroid:** Hydrostatic pressure increases linearly with depth ($p = \rho g h$). Therefore, the lower portions of the surface experience **greater pressure** than the upper portions. This unequal distribution shifts the resultant line of action **below the geometric centroid** of the surface.

---

### **Question 2: Conceptual Application** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the formula for the total pressure on an inclined surface submerged in a liquid and explain the significance of the term $\bar{h}$.

**Model Answer:**

**Formula:** $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$

**Significance of $\bar{h}$:** The term $\bar{h}$ represents the **vertical depth of the centroid (G)** of the immersed inclined surface, measured perpendicularly down to the free liquid surface.

It is crucial to note that:
- $\bar{h}$ is the **vertical depth**, not the slant distance.
- $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$, where $\bar{y}$ is the slant distance to the centroid and $\theta$ is the angle of inclination with the horizontal.

If the inclined surface is replaced by a **horizontal projection** at depth $\bar{h}$ with the same area $A$, the total pressure on it would be **identical**. This is the basis of pressure prism method and the reason why $\bar{h}$ (not slant distance) is used.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** A vertical rectangular gate of width **2 m** and height **3 m** is installed in a dam. The top of the gate is located **2 m** below the water surface. Determine:

**(a)** The total pressure acting on the gate and the depth of the centre of pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the gate is hinged at the top, determine the **position** on the gate (measured from the top) where the resultant thrust acts. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part A(a):**

**Given:**
- Width $b = 2 \, \text{m}$, Height $d = 3 \, \text{m}$
- Depth of top edge below free surface = 2 m
- $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1:** Area of the gate:

$$A = b \times d = 2 \times 3 = 6 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Vertical depth of centroid:

$$\bar{h} = 2 + \frac{3}{2} = 3.5 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Total pressure:

$$P = \rho g \bar{h} A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times 6$$

$$\boxed{P = 206010 \, \text{N} \approx 206.01 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Step 4:** Moment of inertia about centroidal axis (horizontal axis through G):

$$I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 27}{12} = 4.5 \, \text{m}^4$$

**Step 5:** Centre of pressure (vertical depth from free surface):

$$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{h}} = 3.5 + \frac{4.5}{6 \times 3.5} = 3.5 + 0.2143$$

$$\boxed{h^* = 3.7143 \, \text{m below the free surface}}$$

---

#### **Solution to Part A(b):**

**Position from top of gate:**

The top of the gate is at depth 2 m. The centre of pressure is at depth 3.7143 m. So the distance of the centre of pressure **measured from the top of the gate** is:

$$h^*_{\text{from top}} = 3.7143 - 2 = 1.7143 \, \text{m below the top of the gate}$$

$$\boxed{h^*_{\text{from top}} = 1.7143 \, \text{m}}$$

**Alternative expression from centroid:**

$$h^*_{\text{below G}} = h^* - \bar{h} = 3.7143 - 3.5 = 0.2143 \, \text{m below centroid}$$

**Conclusion:** The resultant thrust acts at **1.7143 m below the top** of the gate, or equivalently, at **0.2143 m below the centroid** G.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A **curved gate ABC** in the form of a **quarter circle of radius 3 m** retains water as shown. The gate has a **horizontal radius at the top** and a **vertical radius at the right side**. Water depth above the top of the gate is **2 m**.

**(a)** Determine the **horizontal** and **vertical** components of the hydrostatic thrust on the gate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **magnitude** and **direction** of the resultant thrust. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part B(a):**

**Given:**
- Radius $R = 3 \, \text{m}$
- Water depth above the top of the gate, $h_0 = 2 \, \text{m}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Vertical Projection:**
The vertical projection of the curved gate is a rectangle of width $R = 3 \, \text{m}$ and height $R = 3 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 2 — Depth of Centroid of Vertical Projection:**
The centroid of this rectangle is at depth:

$$\bar{h}_v = h_0 + \frac{R}{2} = 2 + \frac{3}{2} = 3.5 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Area of Vertical Projection:**

$$A_v = R \times R = 3 \times 3 = 9 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Horizontal Force:**

$$F_H = \rho g \bar{h}_v A_v = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times 9$$

$$\boxed{F_H = 309015 \, \text{N} \approx 309.02 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Vertical Force (Weight of Liquid):**
The vertical force equals the **weight of water in the volume above the curved surface** (real water above the arc, between the arc and the vertical line at the right side of the gate).

The volume above the curved surface = Volume of the rectangular block (above the arc, from the top of the gate to the free surface) + Volume of water displaced by the quarter-circle's upper portion.

For a quarter-circle with horizontal top radius and vertical right radius, the water above the curve occupies a region bounded by:
- Top: free water surface
- Bottom: the curved surface
- Right: vertical line at $x = R$
- Left: vertical line at $x = 0$

**Volume of water above the curved surface:**

$$V = (R \times h_0) + \left(\text{Volume of water displaced above the arc}\right)$$

**Method using imaginary water concept:** The vertical force is the weight of (real water + imaginary water) where imaginary water fills the space **above** the curve but **outside** the actual water region.

The volume above the curved surface (real water) = Volume of rectangle (R × h₀) − Volume of the quarter-circle segment above the curve.

Wait — let me reconsider. The water above the curved arc is the region enclosed between:
- The free water surface (top)
- The vertical wall on the right at $x = R$
- The curved arc (bottom-left)

This volume = $R \times h_0 + \int_0^R (R - x) \, dx$ where the curve is $y = R - \sqrt{R^2 - x^2}$... 

**Simpler approach — split into two volumes:**

Volume 1: Rectangular column above the gate, width $R$, height $h_0$:
$$V_1 = R \times h_0 = 3 \times 2 = 6 \, \text{m}^3$$

Volume 2: Volume enclosed between the curved surface and the bounding rectangle (width $R$, height $R$):
This is the **imaginary water** region = Volume of rectangle ($R \times R$) − Volume of quarter-circle ($\frac{\pi R^2}{4}$)

$$V_2 = R^2 - \frac{\pi R^2}{4} = 3^2 - \frac{\pi \times 3^2}{4} = 9 - 7.0686 = 1.9314 \, \text{m}^3$$

Total volume of water above the curved surface:

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 = 6 + 1.9314 = 7.9314 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Vertical Force:**

$$F_V = \rho g V_{\text{total}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 7.9314$$

$$\boxed{F_V = 77807 \, \text{N} \approx 77.81 \, \text{kN (downward)}}$$

---

#### **Solution to Part B(b):**

**Step 1 — Magnitude of Resultant:**

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2} = \sqrt{(309015)^2 + (77807)^2}$$

$$F_R = \sqrt{95490270719 + 6053929200} = \sqrt{101544199919}$$

$$\boxed{F_R = 318660.8 \, \text{N} \approx 318.66 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Step 2 — Direction (angle with horizontal):**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{F_V}{F_H} = \frac{77807}{309015} = 0.2518$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.2518) = 14.13°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 14.13° \text{ below the horizontal}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using slant distance instead of vertical depth** for $\bar{h}$ in inclined-surface problems. The total pressure formula $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ **always uses vertical depth**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sin^2\theta$ term** in the inclined centre of pressure formula. Many students write $h^* = \bar{h} + I_G/(A\bar{h})$ for inclined surfaces, which is **wrong** — this is only valid for vertical surfaces.
>
> 3. **Confusing real and imaginary water** in curved surface problems. If the curved surface has water on the **concave side**, use **real water weight**. If on the **convex side**, use **imaginary water weight** (buoyancy concept).
>
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Always use $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², lengths in metres, and areas in m². Pressure and force will then be in Pa and N respectively.
>
> 5. **Sign convention for vertical force:** If the gate curves such that water pushes **upward** on the gate, $F_V$ is upward. If water sits **above** the gate, $F_V$ is downward (weight of water above).
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert kN to N** (or vice versa) in intermediate steps. KTU expects **consistent units** throughout the calculation.
>
> 7. **Wrong moment of inertia values:** A frequent error is using $\frac{bh^3}{12}$ where $b$ and $h$ are swapped, or forgetting that $I_G$ is about the **centroidal** axis (not the base).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Total Pressure Formula** $P = \rho g \bar{h} A$ is **universal** for plane surfaces (vertical, horizontal, or inclined).
- [x] **Centre of Pressure** always lies **below the centroid** for surfaces with a free liquid surface above.
- [x] **Vertical surface:** $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{h}}$
- [x] **Inclined surface:** $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ — note the $\sin^2\theta$ term!
- [x] **Horizontal surface:** Centre of pressure **coincides with the centroid** (uniform pressure).
- [x] **Curved surface:** Resolve force into **horizontal** ($F_H$) and **vertical** ($F_V$) components.
- [x] $F_H$ on curved surface = pressure on **vertical projection** = $\rho g \bar{h}_v A_v$.
- [x] $F_V$ on curved surface = **weight of liquid** (real or imaginary) above the curve = $\rho g \times V$.
- [x] **Resultant** $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$, **Direction** $\tan\alpha = F_V / F_H$.
- [x] **Standard Moments of Inertia** to memorize:
  - Rectangle: $I_G = bd^3/12$
  - Circle: $I_G = \pi R^4 / 4$
  - Triangle: $I_G = bh^3 / 36$
- [x] **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $\bar{I} = I_G + A y^2$ — used in deriving centre of pressure.
- [x] **Depth of centroid** for common shapes:
  - Rectangle: $d/2$ from base
  - Triangle: $h/3$ from base (or $2h/3$ from apex)
  - Circle: at the center
  - Semicircle: $4R/(3\pi)$ from the diameter
- [x] **KTU exam tip:** Always draw a clear free-body diagram, label all dimensions, and mark the centre of pressure with a separate symbol (e.g., $P^*$ or $C_P$).
- [x] **No slip between SI units:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², lengths in m, areas in m², forces in N.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy and Floatation: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy and Floatation: Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Buoyancy?

> [!NOTE]
> **Buoyancy** is the upward vertical force exerted by a fluid on a body that is either fully submerged in it or only partially immersed in it. This force is a direct consequence of the pressure difference between the lower (higher pressure) and upper (lower pressure) horizontal levels of the fluid acting on the body.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \forall_{displaced}$$

where every term is a standard engineering quantity (see the cheat sheet in Section 2 for units).

## 1.2 What is Floatation?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Floatation** is the phenomenon in which a body remains suspended at the surface of a fluid (partially immersed) or fully immersed at any depth such that the net vertical force and the net moment about any point are both zero. The body is then said to be in **hydrostatic equilibrium**.

For a floating body:

$$W = F_B \quad \text{and} \quad \sum M = 0$$

## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Concepts

**A. Buoyant Force ($F_B$)** — A force, not a property.
**B. Centre of Buoyancy (B)** — The point through which this buoyant force is assumed to act, i.e., the **centroid of the displaced volume of fluid**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a rubber ball held underwater in a swimming pool.** The moment you release it, it shoots upward with great force. Why? Because the water *below* the ball is pushing it up harder than the water *above* the ball is pushing it down. The *net* of all these tiny pressure pushes is a single upward resultant — the **buoyant force**. Now imagine a huge steel ship floating on the ocean. Steel sinks in a glass of water, yet the ship floats. The reason is **shape** — the hollow hull displaces an enormous volume of seawater, and the weight of that displaced seawater is *larger* than the weight of the steel. The sea literally cannot "fit" the ship inside, so the ship sits on top of it like a cork. This is the magic of **Archimedes' Principle**.

## 1.5 Archimedes' Principle — The Governing Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Archimedes' Principle (287–212 BC):** When a body is immersed (either fully or partially) in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts an upward buoyant force on the body equal to the **weight of the fluid displaced by the body**. The buoyant force acts vertically upward through the **centroid of the displaced volume**.

This is the **single most important statement** in this entire topic. Every formula, every stability condition, and every numerical in the KTU exam traces back to this principle.

## 1.6 Centre of Gravity (G) vs. Centre of Buoyancy (B)

| Feature | Centre of Gravity (G) | Centre of Buoyancy (B) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Point through which **weight (W)** acts | Point through which **buoyant force ($F_B$)** acts |
| Geometric meaning | Centroid of the body | Centroid of the **displaced fluid volume** |
| Depends on | Shape, mass distribution of the body | Shape of submerged portion of the body |
| Fixed/Variable | **Fixed** for a rigid body | **Shifts** as the body tilts or submerges deeper |
| Stability role | Tries to **topple** the body | Tries to **right** the body |

## 1.7 The Three States of Immersion

1. **Fully Submerged Body** — Entire body lies below the free surface. The displaced volume = volume of the body (constant). Buoyancy is independent of depth.
2. **Floating Body** — Body rests at the free surface, partially immersed. The displaced volume < volume of the body. As the body tilts, *B moves* (and may describe a curve).
3. **Suspended Body (Neutral)** — Body is fully submerged but neither rises nor sinks. This happens only when the body's mean density **equals** the fluid density.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Does Buoyancy Exist?

A fluid in static equilibrium obeys the **hydrostatic pressure equation**:

$$\frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho_f \cdot g$$

This means pressure **increases linearly with depth**. Consider a small vertical column of a submerged body. The fluid pushes on the **bottom face** with pressure $p_{bottom}$ and on the **top face** with pressure $p_{top}$. Since $p_{bottom} > p_{top}$, the *net* force on the column is **upward**, equal to the weight of the fluid column that would have occupied that same space. Integrating this column-by-column over the entire submerged volume gives the classical Archimedes result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical origin of buoyancy:** Pressure gradient in a static fluid. Not gravity on the body, not surface tension, not anything mysterious — it is purely the **vertical imbalance of hydrostatic pressure**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Buoyancy Calculation

1. Identify the **submerged volume** $V_{sub}$ of the body (or displaced volume $\forall$ for a floating body).
2. Multiply by the **mass density of the fluid** $\rho_f$ to get the mass of displaced fluid: $m_f = \rho_f \cdot V_{sub}$.
3. Multiply by **gravitational acceleration** $g$ to get the weight of displaced fluid.
4. That weight, acting upward through the centroid of $V_{sub}$, **is** the buoyant force.

$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$

## 2.3 Stability of Floating and Submerged Bodies

Stability is **NOT** the same as equilibrium. A body can be in equilibrium (no net force, no net moment) and still be unstable — like a pencil balanced perfectly on its tip.

### 2.3.1 Metacentre (M)

> [!NOTE]
> The **metacentre (M)** of a floating body is defined as the point about which the body starts oscillating when it is given a small angular displacement. Mathematically, when a floating body is tilted through a small angle $\theta$ about a horizontal axis, the buoyant force shifts to act through a new point $B'$, and the line of action of this new buoyant force intersects the original vertical centreline (through the original B and G) at the point **M**.

### 2.3.2 Metacentric Height (GM)

The vertical distance between the **Centre of Gravity (G)** and the **Metacentre (M)** is called the **metacentric height**, denoted **GM** (or sometimes $\overline{GM}$). It is the single most important parameter that determines whether a ship is safe to sail.

### 2.3.3 Stability Conditions

| Body Type | Stable | Neutral | Unstable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Submerged Body** | B is **above** G | B **coincides with** G | B is **below** G |
| **Floating Body** | **GM > 0** (M above G) | **GM = 0** (M at G) | **GM < 0** (M below G) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this table. Every KTU numerical and Part A question on this topic uses one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \forall$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes) | All problems |
| 2 | $W = F_B$ | Equilibrium of floating body | Floatation depth |
| 3 | $F_B = W + F_{\text{apparent}}$ | Apparent weight relation | Tethered / weighed bodies |
| 4 | $\gamma_f = \rho_f \cdot g$ | Specific weight of fluid | Unit conversion |
| 5 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{body}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity | Comparing densities |
| 6 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}$ | Distance B to M (transverse) | Metacentre location |
| 7 | $BM = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{V_{sub}}$ | For axis through centre of floatation | Practical ship stability |
| 8 | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Second moment of waterline area (rectangle) | Rectangular pontoons |
| 9 | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$ | Second moment for circular waterline | Cylindrical floats |
| 10 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Metacentric height of floating body | **Master formula** |
| 11 | $GM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG$ | Expanded form (most-used in exams) | **Master formula** |
| 12 | $T_{period} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ | Time period of oscillation | Ship rolling |
| 13 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\text{shift}}{GM}\right)$ | Tilt angle under off-centre load | List / shift problems |

> **Where:**
> - $\forall$ or $V_{sub}$ = displaced (submerged) volume in m³
> - $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation, in m⁴
> - $BG$ = Vertical distance from B to G (B below G is positive)
> - $k$ = Radius of gyration of the body about its longitudinal axis through G
> - $L$ = length, $B$ = width of the rectangular waterline plane

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Naval Architecture | Designing stable ships, submarines, offshore platforms |
| Civil Engineering | Design of pontoon bridges, caissons, floating docks |
| Hydraulics | Design of dams, gates, intake structures |
| Meteorology | Buoyancy-driven atmospheric circulation |
| Petroleum | Floating Storage and Offloading (FSO) vessels |
| Aerospace | Hot-air balloons (buoyancy of heated air) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Archimedes' Principle from Hydrostatic Pressure

**Statement to prove:** The buoyant force on a fully submerged body equals the weight of the fluid displaced by it.

### Setup

Consider a small elemental vertical column of the submerged body with:
- Horizontal cross-sectional area = $dA$
- Vertical extent from depth $h_1$ (top) to depth $h_2$ (bottom)

### Step 1: Pressures at the two faces

By the hydrostatic pressure equation, the pressure at depth $h$ is $p = p_{atm} + \rho_f g h$. Therefore:

$$p_{top} = p_{atm} + \rho_f g h_1$$

$$p_{bottom} = p_{atm} + \rho_f g h_2$$

### Step 2: Net vertical force on the column

The vertical force on the column is the difference between the upward push on the bottom and the downward push on the top (the side forces cancel by symmetry):

$$dF_{vertical} = p_{bottom} \cdot dA - p_{top} \cdot dA$$

$$dF_{vertical} = \left(p_{atm} + \rho_f g h_2\right) dA - \left(p_{atm} + \rho_f g h_1\right) dA$$

### Step 3: Simplify

The $p_{atm}$ terms cancel because they are the same on both faces:

$$dF_{vertical} = \rho_f g h_2 \, dA - \rho_f g h_1 \, dA$$

$$dF_{vertical} = \rho_f g \left(h_2 - h_1\right) dA$$

But $h_2 - h_1 = dz$, the height of the elemental column. So:

$$dF_{vertical} = \rho_f g \, dz \, dA = \rho_f g \, d\forall$$

where $d\forall = dz \cdot dA$ is the volume of the elemental column.

### Step 4: Integrate over the entire submerged body

$$F_B = \int_{\forall} \rho_f g \, d\forall$$

Since $\rho_f$ and $g$ are constants:

$$F_B = \rho_f g \int_{\forall} d\forall = \rho_f g \forall$$

### Step 5: Final result

$$\boxed{F_B = \rho_f g \forall = \gamma_f \forall}$$

This is the **weight of the displaced fluid** (since $\gamma_f \forall$ is the specific weight of the fluid times the displaced volume). **Archimedes' Principle is proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Position of the Metacentre (M)

**Statement to prove:** $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}$, where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation.

### Setup

Consider a floating body (e.g., a rectangular pontoon) of length $L$, breadth $B$, with the waterline initially horizontal. Let the body be tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis passing through the centroid of the waterline plane (called the **centre of floatation**, F).

### Step 1: Geometry of the tilted position

When the body tilts, the waterline cuts the body along a new plane. The wedge that gets submerged on one side and emerges on the other side has a volume $V_w$ (assumed small and equal on both sides for small $\theta$).

### Step 2: Shift of the centre of buoyancy

The new centre of buoyancy $B'$ is shifted from the original $B$ by a horizontal distance $e$. The buoyant force now acts vertically upward through $B'$. The line of action of the new buoyant force intersects the original vertical axis (through the original $B$) at the metacentre $M$.

### Step 3: Moment balance about the original vertical axis

By Archimedes, the weight of the fluid in the newly submerged wedge equals the weight of fluid in the newly emerged wedge — these must be equal in volume (and hence weight) because the total weight of displaced fluid (= weight of the floating body) must remain constant. Let this wedge volume be $V_w$.

Taking moments of the wedge weights about the original centreline:

$$F_B \cdot e = W_w \cdot \bar{x}_w - W_w \cdot \bar{x}_w'$$

The two wedges are symmetric about the centreline, so their net moment about the centreline is:

$$F_B \cdot e = 2 W_w \bar{x}$$

### Step 4: Express $W_w$ in terms of geometry

The wedge is a thin triangular prism. Its volume is:

$$V_w = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} \cdot L = \frac{1}{2} \cdot B \cdot \left(\frac{B}{2} \tan\theta\right) \cdot L$$

$$V_w = \frac{B^2 L \tan\theta}{8}$$

The centroid of a triangle from its base is at $\bar{x} = \dfrac{B}{6}$ along the base for an isoceles triangle. Combined with the length $L$:

$$\bar{x} \cdot V_w = \text{first moment of waterline area about the centreline}$$

But this is **exactly** the definition of the **first moment of area** about the axis, which when integrated over a small element gives the **second moment of area**:

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

### Step 5: Derive $BM$

For small angle $\theta$, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ and the moment balance gives:

$$F_B \cdot BM \cdot \theta = \rho_f g \cdot \theta \cdot I$$

Dividing both sides by $\theta$ and substituting $F_B = \rho_f g V_{sub}$:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}}$$

### Step 6: Final result

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} = \frac{I_{xx}}{V_{sub}}}$$

This is the **master formula** for finding the metacentre of a floating body. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Master Stability Formula $GM = BM - BG$

### Step 1: Define each segment on the centreline

Place the origin at the bottom of the body. Let:
- $G$ be at height $\bar{y}_G$ above origin
- $B$ be at height $\bar{y}_B$ above origin
- $M$ be at height $\bar{y}_M$ above origin

### Step 2: Express the three distances

$$BG = \bar{y}_G - \bar{y}_B$$

$$BM = \bar{y}_M - \bar{y}_B$$

$$GM = \bar{y}_M - \bar{y}_G$$

### Step 3: Algebraic relation

$$GM = \bar{y}_M - \bar{y}_G = (\bar{y}_M - \bar{y}_B) - (\bar{y}_G - \bar{y}_B)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{GM = BM - BG}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the value of $BM$

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG}$$

This is the **single equation** students must know to solve the majority of KTU numerical problems on floating-body stability.

### Step 5: Interpretation

- If $GM > 0$: M lies above G → **Stable** (the righting couple restores the body)
- If $GM = 0$: M coincides with G → **Neutral** (body remains in the new position)
- If $GM < 0$: M lies below G → **Unstable** (the couple overturns the body)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **fully submerged body**, B is fixed (it is the centroid of the entire body volume). So the only way to check stability is whether B lies above or below G. The metacentre concept does not even arise in the same form.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation in KTU Exam | Apply this formula |
|---|---|
| Find buoyant force on a body | $F_B = \rho_f g V$ |
| Find density of an unknown body | $SG = \dfrac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$ |
| Find depth of immersion of a floating cube | $\rho_{body} \cdot a^3 \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot a^2 \cdot h \cdot g$ |
| Find metacentre of a rectangular pontoon | $BM = \dfrac{L B^3}{12 V_{sub}}$ |
| Find metacentre of a circular float | $BM = \dfrac{\pi D^4 / 64}{V_{sub}}$ |
| Find stability of a ship | $GM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG$, then check sign |
| Find time period of oscillation | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Buoyancy & Floatation Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Body in Fluid] --> B{Body is what type?}
    B -->|Fully Submerged| C[Displaced Volume = Body Volume]
    B -->|Partially Submerged Floating| D[Displaced Volume < Body Volume]
    B -->|Suspended Neutral| E[Body Density = Fluid Density]
    C --> F[Apply Archimedes FB equals rho f g V]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Equilibrium Check}
    G -->|W greater than FB| H[Body Sinks]
    G -->|W less than FB| I[Body Rises Floats]
    G -->|W equals FB| J[Equilibrium Achieved]
    J --> K{Stability Check}
    K -->|Submerged Body| L{B above G?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Stable]
    L -->|No| N[Unstable]
    L -->|Coincide| O[Neutral]
    K -->|Floating Body| P[Compute GM equals BM minus BG]
    P --> Q{GM Sign}
    Q -->|Greater than 0| R[Stable]
    Q -->|Less than 0| S[Unstable]
    Q -->|Equal to 0| T[Neutral]
    F --> U[Compute BM equals I over Vsub]
```
