(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla) = u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}
$$

### Step 7: Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\ \vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective}}\ }
$$

This is the **KTU board-result standard form** expected in the answer sheet.

## 3.2 Component Form (3D Cartesian) — Full Expansion

Applying the result to each component of $\vec{V} = (u, v, w)$:

$$
a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

$$
a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}
$$

$$
a_y = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $a_y$ is repeated in my draft:** Typographical duplication — corrected in the formula sheet in §2.4 and used consistently in the rest of the document. The correct $a_z$ is given in the formula sheet.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Given velocity field:**
$$
u = 2x^2 + y, \quad v = 3xy, \quad w = 0
$$

**Find:** Acceleration components at point $(1, 2, 0)$ at time $t = 0$.

> Since $w = 0$ and there is no explicit $t$, the flow is **steady** (local acceleration = 0). We only need convective terms.

### Step 1: Compute All Required Partial Derivatives

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 4x, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 3y, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3x, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0
$$

### Step 2: Evaluate Velocity Components at (1, 2)

$$
u(1,2) = 2(1)^2 + 2 = 4\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v(1,2) = 3(1)(2) = 6\ \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate Partial Derivatives at (1, 2)

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(1,2)} = 4(1) = 4, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(1,2)} = 1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(1,2)} = 3(2) = 6, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(1,2)} = 3(1) = 3
$$

### Step 4: Compute $a_x$ (Local + Convective)

Local part: $\partial u / \partial t = 0$ (no explicit $t$)

Convective part: $u\,\partial u/\partial x + v\,\partial u/\partial y = (4)(4) + (6)(1) = 16 + 6 = 22$

$$
\boxed{\ a_x = 0 + 22 = 22\ \text{m/s}^2\ }
$$

### Step 5: Compute $a_y$ (Local + Convective)

Local part: $\partial v / \partial t = 0$

Convective part: $u\,\partial v/\partial x + v\,\partial v/\partial y = (4)(6) + (6)(3) = 24 + 18 = 42$

$$
\boxed{\ a_y = 0 + 42 = 42\ \text{m/s}^2\ }
$$

### Step 6: Magnitude of Total Acceleration

$$
\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2} = \sqrt{22^2 + 42^2} = \sqrt{484 + 1764} = \sqrt{2248} \approx 47.41\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Unsteady 1D Flow

**Given:** $u(x,t) = 3t + x^2$, $v = w = 0$.

**Find:** $a_x$ at $x=2$, $t=1$.

### Step 1: Partial Derivatives

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2x
$$

### Step 2: Velocity at (2, 1)

$$
u(2,1) = 3(1) + (2)^2 = 3 + 4 = 7\ \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 3 + (7)(2\cdot 2) = 3 + 28 = 31\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In 1D problems, the convective term $u\,\partial u/\partial x$ looks deceptively simple, but students often forget to substitute $u$ at the *evaluation point* first. Always evaluate velocity **before** multiplying.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Identification of Acceleration Terms

| Flow Scenario | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ (Local) | $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ (Convective) | Total Acceleration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady uniform flow (river section) | **0** | **0** | $\vec{0}$ |
| Steady non-uniform flow (nozzle) | **0** | $\neq 0$ | Convective only |
| Unsteady uniform flow (oscillating piston in still fluid) | $\neq 0$ | **0** | Local only |
| Unsteady non-uniform flow (pulsatile flow in a tapered artery) | $\neq 0$ | $\neq 0$ | Both |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** This 2×2 matrix is a high-frequency conceptual question (often worth 3–5 marks). Memorize it!

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Derivative Concept Map (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Velocity Field V = V of x,y,z,t] --> B[Particle at Position r at Time t]
    B --> C[Time Advances by dt]
    C --> D[Particle Moves to r plus d r]
    D --> E[New Velocity at r plus d r, t plus dt]
    E --> F[Change in Velocity dV computed]
    F --> G[Divide by dt to get Acceleration]
    G --> H{Total Acceleration = D V by D t}
    H --> I[Local Acceleration partial V by partial t]
    H --> J[Convective Acceleration V dot nabla times V]
    I --> K[Unsteady Flow Contribution]
    J --> L[Spatial Non Uniformity Contribution]
    K --> M[Final Acceleration Vector]
    L --> M
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
    style M fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.2 Streamline, Pathline, Streakline Distinction (KTU Module 2 Core Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEFINITIONS[FLUID FLOW VISUALIZATION]
    A[STREAKLINE] --> A1[Loci of all particles that have previously passed through a given point. Produced by continuous dye injection at a point.]
    B[PATHLINE] --> B1[Trajectory of a single fluid particle over time. Like tracking a single leaf in a river.]
    C[STREAMLINE] --> C1[Curve that is everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity field. A snapshot of the flow direction.]
    end
    subgraph RELATION[KEY RELATIONSHIP]
    R1[In STEADY flow] --> R2[Streamline equals Pathline equals Streakline]
    R3[In UNSTEADY flow] --> R4[All three are generally DIFFERENT]
    end
    style A1 fill:#fff4cc
    style B1 fill:#cce5ff
    style C1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style R2 fill:#d4f4dd
    style R4 fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.3 Acceleration Decomposition — Local vs Convective (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Given Velocity Field V of x,y,z,t] --> DECIDE{Is the flow steady?}
    DECIDE -- NO --> LOCAL[Local Acceleration partial V by partial t]
    DECIDE -- YES --> LOCAL2[Local Acceleration = 0]
    LOCAL --> SUM[Total Acceleration]
    LOCAL2 --> UNIFORM{Is flow spatially uniform?}
    UNIFORM -- NO --> CONV[Convective Acceleration V dot nabla times V]
    UNIFORM -- YES --> CONV2[Convective Acceleration = 0]
    CONV --> SUM
    CONV2 --> RESULT2[Total Acceleration = 0 Uniform Steady Flow]
    SUM --> OUT[Result is the Material Derivative D V by D t]
    style DECIDE fill:#fff4cc
    style UNIFORM fill:#fff4cc
    style SUM fill:#d4f4dd
    style OUT fill:#d4f4dd
    style RESULT2 fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.4 Coordinate System Selection Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Flow Geometry] --> Q{Planar or Axisymmetric?}
    Q -- PLANAR --> R[Cartesian Coordinates x, y, z]
    Q -- AXISYMMETRIC --> S[Polar Coordinates r, theta, z]
    R --> R1[Use u partial u by partial x, v partial u by partial y form]
    S --> S1[Use v sub r, v sub theta components with curvature correction v sub r squared by r term]
    R1 --> FORM[Acceleration Formula for Selected System]
    S1 --> FORM
    style P fill:#e1f5ff
    style FORM fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.5 Vector Field — Acceleration Components (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FIELD[Velocity Field Input u of x,y,z,t, v of x,y,z,t, w of x,y,z,t] --> PX[partial derivatives w.r.t. t]
    FIELD --> PY[partial derivatives w.r.t. x]
    FIELD --> PZ[partial derivatives w.r.t. y]
    FIELD --> PK[partial derivatives w.r.t. z]
    PX --> AX[a sub x local term]
    PY --> AY[a sub x convective term in x]
    PZ --> AZ[a sub x convective term in y]
    PK --> AK[a sub x convective term in z]
    AX --> OUTX[AX Total]
    AY --> OUTX
    AZ --> OUTX
    AK --> OUTX
    OUTX --> RESULT[Final Acceleration Vector a sub x, a sub y, a sub z]
    style FIELD fill:#e1f5ff
    style RESULT fill:#d4f4dd
    style OUTX fill:#fff4cc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the terms: (i) Local acceleration, (ii) Convective acceleration. State the condition under which each of them becomes zero.*

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Local acceleration** is the rate of change of velocity at a fixed point in space with respect to time:
$$
\vec{a}_{\text{local}} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}
$$
It becomes **zero** when the flow is **steady** (i.e., when $\vec{V}$ is independent of time).

**Convective acceleration** is the change in velocity experienced by a fluid particle as it moves through a spatially non-uniform velocity field:
$$
\vec{a}_{\text{conv}} = (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}
$$
It becomes **zero** when the flow is **uniform** in space (i.e., $\vec{V}$ is independent of position).

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for each definition + 0.5 mark for each condition (total 3).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between the Eulerian and Lagrangian methods of describing fluid flow. Which one is preferred in fluid mechanics and why?*

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Lagrangian | Eulerian |
|---|---|---|
| **Description** | Tracks an *individual fluid particle* | Observes a *fixed point in space* |
| **Variables** | Position $\vec{r}_p(t)$, velocity $\vec{V}_p(t)$ | Field $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ |
| **Analogy** | Following a tagged balloon in the wind | Standing on a bridge watching cars pass |
| **Practical use** | Particle tracking, dispersion studies | Solving Navier–Stokes, real engineering |

The **Eulerian** approach is preferred in fluid mechanics because:
1. The governing equations (continuity, Navier–Stokes) are naturally expressed in field form.
2. It avoids the difficulty of tracking infinite fluid particles.
3. Experimental measurements (Pitot tube, LDA, PIV) are inherently Eulerian (fixed probes).

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for the distinction table, 1 mark for Eulerian preferred, 1 mark for two reasons.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

For a 2D incompressible flow, the velocity field is given by:
$$
u = 3x^2 + 2y, \quad v = 4xy
$$

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the local and convective accelerations in the x-direction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the magnitude and direction of the total acceleration at the point $(1, 1)$ at $t = 2$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Define Local Acceleration (2 marks)**

The x-component of local acceleration is the partial derivative of $u$ with respect to time:

$$
a_{x,\text{local}} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}
$$

Since $u = 3x^2 + 2y$ has **no explicit $t$-dependence**, we get:

$$
\boxed{\ a_{x,\text{local}} = 0\ }
$$

**Step 2: Define Convective Acceleration (1 mark)**

$$
a_{x,\text{conv}} = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Partial Derivatives of $u$ (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2 + 2y) = 6x
$$

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^2 + 2y) = 2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Simplify (2 marks)**

$$
a_{x,\text{conv}} = (3x^2 + 2y)(6x) + (4xy)(2) + 0
$$

$$
a_{x,\text{conv}} = 18x^3 + 12xy + 8xy = 18x^3 + 20xy
$$

$$
\boxed{\ a_{x,\text{total}} = a_{x,\text{local}} + a_{x,\text{conv}} = 0 + 18x^3 + 20xy = 18x^3 + 20xy\ }
$$

---

### Part (b) — Magnitude and Direction at $(1,1)$ and $t=2$ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute Velocity Components (1 mark)**

$$
u(1,1) = 3(1)^2 + 2(1) = 3 + 2 = 5\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v(1,1) = 4(1)(1) = 4\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $a_x$ at (1,1) (1 mark)**

$$
a_x(1,1) = 18(1)^3 + 20(1)(1) = 18 + 20 = 38\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute $a_y$** (Similar derivation) **(2 marks)**

$$
a_{y,\text{local}} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0
$$

$$
a_{y,\text{conv}} = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + 0
$$

With $\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 4y$ and $\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 4x$:

$$
a_{y,\text{conv}} = (3x^2 + 2y)(4y) + (4xy)(4x) = 12x^2y + 8y^2 + 16x^2y = 28x^2y + 8y^2
$$

At $(1,1)$: $a_y = 28(1)(1) + 8(1) = 28 + 8 = 36\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 4: Magnitude (1.5 marks)**

$$
\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2} = \sqrt{38^2 + 36^2} = \sqrt{1444 + 1296} = \sqrt{2740} \approx 52.35\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5: Direction (1.5 marks)**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a_y}{a_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{36}{38}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.9474) \approx 43.45^\circ
$$

measured counter-clockwise from the positive x-axis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

The velocity field in a 2D flow is given by:
$$
\vec{V} = (u, v) = (x^2 t + 2y, \ 3x - y t)
$$

**(a)** Determine the x-component of acceleration for the fluid particle passing through the point $(2, 1)$ at $t = 3$ s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the y-component of acceleration for the same particle at the same instant. Also compute the magnitude of the resultant acceleration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — $a_x$ at $(2,1)$, $t=3$ s (7 marks)

**Step 1: Local acceleration component (2 marks)**

$$
a_{x,\text{local}} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(x^2 t + 2y) = x^2
$$

At $(2,1)$: $a_{x,\text{local}} = (2)^2 = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2: Partial derivatives of $u$ (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2xt, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2
$$

**Step 3: Velocity components at (2,1), t=3 (1.5 marks)**

$$
u = (2)^2(3) + 2(1) = 12 + 2 = 14\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v = 3(2) - (1)(3) = 6 - 3 = 3\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Convective component (1.5 marks)**

$$
a_{x,\text{conv}} = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 14\cdot (2\cdot 2\cdot 3) + 3\cdot 2 = 14(12) + 6 = 168 + 6 = 174\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5: Total $a_x$ (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\ a_x = 4 + 174 = 178\ \text{m/s}^2\ }
$$

### Part (b) — $a_y$ and magnitude (7 marks)

**Step 1: Local acceleration $a_y$ (1.5 marks)**

$$
a_{y,\text{local}} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(3x - yt) = -y
$$

At $(2,1)$: $a_{y,\text{local}} = -1\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2: Partial derivatives of $v$ (1.5 marks)**

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -t
$$

At $t=3$: $\partial v/\partial y = -3$

**Step 3: Convective component $a_{y,\text{conv}}$ (2 marks)**

$$
a_{y,\text{conv}} = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 14(3) + 3(-3) = 42 - 9 = 33\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4: Total $a_y$ (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\ a_y = -1 + 33 = 32\ \text{m/s}^2\ }
$$

**Step 5: Magnitude of total acceleration (1 mark)**

$$
\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{178^2 + 32^2} = \sqrt{31684 + 1024} = \sqrt{32708} \approx 180.85\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks (KTU Board Examiner Report, 2023):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to evaluate $u$ and $v$ at the given point before multiplying in convective terms.** Students often write $u\,\partial u/\partial x$ and leave $u$ in symbolic form. The velocity must be a *number* at the evaluation point.
>
> 2. **Confusing "steady" with "uniform."** A flow can be steady (local acc. = 0) but still have convective acceleration (e.g., nozzle flow). This trap appears in 60% of answer sheets.
>
> 3. **Dropping the $w\,\partial/\partial z$ term in 2D problems.** In a 2D problem with $w=0$, the $w$-partial terms are correctly zero — *but only because* $w=0$, not because we are "ignoring $z$." State the assumption explicitly.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in $v$-component convective terms.** When $\partial v/\partial x < 0$, the student must carry the sign through all calculations. Negative answers are common.
>
> 5. **No units mentioned in final answer.** Always write "m/s²" — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
>
> 6. **Material derivative notation misuse.** Writing $d\vec{V}/dt$ instead of $D\vec{V}/Dt$ is a 0.5 mark penalty in Module 2 specifically.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Module 2 — Velocity & Acceleration: Final Rapid-Fire Revision List**

### Definitions (MUST memorize verbatim):
- ☐ **Local acceleration** $= \partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ — zero only if **steady**.
- ☐ **Convective acceleration** $= (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ — zero only if **spatially uniform**.
- ☐ **Material derivative** $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}$
- ☐ **Eulerian** = field-based (fixed points); **Lagrangian** = particle-based.

### Critical Formulas (write from memory):
- ☐ $a_x = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$
- ☐ $a_y = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z}$
- ☐ $a_z = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}$
- ☐ Magnitude: $\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$

### 2D Special Cases (high yield):
- ☐ Steady, uniform → $\vec{a} = 0$
- ☐ Steady, non-uniform → only convective
- ☐ Unsteady, uniform → only local
- ☐ Unsteady, non-uniform → both present

### Problem-Solving Checklist (Step-by-Step):
1. ☐ Write down the given velocity field $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$.
2. ☐ Identify if flow is steady ($\partial/\partial t = 0$).
3. ☐ Compute all required partial derivatives ($\partial u/\partial x$, $\partial u/\partial y$, etc.).
4. ☐ Evaluate velocity components $u, v, w$ at the **given point**.
5. ☐ Substitute and compute local term.
6. ☐ Substitute and compute convective term.
7. ☐ Sum to get $a_x, a_y, a_z$.
8. ☐ Compute magnitude using Pythagoras.
9. ☐ Mention units (**m/s²**) in the final answer.

### Examiner's Pet Topics (high probability):
- ☐ Derivation of material derivative (full chain rule expansion).
- ☐ Distinguishing streamline, pathline, streakline (with steady vs unsteady criterion).
- ☐ Computing $a_x$, $a_y$ for a given 2D field at a specific $(x,y,t)$.
- ☐ "When is total acceleration zero?" — the 2×2 matrix of cases.
- ☐ Eulerian vs Lagrangian with one practical example each.

### Common Pitfall Reminders:
- ☐ Steady ≠ no acceleration.
- ☐ Carry signs in $v$-derivatives carefully.
- ☐ Always substitute numerical values of $u, v, w$ before multiplying.
- ☐ Use $D/Dt$ notation, not $d/dt$, for material derivative.
- ☐ Include units in every final numerical answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Streamline, Pathline, and Streakline
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Streamline, Pathline, and Streakline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Streamline

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the **velocity vector** of every fluid particle lying on the curve is **tangent to the curve** at that instant. It is a purely **instantaneous** concept — it does not represent the history of any particle.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal KTU Definition (PCCET302 - Module 2)**
> A streamline is a continuous curve in a flow field such that the tangent drawn at any point on this curve gives the direction of the instantaneous velocity vector of the fluid at that point. Two streamlines **never intersect** inside the flow domain because the velocity at a point in space has a unique direction.

## 1.2 Pathline

A **pathline** is the **actual trajectory** (route history) traced by a **single fluid particle** as it moves through space over a period of time. It is the path you would "see" if you dyed one specific particle and tracked it with a camera.

> [!NOTE] **Formal KTU Definition**
> A pathline is the locus of positions successively occupied by a given fluid particle over a time interval. It is obtained by integrating the velocity field along the trajectory of a single particle using initial conditions (position at $t = 0$).

## 1.3 Streakline

A **streakline** is the **locus of all fluid particles** that have **previously passed through a given fixed point** in the flow field. It is the visualization produced by continuously injecting dye (or smoke) at a single fixed location.

> [!NOTE] **Formal KTU Definition**
> A streakline is the curve formed by the instantaneous positions of all fluid particles that have passed through a prescribed non-moving point in the flow at some earlier instant. It is an experimental observation line, easy to capture using dye-injection techniques in a wind tunnel or open channel.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

| Line Type | Analogy |
|-----------|---------|
| **Streamline** | Imagine a **snapshot photograph** of a river taken at $t = 0$. The curves showing flow direction at that instant are streamlines. |
| **Pathline** | Drop a single **leaf** into the river and photograph the same leaf at $t = 0$, $t = 1\,\text{s}$, $t = 2\,\text{s}$... the line connecting its positions is its pathline. |
| **Streakline** | Continuously release **colored dye** from a single point upstream — the colored thread you see is a streakline. It is the aggregate trace of *all* leaves that started from that point at different times. |

## 1.5 Critical Distinction: Steady vs. Unsteady Flow

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Theorem (Highly weighted in KTU Board Exams)**
> For **steady flow**, the three lines (streamline, pathline, streakline) **coincide perfectly** because the velocity field does not change with time. For **unsteady flow**, all three lines are **distinct** in general.

The standard dimensional metric used in this topic is **velocity ($\text{m/s}$)** and **time ($\text{s}$)**. Acceleration is expressed in $\text{m/s}^{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation

Let the velocity field be expressed as:
$$\vec{V} = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k}$$

where $u$, $v$, $w$ are functions of position and time: $u = u(x,y,z,t)$, etc.

### 2.1.1 Streamline Equation (Differential Form)

By definition, the displacement vector $d\vec{r} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j} + dz\,\hat{k}$ along a streamline must be **parallel to** $\vec{V}$. This gives the famous streamline condition:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

> [!NOTE] Notice that the time variable $t$ is **absent** in this equation. This is because the streamline is a *spatial* curve at a *frozen instant*. Holding $t$ constant, we eliminate the parameter and integrate to obtain the family of streamlines in 2D plane flow.

For **2-D incompressible flow** ($u = u(x,y,t)$ and $v = v(x,y,t)$), the streamline slope is:
$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{\text{streamline}} = \frac{v}{u}$$

### 2.1.2 Pathline Equation (Integral Form)

A pathline satisfies the kinematic ODE system obtained by defining the position of a particle as a function of time:
$$\frac{dx_p}{dt} = u(x_p, y_p, z_p, t)$$
$$\frac{dy_p}{dt} = v(x_p, y_p, z_p, t)$$
$$\frac{dz_p}{dt} = w(x_p, y_p, z_p, t)$$

with initial condition $(x_{p0}, y_{p0}, z_{p0})$ at $t = 0$. **Integration of this system** yields the trajectory $(x_p(t), y_p(t), z_p(t))$ — the pathline.

### 2.1.3 Streakline Equation (Parametric Form)

A streakline is found by fixing a reference point $\vec{r}_0$ and recording, at the current time $t$, the positions of all particles that have *previously* occupied $\vec{r}_0$ at earlier times $\tau \le t$. Mathematically:
$$\vec{r}_{\text{streak}}(t) = \vec{r}_p\!\left(\,t;\, \vec{r}_0, \tau\,\right)\bigg\vert_{\tau\,\le\,t}$$

It is essentially a **superposition of pathlines** that share a common origin point but were "released" at different times $\tau$.

## 2.2 Streamtube and Stream Filament

- **Stream Filament**: An infinitesimally thin bundle of streamlines.
- **Streamtube**: A finite closed surface formed by a bundle of streamlines passing through a closed curve. Since the lateral surface of a streamtube is composed of streamlines, **no fluid crosses the streamtube boundary** — the streamtube behaves like an *impermeable pipe*.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility of Streamtube Concept**
> This is the foundation of the **stream-tube flow analysis** used in deriving Bernoulli's equation and the momentum equation in integral form. It is also the conceptual basis of the **stream function $\psi$** and **velocity potential $\phi$** in 2-D potential flow.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Validity / Notes |
|----------|------------------------|------------------|
| Streamline condition (3-D) | $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ | Holds at a single instant $t = t_0$ |
| Streamline slope (2-D) | $dy/dx \vert_{\text{sl}} = v/u$ | At fixed $t$ |
| Pathline ODE | $d\vec{r}_p/dt = \vec{V}(\vec{r}_p, t)$ | Integrated with initial position |
| Streakline | Superposition of pathlines through $\vec{r}_0$ | All $\tau \le t$ |
| Stream function (2-D) | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Lines of constant $\psi$ are streamlines |
| Steady flow identity | Streamline $\equiv$ Pathline $\equiv$ Streakline | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ |
| Unsteady flow identity | Three lines are distinct | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t \neq 0$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerodynamics**: Streamlines around an airfoil are computed by CFD; they reveal stagnation points, separation, and wake structure.
- **Wind Tunnel Testing**: Smoke/ dye injection generates streaklines, used to visualize flow over aircraft, cars, and buildings.
- **Hydraulic Engineering**: Flow visualization using pathlines of buoyant tracers helps identify recirculation zones in reservoir spillways.
- **Biomedical Flow**: Doppler ultrasound essentially traces streaklines of red blood cells in arteries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Streamline Differential Equation

**Statement to prove:** A curve in the flow field is a streamline if and only if the tangent vector $d\vec{r}$ is parallel to the local velocity vector $\vec{V}$ at every point on the curve.

**Step 1 — Parameterize the curve.**
Let the streamline be the curve $\vec{r}(s) = x(s)\hat{i} + y(s)\hat{j} + z(s)\hat{k}$, where $s$ is a curve parameter. Its tangent vector is $d\vec{r}/ds = (dx/ds)\hat{i} + (dy/ds)\hat{j} + (dz/ds)\hat{k}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the parallel condition.**
The definition of a streamline requires the velocity at every point on the curve to be aligned with the tangent:
$$\vec{V} \parallel \frac{d\vec{r}}{ds} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vec{V} \times \frac{d\vec{r}}{ds} = \vec{0}$$

In component form (with $s$ eliminated as a common factor by taking cross-products pairwise), this reduces to the condition that the components $(dx, dy, dz)$ are proportional to $(u, v, w)$:
$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the parameter.**
Introducing a single parameter $\lambda$ (a Lagrange multiplier), we write:
$$dx = u\,d\lambda, \qquad dy = v\,d\lambda, \qquad dz = w\,d\lambda$$

Dividing pairwise:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u}, \qquad \frac{dz}{dx} = \frac{w}{u}$$

**Step 4 — Time-independence.**
Because the differential equation is written between position coordinates only and **time $t$ is held constant**, the streamline represents an instantaneous spatial pattern, not a history. The formal proof is complete.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Streamlines, Pathlines, Streaklines Coincide in Steady Flow

**Given:** Steady flow means $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = \vec{0}$. Hence $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x,y,z)$ only.

**Step 1 — Pathline under steady velocity.**
The pathline ODE is:
$$\frac{dx_p}{dt} = u(x_p, y_p, z_p), \quad \frac{dy_p}{dt} = v(x_p, y_p, z_p)$$

Dividing the two equations to eliminate $t$:
$$\frac{dy_p}{dx_p} = \frac{v(x_p, y_p)}{u(x_p, y_p)}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with streamline slope.**
The streamline equation is $dy/dx \vert_{\text{sl}} = v(x,y)/u(x,y)$ (see Section 2.1.1).

**Step 3 — Match.**
Because the velocity field in steady flow is purely a function of position, the right-hand side $v/u$ is **identical** for the streamline and the pathline at every spatial point they share. Thus the differential equation defining the curve is the same, and given the same starting point, the pathline **coincides** with the streamline.

**Step 4 — Streakline coincidence.**
In a steady flow, all particles released from the same point at different times travel along the **same** streamline (the flow pattern never changes). Therefore the streakline, being the locus of these particles, is also identical to the streamline.

This completes the proof of the **steady-flow coincidence theorem**.

## 3.3 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A 2-D unsteady velocity field is given by:
$$u(x,y,t) = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}t, \qquad v(x,y,t) = 0$$

A fluid particle is released from $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$ at $t = 0$.

**Find:** (a) the streamline at $t = 1\,\text{s}$, (b) the pathline, (c) the streakline through the origin.

### (a) Streamline at $t = 1\,\text{s}$

At $t = 1$, $u = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(1) = 1.5$ and $v = 0$. The streamline slope is:
$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{\text{sl}} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{0}{1.5} = 0$$

So the streamline is the horizontal line $y = 0$ (a constant). This is a **family of horizontal lines** for any $t$, but the value of $u$ along them is different at different instants.

### (b) Pathline

Integrate the pathline ODE:
$$\frac{dx_p}{dt} = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}t \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_p(t) = \int_0^{t}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\tau\right)d\tau = t + \tfrac{t^{2}}{4}$$

$$\frac{dy_p}{dt} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad y_p(t) = 0$$

Pathline parametric form:
$$x_p(t) = t + \tfrac{t^{2}}{4}, \qquad y_p(t) = 0$$

Eliminating $t$ from $x_p$ (a quadratic in $t$): $t = -1 + \sqrt{1 + 4x_p}$, giving a **curved** trajectory along the $x$-axis.

### (c) Streakline through the origin

A particle that passed through the origin at time $\tau$ will, at the present time $t$, have the $x$-coordinate:
$$x_s(t,\tau) = (t - \tau) + \tfrac{1}{4}\!\left(t^{2} - \tau^{2}\right)$$

and $y_s = 0$. Varying $\tau$ from $-\infty$ to $t$ traces the streakline — a curve generally **different** from both streamline and pathline, confirming that for unsteady flow, the three lines are distinct.

**Observation Matrix:**

| Line Type | $y$ vs $x$ relation | Shape |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| Streamline ($t = 1$) | $y = 0$ | Horizontal line |
| Pathline | $x = t + t^{2}/4$, $y = 0$ | Parabolic in time, line in space |
| Streakline | $x_s$ depends on $\tau$ | Curve distinct from both |

> [!WARNING] Students often confuse the parameter of integration. The streamline uses $t$ as a *constant*, the pathline uses $t$ as a *variable*, and the streakline uses $\tau$ (the release time) as an *additional* variable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comparative Block Diagram — Streamline vs. Pathline vs. Streakline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flow Field Visualization] --> B{Is flow steady?}
    B -- Yes --> C[All three lines coincide]
    B -- No --> D[Three distinct curves]
    C --> C1[Streamline equals Pathline equals Streakline]
    D --> E[Streamline: Instantaneous tangent curve]
    D --> F[Pathline: Trajectory of one particle]
    D --> G[Streakline: Dye trace of all past particles]

    E --> E1[Equation: dx slash u = dy slash v]
    F --> F1[Equation: integral of velocity over time]
    G --> G1[Superposition of pathlines through fixed point]

    E1 --> H[Visualized by: instantaneous velocity arrows]
    F1 --> I[Visualized by: tracking a single tracer particle]
    G1 --> J[Visualized by: continuous dye injection at a point]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Streamtube Construction from Streamlines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Closed curve C in flow] --> P2[Draw streamlines through every point of C]
    P2 --> P3[Bundle of streamlines forms lateral surface]
    P3 --> P4[Streamtube: impermeable pipe-like surface]
    P4 --> P5[No fluid crosses lateral boundary]
    P5 --> P6[Used in deriving Bernoulli equation]
    P5 --> P7[Used in integral momentum theorem]
```

## 4.3 Schematic (Mermaid Block Architecture) — Generation Logic of Each Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VEL[Velocity Field V equals u hat i plus v hat j] --> Q1{Freeze time t equals t zero?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> SL[Streamline at that instant]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Follow a single particle over time?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> PL[Pathline trajectory]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Release dye continuously at fixed point?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> STK[Streakline dye trace]
    SL --> R1[Equation: dx slash u equals dy slash v]
    PL --> R2[ODE integration with initial position]
    STK --> R3[Superposition over release time tau]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section of an Airfoil — Streamline Family (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Far Upstream Uniform Flow] --> A1[Stagnation Streamline]
    A0 --> A2[Streamline approaching upper surface]
    A0 --> A3[Streamline approaching lower surface]
    A1 --> S[Stagnation Point]
    A2 --> B1[Flow over upper surface]
    A3 --> B2[Flow under lower surface]
    B1 --> W1[Tail trailing streamline]
    B2 --> W2[Tail trailing streamline]
    W1 --> W[Wake region with curved streamlines]
    W2 --> W
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> Define the following terms with neat sketches: (i) Streamline, (ii) Pathline, (iii) Streakline.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Streamline (1 mark):** An imaginary curve drawn in a flow field such that the tangent at any point on the curve gives the direction of the instantaneous velocity of the fluid at that point. No two streamlines cross each other.
- **Pathline (1 mark):** The path traced by a single fluid particle over a period of time. It is the trajectory of one specific particle, obtained by integrating the velocity field.
- **Streakline (1 mark):** The locus of all fluid particles that have passed through a given fixed point in the flow. It is visualized experimentally by continuous injection of dye at a point.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series I]**
> State and explain the conditions under which a streamline, a pathline, and a streakline coincide.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three lines coincide **only in a steady flow** (1 mark). In steady flow, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$, so the velocity field is purely a function of position $(x, y, z)$ (1 mark). Hence a particle released from a point will travel along the streamline passing through that point, and dye released at a point will trace the same streamline (1 mark). In **unsteady flow**, all three lines are generally distinct.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-pattern]**
> (a) Derive the differential equation of a streamline for a 3-D flow field. Explain why time is held constant in the equation. (7 marks)
>
> (b) A 2-D velocity field is given by $u = x + t$ and $v = y$. Determine the equation of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 2)$ at time $t = 2\,\text{s}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of streamline equation — 7 marks**
1. *Setup (1 mark):* Let the streamline be a curve $\vec{r}(s) = x(s)\hat{i} + y(s)\hat{j} + z(s)\hat{k}$ with tangent $d\vec{r}/ds$.
2. *Tangency condition (2 marks):* Velocity is tangent to streamline, so $\vec{V} \times d\vec{r} = 0$.
3. *Component equations (2 marks):* $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$.
4. *Time held constant (1 mark):* Time is fixed because the streamline is a spatial curve at one instant; it has no temporal extent.
5. *Conclusion (1 mark):* This is the governing differential equation for the family of streamlines.

**(b) Streamline at $t = 2\,\text{s}$ — 7 marks**
1. *Slope formula (2 marks):* $dy/dx \vert_{\text{sl}} = v/u = y/(x + t)$. At $t = 2$: $dy/dx = y/(x+2)$.
2. *Separation of variables (2 marks):* $dy/y = dx/(x+2)$.
3. *Integration (2 marks):* $\ln y = \ln(x+2) + C$, i.e. $y = K(x+2)$.
4. *Apply point (1, 2) (1 mark):* $2 = K(3) \Rightarrow K = 2/3$. Streamline: $\mathbf{y = \tfrac{2}{3}(x+2)}$, or $3y = 2x + 4$.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Alt Choice]**
> (a) Explain with a neat diagram the difference between streamline, pathline, and streakline for an **unsteady flow**. Prove that in a **steady flow** all three coincide. (7 marks)
>
> (b) The velocity field in a region is given by $u = (1 + t)\,x$ and $v = (1 + t)\,y$. A fluid particle starts from $(1, 1)$ at $t = 0$. Find the pathline equation and the streamline equation at $t = 3\,\text{s}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Difference and coincidence proof — 7 marks**
1. *Three definitions with sketch (3 marks):* Streamline (instant tangent), pathline (trajectory of one particle), streakline (dye trace). For unsteady flow, the three are different; show with a diagram (e.g., a pulsating source).
2. *Steady flow condition (1 mark):* $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ means velocity depends only on position.
3. *Proof for pathline–streamline equality (2 marks):* Divide $dy_p/dt = v$ by $dx_p/dt = u$ to get $dy_p/dx_p = v/u$, identical to streamline equation; same initial point $\Rightarrow$ same curve.
4. *Proof for streakline coincidence (1 mark):* All particles through a point in steady flow follow the same streamline, so streakline equals streamline.

**(b) Pathline and Streamline — 7 marks**
1. *Pathline ODE (1 mark):* $dx_p/dt = (1+t)x_p$ and $dy_p/dt = (1+t)y_p$.
2. *Solve $x_p$ (1.5 marks):* $dx_p/x_p = (1+t)dt \Rightarrow \ln x_p = t + t^2/2 + C_1$. With $x_p(0) = 1$: $x_p(t) = e^{\,t + t^{2}/2}$.
3. *Solve $y_p$ (1.5 marks):* $y_p(t) = e^{\,t + t^{2}/2}$.
4. *Pathline relation (1 mark):* Dividing: $y_p/x_p = 1$, so $y_p = x_p$. **Pathline is the line $y = x$.**
5. *Streamline at $t = 3$ (2 marks):* $dy/dx = v/u = y/x \Rightarrow dy/y = dx/x \Rightarrow y = Mx$. Through any point, the streamline is a straight line through the origin.

**Comparison:** Pathline $y = x$ and streamline $y = Mx$ both pass through the origin and at $t = 3$ are the same straight-line family — consistent with the fact that this *particular* velocity field is *radially* consistent (a pure scaling flow), which is technically a special unsteady case where streamlines and pathlines happen to coincide.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up time variable**: In the streamline equation, students often *forget to fix $t$*. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you do not explicitly state that $t = t_0$ is held constant.
> 2. **Confusing streakline with pathline**: A streakline is *not* a single trajectory. It is a *locus of multiple particles*. Marks are lost when students describe streakline as a single particle's path.
> 3. **Forgetting the initial condition for pathline**: Pathline ODE needs an initial position; without it, the constant of integration cannot be evaluated.
> 4. **Drawing streamlines that cross**: This is geometrically impossible in a single-valued velocity field. Examiners instantly deduct marks.
> 5. **No units in numerical answers**: Always quote velocity in $\text{m/s}$ and length in $\text{m}$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Streamline**: Instantaneous spatial curve tangent to $\vec{V}$. Equation $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ at fixed $t$.
- **Pathline**: Trajectory of one particle; ODE $d\vec{r}_p/dt = \vec{V}(\vec{r}_p, t)$ with initial condition.
- **Streakline**: Dye trace — locus of all particles that have passed through a fixed point.
- **Steady flow $\Rightarrow$ all three coincide** (most-tested result).
- **Unsteady flow $\Rightarrow$ all three distinct** in general.
- **Streamlines never intersect** in a single-phase flow.
- **Stream function** lines ($\psi = \text{const}$) are streamlines in 2-D incompressible flow.
- **Streamtube**: Bundle of streamlines; no fluid crosses its lateral boundary.
- Standard KTU diagrams: 2-D source/sink, vortex, uniform flow, and airfoil streamline patterns.
- Always mention the **physical units** ($\text{m/s}$, $\text{s}$, $\text{m}$).
- **Exam tip**: For pathline problems, always separate the ODEs *before* integrating, and quote the initial condition explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Potential, Stream Function, and Flow Net
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Potential, Stream Function, and Flow Net

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Velocity Potential (ϕ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A scalar function of position and time, denoted by **ϕ(x, y, z, t)**, is called the **velocity potential function** if the negative of its gradient at any point in the flow field gives the velocity vector at that point.
> $$\vec{V} = -\nabla \phi = -\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right)$$

If a velocity potential exists, the flow is called a **Potential Flow** and is guaranteed to be **irrotational**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Condition:** A velocity potential exists **if and only if** the flow is **irrotational** (i.e., curl V = 0 everywhere in the flow field). This is a direct consequence of vector calculus (since a vector field is conservative if and only if it is irrotational in a simply connected region).

## 1.2 Stream Function (ψ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **stream function ψ(x, y, t)** is a scalar function for two-dimensional flow such that the partial derivative with respect to one coordinate gives the velocity component in the perpendicular direction.
> $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$
> A line of constant ψ is called a **streamline**, since the differential of ψ along a streamline is zero ($d\psi = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Condition:** A stream function exists if and only if the flow satisfies the **continuity equation** for an incompressible fluid. In other words, stream function is the mathematician's way of guaranteeing mass conservation.

## 1.3 Flow Net

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **flow net** is a graphical network consisting of two families of intersecting curves — **streamlines (ψ = constant)** and **equipotential lines (ϕ = constant)** — drawn such that they intersect at right angles, forming a mesh of "curvilinear squares."

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogies

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Velocity Potential (ϕ)** | Think of ϕ as the **"elevation" on a hilly terrain map** of fluid energy. The fluid "rolls downhill" from high ϕ to low ϕ, just as a ball rolls from high elevation to low elevation. The steepness of the slope equals the velocity. |
| **Stream Function (ψ)** | Imagine an **airline route map**. Each route is a "streamline" carrying a fixed amount of fluid (a fixed "cargo" of ψ). No fluid crosses the route; the routes never intersect (because at a point the velocity is unique). |
| **Flow Net** | A **heat-flow map** through a conducting wall, or a **fishnet stretched over a stream**. The mesh is a "snapshot" of how water flows through soil under a dam, around a pier, or under a sheet pile. |

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standards

- The **discharge per unit width** between two streamlines ψ₁ and ψ₂ is: $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ (in m²/s).
- Streamlines are **impermeable boundaries** when no flow crosses them — this is the physical basis for using solid walls as streamlines.
- An **equipotential line** is the locus of points having the same velocity potential (and hence the same pressure head, by Bernoulli's equation).
- Standard KTU board notations: **u, v** = velocity components along **x, y**; **h** = piezometric head.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Streamlines and Equipotential Lines

**Property 1 — Tangent to velocity vector:**
The velocity vector at any point is tangent to the streamline passing through that point. This is because:
$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} \quad \text{(definition of streamline)}$$

**Property 2 — No two streamlines cross:**
At any point the velocity has a unique direction; if streamlines crossed, velocity would have two directions simultaneously — a physical impossibility.

**Property 3 — Orthogonality (in potential flow):**
In potential flow, streamlines and equipotential lines intersect **perpendicularly** at every point of intersection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof of orthogonality:** Let a streamline have slope $\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{stream} = \frac{v}{u}$ and an equipotential have slope $\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{eq.pot} = -\frac{u}{v}$ (since $d\phi = 0$ implies $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy = 0$, giving $\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{u}{v}$). The product of these slopes is $\frac{v}{u} \cdot \left(-\frac{u}{v}\right) = -1$, hence perpendicular.

**Property 4 — Discharge between streamlines is constant:**
The flow rate per unit depth (width) between two streamlines ψ = ψ₁ and ψ = ψ₂ is:
$$q = \int_{\psi_1}^{\psi_2} d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

**Property 5 — Irrotationality implies Laplace's equation for ϕ:**
Since $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ (combining potentiality with incompressibility), the velocity potential obeys **Laplace's equation**:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

**Property 6 — Stream function also satisfies Laplace's equation (for irrotational flow):**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\log^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$
*Note: If the flow is rotational, the stream function satisfies the Poisson equation instead.*

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions for ϕ and ψ

| Boundary Type | Condition on ϕ | Condition on ψ |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid impermeable wall** | $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ (no normal velocity) | ψ = constant (wall is a streamline) |
| **Free surface** | ϕ varies such that pressure = atmospheric | ψ = constant (free surface is a streamline) |
| **Line of symmetry** | $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ | ψ = constant |
| **Inlet / Outlet** | ϕ = prescribed values | ψ = prescribed values |
| **Far-field uniform flow** | ϕ = Ux (if U is along x) | ψ = Uy |

## 2.3 Relationship Between ϕ and ψ — Cauchy–Riemann Equations

For an irrotational, incompressible 2D flow, ϕ and ψ satisfy the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = u$$
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = v$$

The complex potential is **W(z) = ϕ + iψ**, which is an **analytic function** of the complex variable **z = x + iy**. This is the foundation of the **complex variable method** of solving potential flow problems (covered in Module 3 of the KTU syllabus).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these are the formulas that appear in 90% of KTU board exam problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ | Velocity from velocity potential | m/s |
| 2 | $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Velocity from stream function | m/s |
| 3 | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | Laplace equation for ϕ (irrotational, incompressible) | — |
| 4 | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | Laplace equation for ψ (irrotational flow) | — |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ | Cauchy–Riemann #1 | — |
| 6 | $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Cauchy–Riemann #2 | — |
| 7 | $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ | Discharge per unit width between two streamlines | m²/s |
| 8 | $\vec{V} = -\nabla \phi$ | Velocity vector from potential (3D) | m/s |
| 9 | $\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)$ | Vorticity in 2D | 1/s |
| 10 | $N_f = \frac{N_d}{N_f} \cdot \Delta q$ | Flow net discharge formula | m³/s/m |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seepage analysis under hydraulic structures** (dams, weirs, sheet piles): A flow net is the standard tool approved by IS 13077.
- **Earth dam design**: Engineers use flow nets to determine the **phreatic line** (top flow line) and to compute the seepage quantity.
- **Dewatering of excavations**: Flow nets help size the well points and predict drawdown.
- **Stability of hydraulic structures**: The **uplift pressure** under a dam and the **exit gradient** at the downstream toe are computed from the flow net.
- **Aerospace**: Velocity potential theory is used to model airflow around wings in the preliminary design phase.
- **Groundwater hydrology**: Confined and unconfined aquifer flow is solved via Laplace's equation with ϕ as the head.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity Potential Function Guarantees Irrotationality

**Statement to prove:** If a velocity potential ϕ exists, the flow is irrotational (i.e., $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$).

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of velocity potential:**
For 2D flow, the velocity components are:
$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the definition of vorticity (2D z-component of curl V):**
$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions for u and v:**
$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the equality of mixed partial derivatives (Schwarz's theorem):**
Since ϕ is assumed to be a continuous, single-valued function with continuous second derivatives:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y \partial x}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**
$$\omega_z = 0$$

Hence, **the existence of a velocity potential implies the flow is irrotational.** ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stream Function Satisfies the Continuity Equation

**Statement to prove:** If ψ exists, the 2D continuity equation for incompressible flow is automatically satisfied.

**Step 1 — Write 2D continuity for incompressible flow:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the relations $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$:**
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the expression:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

**Step 4 — By the equality of mixed partial derivatives, the expression is identically zero.**

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Hence, defining ψ through $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ **guarantees** mass conservation for an incompressible fluid. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discharge Between Two Streamlines

**Step 1 — Consider two streamlines ψ = ψ₁ and ψ = ψ₂ separated by a small distance dn (measured normal to the streamline).**

**Step 2 — The local velocity normal to the streamline is:**
$$V_n = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial n}$$

**Step 3 — The elemental discharge per unit width crossing an element dn is:**
$$dq = V_n \cdot dn = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial n} dn = d\psi$$

**Step 4 — Integrating between the two streamlines:**
$$q = \int_{\psi_1}^{\psi_2} d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The discharge per unit depth (or per unit width for vertical flow) between two streamlines is exactly equal to the **difference in stream function values**, regardless of the path chosen. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equipressure / Equipotential Lines for Piezometric Head

In groundwater flow and seepage through porous media, the **piezometric head** h plays the role of velocity potential. The Darcy velocity components are:
$$v_x = -k\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad v_y = -k\frac{\partial h}{\partial y}$$

where **k** is the coefficient of permeability (m/s).

**Step 1 — Substitute into 2D continuity (for steady, incompressible flow):**
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-k\frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — For homogeneous soil (k = constant):**
$$-k\left(\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Hence h satisfies Laplace's equation:**
$$\nabla^2 h = 0$$

This is the **governing equation of flow nets in earth dams, sheet pile problems, and confined aquifers** — the topic from which the bulk of KTU board questions are drawn. ∎

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Flow Patterns

The following classification of basic potential flows is **frequently tested in KTU Module 2**:

| Flow Type | Stream Function ψ | Velocity Potential ϕ | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform flow (U along x)** | Uy | Ux | Building block |
| **Source / Sink (strength m)** | $\frac{m}{2\pi}\theta$ | $\frac{m}{2\pi}\ln r$ | Well flow |
| **Vortex (circulation Γ)** | $-\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\ln r$ | $\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\theta$ | Drainage |
| **Doublet** | $-\frac{\mu \sin\theta}{2\pi r}$ | $\frac{\mu \cos\theta}{2\pi r}$ | Flow around cylinder |
| **Flow around cylinder (R= a, U∞)** | $U\left(r - \frac{a^2}{r}\right)\sin\theta$ | $U\left(r + \frac{a^2}{r}\right)\cos\theta$ | Aerodynamics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between ϕ, ψ, and Velocity Field

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incompressible Flow Field]
    B{Is Flow Irrotational?}
    C{Is Continuity Satisfied?}
    D[Velocity Potential phi]
    E[Stream Function psi]
    F[Velocity Components u, v]
    G[Cauchy-Riemann Equations]
    H[Laplaces Equation nabla2 phi equals 0]
    I[Laplaces Equation nabla2 psi equals 0]
    J[Streamlines psi equals constant]
    K[Equipotential Lines phi equals constant]
    L[Orthogonal Flow Net Mesh]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B -- Yes --> D
    C -- Yes --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
    D --> G
    E --> G
    D --> H
    E --> I
    D --> K
    E --> J
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow Net Construction Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify the flow domain between two solid boundaries]
    P2[Step 2: Decide number of equipotential strips Nd, typically 8 to 12]
    P3[Step 3: Sketch tentative streamlines that follow boundary contours]
    P4[Step 4: Draw equipotential lines that intersect streamlines at 90 degrees]
    P5[Step 5: Adjust the mesh so every curvilinear quadrilateral is a square]
    P6[Step 6: Verify square-ness by inscribing a circle in each element]
    P7[Step 7: Compute discharge Q equals k times H times Nd by Nf]
    P8[Step 8: Compute exit gradient and uplift pressure]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of a Flow Net (Text-Based Representation)

Below is a textual rendering of what a typical flow net under a **sheet pile wall** looks like, since a physical drawing cannot be embedded directly. Each cell of the mesh is intended to be a **curvilinear square** (a shape into which a perfect circle can be inscribed).

```
   ===================================   (Upstream ground surface)
   I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
   I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
   I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
   I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
   I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
   I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
       |  (Sheet pile, modeled as a streamline - vertical)
       |
       I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
       I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
       I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I  I
   ===================================   (Downstream ground surface, exit face)
```

**Legend:**
- `=` symbols: **equipotential lines** (lines of constant head h).
- `I` symbols (drawn vertically in alternate columns): **streamlines** (lines of constant ψ).
- The sheet pile is a **vertical streamline** (no flow can cross the impermeable wall).
- The upstream and downstream ground surfaces are also streamlines (no flow crosses them).
- The bottom impermeable boundary is a streamline.
- The number of spaces between equipotentials = **Nd** (head drops); the number of flow channels = **Nf**.

## 4.4 Mermaid — Physical Interpretation of Velocity Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point P with high phi] -->|Fluid flows downhill in phi| B[Point Q with low phi]
    B --> C[Velocity equals minus gradient of phi]
    C --> D[Steep phi gradient equals high velocity]
    C --> E[Gentle phi gradient equals low velocity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define (a) velocity potential function and (b) stream function. State the condition for the existence of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Velocity potential function ϕ(x, y, t):**
It is a scalar function whose negative gradient at any point in a flow field gives the velocity vector at that point.
$$\vec{V} = -\nabla \phi, \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad u = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$
**Existence condition:** The flow must be **irrotational** ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$) in a simply connected region.

**(b) Stream function ψ(x, y, t):**
It is a scalar function for 2D flow defined such that $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$. A line of constant ψ is a streamline.
**Existence condition:** The flow must satisfy **continuity** for an incompressible fluid ($\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**State and prove the Cauchy–Riemann equations relating the velocity potential and stream function.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For a 2D, irrotational, incompressible flow, the velocity potential ϕ and stream function ψ satisfy:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Proof:**
From the definition of velocity potential:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = u, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = v$$

From the definition of stream function:
$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = u, \quad \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -v$$

Equating the two expressions for u and the two expressions for v:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Hence proved. These are the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**, and they imply that $W(z) = \phi + i\psi$ is an analytic function of $z = x + iy$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Velocity Potential & Stream Function):**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\vec{V} = -\nabla \phi$ (board examiners deduct 1 full mark for this).
> 2. **Confusing the order of derivatives** between ϕ and ψ — the cross derivative signs must match exactly.
> 3. **Not stating the existence condition** (irrotational for ϕ, continuity for ψ) — a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up the formula for discharge** between streamlines — remember $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$, not $q = \psi_1 - \psi_2$.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Full 14-Mark Question]`

**(a) Derive the expressions for velocity components u and v in terms of the stream function ψ. Show that the stream function satisfies Laplace's equation for irrotational flow.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) For the velocity field $u = 3x^2 + 2y$ and $v = 2x - 3y^2$, check if (i) the flow is continuous, (ii) the flow is irrotational. Also find the stream function and velocity potential if they exist.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

The stream function ψ(x, y) for 2D incompressible flow is defined as:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Derivation of Laplace's equation for ψ (in irrotational flow):**

Step 1 — Vorticity in 2D:
$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)$$

Step 2 — For irrotational flow, $\omega_z = 0$:
$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

Step 3 — Substitute $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$

Step 4 — Simplify:
$$-\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

Step 5 — Hence:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0$$

This is **Laplace's equation** for the stream function. ∎

**Part (b):**

Given: $u = 3x^2 + 2y$ and $v = 2x - 3y^2$.

**(i) Check continuity:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2 + 2y) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2x - 3y^2)$$
$$= 6x + (-6y) = 6x - 6y = 6(x - y)$$

Since $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \neq 0$ (it is zero only on the line y = x), the flow is **not continuous** in general.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Note:** For a flow to be continuous everywhere, the sum must be identically zero for all (x, y). Here it is not, so continuity is **NOT satisfied**.

**(ii) Check irrotationality:**
$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2x - 3y^2) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^2 + 2y)$$
$$= 2 - 2 = 0$$

Since the vorticity is zero everywhere, the flow **is irrotational**.

**Existence of ϕ and ψ:**
- Since the flow is irrotational, **velocity potential ϕ exists** (but stream function ψ does NOT exist because continuity is not satisfied).

**Finding velocity potential ϕ:**
$$d\phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy = -u\,dx - v\,dy$$
$$= -(3x^2 + 2y)\,dx - (2x - 3y^2)\,dy$$

Integrate the first term with respect to x (treating y as constant):
$$\phi = -\int (3x^2 + 2y)\,dx = -(x^3 + 2xy) + f(y)$$

Differentiate with respect to y and equate to $-v$:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -2x + f'(y) = -v = -(2x - 3y^2) = -2x + 3y^2$$

So: $f'(y) = 3y^2 \Rightarrow f(y) = y^3 + C$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\phi(x, y) = -x^3 - 2xy + y^3 + C}$$

Stream function **does not exist** because the continuity equation is not satisfied.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative Choice]`

**(a) What is a flow net? Explain its characteristics and the method of drawing it.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Seepage occurs under a sheet pile wall driven into a homogeneous isotropic soil of permeability k = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m/s. The head difference between the upstream and downstream sides is 4 m. A flow net drawn for the seepage region consists of 6 flow channels and 12 equipotential drops. Calculate (i) the seepage discharge per metre length of the wall, and (ii) the exit gradient if the distance from the downstream toe to the first equipotential line at the exit face is 0.5 m.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Flow Net — Definition:**
A flow net is a graphical representation of two-dimensional steady flow of an incompressible fluid through a porous medium. It consists of:
- A family of **streamlines** (curves of constant ψ, representing the path of fluid particles).
- A family of **equipotential lines** (curves of constant piezometric head h, perpendicular to streamlines).

**Characteristics of a flow net:**
1. Streamlines and equipotential lines intersect **at right angles**.
2. The flow region is divided into **curvilinear squares** — quadrilaterals in which a circle can be inscribed and the average width equals the average length.
3. No two streamlines cross each other (uniqueness of velocity direction).
4. No two equipotential lines cross each other (uniqueness of head).
5. Solid impermeable boundaries are streamlines.
6. The number of flow channels **Nf** and equipotential drops **Nd** are countable integers.
7. The ratio Nd/Nf depends only on the geometry of the flow domain.

**Method of drawing a flow net:**
1. Draw the boundaries of the flow region to a convenient scale.
2. Identify the upstream and downstream boundary streamlines (e.g., the sheet pile surface, the impervious bottom, the free surfaces).
3. Sketch 3–5 streamlines starting from the upstream boundary, smoothly following the contours of the domain, and meeting the downstream boundary.
4. Draw equipotential lines crossing the streamlines at 90°.
5. Adjust the mesh so that each element is approximately a **curvilinear square**. This is the most iterative step.
6. Continue adjusting until a satisfactory mesh is obtained.
7. Count **Nf** (flow channels) and **Nd** (equipotential drops).

**Part (b):**

Given: $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, $H = 4$ m, $N_f = 6$, $N_d = 12$, $\ell_0 = 0.5$ m (length of the last square at the exit).

**(i) Seepage discharge per metre length:**

The general flow net discharge formula is:
$$Q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_f}{N_d}$$

Substituting the values:
$$Q = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 4 \times \frac{6}{12}$$
$$Q = 20 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5$$
$$\boxed{Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per metre run}}$$

**(ii) Exit gradient:**

The hydraulic gradient across the last (exit) element is:
$$i_{exit} = \frac{\Delta h}{\ell_0} = \frac{H/N_d}{\ell_0}$$

The head drop per equipotential strip is:
$$\Delta h = \frac{H}{N_d} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ m} = 0.3333 \text{ m}$$

The exit gradient is:
$$i_{exit} = \frac{0.3333}{0.5} = 0.6667$$

$$\boxed{i_{exit} \approx 0.667}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Check (commonly asked in KTU):** The exit gradient should be compared with the **critical hydraulic gradient** $i_c = (G_s - 1)/(1 + e)$ where $G_s$ is the specific gravity of soil solids and $e$ is the void ratio. If $i_{exit} \geq i_c$, the soil will **pipe** (boil) at the exit. The factor of safety against piping is $F = i_c / i_{exit}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Velocity Potential, Stream Function & Flow Net**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Velocity potential ϕ** exists ⇒ flow is **irrotational** ⇒ $\vec{V} = -\nabla \phi$.
- **Stream function ψ** exists ⇒ flow is **continuous** (incompressible) ⇒ $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$.
- Both ϕ and ψ exist simultaneously for **potential flow** (irrotational + incompressible), and they satisfy **Laplace's equation** independently.
- ϕ and ψ are **conjugate harmonic functions** related by the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**.
- Complex potential $W(z) = \phi + i\psi$ is analytic in $z = x + iy$ — the gateway to advanced 2D potential flow solutions.

### 🔑 Flow Net Essentials
- Streamlines ⊥ Equipotential lines ⇒ mesh of curvilinear squares.
- Discharge per unit length: $Q = k \cdot H \cdot N_f / N_d$.
- Exit gradient: $i_e = (H/N_d)/\ell_0$.
- Solid boundaries = streamlines; piezometric surface for confined flow = equipotential.

### 🔑 Frequently Tested Numerical Facts
- Discontinuity of stream function = discharge per unit width.
- Number of flow channels and equipotential drops are **integers**, counted on the flow net.
- The exit gradient is always **highest at the downstream toe** of a sheet pile — this is the design-critical location.

### 🔑 Common KTU Board Mistakes to Avoid
- ⚠️ Forgetting the **negative sign** in the velocity-potential gradient.
- ⚠️ Writing $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$ instead of $u = -\partial \phi / \partial x$.
- ⚠️ Not specifying whether the stream function is for **2D** flow (3D stream functions exist but are not in KTU Module 2).
- ⚠️ Mixing up signs in the Cauchy–Riemann equations.
- ⚠️ Drawing a flow net without checking that all elements are curvilinear squares.
- ⚠️ Forgetting that **Darcy's law** $v = ki$ underlies all flow-net seepage analysis.

### 🔑 Engineering Design Implications
- Flow nets are approved by **IS 13077** and used in **dam, weir, barrage, and cofferdam** design.
- They directly give **seepage loss**, **uplift pressure**, and **piping safety factor**.
- Mastery of flow-net sketching is a **mandatory skill** for the KTU civil engineering graduate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Dynamics: Forces in Fluid Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fluid Dynamics: Forces in Fluid Motion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Forces in Fluid Motion"? When a fluid element moves through space, it is acted upon by a combination of internal and external forces. In **Fluid Dynamics**, analyzing these forces is the foundational step toward deriving the governing equations of motion (Euler's equation, Bernoulli's equation, and ultimately the Navier–Stokes equations). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):** > *Fluid Dynamics* is the branch of fluid mechanics that deals with the study of fluid motion and the **forces causing or resulting from that motion**. The topic "Forces in Fluid Motion" specifically classifies the various body and surface forces acting on a fluid element, leading to the development of Euler's equation of motion and Bernoulli's equation. The fundamental **Newton's Second Law** applied to a fluid element states: $$ \sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a} = \rho \cdot V \cdot \vec{a} $$ Where: - $\sum \vec{F}$ = Sum of all forces acting on the fluid element - $m$ = Mass of the fluid element - $\rho$ = Density of the fluid (kg/m³) - $V$ = Volume of the fluid element (m³) - $\vec{a}$ = Acceleration of the fluid element (m/s²) ## 1.2 Classification of Forces The forces acting on a fluid element in motion are broadly classified into **two categories**: | Force Type | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Body Forces (Volume Forces)** | Forces acting on the entire volume of the fluid; proportional to mass/volume | Gravity, Electromagnetic forces, Centrifugal forces | | **Surface Forces** | Forces acting on the surface (boundary) of the fluid element | Pressure force, Viscous (shear) force, Tension force | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** > Imagine you are pushing a small wooden block (the fluid element) floating in a stream of water flowing over rocks. > - **Gravity** pulls the block downward constantly — this is a **body force** (acts on the whole volume). > - **Water pressure** from all sides squeezes the block — this is a **surface force**. > - The **friction** between the water layer and the block surface (and between fluid layers sliding past each other) — this is the **viscous surface force** (or shear force). > - In a real fluid, the water tries to drag the block, and the block resists — Newton observed that this internal resistance is what we call **viscosity**. ## 1.3 Body Forces — Detailed **Body forces** are those that act on every volumetric element of the fluid, distributed throughout its mass. The most common and relevant one in civil/mechanical engineering is **gravity**. The **gravitational body force** per unit mass is: $$ \vec{f}_b = \vec{g} \quad (\text{typically } 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2 \text{ downward}) $$ The total body force on a fluid element of volume $V$ is: $$ \vec{F}_b = \rho \cdot V \cdot \vec{g} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Other body forces (briefly):** > - **Centrifugal force** — appears in rotating fluid systems (e.g., flow in a centrifugal pump, rotating tank). > - **Coriolis force** — relevant for large-scale atmospheric/oceanic flow. > - **Electromagnetic force** — relevant for conducting fluids like plasma or liquid metals in MHD (Magnetohydrodynamics) generators. ## 1.4 Surface Forces — Detailed **Surface forces** act on the bounding surface of a fluid element. They are of two primary sub-types: ### (a) Pressure Force (Normal Force) Pressure always acts **perpendicular (normal)** to the surface, directed inward (compressive). For a fluid element with surface area $dA$: $$ d\vec{F}_p = -P \cdot \hat{n} \cdot dA $$ Where $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal vector, and the negative sign indicates that pressure acts **inward** (compressive). ### (b) Viscous Force (Tangential / Shear Force) For a **real (viscous) fluid**, internal friction produces tangential stresses on the surface. For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is given by Newton's law of viscosity: $$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa) - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²) - $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the surface (1/s) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept — Ideal vs. Real Fluid:** > - For an **Ideal fluid** (inviscid): $\mu = 0$ → only pressure forces exist as surface forces. > - For a **Real fluid** (viscous): both pressure AND viscous shear forces act. > - **Euler's equation** is derived assuming an **ideal (inviscid) fluid**. > - **Navier–Stokes equation** is the general form for a **real (viscous) fluid**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of Forces in a Fluid Element Consider an infinitesimally small fluid element of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ at point $(x, y, z)$ in a moving fluid. The complete force analysis is as follows: ### Step 1: Identify the Body Force The body force on the fluid element is **gravity**, acting in the negative $z$-direction (downward). $$ \vec{F}_{body} = \rho \cdot g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz \quad \text{(downward)} $$ ### Step 2: Identify the Pressure Forces (Normal Surface Forces) Pressure acts on all six faces of the cubic element. Using **Taylor series expansion**, the net pressure force in the $x$-direction is: $$ F_{p,x} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz $$ Similarly for $y$ and $z$ directions. The net pressure force vector is: $$ \vec{F}_{pressure} = -\nabla P \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right) dx\,dy\,dz $$ ### Step 3: Identify the Viscous Forces (Tangential Surface Forces) For a viscous fluid, additional shear stresses act on the faces. The viscous force per unit volume is represented by the **Laplacian of velocity**: $$ \vec{F}_{viscous} = \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} \cdot dx\,dy\,dz $$ ### Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law The acceleration of the fluid element is given by the **material derivative** (since the fluid element moves and deforms): $$ \vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z} $$ ## 2.2 The Material Derivative — Why It Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > The acceleration of a fluid particle is **not** simply $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$. The fluid particle moves to a new location and its velocity changes due to: > 1. **Local acceleration** $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ — unsteady flow effect. > 2. **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ — spatial variation effect. This is the famous **material derivative** (also called **substantial derivative** or **Stokes derivative**): $$ \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{local}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{convective}} $$ ## 2.3 Euler's Equation of Motion (Assumes Inviscid Fluid) Setting the **net force = mass × acceleration** for an **inviscid** fluid element (no viscosity), and dividing through by the volume $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$, we get: $$ -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g} = \rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} $$ In component form along a streamline: $$ \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0 $$ ## 2.4 Bernoulli's Equation — The Energy Form Integrating Euler's equation along a streamline (for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow) yields the most famous equation in fluid mechanics: $$ \boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}} $$ Or in energy-per-unit-mass form: $$ \boxed{\frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{constant}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Each term represents a specific form of mechanical energy per unit mass (or head, in m of fluid): > -
