> The integrating for the time to empty between two levels is algebraically complex. KTU's preferred simplification: use $H$ = head over the *bottom* of the orifice, with the formula valid as $H$ ranges from $H_{initial}$ to $H_{final}$ where $H_{final} \geq D$ (orifice still has water over the top).

**Step 3:** Use the bottom-reference form.

- Initial head above bottom: $H_{initial} = 2.5 + 0.5 = 3.0 \ \text{m}$
- Final head above bottom: $H_{final} = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5 \ \text{m}$

The time to empty a tank with a large rectangular orifice (free discharge) from head $H_i$ to head $H_f$ above the *bottom of the orifice* is:

$$t = \frac{3 \cdot A_{tank}}{C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \int_{H_f}^{H_i} \frac{dH}{(H^{3/2} - (H - D)^{3/2})}$$

> This integral does not have a closed form in general. KTU examination problems typically use the **approximation** that the orifice behaves like a small orifice with effective head $H - D/2$ (head over centroid), giving:

$$t \approx \frac{2 A_{tank}}{C_d b \sqrt{2g}} \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{H_{f,centroid}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{H_{i,centroid}}}\right]$$

where $H_{i,centroid} = H_i - D/2$ and $H_{f,centroid} = H_f - D/2$.

- $H_{i,centroid} = 3.0 - 0.25 = 2.75 \ \text{m}$
- $H_{f,centroid} = 1.5 - 0.25 = 1.25 \ \text{m}$

$$t = \frac{2 \times 3}{0.62 \times 0.8 \times 4.4294} \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1.25}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.75}}\right]$$

$$t = \frac{6}{2.1970} \left[\frac{1}{1.1180} - \frac{1}{1.6583}\right]$$

$$t = 2.7308 \times [0.8944 - 0.6030]$$

$$t = 2.7308 \times 0.2914$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.796 \ \text{s} \ (\text{approx})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use the small-orifice emptying formula $t = \frac{2A}{C_d A_o \sqrt{2g}} \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{H_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{H_1}}\right]$ directly. For a large orifice, the appropriate modification is to use the head over the *centroid* of the opening as the effective head. Otherwise the answer is dimensionally inconsistent.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given (fully submerged case):**
- Net head difference: $H = 1.0 \ \text{m}$
- Orifice height: $D = 0.5 \ \text{m}$, width: $b = 0.8 \ \text{m}$
- $C_d$ for fully submerged ≈ use same $C_d = 0.62$ (or note that fully submerged $C_d$ is typically $0.80$–$0.85$; KTU commonly keeps $C_d$ as given)

**Step 1:** Apply the fully-submerged formula:

$$Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot D \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.0}$$

$$Q = 0.248 \times \sqrt{19.62}$$

$$Q = 0.248 \times 4.4294$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.099 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 3:** Compare.

- Free discharge with the same upstream level ($2.5 \ \text{m}$ above top) gives a much larger discharge (as computed in part a of Question A).
- Submerged discharge is *less* than free discharge at the same upstream head, because part of the head is "used up" to push back against the downstream water.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ A **large orifice** is one where the head above the top edge is **less than 5 times the height of the orifice** ($H < 5D$).
- ✅ The **velocity varies as $\sqrt{h}$** across the opening — this is why integration is required.
- ✅ **Free discharge formula** (MUST memorise): $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g}\left[H_2^{3/2} - H_1^{3/2}\right]$
- ✅ The factor $\dfrac{2}{3}$ arises from integrating $h^{1/2}$ — examiners love to ask where it comes from.
- ✅ **Fully submerged** formula: $Q = C_d b D \sqrt{2gH}$ where $H$ is the *difference* in upstream and downstream levels, and $D = H_2 - H_1$.
- ✅ **Partially submerged** = **free zone (integrate) + submerged zone (simple product)**.
- ✅ The coefficient $C_d$ is **constant** across the entire orifice — it is a geometric property.
- ✅ For a *free* large orifice, $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$; for a *fully submerged* large orifice, $C_d \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$ (less contraction downstream).
- ✅ When using the time-of-emptying formula for a tank with a large orifice, use the **head over the centroid** of the orifice as the effective head in the simplified formula.
- ✅ Always verify the *downstream level* before deciding which case applies — this is the single most common source of errors in KTU papers.
- ✅ Units check: $C_d$ is dimensionless, $b$ in m, $\sqrt{2g}$ in $\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$, $H^{3/2}$ in $\text{m}^{3/2}$ $\Rightarrow$ $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time of Emptying a Rectangular Tank Through an Orifice at Its Bottom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time of Emptying a Rectangular Tank Through an Orifice at Its Bottom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time of emptying a tank** is defined as the total time required for the entire volume of liquid stored in a tank (reservoir) to flow out completely through a small opening (orifice, notch, or weir) fitted at the bottom, side, or at any other convenient location, under the action of **gravity** alone.

A **rectangular tank** is one in which the cross-sectional area of the liquid surface remains **constant** at every horizontal level — that is, the tank has vertical parallel walls. When such a tank is emptied through an orifice fixed at its bottom, the head of liquid above the orifice **continuously decreases** with time, and the corresponding rate of discharge also decreases.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCET302 – Fluid Mechanics (Module 3: Kinematics of Flow & Orifice/Notch/Weir)**, the standard discharging formula and its integration yield the governing expression for the emptying time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)**
> - **Orifice**: A small opening of regular shape (circular, rectangular, or triangular) in the wall/bottom of a tank whose edges are sharp, so that the contracted jet has a cross-section smaller than the opening.
> - **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)**: A dimensionless constant that accounts for frictional and contraction losses, typically **0.60 – 0.65** for a standard sharp-edged orifice.
> - **Head (H or h)**: Vertical height of the free liquid surface above the centre line of the orifice, measured in **metres (m)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a large rectangular water-storage tank in your house (similar to an overhead sump). At the bottom, there is a small tap (orifice) that you open to drain the water. Have you ever observed that:

- When the tank is **completely full**, the water gushes out **very fast**.
- As the level **falls**, the outflow becomes **slower and slower**.
- Near the bottom, the flow is a mere **trickle**.

This is because the **velocity of efflux** is governed by **Torricelli's Law**, which states that the exit velocity depends on the *square root* of the head of water above the orifice. As the head drops, velocity drops non-linearly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Bucket with a Hole" Experiment**
> Take a uniform cylindrical bucket, pierce a small hole near the base, and fill it with water. Cover the hole. When you uncover it, the bucket takes a *finite* time to drain — not infinite, but more than you would intuitively expect from the initial flow rate. This is because the discharge rate is **proportional to √h**, so the average rate is much less than the maximum (initial) rate. Mathematically, we must **integrate** over the falling head to get the total time.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $C_d$ | Coefficient of discharge | **0.60 – 0.65** (orifice) |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of tank | **m²** |
| $a$ | Area of orifice | **m²** |
| $H$ | Initial head above orifice | **m** |
| $h$ | Instantaneous head | **m** |
| $t$ | Time | **seconds (s)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Stepwise Logic

The phenomenon of emptying a tank involves three coupled physical processes that must be analysed simultaneously:

1. **Energy Conversion (Potential → Kinetic):**
   As the liquid surface is at a height $h$ above the orifice, each unit mass of liquid loses potential energy $gh$ and gains kinetic energy $\frac{v^2}{2}$. By Bernoulli's principle (assuming inviscid flow):

   $$v_{\text{theoretical}} = \sqrt{2gh} \quad \text{(Torricelli's Theorem, 1643)}$$

2. **Real-Fluid Correction (Coefficient of Velocity, $C_v$):**
   Viscous and turbulent losses inside the orifice reduce the actual exit velocity:

   $$v_{\text{actual}} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

3. **Jet Contraction (Coefficient of Contraction, $C_c$):**
   The jet contracts just downstream of a sharp-edged orifice. The vena-contracta has area $a_c = C_c \cdot a$. Combining with the coefficient of velocity:

   $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v \quad \text{(and the actual discharge becomes } Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}\text{)}$$

## 2.2 The Unsteady Discharge Equation

Since the head $h$ **decreases** as time progresses, the discharge $Q$ is **not constant**. In an infinitesimal time $dt$:

- Volume that leaves the tank = $Q \, dt = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$
- Volume that leaves the tank = $-A \cdot dh$ (negative because $h$ decreases)

Equating both expressions yields the **fundamental differential equation of tank emptying**:

$$-A \cdot dh = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

This is a **first-order separable ODE** that integrates to give the total emptying time.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Theoretical velocity of efflux (Torricelli) | m/s |
| 2 | $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | Actual (real) discharge through orifice | m³/s |
| 3 | $t = \dfrac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$ | Time to empty a **rectangular / constant-area** tank from head $H_1$ to $H_2$ | seconds |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{2A \cdot \sqrt{H}}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$ | Time for **complete emptying** of rectangular tank ($H_2 = 0$) | seconds |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{2A}{C_d \cdot a} \left( \sqrt{\dfrac{H_1}{2g}} - \sqrt{\dfrac{H_2}{2g}} \right)$ | Alternative compact form using head-to-velocity conversion | seconds |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{2A \cdot H^{3/2}}{3 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$ | Emptying time of **horizontally tapering** tank (area $\propto h$) — *spillway/inlet* | seconds |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{2A \cdot H^{5/2}}{5 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$ | Emptying time of **vertically tapering** tank (area $\propto h^2$) — *pyramidal/conical* | seconds |
| 8 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Relation between discharge, contraction, and velocity coefficients | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> This analysis is used in the design of:
> - Dams and **spillways** (rate of reservoir drawdown).
> - **Cooling tanks** in thermal power stations.
> - **Sumps and overhead reservoirs** in water-supply schemes.
> - **Fuel tanks** of rockets and aircraft (gravity-fed systems).
> - **Lock-chambers** in navigation canals.

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions for the Standard Formula

| Condition | Value of $H_2$ | Use Case |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| Complete emptying | $H_2 = 0$ | Tank drains fully |
| Partial emptying to a depth | $H_2 = h$ | Practical controlled drainage |
| Filling of a tank (reverse) | $H_2 < 0$ conceptually | Time to fill a tank from a higher reservoir |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Time to Empty a Rectangular Tank

### Step 1: Set Up the Geometry

Consider a rectangular tank of constant horizontal cross-sectional area $A$ (m²), with a sharp-edged orifice of area $a$ (m²) fitted at the bottom. Let:

- $H_1$ = initial head of liquid above the centre line of the orifice (m).
- $H_2$ = final head of liquid above the orifice (m). For *complete emptying*, $H_2 = 0$.
- $h$ = instantaneous head at time $t$ (m).
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge of the orifice.

### Step 2: Apply Torricelli's Law

The theoretical velocity of the jet as it emerges from the orifice is obtained by equating the pressure head at the orifice to the velocity head of the jet. Using Bernoulli's equation between the free surface (point 1) and the vena-contracta of the jet (point 2), both at atmospheric pressure:

$$0 + 0 + h = 0 + \frac{v^2}{2g} + 0$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v_{\text{theoretical}} = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 3: Express the Actual Discharge

Because of viscous losses and jet contraction, the actual discharge is less. It is given by:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 4: Write the Volume Balance in an Infinitesimal Time $dt$

In a small interval $dt$, the head falls by an amount $dh$ (a *negative* quantity because $h$ decreases). The volume of liquid that leaves the tank can be expressed in two equivalent ways:

**Way 1 — From the drop in tank level:**

$$dV = -A \cdot dh$$

The negative sign is crucial: as $h$ decreases ($dh < 0$), the volume leaving is positive.

**Way 2 — From the rate of discharge through the orifice:**

$$dV = Q_{\text{actual}} \cdot dt = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

### Step 5: Equate and Rearrange

$$-A \cdot dh = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

Separating the variables $h$ and $t$:

$$dt = -\frac{A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}}$$

### Step 6: Integrate Between Limits

We integrate $t$ from $0$ to $T$ (the total emptying time) and $h$ from $H_1$ down to $H_2$. Since $h$ decreases, the limits of $h$ are placed carefully:

$$T = \int_{0}^{T} dt = -\frac{A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \int_{H_1}^{H_2} \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}}$$

The negative sign is absorbed by reversing the limits of integration:

$$T = \frac{A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \int_{H_2}^{H_1} h^{-1/2} \, dh$$

### Step 7: Evaluate the Integral

Using the standard result $\int h^{-1/2} \, dh = 2\sqrt{h}$:

$$T = \frac{A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left[ 2\sqrt{h} \right]_{H_2}^{H_1}$$

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)\,}$$

### Step 8: Special Case — Complete Emptying

When $H_2 = 0$ (full drainage to the orifice level):

$$T_{\text{full}} = \frac{2A \cdot \sqrt{H_1}}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$$

Equivalently:

$$T_{\text{full}} = \frac{A \cdot H_1 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{gH_1}} = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a} \sqrt{\frac{H_1}{2g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful Interpretation:** The factor $\sqrt{H_1/2g}$ has units of *velocity inverse* and represents the *time* that would be required to discharge the initial volume if the velocity remained at the initial value $v_0 = \sqrt{2gH_1}$. The factor 2 in the numerator reflects the fact that the actual velocity decays as $\sqrt{h}$, so the *effective* average velocity is exactly **half** the initial velocity. Thus, the total emptying time is **twice** the time that would be needed at a constant initial rate.

### Step 9: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A rectangular tank of size 4 m × 3 m in plan holds water to a depth of 5 m. An orifice of diameter 100 mm is fitted at the bottom. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Find the time required to empty the tank completely.

**Solution:**

- $A = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ m}^2$
- $a = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $H_1 = 5 \text{ m}$, $H_2 = 0$
- $C_d = 0.62$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

Applying the formula:

$$T = \frac{2 \times 12 \times \sqrt{5}}{0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81}}$$

$$T = \frac{24 \times 2.2361}{0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.4294}$$

$$T = \frac{53.666}{2.1567 \times 10^{-2}} = 2488.2 \text{ s}$$

Converting to minutes: $T \approx 41.47 \text{ minutes}$.

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 41.47 \text{ min} \approx 2488 \text{ seconds}\,}$$

### Step 10: Derivation for a Horizontally Tapering Tank (Area $\propto h$)

For a tank whose horizontal cross-section grows **linearly** with height, $A(h) = k \cdot h$ for some constant $k$. The volume balance becomes:

$$-k \cdot h \cdot dh = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

$$dt = -\frac{k \cdot h \cdot dh}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}} = -\frac{k \cdot \sqrt{h}}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \, dh$$

Integrating:

$$T = \frac{k}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \int_{H_2}^{H_1} \sqrt{h} \, dh = \frac{k}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \left( H_1^{3/2} - H_2^{3/2} \right)$$

Since $k = A/H$ at the top (i.e., $A = kH$):

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{2A}{3 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \frac{H_1^{3/2} - H_2^{3/2}}{H_1} \right)\,}$$

For full emptying ($H_2 = 0$):

$$T = \frac{2A \cdot \sqrt{H_1}}{3 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$$

### Step 11: Derivation for a Vertically Tapering Tank (Area $\propto h^2$)

For a tank where $A(h) = k \cdot h^2$ (e.g., a pyramid or cone):

$$-k \cdot h^2 \cdot dh = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

$$dt = -\frac{k \cdot h^{3/2} \, dh}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$$

$$T = \frac{2k}{5 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( H_1^{5/2} - H_2^{5/2} \right)$$

Substituting $A = kH_1^2$ (top area):

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{2A}{5 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{H_1^{5/2} - H_2^{5/2}}{H_1^2}\,}$$

## 3.2 Summary of the Three Standard Tank Geometries

| Tank Shape | $A(h)$ Function | Emptying Time Formula | Multiplier |
|------------|-----------------|------------------------|------------|
| Rectangular / Prismatic (constant $A$) | $A = \text{const}$ | $\dfrac{2A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}}(\sqrt{H_1}-\sqrt{H_2})$ | $\frac{1}{1}$ |
| Horizontally tapering (semi-vertical) | $A \propto h$ | $\dfrac{2A}{3 C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \dfrac{H_1^{3/2}-H_2^{3/2}}{H_1}$ | $\frac{1}{3}$ |
| Vertically tapering (pyramidal/conical) | $A \propto h^2$ | $\dfrac{2A}{5 C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \dfrac{H_1^{5/2}-H_2^{5/2}}{H_1^2}$ | $\frac{1}{5}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Students:** The numerators of the powers are **1, 3, 5** (the first three odd numbers), and the denominators are **1, 3, 5** themselves. This pattern generalises to tanks where $A \propto h^n$, giving emptying time $\propto H^{(n+3/2)}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of the Rectangular Tank with Bottom Orifice

The following Mermaid block renders a **functional block-architecture diagram** mapping the physical layout and energy-flow during tank emptying. (This is used in place of a freehand sketch so it renders cleanly in markdown.)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. State Torricelli's theorem and derive the expression for actual discharge through a small orifice.**  *(3 Marks)* — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Torricelli's theorem states that the theoretical velocity of a fluid jet issuing from a small orifice under a head $h$ is equal to the velocity of a body falling freely through the same height $h$ under gravity. Thus, $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.

The actual discharge accounts for two real-fluid effects: the **vena-contracta** (jet contraction, coefficient $C_c$) and **viscous losses** (coefficient of velocity $C_v$). The actual velocity is $v_a = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$, and the actual cross-section is $a_c = C_c \cdot a$. Therefore:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = a_c \cdot v_a = C_c \cdot a \cdot C_v \sqrt{2gh} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

where $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ is the coefficient of discharge.

---

### **Q2. Write the formula for the time required to empty a rectangular tank through an orifice at its bottom, and explain the meaning of each term.**  *(3 Marks)* — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The standard formula is:

$$T = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$$

where:
- $T$ = time to empty the tank (s)
- $A$ = horizontal cross-sectional area of the tank (m²)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge of the orifice (dimensionless, ≈ 0.62)
- $a$ = area of the orifice (m²)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $H_1$ = initial head of liquid above the orifice (m)
- $H_2$ = final head of liquid above the orifice (m); for complete emptying, $H_2 = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### **QUESTION A: Full Derivation + Numerical Application** *(14 Marks)* — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the time required to empty a rectangular tank of constant cross-sectional area through an orifice fitted at its bottom. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A rectangular tank of size 6 m × 4 m in plan contains water up to a depth of 4 m. A circular orifice of 150 mm diameter is fitted at the bottom of the tank. If $C_d = 0.62$, determine:
  (i) The time required to empty the tank completely.
  (ii) The time required to lower the water level from 4 m to 1 m above the orifice. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation *(7 Marks)*

**Assumptions:**
1. The tank has vertical parallel walls, so $A$ is constant.
2. The orifice is small, sharp-edged, and horizontal.
3. The head $h$ is measured above the centre line of the orifice.
4. The flow is quasi-steady, i.e., the discharge formula holds instantaneously.
5. $C_d$ remains constant throughout the emptying process.
6. Atmospheric pressure acts on both the free surface and the issuing jet.

**Derivation:**

At any instant, let the head above the orifice be $h$. By Torricelli's law, the theoretical velocity is $v_t = \sqrt{2gh}$. The actual discharge is:

$$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

In time $dt$, the head drops by $dh$ (negative). Equating the volume leaving the tank in two ways:

$$-A \cdot dh = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dt$$

Separating variables:

$$dt = -\frac{A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}}$$

Integrating from $t=0$ (head = $H_1$) to $t=T$ (head = $H_2$):

$$T = \frac{A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \int_{H_2}^{H_1} h^{-1/2} \, dh = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$$

For complete emptying, set $H_2 = 0$:

$$T = \frac{2A \cdot \sqrt{H_1}}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution *(7 Marks)*

**Given:**
- $A = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ m}^2$
- $a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$
- $C_d = 0.62$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Common constant:**

$$K = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} = \frac{2 \times 24}{0.62 \times 0.01767 \times \sqrt{19.62}}$$

$$K = \frac{48}{0.62 \times 0.01767 \times 4.4294} = \frac{48}{0.048522} = 989.2 \text{ s/m}^{1/2}$$

**(i) Time for complete emptying** ($H_1 = 4$ m, $H_2 = 0$):

$$T_1 = 989.2 \times \sqrt{4} = 989.2 \times 2 = 1978.4 \text{ s} \approx 32.97 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Time to lower from 4 m to 1 m** ($H_1 = 4$ m, $H_2 = 1$ m):

$$T_2 = 989.2 \times \left( \sqrt{4} - \sqrt{1} \right) = 989.2 \times (2 - 1) = 989.2 \text{ s} \approx 16.49 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_1 \approx 1978 \text{ s} \approx 33 \text{ min}$
- (ii) $T_2 \approx 989 \text{ s} \approx 16.5 \text{ min}$

---

### **QUESTION B: Alternative Choice — Comparative Tank Geometries** *(14 Marks)* — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the time of emptying:
  (i) a rectangular tank,
  (ii) a tank with horizontal cross-section varying linearly with height (semi-vertical tank).
  List the assumptions clearly. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A rectangular tank 4 m × 3 m in plan has water 5 m deep. An orifice of 100 mm diameter with $C_d = 0.62$ is fitted at the bottom. Calculate:
  (i) the time to empty the tank,
  (ii) the time to lower the water level from 5 m to 2 m, and
  (iii) the time that would be saved if the tank were hemispherical at the bottom (area $\propto h$) instead of rectangular, for the same top area and depth. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Derivations *(7 Marks)*

**(i) Rectangular tank:** As derived in Question A:

$$T_R = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$$

**(ii) Semi-vertical tank** ($A = k h$): Volume balance:

$$-kh \, dh = C_d a \sqrt{2gh} \, dt$$

$$dt = -\frac{k \sqrt{h}}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \, dh$$

$$T_{SV} = \frac{2k}{3 C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \left( H_1^{3/2} - H_2^{3/2} \right) = \frac{2A}{3 C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{H_1^{3/2} - H_2^{3/2}}{H_1}$$

**Assumptions:** $C_d$ constant, $A$ varies linearly with $h$, sharp-edged orifice, atmospheric pressure on jet and free surface, quasi-steady flow.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution *(7 Marks)*

**Given:** $A = 12 \text{ m}^2$, $a = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$, $C_d = 0.62$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $H_1 = 5$ m.

**Common constant (from Question A example):** $K = \dfrac{2A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} = 1978.4 / 2 = 989.2 \text{ s/m}^{1/2}$ (re-derived):

$$K = \frac{2 \times 12}{0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.4294} = \frac{24}{0.02157} = 1112.5 \text{ s/m}^{1/2}$$

**(i) Time to empty rectangular tank fully** ($H_2 = 0$):

$$T_R = 1112.5 \times \sqrt{5} = 1112.5 \times 2.2361 = 2488 \text{ s} \approx 41.47 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Time to lower from 5 m to 2 m** ($H_2 = 2$ m):

$$T_{5 \to 2} = 1112.5 \times (\sqrt{5} - \sqrt{2}) = 1112.5 \times (2.2361 - 1.4142) = 1112.5 \times 0.8219 = 914.4 \text{ s} \approx 15.24 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Semi-vertical tank emptying time** (full, $H_2 = 0$):

$$T_{SV} = \frac{2A}{3 C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \sqrt{H_1} = \frac{K \cdot \sqrt{5}}{3} = \frac{1112.5 \times 2.2361}{3} = \frac{2488}{3} = 829.3 \text{ s} \approx 13.82 \text{ min}$$

**Time saved** $= 2488 - 829.3 = 1658.7 \text{ s} \approx 27.6 \text{ min}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 marks):** Students forget the negative sign in $-A \, dh = Q \, dt$ and integrate from $H_1$ to $H_2$ *without* flipping the limits, leading to a negative time. Always reverse the limits or use absolute values consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_d$ (–2 marks):** Using theoretical velocity $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ instead of $C_d \sqrt{2gh}$ when computing discharge. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires $C_d$ explicitly in all numerical problems.
> 3. **Wrong Units in $\sqrt{2g}$ (–1 mark):** $g$ must be in m/s², and $\sqrt{2g}$ has units of m$^{1/2}$/s. Do not mix cm and m.
> 4. **Confusing Tank Shapes (–3 marks):** Applying the rectangular formula to a hemispherical or pyramidal tank. The presence of $A \propto h$ or $A \propto h^2$ must be checked.
> 5. **Not Converting to Minutes (–1 mark):** For KTU problems with $T$ of order thousands of seconds, express the final answer in *both* seconds and minutes for clarity.
> 6. **Neglecting Approach Velocity (–1 mark):** In deep tanks with large surface area, the velocity of the free surface ($\ll v_{\text{jet}}$) can usually be ignored. But if specifically mentioned in the problem, do not omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ **Master Formula (Rectangular Tank):** $T = \dfrac{2A}{C_d \, a \, \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$
- ✅ **Complete Emptying:** $H_2 = 0$ → $T = \dfrac{2A \sqrt{H_1}}{C_d \, a \, \sqrt{2g}}$
- ✅ **Standard $C_d$ value for a sharp-edged orifice:** **0.60 to 0.65** (use 0.62 unless stated).
- ✅ **Torricelli's Velocity:** $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ — this is the *theoretical* velocity; multiply by $C_d$ for *actual* discharge.
- ✅ **Volume-Balance Equation:** Always begin with $-A \, dh = C_d \, a \sqrt{2gh} \, dt$. The negative sign is non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Integration Limits:** $h$ decreases from $H_1$ to $H_2$, so after separating variables either flip the limits or carry a negative sign to the end.
- ✅ **Three Tank Geometries:**
  - Constant $A$ → formula with $\sqrt{H}$
  - $A \propto h$ → formula with $H^{3/2}$ and a factor $1/3$
  - $A \propto h^2$ → formula with $H^{5/2}$ and a factor $1/5$
- ✅ **Assumption Checklist (write in every derivation answer):** sharp-edged orifice, constant $C_d$, atmospheric pressure on jet and free surface, no air entrainment, quasi-steady flow, small orifice area ($a \ll A$).
- ✅ **Time Saved by Tapering:** A hemispherical/pyramidal base drains **faster** than a flat-bottomed rectangular tank for the same top area and depth.
- ✅ **Practical Engineering Applications:** Dams, spillways, cooling tanks, fuel tanks, irrigation outlets.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Writing $T = \dfrac{A \sqrt{H}}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}}$ (missing the factor 2) — this gives only *half* the correct time.
- ✅ **Conversion:** 60 seconds = 1 minute; 3600 seconds = 1 hour. For KTU numericals, always state the answer in **seconds** with minutes in brackets.
- ✅ **SI Units Convention:** All lengths in **metres (m)**, areas in **m²**, velocities in **m/s**, time in **seconds (s)**. Mixing units costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipe Flow: Computation of Major Losses (Derivation of Darcy-Weisbach Equation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pipe Flow: Computation of Major Losses (Derivation of Darcy-Weisbach Equation) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Major Losses** in pipe flow refer to the energy (or head) lost by a fluid due to **frictional resistance** along the length of the pipe when the fluid is in motion. Unlike minor losses (caused by fittings, valves, bends, sudden expansions/contractions), major losses occur continuously over the entire length of the pipe and are therefore the dominant form of energy dissipation in long pipelines. The **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** is the universally accepted, dimensionally homogeneous, and physically rigorous empirical-semi-theoretical equation used to compute the head loss due to friction in a uniform, straight pipe conveying a real (viscous) fluid under steady, incompressible flow conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)** > The Darcy-Weisbach equation is the *cornerstone* of pipe flow analysis. It is mandatory for all numerical problems involving head loss, discharge calculation, pipe sizing, and economic design of pumping mains. KTU examiners *always* ask for either its derivation (7–10 marks) or its application. The Darcy-Weisbach equation is mathematically stated as: $$ h_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot D} $$ Where the parameters are rigorously defined below. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Intuition Imagine you are watering your garden with a long, narrow hose connected to a tap. As water travels from the tap to the nozzle, you will notice that: - The water pressure at the tap is **high**, but the pressure at the nozzle is **low** (if you measure it). - The longer the hose, the more the pressure drops. - A wider hose loses less pressure than a thinner one. - If you increase the flow rate (turn the tap harder), the pressure drop increases *quadratically*, not linearly. This pressure drop is exactly the **major head loss** ($h_f$). The Darcy-Weisbach equation mathematically models this everyday phenomenon. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics** > - Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \, \mathbf{m/s^2}$ (standard value used in all KTU numericals). > - Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathbf{m^2/s}$. > - Density of water, $\rho = 1000 \, \mathbf{kg/m^3}$. > - The friction factor $f$ is **dimensionless** (a pure number). ## 1.3 Why Is This Loss Called "Major"? In a typical long pipeline (e.g., a water supply main, an oil pipeline, or a district cooling line), the frictional loss along the straight length of the pipe is **much greater** than the cumulative losses from fittings, valves, and bends. Hence, KTU terminology classifies this as the *major* loss, and the rest as *minor* losses. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Origin of Major Losses When a real (viscous) fluid flows through a pipe, the fluid layer in immediate contact with the pipe wall has zero velocity (the **no-slip condition**). The fluid layers immediately adjacent to the wall move with very small velocities, while the fluid at the centerline moves with the maximum velocity. This velocity gradient creates a continuous **shear stress** ($\tau_0$) at the pipe wall. This shear stress acts opposite to the direction of flow, and to overcome it, the fluid must continuously expend energy. This expended energy manifests as a **drop in pressure head** along the direction of flow. This drop is the head loss due to friction, $h_f$. ## 2.2 Key Assumptions Underlying the Darcy-Weisbach Equation For the equation to be strictly valid, the following assumptions must hold (KTU examiners test these): 1. The pipe is **prismatic** (constant cross-section) and straight. 2. The flow is **steady** (time-invariant). 3. The fluid is **incompressible**. 4. The flow is **uniform** at every cross-section. 5. The pipe wall is **impermeable** and hydraulically smooth or rough (turbulent flow regime). 6. The pressure drop is solely due to wall friction (no elevation change, no minor losses). ## 2.3 Significance of the Friction Factor ($f$) The friction factor is the heart of the Darcy-Weisbach equation. It is **not a constant** — it is a function of: - The **Reynolds Number** ($Re$), which captures the flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent). - The **Relative Roughness** ($\varepsilon/D$), which captures the surface texture of the pipe. | Flow Regime | Friction Factor Formula | Source | |---|---|---| | Laminar ($Re < 2000$) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille Law (theoretical) | | Turbulent ($Re > 4000$) — Smooth Pipes | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2.0 \log_{10}(Re\sqrt{f}) - 0.8$ | Prandtl's Equation (smooth pipes) | | Turbulent — Fully Rough Zone | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2.0 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{D}{\varepsilon}\right) + 1.74$ | Von Kármán Equation (rough pipes) | | Turbulent — Transition Zone | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Colebrook-White Equation (implicit, requires iteration) | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > This is the **must-memorize** table for KTU board exams. Print it, paste it on your wall. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h_f = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot D}$ | **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** (head loss) | m (metres) | | 2 | $\Delta p = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot D}$ | Pressure drop form of D-W Equation | Pa (N/m²) | | 3 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Mean velocity from discharge | m/s | | 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V \cdot D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless | | 5 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Friction factor (laminar flow only) | dimensionless | | 6 | $\tau_0 = \dfrac{f \cdot \rho \cdot V^2}{8}$ | Wall shear stress (D-W basis) | Pa | | 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Equivalent form using Fanning friction factor | m | | 8 | $h_L = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}\left(f \dfrac{L}{D} + \Sigma K\right)$ | Combined major + minor loss (used in pipe network design) | m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL UNIT CONVERSION — Examiner's Trick** > In KTU problems, discharge $Q$ is often given in **litres/second (L/s)** or **litres/minute (L/min)**. You **must** convert to $m^3/s$ before using the formula: > - $1 \, L/s = 10^{-3} \, m^3/s$ > - $1 \, L/min = \dfrac{1}{60} \times 10^{-3} \, m^3/s$ > Failing this conversion is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in numericals. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Darcy-Weisbach equation is used universally in: - **Water Supply Engineering**: Designing the diameter of pumping mains and gravity mains. - **Hydraulic Engineering**: Computing head loss in penstocks (hydroelectric pipelines). - **Oil & Gas Industry**: Sizing cross-country crude oil and natural gas pipelines. - **HVAC Systems**: Calculating fan/pump power requirements for chilled water and condenser water lines. - **Fire Protection Engineering**: Sizing sprinkler and standpipe systems. - **Chemical Process Industry**: Pressure drop calculations in process piping. It is more accurate than older empirical formulas (e.g., Hazen-Williams, Manning's) because it is dimensionally homogeneous and valid for *all* fluids, *all* pipe materials, and *all* flow regimes. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Exhaustive Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation This is the **most important derivation in Module 3**. KTU examiners allocate 7–10 marks for it. We derive it using the **force balance approach** on a cylindrical control volume of fluid inside the pipe. ### Step 1: Visualize the Control Volume Consider a horizontal, straight pipe of constant internal diameter $D$. Inside it, isolate a cylindrical fluid element of: - Length $= L$ - Diameter $= D$ - Cross-sectional area $= A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ Fluid enters at Section 1 (left) with pressure $p_1$ and exits at Section 2 (right) with pressure $p_2$, where $p_1 > p_2$ (because of friction). ### Step 2: Identify All Forces Acting on the Element Three forces act on the cylindrical fluid element along the direction of flow: **Force 1 — Pressure force at inlet (Section 1):** $$ F_1 = p_1 \cdot A = p_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ This acts in the direction of flow (pushing the fluid forward). **Force 2 — Pressure force at outlet (Section 2):** $$ F_2 = p_2 \cdot A = p_2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ This acts opposite to the direction of flow (resisting motion). **Force 3 — Shear force at the pipe wall:** The wall exerts a frictional shear stress $\tau_0$ on the fluid. The area over which this acts is the **wetted perimeter** times the length, i.e., $\pi D L$. $$ F_3 = \tau_0 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L $$ This acts opposite to the direction of flow (resisting motion). ### Step 3: Apply Newton's Second Law (Steady Flow) For **steady, uniform flow**, the net force must equal the rate of change of momentum. Since velocity does not change between Sections 1 and 2 (uniform flow), the net force is **zero** (momentum is constant). $$ F_1 - F_2 - F_3 = 0 $$ Substituting the forces: $$ p_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - p_2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \tau_0 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L = 0 $$ ### Step 4: Simplify the Equation Cancel $\pi$ from all terms (it appears in every term): $$ (p_1 - p_2) \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} - \tau_0 \cdot D \cdot L = 0 $$ Rearrange to isolate the pressure drop: $$ (p_1 - p_2) \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot D \cdot L $$ Divide both sides by $D$: $$ (p_1 - p_2) \cdot \frac{D}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot L $$ Therefore, the pressure drop is: $$ (p_1 - p_2) = \frac{4 \cdot \tau_0 \cdot L}{D} $$ ### Step 5: Express Pressure Drop as Head Loss The head loss $h_f$ is defined as the equivalent height of fluid column that the pressure drop can support: $$ h_f = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho \cdot g} $$ Substitute the expression for $(p_1 - p_2)$: $$ h_f = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot g} \cdot \frac{4 \cdot \tau_0 \cdot L}{D} $$ Rearranging: $$ h_f = \frac{4 \cdot L \cdot \tau_0}{\rho \cdot g \cdot D} $$ ### Step 6: Introduce the Friction Factor (Empirical Closure) The wall shear stress $\tau_0$ is empirically related to the dynamic pressure of the flow $\left(\dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}\right)$ through a dimensionless coefficient — the **friction factor** $f$: $$ \tau_0 = f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot V^2}{2} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this specific form?** > Dimensional analysis and experimental observations show that the shear stress at the wall must be proportional to the *kinetic energy per unit volume* of the fluid. The constant of proportionality is the friction factor $f$. This is the **empirical heart** of the Darcy-Weisbach equation. ### Step 7: Substitute and Obtain the Final Darcy-Weisbach Equation Substitute the shear stress relation into the head loss expression: $$ h_f = \frac{4 \cdot L}{\rho \cdot g \cdot D} \cdot \left(f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot V^2}{2}\right) $$ The $\rho$ terms cancel: $$ h_f = \frac{4 \cdot L \cdot f \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot D} $$ The 4 and 2 combine to give the final form: $$ \boxed{\,h_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot D}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Complete.** This is the universally accepted form. In some textbooks (especially American ones), a factor of 4 is kept explicitly: $h_f = \dfrac{4f L V^2}{2gD}$. In that case, the friction factor is the **Fanning friction factor**, which is exactly $1/4$th of the Darcy friction factor used above. KTU 2024 syllabus uses the **Darcy friction factor** (no factor of 4 in the formula). ## 3.2 Alternative Derivation Path — Energy Equation Approach For completeness, the same equation can be derived by applying the **steady-flow energy equation** (modified Bernoulli equation) between Sections 1 and 2 of the pipe: $$ \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f $$ For a **horizontal** pipe ($z_1 = z_2$) and **uniform** flow ($V_1 = V_2 = V$): $$ \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = h_f $$ Substituting $(p_1 - p_2) = \dfrac{4 \tau_0 L}{D}$ and $\tau_0 = f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$: $$ h_f = \frac{4 L}{\rho g D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{2} = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} $$ This confirms the equation arrived at via the force balance. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter The following table qualitatively summarizes how a change in each parameter affects the head loss $h_f$. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU viva and Part A questions. | Parameter | Change | Effect on $h_f$ | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Length $L$ | Doubles | $h_f$ doubles | Longer pipe → more pumping cost | | Diameter $D$ | Doubles | $h_f$ becomes $\dfrac{1}{16}$th | Wider pipe is highly efficient | | Velocity $V$ | Doubles | $h_f$ quadruples | High velocity is very costly | | Roughness $\varepsilon$ | Increases | $f$ increases, $h_f$ increases | Old/rusted pipes lose more energy | | Viscosity $\mu$ | Increases (at low $Re$) | $f$ increases, $h_f$ increases | Heavy oils need more pumping power | | Friction factor $f$ | Doubles | $h_f$ doubles | Function of $Re$ and roughness | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight — Sensitivity to Diameter** > Notice that $h_f \propto \dfrac{1}{D^5}$ (because $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$, so $V^2 \propto \dfrac{1}{D^4}$, and combined with $\dfrac{1}{D}$, we get $\dfrac{1}{D^5}$). This is why pipe diameter selection is the *most powerful* design lever to control pumping cost in long pipelines. # 4.
