# 📐 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Darcy–Weisbach Head Loss in a Pipe

Starting from the **shear-stress integration** on a pipe wall in fully-developed flow, the energy loss per unit weight of fluid (head loss) over a length **L** of pipe of diameter **d** carrying mean velocity **V** is

$$h_f = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{\rho g d}$$

The wall shear stress is empirically related to the dynamic pressure via the **Darcy friction factor** *f*:

$$\tau_0 = f \, \frac{\rho V^2}{8}$$

Substituting τ₀ into the head-loss expression:

$$h_f = \frac{4 L}{\rho g d} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g d}$$

The mean velocity is related to discharge through **A = π d²/4**:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi d^{2}}$$

Squaring and substituting:

$$V^2 = \frac{16 Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} d^{4}}$$

Therefore the head loss in terms of discharge is

$$h_f = \frac{f L}{2 g d} \cdot \frac{16 Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} d^{4}}$$

$$\boxed{\;h_f = \frac{8 f L Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g d^{5}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *fundamental governing equation* for every series, parallel, and equivalent-pipe problem. **Memorise it** — every KTU 2024 numerical on this topic uses it as a starting point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Length of Pipes in Series

Let three pipes (lengths **L₁, L₂, L₃**; diameters **d₁, d₂, d₃**; friction factors **f₁, f₂, f₃**) be connected in series. The total head loss between the upstream and downstream reservoirs is

$$H = h_{f1} + h_{f2} + h_{f3}$$

Expanding using the Darcy–Weisbach equation and noting that the discharge **Q** is the same in every pipe:

$$H = \frac{8 Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g}\!\left(\frac{f_1 L_1}{d_1^{5}} + \frac{f_2 L_2}{d_2^{5}} + \frac{f_3 L_3}{d_3^{5}}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

The equivalent pipe of length **L_e**, diameter **d_e**, and friction factor **f_e** carries the *same* Q and produces the *same* total head loss H:

$$H = \frac{8 f_e L_e Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g \, d_e^{5}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Equating (i) and (ii), the common factor **8 Q² / (π² g)** cancels, leaving:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{f_e L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \frac{f_1 L_1}{d_1^{5}} + \frac{f_2 L_2}{d_2^{5}} + \frac{f_3 L_3}{d_3^{5}}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Special case:** If the equivalent pipe has the same diameter as the *first* pipe (d_e = d₁) and the *same* friction factor *f*, then *f* and *d₁⁵* cancel out, giving the cleanest form used in KTU tutorials:
> $$L_e = L_1 + L_2 \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{5} + L_3 \left(\frac{d_1}{d_3}\right)^{5}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Length of Pipes in Parallel

Let two pipes (lengths **L₁, L₂**; diameters **d₁, d₂**; friction factors **f₁, f₂**) be connected in parallel between nodes *A* and *B*. The head loss is the same in both branches:

$$h_{f1} = h_{f2} = h_f$$

From Darcy–Weisbach:

$$h_f = \frac{8 f_1 L_1 Q_1^{2}}{\pi^{2} g d_1^{5}} = \frac{8 f_2 L_2 Q_2^{2}}{\pi^{2} g d_2^{5}}$$

Solving for the individual branch discharges:

$$Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g d_1^{5} h_f}{8 f_1 L_1}} \quad , \quad Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g d_2^{5} h_f}{8 f_2 L_2}}$$

By continuity at the junction:

$$Q = Q_1 + Q_2 = \sqrt{h_f \cdot \frac{\pi^{2} g}{8}}\left(\sqrt{\frac{d_1^{5}}{f_1 L_1}} + \sqrt{\frac{d_2^{5}}{f_2 L_2}}\right)$$

Squaring both sides:

$$Q^{2} = h_f \cdot \frac{\pi^{2} g}{8}\left(\sqrt{\frac{d_1^{5}}{f_1 L_1}} + \sqrt{\frac{d_2^{5}}{f_2 L_2}}\right)^{2} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

For the equivalent pipe, with the *same head loss* h_f and the *same total discharge* Q:

$$Q^{2} = \frac{\pi^{2} g d_e^{5} h_f}{8 f_e L_e} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Equating (iii) and (iv) and cancelling **π² g h_f / 8**:

$$\frac{d_e^{5}}{f_e L_e} = \left(\sqrt{\frac{d_1^{5}}{f_1 L_1}} + \sqrt{\frac{d_2^{5}}{f_2 L_2}}\right)^{2}$$

Inverting both sides and writing in the standard KTU form:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{f_e L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{d_1^{5}}{f_1 L_1}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{d_2^{5}}{f_2 L_2}}\right)^{2}}\;}$$

Generalised to *n* parallel pipes:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{f_e L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{\frac{f_i L_i}{d_i^{5}}}\right]^{-2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with the series formula:** The series formula has the terms added in the numerator, while the parallel formula has the *square roots* of the reciprocals added. This is the *single most important distinction* in this entire module.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Series Pipeline

**Problem statement (KTU pattern):** Three pipes of diameters **20 cm, 30 cm, 40 cm** and lengths **500 m, 400 m, 300 m** are connected in series. Take **f = 0.024** for all pipes. Find:
*(a)* The equivalent length of a single pipe of diameter **30 cm**.
*(b)* The discharge through the system if the total head loss is **18 m**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the ratios (d₁/d)⁵ for each pipe** (d = 30 cm = 0.30 m):

For pipe 1: d₁ = 0.20 m → (d₁/d)⁵ = (0.20/0.30)⁵ = (2/3)⁵ = 32/243 ≈ 0.1317

For pipe 2: d₂ = 0.30 m → (d₂/d)⁵ = 1.0000

For pipe 3: d₃ = 0.40 m → (d₃/d)⁵ = (0.40/0.30)⁵ = (4/3)⁵ = 1024/243 ≈ 4.2139

**Step 2 — Equivalent length (L_e) using the series formula with d_e = 0.30 m:**

$$L_e = L_1\!\left(\frac{d_1}{d_e}\right)^{5} + L_2\!\left(\frac{d_2}{d_e}\right)^{5} + L_3\!\left(\frac{d_3}{d_e}\right)^{5}$$

$$L_e = (500)(0.1317) + (400)(1.0000) + (300)(4.2139)$$

$$L_e = 65.85 + 400.00 + 1264.18$$

$$\boxed{\;L_e \approx 1730.0 \text{ m}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Discharge from the equivalent pipe using H = 18 m:**

Rearranging the Darcy–Weisbach equation:

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{H \pi^{2} g d_e^{5}}{8 f L_e}}$$

Substituting (H = 18 m, d_e = 0.30 m, f = 0.024, L_e = 1730 m, g = 9.81 m/s²):

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times \pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times (0.30)^{5}}{8 \times 0.024 \times 1730}}$$

Computing the numerator: π² × 9.81 × (0.30)⁵ = 9.8696 × 9.81 × 0.00243 ≈ 0.2353

Numerator = 18 × 0.2353 ≈ 4.2354

Denominator = 8 × 0.024 × 1730 = 332.16

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{4.2354}{332.16}} = \sqrt{0.01275}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 0.113 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}\;}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Parallel Pipeline

**Problem statement:** Two pipes of diameters **15 cm, 30 cm** and lengths **600 m, 300 m** are connected in parallel. Take **f = 0.025** for all pipes.
*(a)* Find the equivalent length of a single pipe of diameter **30 cm**.
*(b)* If the head loss across the parallel system is **12 m**, find the discharge through each pipe and the total discharge.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute √(L/d⁵) for each branch and the equivalent diameter:**

For pipe 1: L₁ = 600 m, d₁ = 0.15 m
$$d_1^{5} = (0.15)^{5} = 7.5938 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{5}$$
$$\sqrt{\frac{L_1}{d_1^{5}}} = \sqrt{\frac{600}{7.5938 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{7.9006 \times 10^{6}} = 2810.8$$

For pipe 2: L₂ = 300 m, d₂ = 0.30 m
$$d_2^{5} = (0.30)^{5} = 2.4300 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{5}$$
$$\sqrt{\frac{L_2}{d_2^{5}}} = \sqrt{\frac{300}{2.4300 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1.2346 \times 10^{5}} = 351.4$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent length using the parallel formula with d_e = 0.30 m:**

The parallel formula in *L_e / d_e⁵* form:

$$\frac{L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \left[\sqrt{\frac{L_1}{d_1^{5}}} \cdot \left(\frac{d_1}{d_e}\right)^{2.5} + \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{d_2^{5}}} \cdot \left(\frac{d_2}{d_e}\right)^{2.5}\right]^{2}$$

Wait — let us re-derive cleanly. From the boxed formula with f_e = f₁ = f₂ = f:

$$\frac{L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \left[\sqrt{\frac{L_1}{d_1^{5}}} + \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{d_2^{5}}}\right]^{-2}$$

With d_e = d₂ = 0.30 m, we need √(L₂/d₂⁵) and √(L₁/d₁⁵) × (d₁/d_e)²·⁵ correction? Let us re-check.

The general (equal *f*) form is

$$\frac{L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \left[\sqrt{\frac{L_1}{d_1^{5}}} \cdot \frac{1}{1} + \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{d_2^{5}}} \cdot \frac{1}{1}\right]^{-2}$$

Only if d_e is *not* pre-imposed. If d_e is *given* (e.g., 0.30 m), use the bare formula:

$$\frac{L_e}{d_e^{5}} = \left[\sqrt{\frac{L_1}{d_1^{5}}} + \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{d_2^{5}}}\right]^{-2}$$

LHS = L_e / (0.30)⁵ = L_e / 2.4300 × 10⁻³

RHS = 1 / (2810.8 + 351.4)² = 1 / (3162.2)² = 1 / (9.9995 × 10⁶) ≈ 1.0001 × 10⁻⁷

Therefore:
$$L_e = 2.4300 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0001 \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.43 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$$

This is an unreasonably small number, indicating the chosen equivalent diameter **0.30 m is much larger** than the geometric mean of the parallel system. A more practical equivalent diameter is the *natural* one emerging from the formula. The cleaner KTU approach is:

**Equivalent diameter solved naturally:**

$$\frac{1}{d_e^{5/2}} = \frac{1}{d_1^{5/2}} \sqrt{\frac{L_1}{L_e}} + \frac{1}{d_2^{5/2}} \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_e}}$$

This is circular. Reverting to the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{\sqrt{L_e}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_1}}\left(\frac{d_1}{d_e}\right)^{2.5} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_2}}\left(\frac{d_2}{d_e}\right)^{2.5}\;}$$

With d_e = 0.30 m:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{L_e}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{600}}\left(\frac{0.15}{0.30}\right)^{2.5} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{300}}\left(\frac{0.30}{0.30}\right)^{2.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{L_e}} = \frac{1}{24.495}(0.5)^{2.5} + \frac{1}{17.321}(1)^{2.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{L_e}} = \frac{0.17678}{24.495} + \frac{1}{17.321} = 0.007218 + 0.057735 = 0.064953$$

$$\sqrt{L_e} = 15.396 \Rightarrow \boxed{\;L_e \approx 237.0 \text{ m of 30 cm pipe}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Discharge in each branch using H = 12 m:**

For pipe 1:
$$Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g d_1^{5} H}{8 f L_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 7.5938 \times 10^{-5} \times 12}{8 \times 0.025 \times 600}}$$

$$Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{0.08839}{120}} = \sqrt{7.366 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.0271 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

For pipe 2:
$$Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g d_2^{5} H}{8 f L_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 2.4300 \times 10^{-3} \times 12}{8 \times 0.025 \times 300}}$$

$$Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2.8276}{60}} = \sqrt{0.04713} \approx 0.2171 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

Total discharge:
$$Q = Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.0271 + 0.2171$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 0.244 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** Branch 2 (30 cm pipe) carries ~8× the discharge of branch 1 (15 cm pipe), consistent with the **d²·⁵** scaling law for parallel pipes at equal head loss. This is the **"Karman-Briggs" scaling intuition** the KTU examiner rewards.

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Pipes in Series — Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RESERVOIR A] --> B[PIPE 1\nL1, d1, f1]
    B --> C[JUNCTION 1]
    C --> D[PIPE 2\nL2, d2, f2]
    D --> E[JUNCTION 2]
    E --> F[PIPE 3\nL3, d3, f3]
    F --> G[RESERVOIR B]
    H[Q constant] -.-> B
    H -.-> D
    H -.-> F
    I[Total H = hf1 + hf2 + hf3] -.-> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** The discharge **Q** is shown as a constant dotted line threading through all three pipes — a visual reminder of the series continuity condition. The total head loss is the *additive sum* of the three branch losses.

---

## 4.2 Pipes in Parallel — Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NODE A] --> B[PIPE 1\nL1, d1, f1]
    A --> C[PIPE 2\nL2, d2, f2]
    A --> D[PIPE 3\nL3, d3, f3]
    B --> E[NODE B]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[OUTLET]
    G[Q1] -.-> B
    H[Q2] -.-> C
    I[Q3] -.-> D
    J[hf1 = hf2 = hf3] -.-> E
    K[Q total = Q1 + Q2 + Q3] -.-> F
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three pipes share the same endpoints A and B, enforcing equal head loss. The discharge splits at A and recombines at B, satisfying the parallel continuity condition.

---

## 4.3 Equivalent Pipe Concept — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYS1[COMPOUND PIPE SYSTEM]
        S1[Pipe 1] --> S2[Pipe 2] --> S3[Pipe 3]
    end
    subgraph SYS2[REPLACEMENT EQUIVALENT PIPE]
        E1[Single Pipe\nLength Le, Diameter de]
    end
    IN[Inlet Head H, Discharge Q] --> SYS1
    IN --> SYS2
    SYS1 --> OUT1[Outlet Head 0]
    SYS2 --> OUT2[Outlet Head 0]
    COND[Condition:\nSame H and Same Q] -.-> SYS1
    COND -.-> SYS2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two parallel subsystems — the actual compound pipeline and its virtual equivalent — both receive the same inlet conditions and must produce the same outlet head. The "Condition" block is the design constraint that determines **L_e** and **d_e**.

---

## 4.4 Series vs. Parallel — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Series Pipe Network]
        SP1[Same Discharge Q] --> SP2[Head Loss Adds]
        SP2 --> SP3[Le/de5 equals sum of Li/di5]
    end
    subgraph PP[Parallel Pipe Network]
        PP1[Same Head Loss hf] --> PP2[Discharge Adds]
        PP2 --> PP3[Le/de5 equals 1 over sum of sqrt Li/di5 squared]
    end
    SP -. COMPARISON .-> PP
```

**Reading the diagram:** A side-by-side mental map of the two regimes. The student should be able to sketch this in 30 seconds during revision.
