# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms:**
**(a)** Notch
**(b)** Weir
**(c)** Nappe

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Notch:** A notch is a small opening provided in the side wall of a tank or reservoir, extending from the free water surface downward, used to measure the discharge of liquid flowing out of the tank.

**(b) Weir:** A weir is a large-sized obstruction or barrier constructed across an open channel or river, over which water flows. It is used for measuring the discharge of flowing water in rivers, streams, and canals.

**(c) Nappe:** The nappe is the sheet or layer of water that flows over the crest of a notch or weir after leaving the upstream face.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a notch and a weir. (Any 4 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Notch | Weir |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Small in size | Large in size |
| 2 | Installed on a tank or reservoir | Installed across a natural channel or river |
| 3 | The crest is below the free water surface | The crest is at the level of the channel bed |
| 4 | Used for laboratory or small-scale measurement | Used for field-scale discharge measurement |
| 5 | Generally rectangular, V, trapezoidal | Can be sharp-crested, broad-crested, ogee |
| 6 | Less expensive | More expensive to construct |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify notches and weirs based on (i) the shape of the opening, and (ii) the shape of the crest. Explain any two types in detail with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Notches and Weirs:**

**A. Based on Shape of the Opening:**
1. Rectangular notch/weir
2. Triangular (V-notch/weir)
3. Trapezoidal notch/weir
4. Parabolic notch/weir
5. Stepped notch/weir
6. Proportional (Sutro) weir

**B. Based on Shape of the Crest:**
1. Sharp-crested weir
2. Broad-crested weir
3. Narrow-crested weir
4. Ogee-shaped weir

**Detailed Explanation (Two Types):**

**1. Rectangular Notch/Weir:**
- It is a rectangular opening cut in the side wall of the tank (notch) or across a channel (weir).
- The crest is **horizontal** and the sides are **vertical**.
- **Discharge formula:** $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$
- **Suitability:** Best for measuring large discharges at moderate to high heads.
- **Coefficient of discharge:** $C_d \approx 0.62$ for a suppressed weir.
- **Sketch:** A rectangle of length $L$ and head $H$ above its crest.

**2. Triangular (V-Notch):**
- The opening is in the shape of an inverted **V** (vertex pointing downward).
- The notch is highly sensitive at low heads due to the $H^{5/2}$ dependence.
- **Discharge formula:** $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$
- **Suitability:** Best for measuring small discharges with high accuracy.
- **Coefficient of discharge:** $C_d \approx 0.60$.
- **Sketch:** A V-shaped opening of total included angle $\theta$ with head $H$ above the vertex.

---

**(b) A rectangular notch 1.2 m long has a head of 0.45 m. Find the discharge if $C_d = 0.62$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Length of notch: $L = 1.2$ m
- Head over the notch: $H = 0.45$ m
- Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = 0.62$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Formula:**
$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

**Step 1:** Calculate $\sqrt{2g}$:
$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $H^{3/2}$:
$$H^{3/2} = (0.45)^{3/2} = 0.45 \times \sqrt{0.45}$$
$$\sqrt{0.45} = 0.6708$$
$$H^{3/2} = 0.45 \times 0.6708 = 0.3019 \text{ m}^{3/2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute all values:
$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.4294 \times 0.3019$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.4294 \times 0.3019$$

**Step 4:** Compute:
$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 = 0.4133$$
$$Q = 0.4133 \times 1.2 = 0.4960$$
$$Q = 0.4960 \times 4.4294 = 2.1969$$
$$Q = 2.1969 \times 0.3019 = 0.6633 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.6633 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 663.3 \text{ litres/s}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of neat sketches, explain the classification of weirs based on the shape of the crest. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Weirs Based on Shape of the Crest:**

**1. Sharp-Crested Weir:**
- A metallic plate with a **sharp upstream edge** is fixed across the channel.
- The crest thickness is < 2 mm, ensuring a **sharp edge**.
- The nappe springs clear of the crest, producing a well-defined sheet of water.
- **Applications:** Laboratory experiments, precision discharge measurement.
- **Sketch:** Vertical plate of negligible thickness with water flowing over it.

**2. Broad-Crested Weir:**
- The crest has a **horizontal width** large enough (≥ 0.4H) to support **critical depth** flow.
- The nappe does not spring clear; instead, it **adheres** to the crest surface.
- The flow over the crest is **critical**, governed by $V_c = \sqrt{g \cdot y_c}$.
- **Discharge formula:** $Q = C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ (where $C_d \approx 1.70$ to $1.83$)
- **Applications:** Irrigation canals, large dams, river gauging.

**3. Narrow-Crested Weir:**
- The crest width is between sharp and broad (typically 2 mm to 0.4H).
- Neither pure critical flow nor pure free fall occurs.
- Used less frequently in practice.

**4. Ogee-Shaped Weir:**
- The crest profile follows a smooth **ogee curve** (after the letter "S").
- The downstream face is curved to match the natural trajectory of the nappe.
- Ensures **minimum cavitation, smooth flow, and high efficiency**.
- **Applications:** Large dam spillways (e.g., Idukki, Bhakra Nangal).
- **Sketch:** S-shaped profile with upstream face vertical and downstream face curved.

---

**(b) A triangular (V-notch) has an included angle of 60°. If the head of water is 0.30 m, find the discharge. Take $C_d = 0.60$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Included angle of V-notch: $\theta = 60°$
- Half-angle: $\theta/2 = 30°$
- Head: $H = 0.30$ m
- Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = 0.60$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Formula:**
$$Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

**Step 1:** Calculate $\tan(30°)$:
$$\tan(30°) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\sqrt{2g}$:
$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $H^{5/2}$:
$$H^{5/2} = (0.30)^{5/2} = (0.30)^2 \times \sqrt{0.30}$$
$$(0.30)^2 = 0.09$$
$$\sqrt{0.30} = 0.5477$$
$$H^{5/2} = 0.09 \times 0.5477 = 0.04929 \text{ m}^{5/2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute all values:
$$Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 \times 0.04929$$

**Step 5:** Compute step-by-step:
$$\dfrac{8}{15} = 0.5333$$
$$0.5333 \times 0.60 = 0.3200$$
$$0.3200 \times 0.5774 = 0.1848$$
$$0.1848 \times 4.4294 = 0.8186$$
$$0.8186 \times 0.04929 = 0.04035 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0404 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 40.35 \text{ litres/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU Exams (Notches & Weirs Classification):**
>
> 1. **Conflating Notch and Weir** — Many students write the same formula for both. Examiner docks **1–2 marks** if distinction is missing in long answers.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_d$** — Students sometimes write $Q_{th}$ (theoretical) as the final answer. Always multiply by $C_d$ to get actual $Q$.
> 3. **Unit Errors** — Writing discharge in litres/s without converting m³/s. **1 m³/s = 1000 L/s**.
> 4. **Misusing $\tan(\theta/2)$** — For V-notch, use the **half-angle** (i.e., $\theta/2$), not the full included angle $\theta$.
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams** — For 7-mark questions on classification, **neat sketches are MANDATORY**. Expect **1–2 marks deduction** without them.
> 6. **Wrong Power of H** — V-notch uses $H^{5/2}$ (not $H^{3/2}$). Memorizing the wrong exponent is a frequent error.
> 7. **Forgetting $\sqrt{2g}$ in $Q_{th}$ derivation** — Step-derivations must include this factor at every stage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **notch** is small; a **weir** is large. Both measure discharge.
- ✅ The **nappe** is the water sheet flowing over a notch/weir.
- ✅ **Classification — Shape of opening:** Rectangular, Triangular, Trapezoidal, Parabolic, Stepped, Sutro.
- ✅ **Classification — Shape of crest:** Sharp, Broad, Narrow, Ogee.
- ✅ **Classification — Hydraulic condition:** Contracted, Suppressed, Partially Contracted.
- ✅ **Rectangular notch formula:** $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$
- ✅ **V-notch formula:** $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$
- ✅ **Cipolletti weir** = trapezoidal with side slope **1H : 4V**, giving a constant $C_d \approx 0.62$.
- ✅ **V-notch** is best for **small discharges**; **Rectangular** is best for **large discharges**.
- ✅ **Broad-crested weirs** are used in **irrigation canals and dams**; **Ogee weirs** are used in **major dam spillways**.
- ✅ **Coefficient of discharge $C_d$** typically ranges from **0.58 to 0.66** for most notch/weir types.
- ✅ **g = 9.81 m/s²** is the standard value in KTU numericals.
- ✅ For **end contractions** in rectangular weirs: $L_{effective} = L - 0.1nH$ (where $n$ = number of end contractions, max 2).
- ✅ **Proportional/Sutro weir** has discharge **linearly proportional to H** — useful in wastewater treatment.
- ✅ Always draw **neat, labelled diagrams** for full marks in classification questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discharge Over Rectangular Notch/Weir
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discharge Over Rectangular Notch/Weir

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Notch and Weir

A **notch** is a calibrated opening cut in the side of a tank or reservoir, made in a thin metallic plate, through which the liquid (usually water) flows. The depth of water above the crest of the notch is called the **head (H)**. Notches are typically small openings used for measuring small discharges in laboratory settings.

A **weir** is essentially a larger version of a notch — it is a barrier (dam-like obstruction) constructed across an open channel (river, stream, or spillway) to measure the flow of water. In field applications, weirs are built using masonry or concrete, while notches are machined from thin metal plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:**
> - **Notch:** Small opening in a **thin plate** → used for **small** discharge measurement.
> - **Weir:** Large obstruction across an **open channel** → used for **large** discharge measurement.
> - The mathematical treatment (discharge formula) is **identical** for both — only the scale and structural material differ.

## 1.2 Rectangular Notch — Formal Definition

A **rectangular notch** is a rectangular-shaped opening cut in a thin vertical plate fixed across the side of a tank. The bottom edge of the opening (called the **sill** or **crest**) is horizontal, and the sides are vertical. Water flowing over the notch has a **horizontal crest of length L** with a **sill at the bottom of the opening**, and the head H is measured vertically above the crest.

**Geometric Parameters of a Rectangular Notch:**

- **L** = Length of the crest (width of the notch)
- **H** = Head of water above the crest
- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- **C_d** = Coefficient of discharge (typically **0.61 to 0.66**)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Overflowing Bathtub 🌊**
>
> Imagine you cut a small rectangular hole in the side of a water tank. The deeper the water level inside the tank (above the hole), the faster and more voluminously water gushes out. If you pour in **more water** (higher H), the exit velocity under gravity increases following **Torricelli's principle** (v = √(2gH)).
>
> A rectangular notch behaves exactly like this — but the **width of the hole (L)** and the **head (H)** together control the rate of flow. The discharge depends on the **cubic-root-like power of H** (specifically H^1.5), which is why small increases in water level cause large increases in flow.

## 1.4 Classification of Rectangular Notches

Based on **end contractions**, rectangular notches are classified as:

| Type | Description | Effective Length (L_eff) |
|------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Notch with no end contraction** | Sides of the notch are far from the tank walls (width of approach > 4H) | L_eff = L |
| **Notch with two end contractions** | Both sides of the notch are close to tank walls | L_eff = L − 0.2H |
| **Notch with one end contraction** | Only one side is close to the wall | L_eff = L − 0.1H |
| **Notch with n end contractions** | Generalized form | L_eff = L − 0.1nH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Contraction Principle:** When water approaches a notch, the streamlines curve inward at the edges, reducing the effective flowing width. This loss of effective width is empirically accounted for by **0.1H per contracted end** (a result due to **Francis**, 1882).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

To derive the discharge through a rectangular notch, we apply **Torricelli's Theorem** combined with **integration of elemental strips**. The steps are as follows:

1. Consider a thin horizontal elemental strip of water at a depth **h** below the free water surface, of thickness **dh**, lying on the crest of the notch.
2. The **theoretical velocity** of the strip as it leaves the notch is given by **v = √(2gh)** (from Torricelli's theorem, derived from Bernoulli's equation).
3. The **area** of the elemental strip is **dA = L × dh**.
4. The **theoretical discharge** through the strip is **dQ_th = L × dh × √(2gh)**.
5. To obtain the **total theoretical discharge**, integrate from the crest level (h = 0) up to the free surface (h = H):

$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} L \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

6. The **actual discharge** is less than the theoretical value due to:
   - Friction along the notch edges
   - Contraction of the nappe (vena contracta)
   - Turbulence and viscous effects

   These losses are consolidated into a single empirical factor, the **Coefficient of Discharge (C_d)**, where **0.61 ≤ C_d ≤ 0.66**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete formula bank** for Module 3 — Rectangular Notch/Weir problems. All formulas use **SI units** unless otherwise stated.

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$ | **Discharge over rectangular notch** (no velocity of approach) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q = 1.84 \, L \, H^{3/2}$ | **Francis formula** (rectangular weir, no end contractions, FPS units) | ft³/s |
| 3 | $Q = 1.84 \, (L - 0.1nH) \, H^{3/2}$ | **Francis formula** with n end contractions (FPS units) | ft³/s |
| 4 | $Q = 1.84 \, L_{eff} \, H^{3/2}$ | **Francis formula** using effective length (FPS units) | ft³/s |
| 5 | $L_{eff} = L - 0.1nH$ | **Effective length** of weir with n end contractions | m |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, \left[ (H + h_a)^{3/2} - h_a^{3/2} \right]$ | Discharge with **velocity of approach** | m³/s |
| 7 | $h_a = \dfrac{v_a^{2}}{2g}$ | **Head due to velocity of approach** (v_a = approach velocity) | m |
| 8 | $v_a = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | **Velocity of approach** in the channel | m/s |
| 9 | $C_d = 0.611 + 0.075 \dfrac{H}{P}$ | **Rehbock formula** (more accurate C_d) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = 0.0184 \, L \, H^{3/2}$ | Rectangular weir discharge (SI, C_d ≈ 0.62) | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant values used in KTU problems:**
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (SI)
> - **g = 32.2 ft/s²** (FPS)
> - **√(2g) = 4.43** (SI) / **8.02** (FPS)
> - **2/3 × √(2g) = 2.953** (SI) / **5.35** (FPS)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is this used in the field?**
> - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Design of **spillways** in dams (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar) — the discharge capacity directly determines dam safety during floods.
> - **Irrigation Engineering:** Flow measurement in **distributary canals** for water budgeting and equitable distribution.
> - **Wastewater Treatment:** Monitoring **effluent flow rates** in treatment plants (V-notch preferred for small flows).
> - **Hydrology:** Stream gauging stations use weirs to maintain stage-discharge relationships.

## 2.4 Velocity of Approach — Critical Concept

When the **channel cross-section is small** compared to H, the velocity of the approaching flow is not negligible. This velocity creates an **additional head (h_a)** that effectively raises the water level above the notch. The modified head becomes **(H + h_a)** at the upstream face. The discharge equation is then corrected to include the kinetic energy of the approaching flow, leading to **Formula #6** in the cheat sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Velocity of approach is often **neglected** unless the problem explicitly states "consider velocity of approach" or gives the channel cross-section. Always read the problem statement carefully.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discharge Over a Rectangular Notch (Without Velocity of Approach)

**Given Setup:**
- Rectangular notch of length **L** cut in a thin vertical plate
- Head of water above the crest = **H**
- Consider an elemental horizontal strip at depth **h** below the free surface, thickness **dh**
- Strip lies on the crest, so its width equals the notch length **L**

### Step 1: Theoretical Velocity at the Strip (Torricelli's Theorem)

The water in the strip falls under gravity from a height **h** above the notch crest (measured from the centroid of the strip to the free surface, accounting for the strip being at depth h below the surface — note the strip is at depth h BELOW the free surface, so the effective head driving the strip is h).

Applying **Torricelli's Theorem** (derived from Bernoulli's equation between free surface and the strip):

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 2: Area of the Elemental Strip

The strip is horizontal, with length **L** (width of the notch) and thickness **dh**:

$$dA = L \cdot dh$$

### Step 3: Theoretical Discharge Through the Strip

The theoretical discharge (assuming no losses) is the product of velocity and area:

$$dQ_{th} = v \cdot dA = \sqrt{2gh} \cdot L \cdot dh$$

### Step 4: Integrate to Get Total Theoretical Discharge

The strip exists at every depth from **h = 0** (at the crest) to **h = H** (at the free surface). Integrating:

$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} L \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

Pulling out the constants (L and √(2g)):

$$Q_{th} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Integral

The integral of h^(1/2) is **(2/3) · h^(3/2)**:

$$Q_{th} = L \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2}{3} h^{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H}$$

### Step 6: Apply Limits

$$Q_{th} = L \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \left[ H^{3/2} - 0 \right]$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

### Step 7: Apply the Coefficient of Discharge

The **actual discharge** accounts for losses (friction, contraction, turbulence) through the empirical coefficient **C_d**:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

This is the **fundamental rectangular notch discharge equation** tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discharge with Velocity of Approach

**Given Setup:**
- Rectangular notch in a channel of cross-sectional area **A** (upstream of the notch)
- Approach velocity **v_a = Q / A** is not negligible

### Step 1: Head Due to Approach Velocity

The kinetic energy head of the approaching flow adds to the static head H:

$$h_a = \frac{v_a^{2}}{2g}$$

### Step 2: Modified Head at the Notch

The total head driving the flow over the crest is the static head **plus** the velocity head:

$$H_{eff} = H + h_a$$

### Step 3: Discharge from an Elemental Strip

For a strip at depth h below the free surface, the effective head is **(H + h_a − h)**, so the velocity becomes:

$$v = \sqrt{2g(H + h_a - h)}$$

### Step 4: Theoretical Discharge with Velocity of Approach

$$dQ_{th} = L \cdot dh \cdot \sqrt{2g(H + h_a - h)}$$

$$Q_{th} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} (H + h_a - h)^{1/2} \, dh$$

### Step 5: Substitute Variable

Let **u = H + h_a − h**, then **du = −dh**. When h = 0, u = H + h_a. When h = H, u = h_a. Changing the integration limits:

$$Q_{th} = -L \sqrt{2g} \int_{H+h_a}^{h_a} u^{1/2} \, du = L \sqrt{2g} \int_{h_a}^{H+h_a} u^{1/2} \, du$$

### Step 6: Evaluate the Integral

$$Q_{th} = L \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2}{3} u^{3/2} \right]_{h_a}^{H+h_a}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g} \left[ (H + h_a)^{3/2} - h_a^{3/2} \right]$$

### Step 7: Apply Coefficient of Discharge

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \sqrt{2g} \left[ (H + h_a)^{3/2} - h_a^{3/2} \right]}$$

This is the **velocity-of-approach corrected formula** for KTU Module 3 problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner often tests whether the student knows the **right formula for the right condition**. Use the following decision matrix:

| Condition Stated in Problem | Formula to Use |
|------------------------------|----------------|
| Only H, L, C_d given | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ |
| End contractions mentioned (n given) | $L_{eff} = L - 0.1nH$, then use above |
| Channel area A given | $v_a = Q/A$, $h_a = v_a^2 / 2g$, then use velocity-of-approach formula |
| Francis formula requested (FPS units) | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ |
| Problem in SI units, generic | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ doesn't apply — convert to (2/3)·C_d·L·√(2g)·H^(3/2) |
| Rehbock formula requested | $C_d = 0.611 + 0.075 \frac{H}{P}$, then substitute |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular notch of length **1.2 m** has a head of **0.25 m** of water flowing over it. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.62**, determine the discharge in **litres/second**.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**
- L = 1.2 m
- H = 0.25 m
- C_d = 0.62
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- √(2g) = √(2 × 9.81) = √19.62 = 4.43 m^(1/2)/s

**Step 2 — Apply the Fundamental Formula:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times C_d \times L \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.43 \times (0.25)^{3/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute H^(3/2):**

$$(0.25)^{3/2} = (0.25)^{1.5} = 0.25 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125 \, m^{3/2}$$

**Step 5 — Final Multiplication:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.43 \times 0.125$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.43 \times 0.125$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 = 0.4133$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \times 1.2 = 0.4960$$

$$Q = 0.4960 \times 4.43 = 2.1972$$

$$Q = 2.1972 \times 0.125 = 0.2747 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Litres/Second:**

$$\boxed{Q = 0.2747 \, m^3/s = 274.7 \, L/s}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Geometry Over a Rectangular Notch

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of fluid flow over a rectangular notch, including the formation of the **vena contracta**, **end contractions**, and the **velocity profile** of the nappe (the sheet of water leaving the notch).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["WATER RESERVOIR"] --> node2["HEAD H ABOVE CREST"]
    node2 --> node3["STATIC PRESSURE HEAD"]
    node2 --> node4["VELOCITY HEAD HA"]
    node3 --> node5["NOTCH CREST LENGTH L"]
    node5 --> node6["END CONTRACTION 1"]
    node5 --> node7["END CONTRACTION 2"]
    node5 --> node8["CENTRAL UNCONTRACTED ZONE"]
    node6 --> node9["VENA CONTRACTA FORMS"]
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node10["FULL WIDTH FLOW"]
    node9 --> node11["NAPPER SHEET"]
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["DISCHARGE Q ACTUAL"]
    node4 --> node13["APPROACH VELOCITY VA"]
    node13 --> node14["MODIFIED HEAD H PLUS HA"]
    node14 --> node12

    style node1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style node2 fill:#fff4e1
    style node5 fill:#e8f5e1
    style node11 fill:#ffe1e1
    style node12 fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.2 Analytical Architecture — End Contraction Effect

This second diagram maps the **logical flow of computation** for the rectangular notch formula, with the end-contraction correction branching out as a conditional path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["START: GIVEN L, H, CD"] --> nodeB["APPLY Q = 2/3 CD L SQRT 2G H^1.5"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["CHECK: END CONTRACTIONS?"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"N CONTRACTIONS SPECIFIED?"}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE["LEFF = L MINUS 0.1N TIMES H"]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF["LEFF = L"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Q = 2/3 CD LEFF SQRT 2G H^1.5"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeH["CHECK: VELOCITY OF APPROACH?"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"CHANNEL AREA A GIVEN?"}
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeJ["VA = Q BY A"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["HA = VA^2 BY 2G"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Q = 2/3 CD L SQRT 2G TIMES BRACKET"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["(H PLUS HA)^1.5 MINUS HA^1.5"]
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeN["FINAL Q IN CUBIC METER PER SEC"]
    nodeM --> nodeN

    style nodeA fill:#d4edda
    style nodeG fill:#cce5ff
    style nodeM fill:#fff3cd
    style nodeN fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of Physical Setup

Since a physical isometric drawing cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following **textual schematic** accurately represents the geometry students must visualize:

```
                  ↑ Free Water Surface
   ┌──────────────┴──────────────────────┐
   │         H (Head)                   │
   │         ↓                           │
   │    ┌──────┐ ←── Notch Crest (L) ──→ │
   │    │      │                         │
   │    │      │  ← Silvertine Plate    │
   │    │      │     (Thin)              │
   │    │      │                         │
   ╞════╧══════╧═════════════════════════╡
   │    │      │      Nappe Sheet        │
   │    │      │     (water sheet)       │
   │    │      │      flowing out        │
   │    │      ↓                         │
   │    │       Vena Contracta           │
   │    │       (narrowest section)      │
   │    │                                │
   └────┴────────────────────────────────┘
        ↓
   Discharge collected
   in measuring tank
```

**Key features labeled in the schematic:**

1. **H** — Head of water, measured vertically from the crest to the free water surface.
2. **L** — Length of the crest (horizontal width of the notch).
3. **Nappe** — The free-falling sheet of water leaving the notch.
4. **Vena Contracta** — The narrowest cross-section of the nappe, formed due to streamline curvature, typically at a depth of approximately **0.5H** below the crest.
5. **Thin Plate** — The metallic plate in which the notch is cut. "Thin" means the nappe springs clear of the plate edge.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the terms:**
**(a) Notch**
**(b) Weir**
**(c) Nappe**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Notch (1 mark):** A notch is a calibrated opening cut in a thin metallic plate fixed on the side of a tank or reservoir for the purpose of measuring the flow of water. The depth of water above the crest of the notch is called the **head**.

**(b) Weir (1 mark):** A weir is a barrier or obstruction constructed across an open channel (such as a stream or river) to measure the discharge of flowing water. It is essentially a large-scale version of a notch, typically built using masonry or concrete.

**(c) Nappe (1 mark):** The nappe is the sheet of water that flows over the crest of a notch or weir and falls freely under gravity. The nappe has a contracted section known as the **vena contracta**, located at a depth of approximately **0.5H** below the crest level.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the discharge formula over a rectangular notch and explain each term. Why is the coefficient of discharge less than unity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Discharge Formula (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Actual discharge (m³/s)
- **C_d** = Coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- **L** = Length of the notch crest (m)
- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- **H** = Head of water above the crest (m)

**Why C_d < 1 (1 mark):** The actual discharge is always less than the theoretical discharge because of:
- **Vena contracta formation** — streamlines curve inward, reducing the effective flow area
- **Frictional losses** along the edges of the notch
- **Turbulence and viscous effects** in the nappe
- **Surface tension** effects at the edges

These combined losses are consolidated into the empirical coefficient **C_d**, which typically ranges from **0.61 to 0.66**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Discharge Problem

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Module 3]**

A rectangular notch is fixed across a rectangular channel of width **2.0 m**. The crest length of the notch is **1.0 m** and the head of water over the notch is **300 mm**. The bed level of the channel is **0.5 m** below the crest of the notch. The channel has a bed slope of **1 in 1000**. Take **C_d = 0.62**.

**Calculate:**

**(a)** The discharge over the notch in **litres/second**, neglecting the velocity of approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The discharge over the notch in **litres/second**, considering the velocity of approach. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Discharge without Velocity of Approach (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data (1 mark):**
- L = 1.0 m
- H = 300 mm = 0.30 m
- C_d = 0.62
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- √(2g) = 4.43 m^(1/2)/s

**Step 2 — State the Formula (1 mark):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times C_d \times L \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate H^(3/2) (1 mark):**

$$H^{3/2} = (0.30)^{3/2} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{0.30} = 0.30 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643 \, m^{3/2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Compute (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.0 \times 4.43 \times 0.1643$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 1.0 \times 4.43 \times 0.1643$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 = 0.4133$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \times 1.0 = 0.4133$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \times 4.43 = 1.8309$$

$$Q = 1.8309 \times 0.1643 = 0.3008 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 5 — Convert to L/s (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Q = 0.3008 \, m^3/s = 300.8 \, L/s}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer Statement (1 mark):**
> The discharge over the rectangular notch, neglecting velocity of approach, is approximately **300.8 L/s** or **0.301 m³/s**.

---

#### Part (b) — Discharge with Velocity of Approach (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate Approach Velocity (1 mark):**

The water depth upstream of the notch = H + (depth of crest above bed) = 0.30 + 0.50 = **0.80 m**

Channel cross-sectional area at upstream:

$$A = 2.0 \times 0.80 = 1.6 \, m^2$$

**Step 2 — Approach Velocity (1 mark):**

$$v_a = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.3008}{1.6} = 0.188 \, m/s$$

**Step 3 — Head Due to Velocity of Approach (1 mark):**

$$h_a = \frac{v_a^{2}}{2g} = \frac{(0.188)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.03534}{19.62} = 0.00180 \, m$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Velocity of Approach Formula (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} \left[ (H + h_a)^{3/2} - h_a^{3/2} \right]$$

Compute (H + h_a)^(3/2):

$$H + h_a = 0.30 + 0.00180 = 0.3018 \, m$$

$$(H + h_a)^{3/2} = (0.3018)^{3/2} = 0.3018 \times \sqrt{0.3018} = 0.3018 \times 0.5494 = 0.1659 \, m^{3/2}$$

Compute h_a^(3/2):

$$h_a^{3/2} = (0.00180)^{3/2} = 0.00180 \times \sqrt{0.00180} = 0.00180 \times 0.04243 = 0.0000764 \, m^{3/2}$$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation (1 mark):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.0 \times 4.43 \times (0.1659 - 0.0000764)$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 1.0 \times 4.43 \times 0.1658$$

$$Q = 0.3010 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 6 — Convert and State (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Q = 0.3010 \, m^3/s = 301.0 \, L/s}$$

**Comparison Insight (1 mark):** The discharge with velocity of approach (301.0 L/s) is slightly higher than without (300.8 L/s) — only by **0.07%**. This is because the approach velocity is very small compared to the jet velocity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Problem

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Module 3]**

A rectangular weir of crest length **0.9 m** is constructed across a rectangular channel of width **1.5 m**. The head of water over the weir is **150 mm**. The crest of the weir is at a height of **0.3 m** above the bed of the channel. The weir has **two end contractions**.

**Calculate:**

**(a)** The discharge over the weir using the **Francis formula**, in **litres/second**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The discharge over the weir using the **Rehbock formula**, in **litres/second**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Discharge Using Francis Formula (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Data (1 mark):**
- L = 0.9 m
- H = 150 mm = 0.15 m
- n = 2 end contractions
- Francis formula constant = 1.84 (for rectangular weir in SI, m and L/s units)

**Note:** Francis formula in SI:

$$Q = 1.84 \times L_{eff} \times H^{3/2}$$

where Q is in L/s (litres per second) when L is in metres and H is in metres.

**Step 2 — Calculate Effective Length (1 mark):**

$$L_{eff} = L - 0.1nH = 0.9 - 0.1 \times 2 \times 0.15 = 0.9 - 0.03 = 0.87 \, m$$

**Step 3 — Calculate H^(3/2) (1 mark):**

$$H^{3/2} = (0.15)^{3/2} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.15 \times 0.3873 = 0.05809 \, m^{3/2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Francis Formula (2 marks):**

$$Q = 1.84 \times L_{eff} \times H^{3/2}$$

$$Q = 1.84 \times 0.87 \times 0.05809$$

$$Q = 1.84 \times 0.87 = 1.6008$$

$$Q = 1.6008 \times 0.05809 = 0.09299 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 5 — Convert to L/s (1 mark):**

$$Q = 0.09299 \, m^3/s = 92.99 \, L/s$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Q \approx 93.0 \, L/s \, \text{(Francis Formula)}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Discharge Using Rehbock Formula (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall Rehbock's Formula (1 mark):**

The Rehbock formula for a rectangular weir is:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{2g} \, C_d \, L_{eff} \, H^{3/2}$$

where **C_d** is given by the Rehbock expression:

$$C_d = 0.611 + 0.075 \frac{H}{P}$$

where **P** is the height of the crest above the channel bed (also called the **sill height**).

**Step 2 — Identify P (1 mark):**
- P = 0.3 m (given)

**Step 3 — Calculate C_d Using Rehbock (1 mark):**

$$C_d = 0.611 + 0.075 \times \frac{0.15}{0.3} = 0.611 + 0.075 \times 0.5 = 0.611 + 0.0375 = 0.6485$$

**Step 4 — Apply Rehbock's Formula (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times 0.6485 \times 0.87 \times 0.05809$$

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 4.43 \times 0.6485 \times 0.87 \times 0.05809$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 4.43 = 2.9533$$

$$Q = 2.9533 \times 0.6485 = 1.9152$$

$$Q = 1.9152 \times 0.87 = 1.6662$$

$$Q = 1.6662 \times 0.05809 = 0.09679 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 5 — Convert to L/s (1 mark):**

$$Q = 0.09679 \, m^3/s = 96.79 \, L/s$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Q \approx 96.8 \, L/s \, \text{(Rehbock Formula)}}$$

**Comparison Insight:** Rehbock formula gives **96.8 L/s** while Francis formula gives **93.0 L/s** — a difference of about **4%**, with Rehbock generally considered more accurate for sharp-crested weirs because it includes the sill height parameter P.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS — KTU 2024 EXAMS ⚠️**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Forgetting end contraction correction (LOSES 2-3 marks):**
> If the problem mentions "two end contractions" or "n end contractions," many students apply the formula with the full length L instead of L_eff. Always check for end contractions first.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing H^(3/2) computation (LOSES 1-2 marks):**
> H^(3/2) is NOT H^1.5 by a simple multiplication. The correct method is **H × √H**. For H = 0.15, it is **0.15 × √0.15 = 0.15 × 0.3873 = 0.05809**. Errors here cascade.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Wrong units in final answer (LOSES 1 mark):**
> If the question asks for **L/s**, you MUST convert m³/s to L/s by multiplying by **1000**. Forgetting this is a very common KTU error.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Applying velocity of approach without the channel area (LOSES 2 marks):**
> Velocity of approach can ONLY be computed if the channel area A is given (or computable). The iterative nature (v_a depends on Q, which depends on h_a) requires an initial guess of v_a = 0 and a second iteration for accuracy.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Using Francis formula in SI without the constant adjustment:**
> The classic Francis formula **Q = 1.84 × L × H^(3/2)** gives Q in **ft³/s** when L is in feet and H in feet. In SI units (metres), the same numerical form **1.84 × L × H^(3/2)** gives Q in **L/s** (litres per second), NOT m³/s. Many students mix this up.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Forgetting to state the vena contracta depth (LOSES 1 mark):**
> In definition-type questions, students often write "vena contracta" without specifying that it occurs at a depth of approximately **0.5H** below the crest. Include this numerical detail.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DISCHARGE OVER RECTANGULAR NOTCH/WEIR 🚀**
>
> ✅ **Core Formula (Memorize First):**
> $$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$$
>
> ✅ **Five Critical Variables:**
> - **Q** = discharge (m³/s or L/s)
> - **C_d** = coefficient of discharge (0.61 to 0.66)
> - **L** = crest length (m)
> - **H** = head above crest (m)
> - **g** = 9.81 m/s²
>
> ✅ **End Contraction Rule:**
> Effective length **L_eff = L − 0.1nH** where n = number of end contractions.
>
> ✅ **Velocity of Approach Formula:**
> $$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} \left[ (H + h_a)^{3/2} - h_a^{3/2} \right]$$
> where $h_a = v_a^2 / 2g$ and $v_a = Q / A$.
>
> ✅ **Francis Formula (SI, Q in L/s):**
> $$Q = 1.84 \times L_{eff} \times H^{3/2}$$
>
> ✅ **Rehbock Formula for C_d:**
> $$C_d = 0.611 + 0.075 \frac{H}{P}$$
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical Constants to Memorize:**
> - √(2g) = **4.43** (SI)
> - (2/3) × √(2g) = **2.953** (SI)
> - 1.84 (Francis constant, SI in L/s)
> - 1 m³/s = **1000 L/s**
>
> ✅ **Notch vs. Weir — One-Line Distinction:**
> Notch = small, thin plate, lab use | Weir = large, masonry/concrete, field use.
>
> ✅ **Vena Contracta Location:** Approximately **0.5H below the crest**.
>
> ✅ **Why C_d < 1:** Losses due to vena contracta, friction, turbulence, surface tension.
>
> ✅ **Order of Computation:**
> 1. Check end contractions → adjust L.
> 2. Check velocity of approach → adjust head.
> 3. Apply the discharge formula.
> 4. Convert units to the required form (m³/s or L/s).
>
> ✅ **Quick Sanity Check:** A rectangular notch with L = 1 m and H = 0.3 m gives Q ≈ 0.3 m³/s. Use this as a benchmark to verify your calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discharge Over Triangular Notch/Weir
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discharge Over Triangular Notch/Weir

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Triangular Notch** (also called a **V-Notch Weir**) is a sharp-edged, V-shaped opening cut into a vertical thin metallic plate (or wooden board) installed across an open channel or tank, with its apex pointing downward. The opening allows the free flow of liquid over it, and the depth of liquid above the apex of the notch is called the **Head (H)**.

The **discharge (Q)** over such a notch is the volume of liquid flowing through the V-shaped opening per unit time, and it is calculated by integrating the velocity of elemental horizontal strips of fluid across the notch area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Notch vs. Weir:**
> - When the opening is **small** compared to the head of liquid, it is called a **Notch**.
> - When the opening has a **considerable width** (crest length is significant), it is called a **Weir**.
> - Both function on the same physical principle; the term "notch" is conventional for small triangular openings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen sink** with a small triangular metal flap installed at the drain. When water fills the sink, it spills over the inverted-triangle opening. The higher the water level (head, H) above the apex, the faster and more voluminous the outflow. The triangular shape means the **effective flow area grows as a square** of the head (area ∝ H²), so even a small increase in water level produces a **dramatic increase in discharge** — much more sensitive than a rectangular opening of the same size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a triangular notch?**
> - Triangular notches are **preferred for measuring low discharges** with high accuracy.
> - A **rectangular weir** has a minimum flow even at zero head (theoretically), whereas a triangular notch **closes completely** when H = 0, giving a **wide range of flow measurement** from very low to moderate discharges.
> - Standard laboratory notches are usually cut at **θ = 90°** (right-angled V).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- **C_d** = Coefficient of discharge for triangular notch = **0.60 to 0.62** (commonly taken as **0.61** for a 90° V-notch in KTU problems unless otherwise specified).
- **θ** = Angle of the notch at the apex (in degrees or radians).
- **H** = Head of liquid above the apex of the notch, measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Q** = Discharge in **m³/s** (or **litres/second** = multiply by 1000).

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numericals, always keep H in metres and g in m/s² to obtain Q directly in m³/s.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Setup & Underlying Assumptions

The derivation of discharge over a triangular notch is built on the following **idealized assumptions** (Krishna Raju, Modi & Seth, K Subramanya standard textbook framework):

1. The notch is a **sharp-edged**, V-shaped opening.
2. The plate is **thin**, so the nappe (flowing sheet) springs clear and atmospheric pressure acts below the nappe.
3. The **velocity of approach** is negligible (the channel cross-section is large compared to the notch area).
4. The **head H is measured at a point sufficiently far upstream** where the liquid surface is nearly horizontal.
5. The flow obeys **Torricelli's law** at every elemental strip: $v = \sqrt{2g \cdot h}$, where $h$ is the depth of that strip below the free surface.
6. The **coefficient of discharge C_d** accounts for all real-world losses (contraction, viscosity, turbulence).

### 2.2 Logic Steps — How the Formula Is Built

1. **Identify a horizontal elemental strip** at depth $h$ below the free water surface, with thickness $dh$.
2. The strip lies inside the V-notch, so its **width (b)** depends on its vertical position through similar triangles: $b = 2h \tan(\theta/2)$.
3. **Area of the strip** = $b \cdot dh = 2h \tan(\theta/2) \, dh$.
4. **Theoretical velocity** of water through the strip (from Torricelli's theorem) = $\sqrt{2gh}$.
5. **Theoretical discharge through the strip** = Area × Velocity = $2h \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$.
6. **Integrate** from $h = 0$ to $h = H$ to get the **theoretical discharge**.
7. **Multiply by C_d** to get the **actual discharge** over the triangular notch.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Theoretical discharge (Q_th) | $Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ | m³/s | Assumes ideal fluid, no losses |
| 2 | Actual discharge (Q) | $Q = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ | m³/s | **Master KTU formula** |
| 3 | For 90° V-notch (θ = 90°) | $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ | m³/s | Since $\tan(45°) = 1$ |
| 4 | Numerical value of constant | $Q \approx 2.22 \, C_d \, H^{5/2}$ (for 90°) | m³/s | Using $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 5 | Often-used C_d value | 0.60 – 0.62 | dimensionless | KTU problems: 0.61 unless stated |
| 6 | Area of flow at any depth h | $a = 2h \tan(\theta/2) \, dh$ | m² | Elemental strip area |
| 7 | Velocity at depth h | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s | Torricelli's law |
| 8 | Head from Q (inversion) | $H = \left[ \frac{15 \, Q}{8 \, C_d \, \tan(\theta/2) \, \sqrt{2g}} \right]^{2/5}$ | m | Useful for calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Law Insight:** The discharge varies as $H^{5/2}$ (i.e., $H^{2.5}$). This is **steeper than the 3/2 power** law of a rectangular weir, which is why triangular notches are excellent for **low-flow sensitivity** and **wide dynamic range**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stream gauging stations** use 90° V-notch weirs to measure small stream flows precisely.
- **Cooling water outlets** in power plants and chemical industries use V-notch weirs for accurate discharge measurement.
- **Sewage treatment plants** employ V-notch weirs at the outflow to monitor treated water quantity.
- **Irrigation canals** use triangular notches as efficient flow dividers and measurement devices.
- **Chemical dosing tanks** rely on V-notch weirs for precise small-volume discharge control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Triangular Notch Discharge Formula

**Setup (refer to the schematic in Section 4):**
- Let θ be the full angle of the V-notch at its apex.
- Let H be the head of liquid measured vertically from the apex up to the free water surface.
- Consider a thin horizontal elemental strip of water at depth $h$ below the free surface, with thickness $dh$.

**Step 1 — Determine the width of the elemental strip using similar triangles.**

At the free surface ($h = H$), the width of the notch is $B = 2H \tan(\theta/2)$.

At depth $h$, by **similar triangles** (triangles formed by the V-notch walls are geometrically similar at every elevation):

$$
b = 2h \tan(\theta/2)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the elemental area.**

$$
dA = b \cdot dh = 2h \tan(\theta/2) \, dh
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Torricelli's theorem for the theoretical velocity at depth h.**

The velocity of a jet of liquid leaving an orifice (or notch) under a head $h$ is given by Torricelli's theorem:

$$
v = \sqrt{2gh}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the theoretical elemental discharge (dQ_th).**

$$
dQ_{th} = dA \cdot v = 2h \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2gh} \, dh
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the integrand.**

$$
dQ_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{3/2} \, dh
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $h = 0$ (at the apex) to $h = H$ (at the free surface).**

$$
Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{3/2} \, dh
$$

**Step 7 — Carry out the integration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{h^{5/2}}{5/2} \right]_{0}^{H} \\
Q_{th} &= 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{5} \left[ H^{5/2} - 0 \right] \\
Q_{th} &= \frac{4}{5} \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Introduce the coefficient of discharge C_d.**

The real discharge is always less than the theoretical due to friction, contraction, and viscous effects. We multiply by $C_d$:

$$
Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C_d \cdot \frac{4}{5} \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \\
Q &= \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master governing equation** for discharge over a triangular notch — to be memorised for all KTU numerical problems.

**Step 9 — Special case: 90° V-notch (θ = 90°).**

When $\theta = 90°$, then $\theta/2 = 45°$, and $\tan(45°) = 1$. The equation simplifies to:

$$
Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
$$

Substituting $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ and $C_d = 0.61$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2g} &= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} \approx 4.429 \, m^{1/2}/s \\
\frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} &= \frac{8}{15} \times 0.61 \times 4.429 \\
&\approx 1.441 \times 0.61 \\
&\approx 1.441 \times 0.61
\end{aligned}
$$

Recomputing carefully:

$$
\frac{8}{15} = 0.5333, \quad 0.5333 \times 0.61 = 0.3253, \quad 0.3253 \times 4.429 \approx 1.441
$$

So the simplified empirical formula is:

$$
Q \approx 1.441 \, H^{5/2} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}
$$

The commonly quoted "round" empirical form is:

$$
Q \approx 2.22 \, C_d \, H^{5/2} \quad \text{(verified: } 2.22 \times 0.61 \approx 1.354 \text{, close to above)}
$$

Either form is acceptable in KTU solutions; the **exact formula** $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ is preferred for full marks.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 type):**
A 90° V-notch is fitted in a vertical wall. The head of water above the apex is **20 cm**. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.62**, calculate the discharge over the notch in **litres/second**. Take $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- θ = 90°, so tan(θ/2) = tan(45°) = 1
- H = 20 cm = **0.20 m**
- C_d = 0.62
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Formula:**

$$
Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
Q = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.62 \times 1 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.20)^{5/2}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \, m^{1/2}/s
$$

$$
(0.20)^{5/2} = (0.20)^{2.5} = (0.20)^2 \times \sqrt{0.20} = 0.04 \times 0.4472 = 0.01789 \, m^{5/2}
$$

$$
\frac{8}{15} \times 0.62 \times 4.4294 = 0.5333 \times 0.62 \times 4.4294 = 0.3307 \times 4.4294 = 1.4650
$$

$$
Q = 1.4650 \times 0.01789 = 0.02621 \, m^3/s
$$

**Convert to litres/second:**

$$
Q = 0.02621 \times 1000 = 26.21 \, L/s
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always **double-check the units of H** (cm vs m). One of the most common KTU mistakes is feeding H in cm directly into the formula, which inflates Q by a factor of $\sqrt{1000}^5 \approx 3.16 \times 10^9$.

**Answer:** $Q \approx 26.21 \, L/s$

### 3.3 Inversion of Formula — Finding H from a Given Q

**Problem:** A 60° V-notch discharges **0.05 m³/s** of water. If C_d = 0.60, find the head H. Take $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Solution:**

**Given:** θ = 60°, so tan(30°) = 0.5774; Q = 0.05 m³/s; C_d = 0.60.

**Rearrange the formula:**

$$
H^{5/2} = \frac{15 \, Q}{8 \, C_d \, \tan(\theta/2) \, \sqrt{2g}}
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H^{5/2} &= \frac{15 \times 0.05}{8 \times 0.60 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294} \\
H^{5/2} &= \frac{0.75}{12.2725} \\
H^{5/2} &= 0.06112
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solve for H:**

$$
H = (0.06112)^{2/5} = (0.06112)^{0.4}
$$

Taking the logarithm: $\log(0.06112) = -1.2139$, multiplied by 0.4 = $-0.4856$. Antilog = $10^{-0.4856} \approx 0.327$.

$$
H \approx 0.327 \, m = 32.7 \, cm
$$

**Answer:** $H \approx 32.7 \, cm$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of Discharge Through a Triangular Notch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reservoir Tank with Static Water"] --> B["Free Water Surface at Elevation H"]
    B --> C["Notch Plate Vertical Thin Wall"]
    C --> D["V-Shaped Notch Angle theta at Apex"]
    D --> E["Elemental Strip at Depth h, Thickness dh"]
    E --> F["Strip Width b equals 2h tan theta over 2"]
    E --> G["Theoretical Velocity v equals sqrt 2gh"]
    F --> H["Elemental Area dA equals b times dh"]
    H --> I["Elemental Discharge dQ equals dA times v"]
    G --> I
    I --> J["Integrate dQ from 0 to H"]
    J --> K["Theoretical Discharge Qth equals 8 over 15 tan theta over 2 sqrt 2g H power 5 over 2"]
    K --> L["Multiply by Cd equals 0.61"]
    L --> M["Actual Discharge Q in cubic metres per second"]
    M --> N["Convert to Litres per second by multiplying by 1000"]
    M --> O["Engineering Application Stream Gauging, Sewage Plant, Irrigation"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Mapping — Real Flow Architecture

The above Mermaid diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps every computational step from the physical reservoir down to the engineering applications. Each node represents a transformation in the discharge calculation pipeline:

| Node | Stage | Output |
|------|-------|--------|
| A → B | Identify head H | Head measurement location |
| C → D | Geometry | Notch angle |
| D → E | Slice the flow | Elemental strip |
| E → F | Similar triangles | Strip width function |
| E → G | Torricelli's law | Velocity profile |
| H, I → J | Definite integral | Total theoretical discharge |
| J → K | Analytical result | $Q_{th}$ formula |
| K → L | Empirical correction | $C_d$ multiplication |
| L → M | Final answer | Discharge in m³/s |
| M → N, O | Unit conversion & use | Litres/s, real-world use |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU viva-voce, drawing this flow diagram and explaining each block carries **3 to 5 marks** in many questions on flow measurement devices. Practice reproducing it from memory.

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic of a Triangular Notch (For Rough Sketches in Exam)

```
              Free Water Surface
   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
   |                                |
   |   ↑ Head H measured here       |
   |   |                            |
   |   |     Liquid                 |
   |   |                            |
   |   |        /\                  |
   |   |       /  \                 |
   |   |      /    \  θ (apex angle)|
   |   |     /      \               |
   |   |    /        \              |
   |   |   /__________\  ← Apex (tip of V)
   |   |________________________
   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
            ↓ Nappe (flowing sheet)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define a triangular notch. Why is it preferred over a rectangular notch for measuring small discharges?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **triangular notch** is a V-shaped opening cut into a thin vertical plate fitted across a channel/tank. The vertex points downward, and liquid flows over it under a head H measured above the apex.

It is preferred over a rectangular notch for **measuring small discharges** because:
1. The discharge varies as $H^{5/2}$ (steeper relation), giving **higher sensitivity at low heads**.
2. The flow **ceases completely when H = 0**, allowing accurate measurement of very small flows.
3. The **discharge coefficient remains more stable** over a wide range of heads compared to a rectangular weir.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **State the formula for actual discharge over a 90° V-notch and explain the significance of the coefficient of discharge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The actual discharge over a 90° V-notch is:

$$
Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
$$

where C_d is the **coefficient of discharge** (typically 0.60 – 0.62).

**Significance of C_d:** The theoretical derivation assumes ideal flow (no friction, perfect contraction, no viscous losses). In reality, these losses reduce the actual discharge. The coefficient $C_d$ (a dimensionless factor less than 1) empirically accounts for **all these real-world losses**, so multiplying the theoretical discharge by $C_d$ gives the **actual/observed discharge**.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] (Module 3, 14 Marks)**

**Question A — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
Derive an expression for the discharge over a triangular notch with apex angle θ in terms of the head H above the apex and the coefficient of discharge C_d. State the assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
*(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above, with assumptions listed in Section 2.1.)*

**Key steps to present:**
1. State the 5–6 assumptions.
2. Draw a labeled diagram (or use the ASCII schematic in Section 4.3).
3. Define an elemental strip at depth $h$, with thickness $dh$, width $b = 2h \tan(\theta/2)$.
4. $dA = b \cdot dh$, $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.
5. $dQ = dA \cdot v$.
6. Integrate: $Q_{th} = \int_0^H 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, h^{3/2} \, dh = \frac{8}{15} \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$.
7. Multiply by $C_d$: $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$.

**Question A — Part (b) [7 marks]:**
A 90° V-notch is used to measure a discharge of 0.04 m³/s. If the coefficient of discharge is 0.61, find the head over the notch. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** θ = 90°, $\tan(45°) = 1$, $C_d = 0.61$, $Q = 0.04 \, m^3/s$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Formula:**

$$
Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
$$

**Rearrange:**

$$
H^{5/2} = \frac{15 \, Q}{8 \, C_d \sqrt{2g}}
$$

**Compute constants:**

$$
\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294
$$

$$
8 \times 0.61 \times 4.4294 = 21.616
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
H^{5/2} = \frac{15 \times 0.04}{21.616} = \frac{0.60}{21.616} = 0.02775
$$

**Solve for H:**

$$
H = (0.02775)^{0.4}
$$

Using logarithms: $\log(0.02775) = -1.5567$. Multiply by 0.4: $-0.6227$. Antilog: $10^{-0.6227} = 0.2383$.

$$
\boxed{H \approx 0.238 \, m = 23.8 \, cm}
$$

---

**Question B — Part (a) [7 marks] (Alternative choice):**
A 60° triangular notch discharges water at a rate of 25 L/s. Calculate the head over the notch. Take $C_d = 0.60$ and $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** θ = 60°, $\tan(30°) = 0.5774$, $C_d = 0.60$, $Q = 25 \, L/s = 0.025 \, m^3/s$.

**Formula:**

$$
Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}
$$

**Rearrange:**

$$
H^{5/2} = \frac{15 \, Q}{8 \, C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g}}
$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$
8 \times 0.60 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 = 2.4 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 = 2.4 \times 2.5575 = 6.138
$$

**Substitute Q:**

$$
H^{5/2} = \frac{15 \times 0.025}{6.138} = \frac{0.375}{6.138} = 0.06110
$$

**Solve for H:**

$$
H = (0.06110)^{0.4}
$$

$\log(0.06110) = -1.2141$. Times 0.4: $-0.4856$. Antilog: $0.3269$.

$$
\boxed{H \approx 0.327 \, m = 32.7 \, cm}
$$

**Question B — Part (b) [7 marks]:**
Explain **any three advantages** and **any two limitations** of triangular notches/weirs as flow measurement devices.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **High sensitivity at low flows** — the $H^{5/2}$ relationship makes even tiny head changes produce measurable discharge variations.
2. **Wide measurement range** — from nearly zero to moderate discharge, since the flow stops completely at H = 0.
3. **Stable discharge coefficient** — $C_d$ remains nearly constant over a wide head range, making calibration reliable.
4. **Self-cleaning to a degree** — the sharp V-shape and high velocities reduce sediment deposition at the apex.
5. **Simpler construction** — only a single plate with a V-cut is needed.

**Limitations:**
1. **Not suitable for large discharges** — the V-opening restricts flow; for high discharges a rectangular or broad-crested weir is preferred.
2. **Requires careful installation** — must be truly vertical and the plate must be thin; otherwise the nappe does not spring clear and the formula becomes invalid.
3. **Head must be measured at a distance ≥ 4H upstream** to avoid the drawdown curve's influence.
4. **Submerged/weeping nappe** conditions (downstream water level rising above the apex) violate the derivation assumptions and need correction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency (–2 to –3 marks):** Feeding H in centimetres directly into the formula is the #1 mistake. Always convert to metres **first**.
> 2. **Forgetting the V-notch half-angle:** When using $H^{5/2}$, students sometimes write the formula for a rectangular weir ($H^{3/2}$) — instant loss of marks in derivation.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram (–1 to –2 marks):** Always draw a labeled free-body diagram of the notch with H, θ, h, dh, and the elemental strip.
> 4. **Rounding C_d too aggressively:** Use 0.60, 0.61, or 0.62 — never "0.6" if a more precise value is given in the problem.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** For a derivation question, listing at least 4–5 assumptions is mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Incorrect integration bounds:** Limits must be 0 to H, not –H to +H. The notch starts at the apex (h = 0).
> 7. **Inverse problem (find H from Q):** Many students forget to raise both sides to the 2/5 power and stop at $H^{5/2}$, leaving the answer half-solved — guaranteed mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU Fluid Mechanics Module 3**

- ✅ **Master formula:** $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ — must be committed to memory.
- ✅ For **90° V-notch**: $\tan(45°) = 1$, so the formula simplifies to $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$.
- ✅ For **60° V-notch**: $\tan(30°) = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.5774$.
- ✅ **C_d for triangular notch** is typically **0.60 – 0.62**; use **0.61** if not specified.
- ✅ **H must be in metres** before plugging into the formula; g in m/s² gives Q in m³/s.
- ✅ **Power law:** $Q \propto H^{5/2}$ — steeper than the rectangular weir's $H^{3/2}$.
- ✅ Triangular notches are **best for small discharges** with high precision.
- ✅ **Assumptions** (state in derivations): thin plate, sharp edges, no velocity of approach, atmospheric pressure under nappe, ideal fluid behavior except C_d correction.
- ✅ **Conversion:** 1 m³/s = 1000 L/s; always check the units requested in the problem.
- ✅ **Inverse formula:** $H = \left[ \frac{15 Q}{8 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g}} \right]^{2/5}$ — raise to the 2/5 power, not 1/(5/2) (same math, but write it explicitly).
- ✅ Triangular notches are used in **stream gauging, sewage plants, irrigation, and chemical dosing** — mention at least one application in theory questions for bonus context.
- ✅ **Velocity of approach** is neglected in the basic KTU-level formula; it is a higher-syllabus correction factor (not required in this module's basic treatment).
- ✅ Triangular notches give **higher accuracy at low heads** than rectangular weirs; mention this comparison whenever a comparison question is asked.
- ✅ **Diagrammatic representation** (V-notch with H, θ, h, dh, elemental strip) is mandatory for full marks in KTU 14-mark derivation questions.
- ✅ For 90° V-notch with C_d = 0.61, the **empirical shorthand** is $Q \approx 1.44 \, H^{5/2}$ (in SI units) — useful for sanity-checking numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discharge Over Trapezoidal Notch/Weir
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Discharge Over Trapezoidal Notch/Weir # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Trapezoidal Notch / Weir (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A **trapezoidal notch** is a **V-shaped opening with a flat bottom (crest)** cut into a vertical plate or wall across an open channel. It is a **hydraulic composite structure** formed by the **superposition of a rectangular weir and a triangular (V-notch) weir**. When the opening is large enough to be classified as a weir (i.e., the crest length **L ≥ 0.5 m** in practice), it is called a **trapezoidal weir**. **Geometric Specification:** - **Crest length (L):** horizontal flat bottom of the notch (in metres, **m**). - **Side slope angle (θ/2):** the half-angle made by each inclined side with the vertical centre-line. - **Head over the notch (H):** the vertical height of water above the crest, measured at a point sufficiently upstream to avoid the draw-down curve (in metres, **m**). - **Discharge coefficient (Cd):** dimensionless constant, typically **0.60 – 0.62** for a standard trapezoidal notch. The classical "Cipolletti weir" is a special case where the side slopes are fixed at **1 horizontal : 4 vertical** (i.e., tan(θ/2) = 1/4). For this geometry, the discharge coefficient **Cd ≈ 0.63** and remains practically **constant** over a wide range of heads — a property that makes the Cipolletti weir especially valuable in irrigation and stream gauging. --- ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Analogy — The "Bathtub Overflow"** > Imagine your bathtub has a flat-bottomed, V-sided cut-out at its overflow edge. > - The **flat bottom** behaves like a small **rectangular spillway** — water spills uniformly across a fixed width. > - The **slanted sides** behave like a **V-notch** — they get wider as the water level rises, so they "absorb" extra discharge at higher heads. > - The trapezoidal notch simply **combines both behaviours**: a fixed-width contribution plus a head-sensitive triangular contribution. > > **Why use it?** A pure rectangular weir fails at low heads (it under-measures small flows), and a pure V-notch fails at high heads (it over-measures due to viscous effects and the crest gets drowned). A **trapezoidal notch balances both regimes** — accurate from low to high discharges — which is exactly what KTU examiners test in numerical problems. --- ## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | | Density of water | ρ | **1000 kg/m³** | | Coefficient of discharge | Cd | **0.60 – 0.63** (dimensionless) | | Cipolletti side slope | 1H : 4V | tan(θ/2) = **0.25** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Trapezoidal Section? The discharge over any open-channel notch is governed by **Torricelli's Law** integrated across the wetted area of the opening. The choice of opening shape is a trade-off between: 1. **Sensitivity at low heads** → favours a V-notch (small H → large proportional change in width). 2. **Capacity at high heads** → favours a rectangular notch (large H → large constant crest length). 3. **Constant Cd** → favours a trapezoidal section (Cipolletti geometry). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The trapezoidal weir is presented in Module 3 as the **practical synthesis** of the rectangular weir (Module 3) and the triangular weir (Module 3). Examiners often ask students to **derive** the discharge equation by combining the two results rather than re-deriving from first principles — so memorising the **final Q-formula** is high-yield. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Discharge Equation 1. **Consider a horizontal elemental strip** at depth *h* below the free water surface, of thickness *dh*, and located at height *(H − h)* above the crest. 2. The **length of this strip** is the crest length *L* plus the contribution from the two slanted sides: *Strip length = L + 2(H − h)·tan(θ/2)* 3. **Elemental area**: dA = [L + 2(H − h)·tan(θ/2)]·dh 4. **Theoretical velocity** (Torricelli): v = √(2gh) 5. **Elemental theoretical discharge**: dQ = v·dA = √(2g)·√h·[L + 2(H − h)·tan(θ/2)]·dh 6. **Integrate** from *h = 0* to *h = H* to get total theoretical discharge. 7. **Multiply by Cd** to obtain the **actual discharge**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for KTU Board Exam** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Length of elemental strip | $\ell(h) = L + 2(H-h)\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | m | Combines rect. + triangular parts | | 2 | Theoretical velocity (Torricelli) | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s | h = depth below free surface | | 3 | Theoretical discharge (rectangular part) | $Q_{th,rect} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | m³/s | Standard rectangular weir | | 4 | Theoretical discharge (triangular part) | $Q_{th,tri} = \dfrac{8}{15}\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ | m³/s | Standard V-notch | | 5 | **Total actual discharge (GENERAL trapezoidal)** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2} + \dfrac{8}{15}\,C_d\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ | m³/s | **KTU Board Master Equation** | | 6 | **Cipolletti weir** (1H : 4V, Cd ≈ 0.63) | $Q = 1.84\,L\,H^{3/2}$ | m³/s | Simplified empirical form | | 7 | Numerical constant | $\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = 4.429$ | m^0.5 / s | Use 4.43 in KTU problems | | 8 | Numerical constant (pre-multiplied) | $\dfrac{2}{3} \times 4.429 = 2.953$ | — | Common prefactor | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Irrigation Channels & Canal Headworks:** Cipolletti weirs are installed at the head of distributaries to measure discharge to fields accurately without requiring frequent re-calibration. - **Stream Gauging Stations:** Used in hydrology to measure river discharge in the range 0.005 to 30 m³/s. - **Effluent Treatment Plants:** Measure flow of treated wastewater before discharge into natural water bodies. - **Hydraulic Laboratory Experiments:** Trapezoidal notches are used as control structures in physical models of spillways and barrages. - **Cooling Water Circuits in Thermal Power Plants:** Measure make-up water flow into cooling towers. > **Why this matters in KTU:** A Civil/Mechanical engineer designing a small hydropower intake or an irrigation network must know that the **trapezoidal weir provides a near-constant Cd** — saving significant calibration cost over its operating life. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Discharge Equation > **Assumption (KTU Standard):** Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow; streamline curvature negligible; velocity at any strip = √(2gh); correction applied globally via Cd. ### Step 1 — Set up the elemental strip Consider an elementary horizontal strip at depth **h** below the free water surface, of thickness **dh**. The strip is located at a height **(H − h)** above the crest. The length of this strip is the sum of the flat crest **L** and the two slanted side contributions: $$ \ell(h) = L + 2\,(H - h)\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) $$ ### Step 2 — Elemental area $$ dA = \ell(h)\,dh = \left[L + 2\,(H-h)\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right] dh $$ ### Step 3 — Elemental theoretical velocity (Torricelli's Law) $$ v = \sqrt{2gh} $$ ### Step 4 — Elemental theoretical discharge $$ dQ_{th} = v \cdot dA = \sqrt{2g}\,\sqrt{h}\,\left[L + 2(H-h)\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right] dh $$ ### Step 5 — Expand the bracket $$ dQ_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\,\left[L\,h^{1/2} + 2H\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)h^{1/2} - 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)h^{3/2}\right] dh $$ ### Step 6 — Integrate from h = 0 to h = H $$ Q_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{H}\left[L\,h^{1/2} + 2H\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)h^{1/2} - 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)h^{3/2}\right] dh $$ Apply the power rule $\int h^{n}\,dh = \dfrac{h^{n+1}}{n+1}$ term by term: $$ Q_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\left[L\cdot\frac{2}{3}H^{3/2} + 2H\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\cdot\frac{2}{3}H^{3/2} - 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\cdot\frac{2}{5}H^{5/2}\right] $$ ### Step 7 — Simplify each term $$ Q_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{3}L\,H^{3/2} + \frac{4}{3}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2} - \frac{4}{5}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2}\right] $$ Combine the two triangular terms using the common denominator **15**: $$ \frac{4}{3} - \frac{4}{5} = \frac{20 - 12}{15} = \frac{8}{15} $$ Therefore: $$ Q_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{3}L\,H^{3/2} + \frac{8}{15}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2}\right] $$ ### Step 8 — Apply the coefficient of discharge The actual discharge is **Cd** times the theoretical discharge: $$ \boxed{\,Q = C_{d}\,\sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{3}L\,H^{3/2} + \frac{8}{15}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2}\right]\,} $$ > **Verification:** If L → 0, the formula reduces to the **V-notch equation**: $Q = \dfrac{8}{15}C_d\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ ✓ > If θ → 0, the formula reduces to the **rectangular weir equation**: $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}C_dL\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ ✓ --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Problem — 7 Marks]** > A trapezoidal notch has a crest length of 0.5 m and side slopes of 1H : 1V. The head of water over the notch is 200 mm. If Cd = 0.62, find the discharge over the notch. **Given:** - L = 0.5 m - tan(θ/2) = 1 (since 1H : 1V → 45° half-angle) - H = 0.2 m - Cd = 0.62 - g = 9.81 m/s² **Step 1 — Compute √(2g)** $$ \sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute H^(3/2)** $$ H^{3/2} = (0.2)^{3/2} = 0.0894 \text{ m}^{3/2} $$ **Step 3 — Compute H^(5/2)** $$ H^{5/2} = (0.2)^{5/2} = 0.01789 \text{ m}^{5/2} $$ **Step 4 — Rectangular-part contribution** $$ Q_1 = \frac{2}{3}\times 0.62 \times 0.5 \times 4.429 \times 0.0894 $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.4133 \times 4.429 \times 0.0894 \times 0.5 \times 0.62 $$ Detailed multiplication: $$ Q_1 = 0.4133 \times 0.198 \approx 0.0819 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 5 — Triangular-part contribution** $$ Q_2 = \frac{8}{15}\times 0.62 \times 1 \times 4.429 \times 0.01789 $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.5333 \times 0.62 \times 4.429 \times 0.01789 $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.3307 \times 0.07924 \approx 0.0262 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 6 — Total discharge** $$ Q = Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.0819 + 0.0262 = 0.1081 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{\,Q \approx 0.108 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 108 \text{ litres/s}\,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Three Notches | Feature | Rectangular Notch | Triangular (V) Notch | Trapezoidal Notch | |---|---|---|---| | Head sensitivity | Low (∝ H^1.5) | High (∝ H^2.5) | Balanced | | Cd constancy | Moderate | Variable with H | **Highest** (Cipolletti) | | Best suited for | High discharges | Low discharges | **Wide range** | | Modular limit | H/P ≤ 1 (approx.) | H/P ≤ 0.4 | H/P ≤ 0.36 (Cipolletti) | | Velocity of approach | Must correct | Must correct | Must correct | | KTU exam frequency | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | **★★★★★** | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks** **"What is a trapezoidal notch? State its advantages over rectangular and triangular notches."** **Model Answer:** > A trapezoidal notch is a **V-shaped notch with a flat horizontal bottom (crest)** cut into a vertical plate, combining the features of a rectangular weir (fixed crest length *L*) and a triangular/V-notch (inclined sides with half-angle θ/2). > > **Advantages:** > 1. **Higher sensitivity at low heads** than a rectangular notch (because of the V-shaped sides). > 2. **Higher discharge capacity at high heads** than a pure V-notch (because of the constant crest contribution). > 3. **Coefficient of discharge Cd is nearly constant** over a wide range of heads — particularly for the **Cipolletti weir** (1H : 4V), where Cd ≈ 0.63. > 4. It can measure a **wider range of discharges** with a single structure, reducing capital cost. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks** **"Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge over a trapezoidal notch having crest length L and side slope 1 horizontal to n vertical."** **Model Answer:** For a horizontal elemental strip at depth *h* below the free surface and thickness *dh*: - Length of strip: $\ell = L + 2(H-h)\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ - Area: $dA = [L + 2(H-h)\tan(\theta/2)]\,dh$ - Velocity (Torricelli): $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ - Elemental discharge: $dQ_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\left[Lh^{1/2} + 2H\tan(\theta/2)h^{1/2} - 2\tan(\theta/2)h^{3/2}\right]dh$ Integrating from 0 to H: $$ Q_{th} = \sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{3}L\,H^{3/2} + \frac{8}{15}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2}\right] $$ > **Note:** Since slope is 1H : nV, $\tan(\theta/2) = 1/n$. Hence the final expression is: $$ Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3}L\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2} + \frac{8}{15n}\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2} $$ --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, Module 3 Choice Pattern]** **(a)** A trapezoidal notch has a crest length of 1.2 m and sides sloping at 1H : 4V. The head of water over the notch is 250 mm. If the coefficient of discharge Cd = 0.62, calculate the **discharge over the notch in litres per second**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same notch, determine the **percentage contribution of the rectangular and triangular portions** to the total discharge. State which part dominates and why. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Given:** L = 1.2 m, slope = 1H : 4V → tan(θ/2) = 1/4 = 0.25, H = 0.25 m, Cd = 0.62, g = 9.81 m/s². **Step 1 — Compute the constants:** $$ \sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{s} $$ $$ H^{3/2} = (0.25)^{3/2} = 0.125 \text{ m}^{3/2} $$ $$ H^{5/2} = (0.25)^{5/2} = 0.03125 \text{ m}^{5/2} $$ **Step 2 — Rectangular part Q₁:** $$ Q_1 = \frac{2}{3} \times C_d \times L \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{3/2} $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.429 \times 0.125 $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 0.6638 = 0.2742 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 3 — Triangular part Q₂:** $$ Q_2 = \frac{8}{15} \times C_d \times \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{5/2} $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.5333 \times 0.62 \times 0.25 \times 4.429 \times 0.03125 $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.5333 \times 0.62 \times 0.0346 = 0.01144 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Total discharge:** $$ Q = Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.2742 + 0.01144 = 0.2857 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{\,Q \approx 0.2857 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 285.7 \text{ L/s}\,} $$ --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Compute percentages:** $$ \%\,\text{Rectangular} = \frac{Q_1}{Q}\times 100 = \frac{0.2742}{0.2857}\times 100 \approx 95.99\% \approx 96\% $$ $$ \%\,\text{Triangular} = \frac{Q_2}{Q}\times 100 = \frac{0.01144}{0.2857}\times 100 \approx 4.01\% \approx 4\% $$ **Step 2 — Discussion:** > The **rectangular portion dominates** (~96%) because: > 1. The crest length L = 1.2 m is large compared to the head H = 0.25 m. > 2. The slope is mild (1H : 4V), giving a small tan(θ/2) = 0.25. > 3. H^(5/2) ≪ H^(3/2) for small H, so the triangular term contributes minimally. > > **Engineering Insight:** For low heads, the trapezoidal notch behaves almost like a **rectangular weir**. The triangular correction only becomes significant when the head is large or the crest length is short. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, Module 3 Choice Pattern]** **(a)** A Cipolletti weir has a crest length of 0.9 m. Calculate the **discharge** when the head over the weir is 300 mm. Take Cd = 0.63. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the same discharge is to be measured by a **right-angled V-notch** (θ = 90°) with the same Cd, find the **head required** over the V-notch. Comment on the relative sensitivity of the two devices. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Given:** L = 0.9 m, H = 0.3 m, Cd = 0.63, g = 9.81 m/s², tan(θ/2) = 1/4 (Cipolletti). **Step 1
