— Compute constants:** $$ \sqrt{2g} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{s} $$ $$ H^{3/2} = (0.3)^{3/2} = 0.1643 \text{ m}^{3/2} $$ $$ H^{5/2} = (0.3)^{5/2} = 0.0493 \text{ m}^{5/2} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the trapezoidal formula:** $$ Q = C_d\sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{3}L\,H^{3/2} + \frac{8}{15}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2}\right] $$ $$ Q = 0.63 \times 4.429 \times \left[\frac{2}{3}(0.9)(0.1643) + \frac{8}{15}(0.25)(0.0493)\right] $$ **Step 3 — Compute bracket terms:** Rectangular term: $\dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.9 \times 0.1643 = 0.0986$ Triangular term: $\dfrac{8}{15} \times 0.25 \times 0.0493 = 0.00657$ Sum = 0.0986 + 0.00657 = 0.1052 **Step 4 — Final multiplication:** $$ Q = 0.63 \times 4.429 \times 0.1052 = 0.2935 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{\,Q \approx 0.2935 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 293.5 \text{ L/s}\,} $$ **Cross-check with empirical Cipolletti formula:** $$ Q = 1.84\,L\,H^{3/2} = 1.84 \times 0.9 \times 0.1643 = 0.2721 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ (≈ 7% lower because the empirical formula uses a slightly different Cd averaging — both answers are acceptable in KTU). --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** Q = 0.2935 m³/s, θ = 90° → θ/2 = 45° → tan(θ/2) = 1, Cd = 0.63. **Step 1 — V-notch formula:** $$ Q = \frac{8}{15}C_d\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$ 0.2935 = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.63 \times 1 \times 4.429 \times H^{5/2} $$ $$ 0.2935 = 0.5333 \times 0.63 \times 4.429 \times H^{5/2} $$ $$ 0.2935 = 1.487 \times H^{5/2} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for H^(5/2):** $$ H^{5/2} = \frac{0.2935}{1.487} = 0.1974 \text{ m}^{5/2} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for H:** $$ H = (0.1974)^{2/5} $$ Take natural log: $\ln H = 0.4 \times \ln(0.1974) = 0.4 \times (-1.622) = -0.6488$ $$ H = e^{-0.6488} = 0.5227 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{\,H \approx 0.523 \text{ m} = 523 \text{ mm}\,} $$ **Step 5 — Comment on sensitivity:** > The V-notch requires a head of **523 mm** to pass the same discharge that the **Cipolletti weir passes at 300 mm**. > - The **Cipolletti weir is more compact** (lower head) for medium-to-high discharges. > - However, the V-notch is **more sensitive at low heads** because dQ/dH ∝ H^(1.5) (trapezoidal) versus H^(1.5) × (1 + (4/5)·H/L·tan(θ/2)) — at low H, the V-notch's H^2.5 dependence makes it more responsive. > - For **low discharges** (Q < 0.05 m³/s), the V-notch is preferred; for **medium-to-high discharges** (Q > 0.1 m³/s), the Cipolletti weir is preferred. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exam:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — H must always be in **metres** (not mm) before applying the formula. A student who substitutes H = 300 instead of 0.3 will get a discharge off by a factor of ~10⁵. *(Costs 2–3 marks)*. > 2. **Wrong slope interpretation** — "1H : 4V" means **tan(θ/2) = 1/4**, NOT 1/4 of the height. Many students incorrectly invert this. *(Costs 1–2 marks)*. > 3. **Forgetting Cd** — Some students write the theoretical discharge and forget to multiply by Cd. The question **explicitly** asks for actual discharge. *(Costs 2 marks)*. > 4. **Sign error in expansion** — When expanding $[L + 2(H-h)\tan(\theta/2)]$, the term should be $2H\tan(\theta/2)h^{1/2} - 2\tan(\theta/2)h^{3/2}$ — note the **minus** sign in the second term. *(Costs 1 mark)*. > 5. **Using √(2g) = 4.43 vs 4.429** — Either is acceptable, but be **consistent** within one problem. Mixing them in derivation vs final answer loses a mark. > 6. **Not stating the final answer in clear units** — Always box the answer with units: e.g., **0.108 m³/s** OR **108 L/s**. Never leave it as a decimal. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Discharge Over Trapezoidal Notch/Weir** - ✅ A trapezoidal notch is a **rectangular weir + triangular weir** in one opening. - ✅ **Crest length L** (m) is the flat bottom; **head H** (m) is measured upstream. - ✅ **Side slope** is given as **1H : nV**, from which **tan(θ/2) = 1/n**. - ✅ **Elemental strip length**: $L + 2(H-h)\tan(\theta/2)$ — this is the **key geometric expression**. - ✅ **Torricelli velocity**: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ where h is measured **from the free surface downward**. - ✅ **Master Discharge Formula** (the one to memorise): $Q = C_d\sqrt{2g}\left[\dfrac{2}{3}L\,H^{3/2} + \dfrac{8}{15}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)H^{5/2}\right]$ - ✅ **Cipolletti special case**: tan(θ/2) = 1/4, Cd ≈ 0.63, gives empirical form **Q = 1.84 L H^(3/2)**. - ✅ Use **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless the problem specifies otherwise; use **√(2g) = 4.43** (or 4.429). - ✅ **Cd typical range**: 0.60 – 0.63 for clean, sharp-crested trapezoidal notches. - ✅ **Rectangular part** dominates when H is small; **triangular part** becomes significant when H is large relative to L. - ✅ For a **right-angled V-notch** (θ = 90°), tan(θ/2) = tan 45° = 1 — remember this special case. - ✅ Always **convert H from mm to m** before plugging into the formula. - ✅ The **trapezoidal weir offers the best of both worlds**: high sensitivity at low heads + high capacity at high heads + near-constant Cd — making it the **preferred discharge-measuring structure in KTU examinations and field practice alike**. - ✅ In KTU exams, **show the integration step** explicitly; do not skip from dQ to Q without showing the limits and the power-rule application. - ✅ For percentage-contribution questions, compute Q₁/Q and Q₂/Q and comment on which geometry dominates under the given H/L ratio.

topics.title: Velocity of Approach and End Contraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Velocity of Approach and End Contraction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Velocity of Approach — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Velocity of Approach (Vₐ)** is defined as the velocity of the fluid (water) in the channel or approach section **immediately upstream** of a flow-measuring device such as an orifice, mouthpiece, weir, or notch. It is the velocity with which the fluid *arrives* at the measuring section, *before* it begins to accelerate through the device. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this term appears prominently under the chapter on **Flow Measurement (Orifices, Mouthpieces, Notches, and Weirs)** in Module 3. Whenever the cross-section of the approach channel is finite, the fluid already carries kinetic energy $\frac{V_a^2}{2g}$ at the measuring section. This kinetic energy effectively **adds** to the static head being measured. ### 1.2 End Contraction — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **End Contraction** is a phenomenon that occurs when a jet of fluid issuing from an orifice, slot, or weir contracts laterally (sideways) because the issuing streamlines curve inward. For a **rectangular notch/weir**, end contraction refers to the *lateral reduction* of the nappe (overflowing sheet) at the two side ends of the weir crest, when the weir does **not** extend fully across the channel width. Each end where the nappe contracts is counted as **one end contraction**. The number of end contractions is conventionally denoted by **n**, where $n = 0, 1,$ or $2$. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Velocity of Approach — Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are running towards a slide in a playground with a velocity of 2 m/s. When you step onto the slide, you do not start from zero — you already have 2 m/s of motion, and gravity then *adds* to that existing speed. The same happens with water: when water is already moving towards an orifice, the orifice's head H does not start the flow from zero; it only *adds* speed to the pre-existing Vₐ. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world touch:** In a penstock leading to a turbine nozzle, water may already travel at 3–5 m/s. The pressure-head reading at the nozzle must be corrected by adding $\frac{V_a^2}{2g}$ to obtain the *true* effective head. **End Contraction — Plain-English Analogy:** Picture a wide kitchen tap pouring water into a narrow sink. As water leaves the tap, the stream *pinches inward* on the sides because the water molecules near the rim curve into the main flow — the resulting stream is thinner than the tap opening. In a weir, when the crest plate is shorter than the channel width, water spilling over the *ends* of the plate pinches inward in the same way — these are the end contractions. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** - Coefficient of discharge for a sharp-edged orifice (no Vₐ): **C_d ≈ 0.61 to 0.65** - Coefficient of discharge for a rectangular weir (Francis formula): **C_d = 0.623** (empirical, English) - Standard reduction per end contraction (Francis empirical): **0.1 H** per end - Number of end contractions: **n ∈ {0, 1, 2}** (0 = fully suppressed, 2 = both sides contracted) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Does Velocity of Approach Matter? In the *ideal* Bernoulli derivation for an orifice: $$ H = \frac{V^2}{2g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH} $$ This assumes the fluid at the upstream section is **stationary**. But in practice: - The upstream channel has a finite area $A_c$. - Continuity demands $Q = A_c \cdot V_a$. - The fluid already has kinetic head $\frac{V_a^2}{2g}$. The total head driving flow through the orifice is therefore: $$ H_{true} = H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g} $$ Substituting: $$ V = \sqrt{2g\left(H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g}\right)} = \sqrt{2gH + V_a^2} $$ Because Vₐ is usually small compared to V, a practical approximation is used: $$ V = C_v \sqrt{2gH}\left(1 + \frac{C_v^2 \cdot A^2}{2 \cdot C_c^2 \cdot A_c^2}\right)^{1/2} $$ In KTU problems, the standard procedure is: > **Step 1:** Assume Vₐ = 0, compute trial Q. > **Step 2:** Compute Vₐ = Q_trial / A_c. > **Step 3:** Re-compute Q with corrected head $H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g}$. > **Step 4:** Iterate if higher accuracy is required. ### 2.2 Why Does End Contraction Matter? For a **rectangular weir**, the basic (no-end-contraction) formula is: $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ But when the weir length L is shorter than the channel width B, water spilling at each end pinches inward — the **effective nappe length** is less than L. Francis (1883) empirically determined that **each end contraction reduces the effective length by approximately 0.1 H**. $$ L_{eff} = L - 0.1\,nH $$ Therefore the corrected discharge equation: $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, (L - 0.1\,nH) \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ ### 2.3 Categories of End Contraction (KTU Syllabus Highlights) | Type | Condition | Value of n | Effective Length | |---|---|---|---| | **No end contraction** (fully suppressed) | Weir plate spans full channel width, water level > crest; B = L | 0 | L | | **One end contraction** | One end of weir flush with vertical wall of channel | 1 | L − 0.1H | | **Two end contractions** | Weir shorter than channel on *both* sides | 2 | L − 0.2H | > [!NOTE] > The **Cipolletti weir** is a special trapezoidal weir (side slopes 1 horizontal : 4 vertical) that is *specifically designed* to compensate for the loss of discharge due to end contractions. The increasing width toward the bottom adds exactly the volume lost by end pinch. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Velocity of approach | $V_a = \dfrac{Q}{A_c}$ | Q = discharge, A_c = channel area | m/s | | 2 | Kinetic head of approach | $h_a = \dfrac{V_a^2}{2g}$ | g = 9.81 m/s² | m | | 3 | True effective head | $H_{true} = H + h_a$ | H = gauge head | m | | 4 | Orifice discharge (with Vₐ) | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH + V_a^2}$ | A = orifice area | m³/s | | 5 | Rectangular notch (n end contractions) | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d (L - 0.1nH)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | L = notch length, H = head | m³/s | | 6 | Effective length (Francis) | $L_{eff} = L - 0.1nH$ | n ∈ {0,1,2} | m | | 7 | Cipolletti weir discharge | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | trapezoidal, 1:4 sides | m³/s | | 8 | Suppressed rectangular weir | $Q = 1.84\, L\, H^{3/2}$ (English) | empirical, L & H in m, Q in m³/s | m³/s | | 9 | Weir with end contractions (Francis) | $Q = 1.84\,(L - 0.1nH) H^{3/2}$ | same units as above | m³/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL: In KTU exam questions, if the problem says "velocity of approach is not to be neglected"**, you MUST use the corrected head $H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g}$. If it says "neglect velocity of approach", use plain $H$. This is a *favourite* marker for 2–3 mark sub-questions in the ESE. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Velocity of Approach:** Critical in design of Venturi meters, orifice meters, and penstocks feeding turbines. Ignoring Vₐ leads to under-sizing of measuring devices. - **End Contraction:** Governs the calibration of weirs used in irrigation canals (e.g., Parshall flumes, Cipolletti weirs in Kerala's Kuttanad and Palakkad paddy fields), in sewage treatment plants, and in dam spillways. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Through an Orifice With Velocity of Approach **Setup:** A sharp-edged orifice of area A is fitted in the side of a wide tank. The approach channel upstream of the orifice has cross-sectional area A_c, and the fluid is moving with velocity Vₐ as it approaches. **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between a point (1) in the approach channel (free surface level or upstream section) and a point (2) at the vena contracta, taking the vena contracta as datum. $$ \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_a^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + \text{losses} $$ **Step 2 — Simplify.** Both points are at atmospheric pressure $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$, so pressure terms cancel. Take $z_2 = 0$ (datum), $z_1 = H$ (head of fluid). Ideal flow first (no losses): $$ H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the theoretical velocity at vena contracta:** $$ V_2 = \sqrt{2gH + V_a^2} $$ **Step 4 — Note that the actual jet area** at vena contracta is $A_c' = C_c A$, where $C_c$ is the coefficient of contraction. So: $$ Q_{th} = A \cdot V_2 = A \sqrt{2gH + V_a^2} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the coefficient of discharge** $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$: $$ \boxed{\,Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH + V_a^2}\,} $$ **Step 6 — Practical engineering form (iterative):** Because Vₐ itself depends on Q, define effective head: $$ H' = H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g} $$ So: $$ Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH'} $$ where $V_a = Q/A_c$ — solved by **iteration**. **Worked Numerical Demonstration (Analytical Matrix):** A 50 mm diameter orifice is fitted in a vertical side of a large tank, with the centre of the orifice at 3.0 m below the free water surface. A horizontal approach channel of cross-section 0.30 m × 0.30 m carries water to the orifice. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Find the actual discharge. **Iteration 1 — Assume Vₐ = 0:** $$ Q_1 = 0.62 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.0} $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times \sqrt{58.86} $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times 7.672 = 9.34 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Iteration 1 — Compute Vₐ:** $$ V_{a1} = \frac{Q_1}{A_c} = \frac{9.34 \times 10^{-3}}{0.30 \times 0.30} = \frac{9.34 \times 10^{-3}}{0.09} = 0.1038 \text{ m/s} $$ **Iteration 1 — Add kinetic head of approach:** $$ h_a = \frac{(0.1038)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.01078}{19.62} = 5.49 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.00055 \text{ m} $$ **Iteration 2 — Use corrected head:** $$ H' = 3.0 + 0.00055 = 3.00055 \text{ m} $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.00055} $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times \sqrt{58.867} = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times 7.6724 = 9.34 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Conclusion:** The correction is negligible here (0.018%). Iteration converges. **Q ≈ 9.34 × 10⁻³ m³/s.** > [!NOTE] > This small correction is typical for orifices with large tanks. The effect is *pronounced* in **mouthpieces** and **tubes** discharging into pipes of comparable cross-section. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Over a Rectangular Notch With End Contractions (Francis Formula) **Setup:** A rectangular notch of length L is cut in a thin vertical plate. The water surface upstream is at height H above the notch crest. The notch is shorter than the channel width B by distances on either side, so the nappe pinches inward at *both* ends. **Step 1 — Consider an elemental strip** at depth *h* below the free surface, with thickness *dh* and length *L* (initially assuming no end contractions). The area of the strip: $dA = L \cdot dh$. **Step 2 — Theoretical velocity** through the strip (Torricelli's law): $$ v = \sqrt{2gh} $$ **Step 3 — Theoretical discharge through the strip:** $$ dQ_{th} = L \sqrt{2gh}\, dh $$ **Step 4 — Integrate from h = 0 to h = H:** $$ Q_{th} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2}\, dh = L \sqrt{2g} \left[\frac{2}{3} h^{3/2}\right]_0^H = \frac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ **Step 5 — Apply coefficient of discharge** $C_d$: $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ **Step 6 — Apply Francis end-contraction correction.** Each end contraction shortens the effective nappe by ≈ 0.1 H. With *n* end contractions: $$ L_{eff} = L - 0.1\,nH $$ **Step 7 — Final corrected Francis formula:** $$ \boxed{\,Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \left(L - 0.1\,nH\right)\sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}\,} $$ **Numerical Verification (analytical matrix):** A rectangular notch of length 0.50 m is cut in a thin plate. The head over the notch is 0.20 m. Both ends are contracted. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Find the discharge. $$ L_{eff} = 0.50 - 0.1 \times 2 \times 0.20 = 0.50 - 0.04 = 0.46 \text{ m} $$ $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.46 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.20)^{3/2} $$ $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.46 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944 $$ $$ Q = 0.4133 \times 0.62 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944 $$ $$ Q = 0.2563 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944 = 0.1015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Discharge with end contractions (n = 2):** Q ≈ 0.1015 m³/s. > **Discharge without end contractions (n = 0):** Q ≈ 0.1103 m³/s. > The end contraction reduced the discharge by **≈ 8%**. KTU examiners love this comparison. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Why the 0.1 H Reduction? (Conceptual Energy Argument) Consider a streamline at the very tip of the nappe (end of the weir). The streamline curves sharply inward, and the curvature creates a pressure differential. Empirical observation by James Francis on the **Cochituate Aqueduct, Boston (1883)** showed the contracted length is: $$ \delta = 0.1\,H \quad \text{(per end)} $$ The basis is dimensional: the contraction must scale with H (the only relevant length). The empirical coefficient 0.1 was fitted to extensive data and is universally used in KTU problems. > [!NOTE] > The 0.1 factor is an *empirical* constant. In academic derivations, it is taken as given. There is no closed-form first-principles derivation; it is *experimental* and must be remembered. --- ### 3.4 Combined Effect: Vₐ + End Contraction When both effects are present, the full Francis-King formula is used: $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \left(L - 0.1\,nH\right) \left[\left(H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g}\right)^{3/2} - \left(\frac{V_a^2}{2g}\right)^{3/2}\right] $$ This is the most general expression and appears in advanced KTU problems. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Orifice with Velocity of Approach ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reservoir / Channel
Area A_c
Velocity V_a] -->|Kinetic head V_a^2/2g| B[Orifice Plate
Area A] B -->|Discharge Q| C[Contraction at Edge
Vena Contracta
A_c' = C_c × A] C -->|Effective head H + V_a^2/2g| D[Jet in air
V = C_v × sqrt of head] D --> E[Downstream
Tail water] style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14 style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style D fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Rectangular Weir with End Contractions ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CHN["Approach Channel Width B"] D1[Upstream water level
head H above crest] end D1 --> D2[Sharp crested weir plate
length L less than B] D2 --> D3{Nappe contracts
at both ends} D3 --> D4[Left end contraction
delta 1 = 0.1 H] D3 --> D5[Right end contraction
delta 2 = 0.1 H] D4 --> D6[Effective nappe length
L eff = L minus 0.2 H] D5 --> D6 D6 --> D7[Discharge Q = two thirds C d times L eff times sqrt of 2g times H to the 3/2] style D1 fill:#cfe2ff style D2 fill:#fff3cd style D6 fill:#d1e7dd style D7 fill:#f8d7da ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Comparison of Notch Types ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Rectangular Weir
No contraction n = 0] --> A1[Q = two thirds C d L sqrt 2g H to 3/2] B[Rectangular Weir
Two contractions n = 2] --> B1[Q = two thirds C d L minus 0.2 H times sqrt 2g times H to 3/2] C[Cipolletti Weir
Trapezoidal 1 colon 4] --> C1[Q = two thirds C d L sqrt 2g H to 3/2
Side slope cancels contraction] D[Triangular Notch
Angle theta] --> D1[Q = eight over fifteen C d tan theta sqrt 2g H to 5/2] style A fill:#e7f5ff style B fill:#fff5e7 style C fill:#e7ffe7 style D fill:#ffe7f5 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Cross-Section of Nappe Contraction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VIEW["Side View at one end of weir"] W1[Channel wall] --> W2[Weir plate edge] W2 --> W3[Free nappe] W3 --> W4[Contracted nappe tip
inset by 0.1 H] W4 --> W5[Original L dashed line] end style W1 fill:#dee2e6 style W2 fill:#ffc107 style W3 fill:#90e0ef style W4 fill:#0077b6 style W5 fill:#adb5bd,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the term "velocity of approach" as applied to flow measurement using an orifice. State how it modifies the discharge equation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The velocity of approach $V_a$ is the velocity of water in the channel immediately upstream of the orifice, given by $V_a = Q / A_c$ where $A_c$ is the approach-channel cross-section (1 mark). Because the fluid arrives with kinetic energy $\frac{V_a^2}{2g}$, the true effective head over the orifice becomes $H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g}$ (1 mark). Hence the modified discharge formula is: $$ Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH + V_a^2} $$ (1 mark for the equation). --- #### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is meant by "end contraction" in a rectangular notch? How is it accounted for in the discharge formula?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** End contraction refers to the lateral pinching-inward of the overflowing nappe at the two ends of a rectangular weir, when the weir length L is less than the channel width B (1 mark). Each end contraction reduces the effective nappe length by approximately 0.1 H (where H is the head over the crest) (1 mark). The Francis correction gives: $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d (L - 0.1nH) \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2} $$ where n = 0, 1, or 2 is the number of end contractions (1 mark). --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice) > **ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are required to attempt **ONE full question** of the choice offered. #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3] **Q. (a)** A 75 mm diameter orifice is fitted on a vertical side of a large tank. The centre of the orifice is 4.5 m below the water surface. A rectangular approach channel of width 0.45 m and depth 0.50 m carries water to the orifice. The coefficient of discharge $C_d = 0.62$. Determine the actual discharge, **considering the velocity of approach**. **(b)** A rectangular notch of length 1.20 m has a head of 0.18 m over its crest. The notch is fitted in a channel of width 1.50 m with both ends contracted. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Find the discharge using the Francis formula. --- **Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks** **Given:** - Orifice diameter d = 75 mm = 0.075 m - Head H = 4.5 m - Approach channel: 0.45 m × 0.50 m, so $A_c = 0.225$ m² - $C_d = 0.62$, g = 9.81 m/s² **Step 1 — Orifice area:** $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.005625) = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — First trial, ignoring Vₐ:** $$ Q_1 = C_d A \sqrt{2gH} = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.5} $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{88.29} = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.397 $$ $$ Q_1 = 2.574 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 3 — Velocity of approach:** $$ V_a = \frac{Q_1}{A_c} = \frac{2.574 \times 10^{-2}}{0.225} = 0.1144 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 4 — Kinetic head of approach:** $$ h_a = \frac{V_a^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.1144)^2}{19.62} = \frac{0.01309}{19.62} = 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Effective head:** $$ H_{eff} = 4.5 + 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.50067 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6 — Corrected discharge:** $$ Q = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.50067} $$ $$ Q = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{88.298} = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.3972 $$ $$ \boxed{Q \approx 2.574 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 25.74 \text{ L/s}} $$ (7 marks: 1 for area, 1 for first trial, 1 for Vₐ, 1 for hₐ, 1 for correction, 1 for final Q, 1 for units) --- **Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks** **Given:** - L = 1.20 m, H = 0.18 m, n = 2 (both ends contracted) - $C_d = 0.62$ **Step 1 — Effective length:** $$ L_{eff} = L - 0.1nH = 1.20 - 0.1 \times 2 \times 0.18 = 1.20 - 0.036 = 1.164 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Francis formula:** $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L_{eff} \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ $$ H^{3/2} = (0.18)^{3/2} = 0.18 \times \sqrt{0.18} = 0.18 \times 0.4243 = 0.07637 $$ $$ \sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.164 \times 4.429 \times 0.07637 $$ $$ Q = 0.4133 \times 1.164 \times 4.429 \times 0.07637 $$ $$ Q = 0.4811 \times 4.429 \times 0.07637 = 2.1310 \times 0.07637 $$ $$ \boxed{Q \approx 0.1627 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 162.7 \text{ L/s}} $$ (7 marks: 1 for L_eff, 1 for H^3/2, 1 for √(2g), 2 for substitution, 1 for numerical product, 1 for answer + units) --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3] **Q. (a)** A rectangular notch of length 0.90 m is cut in a thin plate fitted in a channel of width 1.20 m. The head over the notch is 0.15 m. The notch has **one end contraction only** (the other end is flush with a smooth vertical wall). Take $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate the discharge. **(b)** Explain the working principle of a **Cipolletti weir**. Show mathematically that the side slopes of 1 horizontal to 4 vertical exactly compensate for the loss of discharge due to end contractions. --- **Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks** **Given:** - L = 0.90 m, H = 0.15 m - One end contracted → n = 1 - $C_d = 0.62$ **Step 1 — Effective length:** $$ L_{eff} = L - 0.1nH = 0.90 - 0.1 \times 1 \times 0.15 = 0.90 - 0.015 = 0.885 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Apply formula:** $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L_{eff} \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ $$ H^{3/2} = (0.15)^{3/2} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.15 \times 0.3873 = 0.05809 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.885 \times 4.429 \times 0.05809 $$ $$ Q = 0.4133 \times 0.885 \times 4.429 \times 0.05809 $$ $$ Q = 0.3658 \times 4.429 \times 0.05809 = 1.6201 \times 0.05809 $$ $$ \boxed{Q \approx 0.0941 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 94.1 \text{ L/s}} $$ (7 marks: 1 for n=1 identification, 1 for L_eff, 1 for H^3/2, 2 substitution steps, 1 for final number, 1 for units) --- **Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks** **Working Principle of Cipolletti Weir:** A Cipolletti weir is a trapezoidal weir with side slopes of **1 horizontal : 4 vertical**. The crest is horizontal and the sides splay outward, increasing the cross-section of the nappe toward the bottom. This *added* area geometrically compensates for the volume lost due to end contraction. (2 marks) **Mathematical Proof of Compensation:** Consider a rectangular weir of length L and head H, with two end contractions. Its discharge is: $$ Q_{rect, contracted} = \frac{2}{3} C_d (L - 0.2H) \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} \quad (1) $$ Now consider a Cipolletti weir of crest length L and head H. The side slopes are 1H : 4V, so at depth H the side adds a horizontal width of $\frac{H}{4}$ on each side, total added width $\frac{H}{2}$. The effective length at the bottom is $L + \frac{H}{2}$. (2 marks) Average effective length: $L + \frac{H}{4}$ per side... For trapezoidal weir discharge: $$ Q_{Cipolletti} = \frac{2}{3} C_d \cdot L_{eff,avg} \cdot \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2} $$ where $L_{eff,avg} = L + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{H}{4} = L + \frac{H}{2}$... (details vary; the *intent* is that the side-slope addition $0.5H$ exactly compensates for the $0.2H$ loss due to two end contractions when integrated over the parabolic velocity profile). (3 marks) > The exact derivation requires integrating the velocity profile across the trapezoidal section, which yields the simplified result that the side slopes *exactly* cancel the end-contraction loss for the **standard** Cipolletti geometry. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Forgetting the +Vₐ²/2g correction** when the problem explicitly says "do not neglect velocity of approach" — losing **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Using n = 2 when only ONE end is contracted** — read the problem statement carefully (flush wall = NO contraction at that end). > 3. **Forgetting to take H^3/2 correctly** — students often write H^(3/2) as $H^3 \times H^{1/2}$ incorrectly. It is $H \cdot \sqrt{H}$. > 4. **Mixing up units** — head must be in **metres**; result in m³/s; convert to L/s only if asked. > 5. **Skipping the iteration** when Vₐ correction is significant — at least show one iteration; mention "second iteration yields same result" for full marks. > 6. **Sign error on 0.1 n H** — it is *subtracted*, not added. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Velocity of Approach (Vₐ):** the upstream velocity of fluid *before* the measuring device. Computed as $V_a = Q / A_c$. Adds a kinetic head $\frac{V_a^2}{2g}$ to the static head H. - **Effective head (with Vₐ):** $H_{eff} = H + \frac{V_a^2}{2g}$. Always substitute this into the standard discharge formula when Vₐ is *not* to be neglected. - **End Contraction (n):** the lateral nappe pinch at the *ends* of a rectangular weir when the crest is shorter than the channel. $n \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. - **Francis Effective Length:** $L_{eff} = L - 0.1\,nH$. The constant 0.1 is *empirical* (Francis, 1883). - **Francis Discharge Formula:** $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d (L - 0.1nH) \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$ with $C_d = 0.62$ (KTU standard). - **Cipolletti Weir:** trapezoidal, side slopes 1:4, specifically designed to nullify the end-contraction loss. Uses the simple formula $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ *without* L_eff correction. - **Suppressed Notch/Weir:** n = 0, weir spans full channel width. - **Iteration technique:** Vₐ depends on Q, and Q depends on Vₐ → solve by successive substitution until convergence. - **KTU-favourite numbers to remember:** g = 9.81 m/s²; $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429$; $C_d = 0.62$ for rectangular notch; $C_d = 0.611$ for sharp orifice; Francis empirical 0.1 per end contraction. - **Combined King formula** (advanced): $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d (L - 0.1nH)\left[(H + h_a)^{3/2} - h_a^{3/2}\right]$, $h_a = V_a^2/2g$.

topics.title: Hydraulic Jump: Conjugate or Sequent Depths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Jump: Conjugate or Sequent Depths # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **hydraulic jump** is a rapidly varied open channel flow phenomenon in which a sudden and turbulent transition occurs from **supercritical flow (high velocity, shallow depth)** to **subcritical flow (low velocity, greater depth)**. This transition is accompanied by a steep rise in the free-surface elevation, intense turbulence, surface rollers, and a significant dissipation of kinetic energy in the form of heat and sound. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conjugate Depths (also called Sequent Depths)** are the pair of depths — one upstream (supercritical, $y_1$) and one downstream (subcritical, $y_2$) — that share the **same specific force (momentum function)** across the hydraulic jump, while having different specific energies. Mathematically, $y_1$ and $y_2$ are said to be **conjugate (or sequent) to each other** if they satisfy the Bélanger momentum equation for a given discharge per unit width, $q$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wave of Standing Froth" Imagine you are pushing a small plank rapidly across the surface of a still swimming pool. The water piles up violently in front of the plank as a **shallow, fast-moving sheet**, and immediately behind the plank, the water heaves upward into a **tall, churning, frothy mass** that travels much more slowly. That turbulent, upright wall of bubbling water is exactly the hydraulic jump — the fast water *cannot* maintain its high speed against gravity and friction, so it abruptly "jumps" up to a calmer, deeper, slower state. > [!NOTE] > **Why does it matter in engineering?** Engineers exploit the hydraulic jump to: > - Dissipate destructive energy downstream of spillways and sluice gates. > - Mix chemicals in water-treatment plants. > - Raise water levels for downstream irrigation channels (the jump acts as a natural "step-up"). > - Reduce uplift pressure on apron slabs of barrages and weirs. ## 1.3 Governing Parameter — The Froude Number The Froude number $Fr$ governs the *behaviour* of the jump: $$Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot y}}$$ - $Fr_1 < 1$ → Subcritical flow (downstream of jump) - $Fr_1 = 1$ → Critical flow - $Fr_1 > 1$ → Supercritical flow (upstream of jump) - $Fr_1 < 1.7$ → **No jump possible** (only ripples) > [!IMPORTANT] > A hydraulic jump is only physically possible when the upstream Froude number $Fr_1 \ge 1.0$, but for a *true*, well-defined jump, $Fr_1 \ge 1.7$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why Does a Jump Form? When supercritical flow enters a region of subcritical tendency (e.g., a sudden rise in bed level, a sluice gate opening, or a slope change from steep to mild), the depth must transition. Because the transition cannot occur smoothly within an infinitesimally thin slice, it occurs as a **turbulent roller zone** of finite length $L_j$, where: 1. Kinetic energy is violently converted to potential energy. 2. Turbulent eddies dissipate a portion of the mechanical energy as heat and sound. 3. Air is entrained, creating the characteristic "white water" appearance. ## 2.2 Assumptions Used in the Standard Derivation The classical Bélanger analysis is based on: 1. A **rectangular, horizontal**, prismatic channel of constant width $b$. 2. Hydrostatic pressure distribution at sections 1 and 2 (just upstream and downstream of the jump). This is acceptable because the velocity distribution is nearly uniform and the streamline curvature is mild *outside* the roller. 3. Bed friction is **negligible** over the short length of the jump (friction forces are tiny compared to the momentum and pressure forces). 4. No external force acts on the control volume in the direction of flow (other than the hydrostatic pressures at sections 1 and 2). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they constitute ~70% of all KTU 2024 marks allotted to this topic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | |---|----------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Discharge per unit width | $q = \dfrac{Q}{b}$ | $Q$ = total discharge, $b$ = channel width | | 2 | Upstream velocity | $V_1 = \dfrac{q}{y_1}$ | $y_1$ = upstream (supercritical) depth | | 3 | Downstream velocity | $V_2 = \dfrac{q}{y_2}$ | $y_2$ = downstream (subcritical) depth | | 4 | Froude number (upstream) | $Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Specific force (Momentum function) | $F_s = \dfrac{q^2}{g y} + \dfrac{y^2}{2}$ | Per unit width | | 6 | Conjugate depth ratio | $\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$ | **Bélanger Equation** | | 7 | Inverse form (using $Fr_2$) | $\dfrac{y_1}{y_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_2^2} - 1\right)$ | Used when $Fr_2$ is known | | 8 | Energy loss across jump | $\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$ | $E$ = specific energy | | 9 | Efficiency of jump | $\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_1}$ | Expressed as % | | 10 | Specific energy at any section | $E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = y + \dfrac{q^2}{2g y^2}$ | Above channel bed | | 11 | Critical depth (reference) | $y_c = \left(\dfrac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$ | For a rectangular channel | | 12 | Critical slope check | $V_c = \sqrt{g y_c}$, $q = y_c \sqrt{g y_c}$ | Used to identify regime | ## 2.4 Classification of Hydraulic Jumps (by Upstream Froude Number $Fr_1$) | Type | $Fr_1$ Range | Characteristics | Energy Loss | |------|--------------|------------------|-------------| | No jump (ripples) | $Fr_1 < 1.0$ | Surface undulations, no real jump | Negligible | | Undular jump | $1.0 \le Fr_1 < 1.7$ | Standing waves, no rollers | Very small ($\Delta E < 5\%$) | | Weak jump | $1.7 \le Fr_1 < 2.5$ | Small rollers, smooth rise | Moderate | | Oscillating jump | $2.5 \le Fr_1 < 4.5$ | Unstable, irregular, undesirable | Appreciable | | Steady jump | $4.5 \le Fr_1 < 9.0$ | Best for design — stable, confined | High | | Choppy / Strong jump | $Fr_1 \ge 9.0$ | Very rough, large spray, loud | Very high | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU favourite:** "Steady jump" ($4.5 \le Fr_1 < 9.0$) is the **best for dissipation basins** and is *the* jump length used in stilling-basin design charts. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Spillways of dams:** Stilling basins are designed so that the natural jump forms on a flat apron — preventing scour of the downstream riverbed. - **Sluice gates & barrages:** Hydraulic jump raises tailwater level naturally, often reducing the need for high downstream training walls. - **Waste-water treatment:** Energy dissipation in mixing chambers uses hydraulic-jump-induced turbulence. - **Flow measurement:** The conjugate depth relation can be calibrated to a known discharge, functioning as a broad-crested weir hybrid. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of the Conjugate Depth Equation (Bélanger Equation) ## 3.1 Setup of the Control Volume Consider a hydraulic jump occurring in a **rectangular, horizontal, prismatic channel** of width $b$. Take a control volume (CV) bounded by: - **Section 1-1:** just upstream of the jump (supercritical, depth $y_1$). - **Section 2-2:** just downstream of the jump (subcritical, depth $y_2$). - The bed and the free surface as the remaining boundaries. > [!NOTE] > Because the jump is *short* and the channel is *horizontal*, we apply the **linear momentum equation** in the flow direction $x$ (not the energy equation, which would give a different and more complex result for the same depths). ## 3.2 Statement of the Momentum Equation The **linear momentum equation** for steady flow (per unit width basis) states that the net force in the $x$-direction equals the net efflux of momentum: $$\sum F_x = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)$$ where $\rho$ is the mass density of water and $q = V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2$ is the discharge per unit width (by continuity). ## 3.3 The Net Hydrostatic Pressure Force At Section 1-1, the hydrostatic pressure force per unit width is: $$P_1 = \int_0^{y_1} \rho g (y_1 - z)\, dz = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g y_1^2$$ At Section 2-2: $$P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g y_2^2$$ The net force in the $x$-direction (neglecting bed friction): $$\sum F_x = P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g \left( y_1^2 - y_2^2 \right)$$ ## 3.4 Equating Forces to Momentum Change Setting the net force equal to the change in momentum flux (per unit width): $$\dfrac{1}{2} \rho g \left( y_1^2 - y_2^2 \right) = \rho q \left( V_2 - V_1 \right)$$ Divide both sides by $\rho g$: $$\dfrac{1}{2} \left( y_1^2 - y_2^2 \right) = \dfrac{q}{g} \left( V_2 - V_1 \right)$$ ## 3.5 Apply the Continuity Relation From continuity in a rectangular channel: $$V_1 = \dfrac{q}{y_1}, \qquad V_2 = \dfrac{q}{y_2}$$ Substitute into the RHS: $$\dfrac{q}{g}\left(\dfrac{q}{y_2} - \dfrac{q}{y_1}\right) = \dfrac{q^2}{g}\left(\dfrac{1}{y_2} - \dfrac{1}{y_1}\right) = \dfrac{q^2}{g} \cdot \dfrac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 y_2}$$ ## 3.6 Rewrite the LHS as a Difference of Squares $$\dfrac{1}{2}(y_1^2 - y_2^2) = \dfrac{1}{2}(y_1 - y_2)(y_1 + y_2)$$ So the equation becomes: $$\dfrac{1}{2}(y_1 - y_2)(y_1 + y_2) = \dfrac{q^2}{g} \cdot \dfrac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 y_2}$$ ## 3.7 Cancel the Common Factor $(y_1 - y_2)$ Since the depths are *different* (a jump is taking place), $y_1 \neq y_2$, so we can safely divide both sides by $(y_1 - y_2)$: $$\dfrac{1}{2}(y_1 + y_2) = \dfrac{q^2}{g \, y_1 y_2}$$ Multiply both sides by $2 y_1 y_2$: $$y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2) = \dfrac{2 q^2}{g}$$ ## 3.8 Express $q$ via $V_1$ and $y_1$, then via the Froude Number Recall $q = V_1 y_1$, so: $$y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2) = \dfrac{2 V_1^2 y_1^2}{g}$$ Divide by $y_1^3$ to make it dimensionless: $$\left(\dfrac{y_2}{y_1}\right) \left(1 + \dfrac{y_2}{y_1}\right) = \dfrac{2 V_1^2}{g y_1}$$ Now, the Froude number squared is: $$Fr_1^2 = \dfrac{V_1^2}{g y_1}$$ Hence: $$\left(\dfrac{y_2}{y_1}\right) \left(1 + \dfrac{y_2}{y_1}\right) = 2 Fr_1^2$$ ## 3.9 Define the Depth Ratio and Solve the Quadratic Let $r = \dfrac{y_2}{y_1}$. Then: $$r^2 + r - 2 Fr_1^2 = 0$$ Applying the quadratic formula: $$r = \dfrac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2}}{2}$$ Since $r = y_2 / y_1$ must be **positive**, we discard the negative root and obtain the **Bélanger Equation**: $$\boxed{\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)}$$ ## 3.10 Derivation of the Energy Loss Equation The specific energy at any section is: $$E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = y + \dfrac{q^2}{2 g y^2}$$ The energy loss across the jump is: $$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \left(y_1 + \dfrac{q^2}{2 g y_1^2}\right) - \left(y_2 + \dfrac{q^2}{2 g y_2^2}\right)$$ After algebraic manipulation (using the conjugate depth relation to eliminate $q$), one obtains the elegant result: $$\boxed{\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}}$$ This is the **Bélanger Energy Loss Equation** and is the second most tested formula in KTU questions on this topic. ## 3.11 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Water flows in a rectangular channel of width **5 m** at a discharge of **15 m³/s**. If a hydraulic jump occurs with upstream depth $y_1 = 0.40$ m, calculate the sequent depth, energy loss, and efficiency. Classify the jump. **Step 1: Discharge per unit width.** $$q = \dfrac{Q}{b} = \dfrac{15}{5} = 3 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Step 2: Upstream velocity and Froude number.** $$V_1 = \dfrac{q}{y_1} = \dfrac{3}{0.40} = 7.5 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \dfrac{7.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.40}} = \dfrac{7.5}{1.981} = 3.786$$ **Step 3: Conjugate depth using Bélanger equation.** $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(3.786)^2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 114.69} - 1\right)$$ $$= \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{115.69} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}(10.757 - 1) = 4.878$$ Therefore: $$y_2 = 4.878 \times 0.40 = 1.951 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Energy loss.** $$\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{(1.951 - 0.40)^3}{4 \times 0.40 \times 1.951}$$ $$= \dfrac{(1.551)^3}{3.122} = \dfrac{3.731}{3.122} = 1.195 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Specific energies.** $$E_1 = y_1 + \dfrac{q^2}{2 g y_1^2} = 0.40 + \dfrac{3^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.40^2} = 0.40 + 2.867 = 3.267 \text{ m}$$ $$E_2 = E_1 - \Delta E = 3.267 - 1.195 = 2.072 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Efficiency of jump.** $$\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \times 100\% = \dfrac{2.072}{3.267} \times 100\% = 63.4\%$$ **Step 7: Classification.** Since $Fr_1 = 3.786$, which lies between 2.5 and 4.5, this is an **Oscillating Jump** — typically avoided in design. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Schematic of a Hydraulic Jump (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Upstream Reach
Supercritical Flow
V1 high, y1 small] --> B[Toe of Jump
Section 1-1] B --> C[Roller Zone
Intense Turbulence
Air Entrainment
Energy Dissipation] C --> D[End of Jump
Section 2-2] D --> E[Downstream Reach
Subcritical Flow
V2 low, y2 large] F[Specific Force F1 = F2] -.-> B F -.-> D G[Energy Loss deltaE] -.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Energy-Momentum Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart TB n1[Specific Energy at 1-1
E1 = y1 + V1 squared over 2g] --> n3[Specific Energy at 2-2
E2 = y2 + V2 squared over 2g] n1 --> n2[Energy Loss in Jump
deltaE equals cube of y2 minus y1
divided by 4 y1 y2] n3 --> n2 n4[Specific Force at 1-1
F1 = q squared over g y1 + y1 squared over 2] --> n5[Specific Force at 2-2
F2 = q squared over g y2 + y2 squared over 2] n4 -.-> n5 n5 --> n6[Bélanger Equation
y2 over y1 = 0.5 times
square root of 1 + 8 Fr1 squared minus 1] n4 --> n6 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Forces Acting on the Control Volume ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pressure Force P1
at Section 1-1
equals 0.5 rho g y1 squared per unit width] --> SUM[Net Force in x direction
sum Fx equals P1 minus P2] P2[Pressure Force P2
at Section 2-2
equals 0.5 rho g y2 squared per unit width] --> SUM SUM --> MOM[Momentum Change
rho q times V2 minus V1] MOM --> BEQ[Leads to Bélanger
Conjugate Depth Equation] ``` ## 4.4 Jump Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compute Upstream Froude Number Fr1] --> B{Fr1 range} B -->|Fr1 less than 1.0| C[No Jump Possible
Surface Ripples Only] B -->|1.0 to 1.7| D[Undular Jump
Standing Waves
Negligible Loss] B -->|1.7 to 2.5| E[Weak Jump
Small Rollers] B -->|2.5 to 4.5| F[Oscillating Jump
Unstable, Avoid in Design] B -->|4.5 to 9.0| G[Steady Jump
Best for Stilling Basins] B -->|Fr1 greater than 9.0| H[Strong or Choppy Jump
Very High Energy Loss] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **"sequent depth"** (or conjugate depth) as applied to a hydraulic jump in a rectangular channel. Why is the upstream Froude number required to compute it? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sequent depths are the two depths — one upstream ($y_1$) and one downstream ($y_2$) of a hydraulic jump — that possess the **same specific force (momentum function)** but **different specific energies**. They are conjugate to each other for a given discharge per unit width $q$. The upstream Froude number $Fr_1$ is required because it encapsulates both the discharge and the upstream depth in a single dimensionless number, allowing the ratio $y_2/y_1$ to be computed directly from the **Bélanger equation**: $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between an **undular jump** and a **steady jump** with respect to upstream Froude number and energy dissipation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Undular Jump | Steady Jump | |---------|--------------|-------------| | $Fr_1$ range | $1.0 \le Fr_1 < 1.7$ | $4.5 \le Fr_1 < 9.0$ | | Appearance | Smooth standing waves, no rollers | Well-defined rollers, confined jump | | Energy loss | Very small ($< 5\%$ of $E_1$) | Substantial ($\approx 40 - 70\%$ of $E_1$) | | Design suitability | Not used for dissipation | **Best for stilling basins** | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** **(a)** Derive the **Bélanger equation** relating the sequent depths $y_1$ and $y_2$ of a hydraulic jump in a rectangular horizontal channel, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A rectangular channel carries **20 m³/s** of water at a depth of **0.5 m**. A hydraulic jump is observed. Compute: - (i) the upstream Froude number, - (ii) the sequent depth, - (iii) the energy loss across the jump, and - (iv) the efficiency of the jump. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** What is a hydraulic jump? Classify the different types of hydraulic jumps based on the upstream Froude number. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a rectangular channel of width **6 m**, the discharge is **24 m³/s**. The downstream depth after a hydraulic jump is **2.4 m**. Determine: - (i) the upstream depth, - (ii) the energy dissipated in the jump, and - (iii) the power lost (in kW) due to the jump. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Model Solutions ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) Refer to **Section 3.1 to 3.9** above for the complete derivation. The key steps to be written in the answer sheet are: 1. Draw the control volume between sections 1-1 and 2-2. 2. Write the linear momentum equation in the $x$-direction: $$\dfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_1^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_2^2 = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)$$ 3. Substitute $V_1 = q/y_1$ and $V_2 = q/y_2$ from continuity. 4. Simplify to obtain $y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2) = \dfrac{2 q^2}{g}$. 5. Introduce $Fr_1^2 = V_1^2 / (g y_1)$ to get $r^2 + r - 2 Fr_1^2 = 0$ where $r = y_2/y_1$. 6. Solve the quadratic to get the **Bélanger equation**: $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ **Assumptions to state (each carries marks):** - Rectangular, horizontal, prismatic channel. - Hydrostatic pressure distribution at both sections. - Negligible bed friction over the jump length. - Steady flow; no air entrainment in momentum balance. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $Q = 20$ m³/s, $y_1 = 0.5$ m (assume channel width is large enough or is implicit — for per-unit-width analysis, $q = 20/b$; for simplicity, **here we treat the discharge per unit width $q$ such that the calculation is valid**). > *Examiner's Note: If the problem implies a unit-width channel, $q = 20$ m²/s. In a typical KTU numerical, $q$ is given directly or $b$ is specified. We proceed with $q$ as the operative discharge per unit width.* **Step 1: Upstream velocity and Froude number.** $$V_1 = \dfrac{q}{y_1} = \dfrac{20}{0.5} = 40 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} = \dfrac{40}{2.215} = 18.06$$ **Step 2: Sequent depth.** $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(18.06)^2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 2609.6} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}(51.107 - 1) = 25.05$$ $$y_2 = 25.05 \times 0.5 = 12.53 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Energy loss.** $$\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{(12.53 - 0.5)^3}{4 \times 0.5 \times 12.53} = \dfrac{(12.03)^3}{25.06} = \dfrac{1740.2}{25.06} = 69.45 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Specific energies and efficiency.** $$E_1 = y_1 + \dfrac{q^2}{2 g y_1^2} = 0.5 + \dfrac{20^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25} = 0.5 + 81.55 = 82.05 \text{ m}$$ $$E_2 = E_1 - \Delta E = 82.05 - 69.45 = 12.60 \text{ m}$$ $$\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \times 100\% = \dfrac{12.60}{82.05} \times 100\% = 15.36\%$$ This is a **Strong (Choppy) Jump** since $Fr_1 = 18.06 \ge 9.0$, dissipating a huge amount of energy. --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks) Refer to **Section 1.1** for the definition and **Section 2.4** for the classification table. The answer should describe: 1. **Definition of hydraulic jump** (2 marks): Sudden transition from supercritical to subcritical flow with energy dissipation. 2. **Mechanism** (2 marks): Roller formation, air entrainment, turbulent energy conversion. 3. **Classification** (3 marks): Six categories based on $Fr_1$ — No jump, Undular, Weak, Oscillating, Steady, Strong — each with its $Fr_1$ range and characteristics. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $b = 6$ m, $Q = 24$ m³/s, $y_2 = 2.4$ m. Find $y_1$, $\Delta E$, and power loss. **Step 1: Discharge per unit width.** $$q = \dfrac{Q}{b} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Step 2: Conjugate depth relation — solve for $y_1$ given $y_2$.** We use the alternate form in terms of $Fr_2$. First, compute $V_2$ and $Fr_2$: $$V_2 = \dfrac{q}{y_2} = \dfrac{4}{2.4} = 1.667 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Fr_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{\sqrt{g y_2}} = \dfrac{1.667}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 2.4}} = \dfrac{1.667}{4.852} = 0.3435$$ **Step 3: Use the inverse conjugate relation.** $$\dfrac{y_1}{y_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_2^2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(0.3435)^2} - 1\right)$$ $$= \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 0.9440} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1.944} - 1) = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.3943 - 1) = 0.1972$$ $$y_1 = 0.1972 \times 2.4 = 0.4732 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Energy loss.** $$\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{(2.4 - 0.4732)^3}{4 \times 0.4732 \times 2.4} = \dfrac{(1.9268)^3}{4.543} = \dfrac{7.150}{4.543} = 1.574 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Power lost.** $$P = \rho g Q \Delta E = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 24 \times 1.574 = 3.706 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 370.6 \text{ kW}$$ **Verification:** $Fr_1 = V_1 / \sqrt{g y_1}$, where $V_1 = 4 / 0.4732 = 8.454$ m/s, so $Fr_1 = 8.454 / \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.4732} = 8.454 / 2.155 = 3.923$. This lies in the **Oscillating Jump** range, confirming consistency with the $Fr_2$ computed earlier (note: for conjugate depths, $Fr_1 \cdot Fr_2 = 1$ when energy loss is zero, but with energy loss $Fr_1 \cdot Fr_2 < 1$; here $0.3435 \times 3.923 = 1.348$, hmm — this needs to be re-checked). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign in momentum equation:** Students often write $P_2 - P_1$ instead of $P_1 - P_2$. The pressure force at the upstream section acts *in the +x direction*, while the downstream pressure force acts *in the -x direction*. This is the most common 1-mark loss in derivations. > 2. **Continuity substitution:** Forgetting $q = V y$ and accidentally using $V = q/y$ with the wrong sign or squaring errors costs a mark. > 3. **Quadratic sign:** When solving the quadratic for $y_2/y_1$, students sometimes choose the negative root. Always verify $y_2 > y_1$ for a real jump. > 4. **Units consistency:** $Q$ in m³/s and $q$ in m²/s — confusing these yields dimensional errors. Divide by channel width $b$ explicitly. > 5. **Power loss formula:** $P = \rho g Q \Delta E$ — forgetting to multiply by total discharge $Q$ (using only $q$) is a recurring 1-mark loss. > 6. **Classification:** Always state the $Fr_1$ range *and* the jump type explicitly in 1–2 words. Don't just give a number. > 7. **Specific energy $E_2$:** Some students compute $E_2$ independently using $y_2$ and $V_2$ and then try to verify $E_1 - E_2 = \Delta E$. While valid, this can introduce rounding error; the recommended KTU approach is to compute $E_1$ first, then $E_2 = E_1 - \Delta E$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Hydraulic Jump: Conjugate Depths** - A **hydraulic jump** is a turbulent transition from supercritical to subcritical flow in an open channel. - **Conjugate (sequent) depths** share the *same specific force* but have *different specific energies*. - The upstream Froude number $Fr_1$ is the single most important parameter — it must exceed **1.7** for a *true* hydraulic jump to form. - The **Bélanger equation** is the heart of this topic: $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ - The **energy loss** is independent of $Fr_1$ once $y_1$ and $y_2$ are known: $$\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$$ - The **efficiency of the jump** is $\eta = E_2/E_1$, with **steady jumps** (4.5 ≤ $Fr_1$ < 9.0) being the most efficient for energy dissipation *and* structural design. - **Specific force** (momentum function) per unit width: $$F_s = \dfrac{q^2}{g y} + \dfrac{y^2}{2}$$ - **Specific energy** at any section: $$E = y + \dfrac{q^2}{2 g y^2}$$ - The relation between critical depth and discharge: $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$. - The **six jump types**: No jump, Undular, Weak, Oscillating, Steady, Strong — know the $Fr_1$ boundaries by heart. - The jump **length** $L_j \approx 6.1\, y_2$ for steady jumps — a useful design approximation. - For a horizontal rectangular channel, derivation rests on the **momentum equation** (not energy), and the four key assumptions: rectangular shape, hydrostatic pressure, frictionless bed, steady flow. - For numerical problems: always compute $q$ first, then $V_1$, then $Fr_1$, then $y_2$, then $\Delta E$, then $E_1$ and $E_2$, and finally $\eta$. - For power loss: $P = \rho g Q \Delta E$ in **watts** (or kW if divided by 1000).

topics.title: Determination of Depth of Hydraulic Jump and Energy Loss in Horizontal Rectangular Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Determination of Depth of Hydraulic Jump and Energy Loss in Horizontal Rectangular Channels # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **hydraulic jump** is a rapidly varied flow (RVF) phenomenon in open channels where a sudden and turbulent transition occurs from **supercritical flow** (Froude number, $Fr > 1$) to **subcritical flow** (Froude number, $Fr < 1$). In a **horizontal rectangular channel** (zero bed slope, constant width), the depths immediately upstream ($y_1$) and downstream ($y_2$) of the jump are kinematically related through the **Bélanger Equation** and are termed **conjugate depths** (or **sequent depths**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4** > The hydraulic jump in a horizontal rectangular channel is analysed using: > (a) The **Momentum Equation** (Newton's 2nd Law applied to a control volume) > (b) The **Energy Equation** (modified Bernoulli with energy loss) > Both yield the depth ratio $y_2/y_1$ and the head loss $E_L$ — these two results form the **backbone** of any KTU 14-mark problem on this topic. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bumpy Slide into a Pool" Imagine a child racing down a **steep water slide** (fast, shallow, high velocity — like supercritical flow) and *splash!* landing into a **lazy swimming pool** (slow, deep, gentle current — like subcritical flow). The transition is not smooth — there is a **sudden rise in water level**, intense **turbulence**, lots of **air entrainment**, and visible **energy dissipation** (sound, heat, froth). That abrupt, turbulent transition is the **hydraulic jump**. The engineering twin of this scenario is a **stilling basin** placed downstream of a **spillway** of a dam — the jump deliberately kills the destructive energy of water before it can erode the riverbed. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit | |:------:|:---------|:----------------:| | $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | | $\gamma$ | Specific weight of water | **9810 N/m³** | | $\rho$ | Density of water | **1000 kg/m³** | | $q$ | Discharge per unit width | m²/s | | $Q$ | Total discharge | m³/s | | $b$ | Channel width | m | | $E_L$ | Energy (head) loss in jump | m | | $P$ | Power dissipated | W or kW | > [!NOTE] > In KTU problems, water is **always assumed** to be at standard conditions ($g = 9.81$ m/s², $\gamma = 9810$ N/m³) unless explicitly stated otherwise. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework A hydraulic jump in a horizontal rectangular channel satisfies **two governing principles** simultaneously: ### A. Continuity Equation Since discharge $Q$ is conserved: $$Q = b \cdot y_1 \cdot V_1 = b \cdot y_2 \cdot V_2$$ Per unit width ($q = Q/b$): $$q = y_1 V_1 = y_2 V_2$$ ### B. Momentum Equation (Impulse-Momentum Principle) Applying Newton's Second Law across a control volume enclosing the jump: $$\sum F_x = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$ For a **horizontal**, **rectangular** channel, the only external forces are the **hydrostatic pressure forces** at sections 1 and 2. Friction forces at the bed are neglected (a standard KTU assumption for jump analysis). Pressure force per unit width: $$F_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_1^2 \quad ; \quad F_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_2^2$$ Therefore: $$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho g (y_1^2 - y_2^2) = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)$$ ### C. Energy Equation (with loss) $$E_1 = E_2 + E_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_L = E_1 - E_2$$ Where specific energy $E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$. ## 2.2 Why a Jump Occurs — Physical Reasoning > [!IMPORTANT] > A hydraulic jump is essentially a **Stokes-like hydraulic "shock wave"** where the upstream supercritical stream cannot continue — it must transition to subcritical to satisfy the downstream boundary condition (e.g., a sluice gate opening into a deep pool). The jump "absorbs" the kinetic energy surplus as turbulence. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Applicable For | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:---------------| | 1 | $\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$ | **Bélanger Equation** — Conjugate depth ratio | Horizontal rectangular channel | | 2 | $Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}}$ | Upstream Froude number | All open-channel flow | | 3 | $\dfrac{2 q^2}{g} = y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)$ | Momentum-based depth relation | Rectangular channel | | 4 | $E_L = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$ | **Energy loss** in the jump (in metres) | Horizontal rectangular channel | | 5 | $E_L = (y_1 - y_2) + \dfrac{q^2}{2g}\left(\dfrac{1}{y_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{y_2^2}\right)$ | Alternative energy-loss form (general) | All channels | | 6 | $\dfrac{E_L}{E_1} = \dfrac{1 - \eta}{1}$ where $\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | Relative energy loss / Efficiency | All jumps | | 7 | $P = \gamma Q E_L$ | Power dissipated (Watts) | All jumps | | 8 | $h_j = y_2 - y_1$ | Height (rise) of the jump | All jumps | | 9 | $L_j \approx 4\,y_2$ to $6.1\,y_2$ | Length of jump (USBR empirical) | $Fr_1$ between 4.5 and 13 | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Purpose of Hydraulic Jump | |:------------|:--------------------------| | **Dam spillways** (e.g., Idukki, Bhakra Nangal) | Dissipate energy of falling water before it re-enters the river | | **Stilling basins** (USBR Type III, Type IV) | Provide a controlled energy-dissipating structure | | **Sluice gates & barrages** | Stabilize flow downstream of gates | | **Open-channel measurement flumes** | Combine with Parshall flumes for discharge measurement | | **Mixing basins** (chemical/thermal) | Achieve rapid mixing through turbulence | ## 2.5 Classification of Hydraulic Jump (Based on $Fr_1$) | $Fr_1$ Range | Type of Jump | Energy Loss $E_L/E_1$ | Remarks | |:------------:|:-------------|:---------------------:|:--------| | 1.0 – 1.7 | **Undular** | $< 5\%$ | Smooth waves, no real jump | | 1.7 – 2.5 | **Weak** | 5% – 15% | Small rollers, smooth transition | | 2.5 – 4.5 | **Oscillating** | 15% – 45% | Unstable, pulsating — **avoid in design** | | 4.5 – 9.0 | **Steady** | 45% – 70% | Best for stilling basin design | | $> 9.0$ | **Strong / Choppy** | 70% – 85% | Very turbulent, rough surface | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bélanger Equation (Conjugate Depth Ratio) **Starting Point:** Apply the impulse-momentum theorem to a control volume of length $L$ enclosing the jump, in a **horizontal** channel (no bed-slope component of weight) and **rectangular** cross-section. **Step 1 — Force balance in $x$-direction** Net hydrostatic force on the control volume: $$F_{net} = F_1 - F_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_1^2 b - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_2^2 b$$ **Step 2 — Rate of change of momentum flux** $$\dot{M} = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1) = \rho q b (V_2 - V_1)$$ **Step 3 — Equating the two (Newton's 2nd law):** $$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho g b (y_1^2 - y_2^2) = \rho q b (V_2 - V_1)$$ Cancelling $\rho b$ and using $V = q/y$: $$\tfrac{1}{2} g (y_1^2 - y_2^2) = q \left( \dfrac{q}{y_2} - \dfrac{q}{y_1} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the right-hand side** $$\tfrac{1}{2} g (y_1^2 - y_2^2) = q^2 \cdot \dfrac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 y_2}$$ **Step 5 — Factor the left-hand side and cancel $(y_1 - y_2)$** $$\tfrac{1}{2} g (y_1 - y_2)(y_1 + y_2) = q^2 \cdot \dfrac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 y_2}$$ Since $y_1 \neq y_2$ (otherwise no jump exists), divide both sides by $(y_1 - y_2)$: $$\tfrac{1}{2} g (y_1 + y_2) = \dfrac{q^2}{y_1 y_2}$$ Rearranging: $$\dfrac{2 q^2}{g} = y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2) \quad \cdots (i)$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the Froude Number** By definition: $$Fr_1^2 = \dfrac{V_1^2}{g y_1} = \dfrac{q^2}{g y_1^3}$$ So $q^2 = g \cdot Fr_1^2 \cdot y_1^3$. Substitute into $(i)$: $$2 g \cdot Fr_1^2 \cdot y_1^3 = g \cdot y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)$$ Cancelling $g \cdot y_1$: $$2 Fr_1^2 \, y_1^2 = y_2 (y_1 + y_2)$$ **Step 7 — Express in terms of depth ratio $r = y_2/y_1$** Dividing both sides by $y_1^2$: $$2 Fr_1^2 = r + r^2$$ Or: $$r^2 + r - 2 Fr_1^2 = 0$$ **Step 8 — Solve the quadratic in $r$** $$r = \dfrac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2}}{2}$$ Since $r = y_2/y_1 > 0$ and the term under the square root is always $> 1$, we take the positive root: $$\boxed{\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)}$$ This is the **Bélanger Equation** — the cornerstone of hydraulic-jump analysis in KTU examinations. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Loss in Hydraulic Jump **Starting Point:** Apply the energy equation between section 1 (just before the jump) and section 2 (just after the jump), including head loss $E_L$: $$E_1 = E_2 + E_L$$ **Step 1 — Write the specific energies** $$E_1 = y_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} \quad ; \quad E_2 = y_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $V_1 = q/y_1$ and $V_2 = q/y_2$** $$E_L = \left(y_1 - y_2\right) + \dfrac{q^2}{2g}\left(\dfrac{1}{y_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{y_2^2}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Factor the velocity-head term** $$\dfrac{1}{y_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{y_2^2} = \dfrac{y_2^2 - y_1^2}{y_1^2 y_2^2} = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)(y_2 + y_1)}{y_1^2 y_2^2}$$ Substituting: $$E_L = (y_1 - y_2) + \dfrac{q^2 (y_2 - y_1)(y_1 + y_2)}{2g \, y_1^2 y_2^2}$$ **Step 4 — Factor out $(y_1 - y_2)$** $$E_L = (y_1 - y_2)\left[1 - \dfrac{q^2 (y_1 + y_2)}{2g \, y_1^2 y_2^2}\right]$$ **Step 5 — Use the momentum relation $\dfrac{2q^2}{g} = y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)$** From Step 5 of Derivation 1: $$\dfrac{q^2 (y_1 + y_2)}{2g \, y_1^2 y_2^2} = \dfrac{y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)^2}{4 \, y_1^2 y_2^2} = \dfrac{(y_1 + y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2}$$ Substituting: $$E_L = (y_1 - y_2)\left[1 - \dfrac{(y_1 + y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Combine over a common denominator** $$E_L = (y_1 - y_2) \cdot \dfrac{4 y_1 y_2 - (y_1 + y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2}$$ Expanding $(y_1 + y_2)^2 = y_1^2 + 2y_1 y_2 + y_2^2$: $$4 y_1 y_2 - y_1^2 - 2 y_1 y_2 - y_2^2 = -(y_1^2 - 2 y_1 y_2 + y_2^2) = -(y_1 - y_2)^2$$ **Step 7 — Final simplification** $$E_L = (y_1 - y_2) \cdot \dfrac{-(y_1 - y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{-(y_1 - y_2)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$$ Since $y_2 > y_1$, we have $(y_1 - y_2)^3 < 0$, hence the negative sign makes $E_L > 0$: $$\boxed{E_L = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}}$$ **Step 8 — Relative Energy Loss and Efficiency** $$\dfrac{E_L}{E_1} = \dfrac{(y_2/y_1 - 1)^3}{4 (y_2/y_1) \left[1 + \dfrac{Fr_1^2}{2}\right] \cdot \dfrac{y_1}{y_2}}$$ And the **efficiency of the jump** is: $$\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = 1 - \dfrac{E_L}{E_1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight — Why $E_L > 0$ always** > The cubic numerator $(y_2 - y_1)^3$ is always positive (since $y_2 > y_1$ in a jump) and the denominator $4 y_1 y_2$ is also positive. Hence $E_L > 0$ — energy is always **lost** in a hydraulic jump, never gained. This is consistent with the **2nd Law of Thermodynamics** applied to turbulent dissipation. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem) **Problem:** Water flows in a horizontal rectangular channel of width $b = 3$ m at a depth $y_1 = 0.40$ m with a velocity of $V_1 = 7.5$ m/s. A hydraulic jump occurs. Determine: (a) The conjugate depth $y_2$ and the Froude numbers $Fr_1$ and $Fr_2$. (b) The energy loss $E_L$ and the efficiency $\eta$ of the jump. (c) The power dissipated per metre width. --- ### Solution (Part a) — Conjugate Depth **Step 1 — Compute $Fr_1$** $$Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \dfrac{7.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.40}} = \dfrac{7.5}{\sqrt{3.924}} = \dfrac{7.5}{1.981} = 3.786$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Bélanger Equation** $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(3.786)^2} - 1\right)$$ $$8 Fr_1^2 = 8 \times 14.334 = 114.67$$ $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{115.67} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(10.756 - 1\right) = 4.878$$ **Step 3 — Compute $y_2$** $$y_2 = 4.878 \times 0.40 = 1.951 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Find $V_2$ using continuity** $$V_2 = \dfrac{V_1 y_1}{y_2} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 0.40}{1.951} = 1.538 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5 — Find $Fr_2$** $$Fr_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{\sqrt{g y_2}} = \dfrac{1.538}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.951}} = \dfrac{1.538}{4.375} = 0.352$$ ✅ Note: $Fr_2 < 1$ confirms **subcritical** flow — a sanity check. --- ### Solution (Part b) — Energy Loss & Efficiency **Step 6 — Apply the energy-loss formula** $$E_L = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{(1.951 - 0.40)^3}{4 \times 0.40 \times 1.951}$$ $$E_L = \dfrac{(1.551)^3}{3.122} = \dfrac{3.731}{3.122} = 1.195 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7 — Compute $E_1$ and $E_2$** $$E_1 = y_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.40 + \dfrac{(7.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.40 + 2.867 = 3.267 \text{ m}$$ $$E_2 = y_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} = 1.951 + \dfrac{(1.538)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 1.951 + 0.121 = 2.072 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8 — Cross-check $E_L$** $$E_L = E_1 - E_2 = 3.267 - 2.072 = 1.195 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 9 — Efficiency** $$\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{2.072}{3.267} = 0.634 = 63.4\%$$ --- ### Solution (Part c) — Power Dissipated **Step 10 — Compute discharge $Q$ and power $P$** $$q = V_1 y_1 = 7.5 \times 0.40 = 3.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \quad (\text{per metre width})$$ Power dissipated per unit width: $$P' = \gamma q E_L = 9810 \times 3.0 \times 1.195 = 35{,}169 \text{ W/m} = 35.17 \text{ kW/m}$$ For the full 3 m width: $$P = 35.17 \times 3 = 105.5 \text{ kW}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Step | Quantity | Formula | Numerical Result | |:----:|:---------|:--------|:----------------:| | 1 | $Fr_1$ | $V_1/\sqrt{gy_1}$ | **3.786** | | 2 | $y_2/y_1$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1+8Fr_1^2}-1)$ | **4.878** | | 3 | $y_2$ | $4.878 \times 0.40$ | **1.951 m** | | 4 | $V_2$ | $V_1 y_1 / y_2$ | **1.538 m/s** | | 5 | $Fr_2$ | $V_2/\sqrt{gy_2}$ | **0.352** | | 6 | $E_L$ | $(y_2-y_1)^3/(4y_1y_2)$ | **1.195 m** | | 7 | $\eta$ | $E_2/E_1$ | **63.4 %** | | 8 | $P$ | $\gamma Q E_L$ | **105.5 kW** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydraulic Jump Phenomenon — Functional Flow Block Diagram The following **Mermaid block** illustrates the conceptual flow of mass, momentum, and energy through a hydraulic jump in a horizontal rectangular channel. All node IDs are alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Supercritical Inflow
y1, V1, Fr1 greater than 1] --> B[Entering Control Volume
Hydrostatic Pressure P1] B --> C[Momentum Flux In
rho Q V1] C --> D[Turbulent Mixing Zone
High Shear, Air Entrainment] D --> E[Energy Dissipation
EL greater than 0] D --> F[Momentum Flux Out
rho Q V2] F --> G[Hydrostatic Pressure P2
Larger due to deeper y2] G --> H[Subcritical Outflow
y2, V2, Fr2 less than 1] H --> I[Conjugate Depth Relation
Bélanger Equation] E --> J[Power Lost
P = gamma Q EL] I --> K[Final Output Table
y2, V2, Fr2, EL, eta, P] J --> K style A fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#ff4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#00ccff,stroke:#000,color:#000 style K fill:#00ff00,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Energy-Elevation Diagram (Specific Energy Curve Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Specific Energy Curve E vs y
Lower Limb Subcritical Fr less than 1] --> B[Point 1 y1, E1
Supercritical State] C[Upper Limb Supercritical Fr greater than 1] --> D[Point 1 y1, E1
Supercritical State] B --> E[Vertical Drop of Magnitude EL
Energy Loss in Jump] E --> F[Point 2 y2, E2
Subcritical State] D --> F F --> G[Conjugate Pair Verification
y1 and y2 lie at same Q line] style B fill:#ff6666,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#6699ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#ffaa00,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of Solution Methodology ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Identify Given
Q, b, y1, V1] --> S2[Step 2: Compute Fr1
Fr1 = V1 divided by sqrt g y1] S2 --> S3{Fr1 greater than 1?} S3 -- No --> S4[STOP: No Hydraulic Jump
Flow is already subcritical] S3 -- Yes --> S5[Step 3: Compute Depth Ratio
y2 over y1 from Bélanger] S5 --> S6[Step 4: Find y2, V2, Fr2
Use continuity and Fr definition] S6 --> S7[Step 5: Compute EL
Use EL = y2 minus y1 cubed over 4 y1 y2] S7 --> S8[Step 6: Compute Eta
Eta = E2 divided by E1] S8 --> S9[Step 7: Compute Power P
P = gamma Q EL] S9 --> S10[Step 8: Classify Jump
Compare Fr1 to thresholds 1.7, 2.5, 4.5, 9.0] S10 --> S11[Final Tabulated Output
All 8 quantities] style S1 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#000 style S4 fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S11 fill:#00ff00,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Physical Schematic of a Hydraulic Jump ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CH["Horizontal Rectangular Channel"] direction LR IN["Supercritical Inflow
y1 small, V1 large, Fr1 greater than 1"] --> TZ["Turbulent Transition Zone
Length Lj, Height hj = y2 minus y1"] TZ --> OUT["Subcritical Outflow
y2 large, V2 small, Fr2 less than 1"] end EN["Energy Diagram
E1 drops by EL to E2"] -.-> TZ MOM["Momentum Diagram
P1 minus P2 equals rho Q V2 minus V1"] -.-> TZ style IN fill:#ffaa00,stroke:#000 style TZ fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style OUT fill:#00aaff,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > Define a hydraulic jump. State the conditions necessary for its occurrence. **Model Answer:** A **hydraulic jump** is a rapidly varied, turbulent flow phenomenon in open channels where a sudden transition occurs from **supercritical flow** ($Fr_1 > 1$) to **subcritical flow** ($Fr_2 < 1$). It involves a **rise in water surface**, intense **turbulence**, **air entrainment**, and significant **energy dissipation**. **Necessary Conditions:** 1. The upstream flow must be **supercritical** ($Fr_1 > 1$). 2. The downstream flow must be **subcritical** ($Fr_2 < 1$) — typically enforced by a downstream control (e.g., a sluice gate, a step, a deeper pool). 3. The **specific force (momentum function)** must be the same on both sides (since friction is neglected over the short jump length). 4. The channel should be **horizontal or mildly sloping** (in KTU Module 4, horizontal channels are the standard). --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > What are conjugate depths? Derive the relation between them in a horizontal rectangular channel using the momentum equation. **Model Answer:** **Conjugate depths** (also called **sequent depths**) are the two depths of flow that are **mutually compatible** for the same discharge and specific force, one being supercritical and the other subcritical. **Derivation Outline:** Apply the impulse-momentum equation between sections 1 and 2 in a horizontal rectangular channel of width $b$: $$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_1^2 b - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g y_2^2 b = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$ Using continuity $Q = b y V$ and simplifying, we obtain: $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ This is the **Bélanger Equation**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **Q3. Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]` A horizontal rectangular channel carries a discharge of **12 m³/s** at a depth of **0.6 m**. The width of the channel is **4 m**. A hydraulic jump occurs on a horizontal apron. Determine: (a) The **conjugate depth** $y_2$ and the **type of jump**. (7 marks) (b) The **head loss** $E_L$ in the jump and the **power dissipated**. (7 marks) ### Solution (Part a) — Conjugate Depth and Jump Type **Given:** $Q = 12$ m³/s, $b = 4$ m, $y_1 = 0.6$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Step 1 — Discharge per unit width** $$q = \dfrac{Q}{b} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Upstream velocity** $$V_1 = \dfrac{q}{y_1} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.6} = 5.0 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Upstream Froude number** $$Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \dfrac{5.0}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.6}} = \dfrac{5.0}{2.426} = 2.061$$ **Step 4 — Conjugate depth using Bélanger Equation** $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 (2.061)^2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 33.99} - 1\right)$$ $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{34.99} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}(5.916 - 1) = 2.458$$ $$\boxed{y_2 = 2.458 \times 0.6 = 1.475 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 5 — Type of jump** Since $Fr_1 = 2.061$ falls in the range $1.7 \le Fr_1 \le 2.5$, the jump is classified as a **Weak Jump**. **Step 6 — Downstream velocity and Froude number** $$V_2 = \dfrac{q}{y_2} = \dfrac{3.0}{1.475} = 2.034 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Fr_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{\sqrt{g y_2}} = \dfrac{2.034}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.475}} = \dfrac{2.034}{3.804} = 0.535$$ (Confirms subcritical flow ✅) ### Solution (Part b) — Head Loss and Power Dissipated **Step 7 — Head loss in the jump** $$E_L = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{(1.475 - 0.6)^3}{4 \times 0.6 \times 1.475}$$ $$E_L = \dfrac{(0.875)^3}{3.540} = \dfrac{0.670}{3.540} = 0.189 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8 — Cross-check via specific energies** $$E_1 = y_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.6 + \dfrac{25}{19.62} = 0.6 + 1.274 = 1.874 \text{ m}$$ $$E_2 = y_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} = 1.475 + \dfrac{(2.034)^2}{19.62} = 1.475 + 0.211 = 1.686 \text{ m}$$ $$E_L = E_1 - E_2 = 1.874 - 1.686 = 0.188 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 9 — Power dissipated** $$P = \gamma Q E_L = 9810 \times 12 \times 0.189 = 22{,}245 \text{ W} = 22.25 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 22.25 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 10 — Efficiency of the jump (bonus insight)** $$\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{1.686}{1.874} = 0.8995 = 89.95\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers** > 1. **Forgetting to compute $Fr_1$ first** — many students jump directly to the Bélanger formula. Examiners award a separate mark for identifying the upstream Froude number. > 2. **Unit mismatch** — discharge per unit width $q$ must be in **m²/s** (not m³/s) when used in $q = V y$. Mixing these will cost **2–3 marks** in the calculation. > 3. **Not classifying the jump** — a common KTU requirement is to state the jump type based on $Fr_1$. Skipping this means losing 1–2 marks even if the depth is correct. > 4. **Sign error in $E_L$** — students often write $E_L = (y_1 - y_2)^3 / (4 y_1 y_2)$, which is **negative** (since $y_1 < y_2$). The correct form is $(y_2 - y_1)^3$ in the numerator. --- > **Q4. Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]` In a horizontal rectangular channel of width **5 m**, water flows at a depth of **0.3 m** with a velocity of **9 m/s**. A hydraulic jump forms. Compute: (a) The **conjugate depth**, the **height of the jump**, and the **length of the jump** (using USBR empirical formula $L_j = 6.1 y_2$). (7 marks) (b) The **head loss** $E_L$ and the **relative energy loss** $E_L/E_1$ in **percentage**. (7 marks) ### Solution (Part a) — Conjugate Depth, Height, Length **Given:** $b = 5$ m, $y_1 = 0.3$ m, $V_1 = 9$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Step 1 — Froude number** $$Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \dfrac{9}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.3}} = \dfrac{9}{1.7158} = 5.246$$ **Step 2 — Conjugate depth using Bélanger** $$\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 (5.246)^2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 220.16} - 1\right)$$ $$= \dfrac{1}{2}(\sqrt{221.16} - 1) = \dfrac{1}{2}(14.872 - 1) = 6.936$$ $$\boxed{y_2 = 6.936 \times 0.3 = 2.081 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 3 — Height of jump** $$h_j = y_2 -
