y_1 = 2.081 - 0.3 = 1.781 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Length of jump (USBR empirical)** $$L_j = 6.1 \, y_2 = 6.1 \times 2.081 = 12.69 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Verify jump type** Since $Fr_1 = 5.246$ lies in the range $4.5 < Fr_1 \le 9.0$, this is a **Steady Jump** — ideal for stilling basin design. **Step 6 — Downstream velocity** $$V_2 = \dfrac{V_1 y_1}{y_2} = \dfrac{9 \times 0.3}{2.081} = 1.298 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Fr_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{\sqrt{g y_2}} = \dfrac{1.298}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 2.081}} = \dfrac{1.298}{4.519} = 0.287$$ ### Solution (Part b) — Head Loss and Relative Energy Loss **Step 7 — Head loss** $$E_L = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \dfrac{(2.081 - 0.3)^3}{4 \times 0.3 \times 2.081}$$ $$= \dfrac{(1.781)^3}{2.4972} = \dfrac{5.648}{2.4972} = 2.262 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8 — Specific energies** $$E_1 = y_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.3 + \dfrac{81}{19.62} = 0.3 + 4.128 = 4.428 \text{ m}$$ $$E_2 = y_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} = 2.081 + \dfrac{(1.298)^2}{19.62} = 2.081 + 0.0859 = 2.167 \text{ m}$$ **Cross-check:** $E_1 - E_2 = 4.428 - 2.167 = 2.261 \text{ m} \approx E_L$ ✅ **Step 9 — Relative energy loss** $$\dfrac{E_L}{E_1} = \dfrac{2.262}{4.428} = 0.5108 = 51.08\%$$ **Step 10 — Power dissipated** $$Q = V_1 y_1 b = 9 \times 0.3 \times 5 = 13.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$P = \gamma Q E_L = 9810 \times 13.5 \times 2.262 = 299{,}569 \text{ W} = 299.6 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B** > 1. **Forgetting to specify the jump type** — A common 1-mark loss. Always state *"Since $Fr_1 = 5.246$, the jump is a **Steady Jump** (most efficient for energy dissipation)."* > 2. **Using $L_j = 4 y_2$ without justification** — Different empirical formulas (USBR: $6.1 y_2$, Khurgi's: $4$ to $5 y_2$, etc.) apply to different $Fr_1$ ranges. Always **state which formula** you are using and its validity range. > 3. **Mistaking $E_L/E_1$ for $E_L$ itself** — these are two distinct quantities. The first is **dimensionless** (a ratio), the second has units of **metres**. > 4. **Power formula mistakes** — $P = \gamma Q E_L$ requires $Q$ in **m³/s** and $E_L$ in **metres**. The unit of $P$ is then **W (watts)**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hydraulic Jump in Horizontal Rectangular Channels** - ✅ A hydraulic jump requires $Fr_1 > 1$ upstream and $Fr_2 < 1$ downstream. - ✅ The **Bélanger Equation** $y_2/y_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1+8 Fr_1^2}-1)$ is the **first tool** in every KTU problem. - ✅ The **head loss** is $E_L = (y_2-y_1)^3 / (4 y_1 y_2)$ — memorize this verbatim. - ✅ Always **verify** the result using $E_L = E_1 - E_2$ for cross-check marks. - ✅ Discharge per unit width $q = Q/b$ is **not the same as** $Q$ — a frequent KTU trap. - ✅ Classification: Undular ($1.0 < Fr_1 < 1.7$), Weak ($1.7$–$2.5$), Oscillating ($2.5$–$4.5$), Steady ($4.5$–$9.0$), Strong ($> 9.0$). - ✅ Power dissipated: $P = \gamma Q E_L$ in **watts** (or kW = W/1000). - ✅ Length of jump: USBR formula $L_j = 6.1 y_2$ is valid for $4.5 \le Fr_1 \le 13$. - ✅ Efficiency $\eta = E_2/E_1 = 1 - E_L/E_1$ — useful for design check. - ✅ Always confirm $Fr_2 < 1$ at the end of every problem — a **sanity check** examiners appreciate. - ✅ **Neglect bed friction** within the jump (standard KTU assumption for Module 4). - ✅ **Horizontal** channels only in Module 4 — sloping-channel jumps belong to advanced/elective courses. - ✅ The momentum equation used is $\sum F = \rho Q \Delta V$, **not** Newton's law in differential form. - ✅ In KTU 14-mark questions, **label every step** with the equation number or principle used — examiners award **methodology marks** even if the final answer is wrong. - ✅ Common student traps: sign error in $E_L$, mixing $q$ and $Q$, forgetting $\sqrt{}$ in Froude number, mis-classifying the jump type. - ✅ Final answer format expected by KTU: a clearly boxed answer for every sub-part, with **3 significant figures** minimum. --- ### Quick-Reference Card (For Last-Minute Revision) | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value Range (KTU) | |:---------|:--------|:--------------------------:| | $Fr_1$ | $V_1/\sqrt{gy_1}$ | $1.5$ – $8.0$ | | $y_2/y_1$ | $0.5(\sqrt{1+8Fr_1^2}-1)$ | $1.5$ – $10+$ | | $E_L$ | $(y_2-y_1)^3/(4y_1 y_2)$ | $0.05$ – $3.0$ m | | $\eta$ | $E_2/E_1$ | $30\%$ – $95\%$ | | $L_j$ | $6.1\,y_2$ (USBR) | $5$ – $20$ m | **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Hydraulic Jump in Horizontal Rectangular Channels**

topics.title: Types, Uses, and Characteristics of Hydraulic Jump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET302 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types, Uses, and Characteristics of Hydraulic Jump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Jump:** A hydraulic jump is a **rapidly varied flow (RVF) phenomenon** in open channel flow where a shallow, high-velocity **supercritical flow** (Froude number $Fr > 1.0$) abruptly and violently transitions into a deep, low-velocity **subcritical flow** (Froude number $Fr < 1.0$), accompanied by a sudden rise in the free surface, intense turbulence, air entrainment, surface rollers, and significant **kinetic energy dissipation**.

The depths $y_1$ (before the jump) and $y_2$ (after the jump) are thermodynamically and dynamically linked and are called **conjugate depths** or **sequent depths**. The classical hydraulic jump occurs in a **horizontal, rectangular, prismatic channel** with **negligible bed friction** over the short length of the jump.

### Key Terminology Standardized to KTU 2024 Nomenclature

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $y_1$ | Pre-jump (supercritical) depth | **m** |
| $y_2$ | Post-jump (subcritical) depth | **m** |
| $V_1, V_2$ | Pre-jump and post-jump mean velocities | **m/s** |
| $q$ | Discharge per unit width ($V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2$) | **m²/s** |
| $Fr_1$ | Upstream Froude number $V_1 / \sqrt{g y_1}$ | dimensionless |
| $E_1, E_2$ | Specific energies at sections 1 and 2 | **m** |
| $\Delta E$ | Energy loss across the jump | **m** |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency of the jump $E_2 / E_1$ | dimensionless |
| $L_j$ | Length of the hydraulic jump | **m** |
| $P$ | Power dissipated per unit width | **W/m** or **kW/m** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine you are pushing a small child on a swing. As the swing approaches the bottom of its arc, it is moving very fast and is low to the ground. The chain then snaps the swing back up high, and the swing rises to a near-stop at the peak. The conversion from "fast-and-low" to "slow-and-high" is sudden, turbulent, and the chain rattles violently during the transition. **A hydraulic jump is the open-channel version of this exact event** — water moving fast and shallow suddenly "jumps" up to become slow and deep, with all the lost kinetic energy going into turbulence, eddies, and heat.

In spillways, the same violent conversion of kinetic energy to turbulent energy is a designer's best friend because it **destroys the destructive energy of fast-moving water before it can erode the downstream riverbed**.

## 1.3 Why the Jump Occurs

A hydraulic jump is fundamentally a **conjugate depth transition** forced by the momentum equation when the downstream control (e.g., a tailwater depth) is high enough to "drown" the supercritical flow.

- The upstream flow is **supercritical** ($Fr_1 > 1$).
- Downstream conditions demand a **subcritical depth** ($y_2 > y_c$).
- The only physically admissible way to connect them (with the conservation laws of mass, momentum, and energy) is a **jump**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Occurrence Condition:** A classical hydraulic jump forms only when the incoming Froude number $Fr_1 \geq 1.0$ and the tailwater depth $y_t$ is approximately equal to the sequent depth $y_2$. If $y_t < y_2$, the jump moves **downstream**; if $y_t > y_2$, the jump is **submerged** and pushed upstream toward the sluice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Assumptions of the Classical Bélanger Jump

1. The channel is **rectangular** and **horizontal** (zero bed slope).
2. The pressure distribution at sections 1 and 2 is **hydrostatic** (valid because the jump length is short and the surface rollers do not propagate deep into the body of flow).
3. **Boundary friction** over the short length of the jump is **negligible**.
4. The jump is treated as a **stationary control volume** (steady flow).
5. **No external forces** (other than hydrostatic pressure and gravity) act on the control volume.

## 2.2 The Three Conservation Laws Applied

| Law | Equation Applied Between Sections 1 and 2 | Resulting Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Continuity** | $V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2 = q$ | $q$ is constant |
| **Momentum** | Sum of forces = rate of change of momentum | $2 q^2 / g = y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)$ |
| **Energy** | $E_1 = E_2 + \Delta E$ (with $\Delta E > 0$) | Energy is dissipated |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Hydraulic Jump

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula bank**. Every KTU 2024 question on this topic can be solved using only these equations. Units are SI unless stated otherwise.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Froude Number (rectangular) | $Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}}$ | $V_1$ in m/s, $y_1$ in m, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 2 | Specific Energy | $E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = y \left( 1 + \dfrac{Fr^2}{2} \right)$ | Energy in metres of fluid |
| 3 | **Bélanger Equation** (Sequent Depth Ratio) | $\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right]$ | $y_1, y_2$ in m, $Fr_1$ dimensionless |
| 4 | Energy Loss across Jump | $\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$ | $\Delta E$ in m |
| 5 | Efficiency of Jump | $\eta = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_1}$ | Expressed as a decimal or % |
| 6 | Power Dissipated (per unit width) | $P = \rho g q \Delta E$ | $P$ in W/m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ |
| 7 | Length of Jump (USBR, steady type) | $L_j \approx 6.1 \, y_2$ | Empirical, in m |
| 8 | Height of Jump | $h_j = y_2 - y_1$ | m |
| 9 | Critical Depth (rectangular) | $y_c = \left( \dfrac{q^2}{g} \right)^{1/3}$ | m |
| 10 | Discharge Intensity (rectangular) | $q = y_c \sqrt{g y_c}$ | m²/s |

## 2.4 USBR Classification of Hydraulic Jumps

The **United States Bureau of Reclamation (USBR)** classification (still the KTU board standard) is based on the **incoming Froude number $Fr_1$**:

| Type of Jump | $Fr_1$ Range | Key Visual & Physical Features | Energy Loss |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **No Jump** | $Fr_1 = 1.0$ | Flow is critical, no jump forms | $\Delta E = 0$ |
| **Undular Jump** | $1.0 < Fr_1 < 1.7$ | Standing waves, smooth rise, no breaking | Negligible ($\Delta E / E_1 < 5\%$) |
| **Weak Jump** | $1.7 \leq Fr_1 < 2.5$ | Small rollers on the surface, smooth rise | $\Delta E / E_1 = 5\% - 15\%$ |
| **Oscillating Jump** | $2.5 \leq Fr_1 < 4.5$ | Unstable, irregular jet, pulsating — **avoid in design** | $\Delta E / E_1 = 15\% - 45\%$ |
| **Steady Jump** | $4.5 \leq Fr_1 < 9.0$ | Stable, well-defined rollers, best dissipator | $\Delta E / E_1 = 45\% - 70\%$ |
| **Strong / Choppy Jump** | $Fr_1 \geq 9.0$ | Very rough, highly turbulent, spray, loud roar | $\Delta E / E_1 > 70\%$ |

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Uses

1. **Energy Dissipator** below spillways, weirs, and sluice gates (most common).
2. **Raising water level** on the downstream side of a measuring flume.
3. **Mixing of chemicals** in water-treatment plants (induced jump).
4. **Reduction of uplift pressure** on the aprons of hydraulic structures.
5. **Aeration of flow** (improves dissolved oxygen content in rivers below dams).
6. **Desalination** and industrial processing plants.
7. **Flow measurement** in conjunction with critical-depth flumes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bélanger Equation (Momentum Approach)

**Step 1 — Define the control volume.** Take a control volume bounded by sections 1 and 2 just upstream and downstream of the jump. The channel is rectangular, horizontal, and friction is neglected.

**Step 2 — Continuity equation.**

$$
q = V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2 = \text{constant per unit width}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the momentum equation in the x-direction** (net force = rate of change of momentum flux):

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)
$$

where the hydrostatic pressure forces per unit width are $P_1 = \rho g y_1^2 / 2$ and $P_2 = \rho g y_2^2 / 2$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\frac{\rho g y_1^2}{2} - \frac{\rho g y_2^2}{2} = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)
$$

$$
\frac{g}{2} (y_1^2 - y_2^2) = q \left( \frac{q}{y_2} - \frac{q}{y_1} \right) = q^2 \left( \frac{1}{y_2} - \frac{1}{y_1} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Factor the left side** as a difference of squares:

$$
\frac{g}{2} (y_1 - y_2)(y_1 + y_2) = q^2 \frac{(y_1 - y_2)}{y_1 y_2}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $(y_1 - y_2)$** (which is non-zero for a jump):

$$
\frac{g}{2} (y_1 + y_2) = \frac{q^2}{y_1 y_2}
$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the Froude number** $Fr_1^2 = V_1^2 / (g y_1) = q^2 / (g y_1^3)$, so $q^2 = Fr_1^2 g y_1^3$:

$$
\frac{g}{2} (y_1 + y_2) = \frac{Fr_1^2 g y_1^3}{y_1 y_2}
$$

$$
y_1 + y_2 = \frac{2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2}{y_2}
$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange** into a quadratic in $r = y_2 / y_1$:

$$
y_2 (y_1 + y_2) = 2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2
$$

$$
y_1 y_2 + y_2^2 = 2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2
$$

$$
r + r^2 = 2 Fr_1^2
$$

$$
r^2 + r - 2 Fr_1^2 = 0
$$

**Step 9 — Solve the quadratic** using the positive root (since $r > 0$):

$$
r = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2}}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bélanger Equation (Final):**
>
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Energy Loss Equation

**Step 1 — Write the specific energies:**

$$
E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_1 \left( 1 + \frac{Fr_1^2}{2} \right)
$$

$$
E_2 = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 2 — Use continuity** to find $V_2$ and the post-jump Froude number. From $V_2 = V_1 / r$ and $y_2 = r y_1$:

$$
Fr_2^2 = \frac{V_2^2}{g y_2} = \frac{V_1^2 / r^2}{g r y_1} = \frac{Fr_1^2}{r^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $E_2$ in terms of $y_1$ and $r$:**

$$
E_2 = r y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2 g r^2} = r y_1 + \frac{Fr_1^2 y_1}{2 r^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference $\Delta E = E_1 - E_2$:**

$$
\Delta E = y_1 \left( 1 + \frac{Fr_1^2}{2} \right) - y_1 \left( r + \frac{Fr_1^2}{2 r^2} \right)
$$

$$
\Delta E = y_1 \left[ (1 - r) + \frac{Fr_1^2}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{r^2} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $(1 - r)$ and use $Fr_1^2 = r(1 + r)/2$** from the momentum relation:

$$
\Delta E = y_1 (1 - r) \left[ -1 + \frac{Fr_1^2 (1 + r)}{2 r^2} \right]
$$

Substituting $Fr_1^2 = r(1 + r) / 2$:

$$
\Delta E = y_1 (1 - r) \left[ -1 + \frac{(1 + r)^2}{4 r} \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Combine the bracket** over a common denominator $4r$:

$$
-1 + \frac{(1 + r)^2}{4r} = \frac{-4r + (1 + r)^2}{4r} = \frac{1 - 2r + r^2}{4r} = \frac{(1 - r)^2}{4r}
$$

**Step 7 — Final simplification:**

$$
\Delta E = y_1 (1 - r) \cdot \frac{(1 - r)^2}{4r} = \frac{y_1 (1 - r)^3}{4r}
$$

Since $r > 1$, $(1 - r)^3$ is negative; taking the magnitude and writing $r = y_2 / y_1$:

$$
|\Delta E| = \frac{y_1 (r - 1)^3}{4r} = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Loss Equation (Final):**
>
> $$\boxed{\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}}$$

## 3.3 Efficiency of the Jump

$$
\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 1 - \frac{\Delta E}{E_1}
$$

For a **steady jump** ($4.5 \leq Fr_1 < 9.0$), efficiency is typically **40% – 55%** — meaning **45% – 60% of the upstream specific energy is destroyed** in the jump, which is exactly the design intent.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]:**
> A hydraulic jump occurs in a horizontal rectangular channel of width 6 m. The pre-jump depth is 0.6 m and the pre-jump velocity is 10 m/s. Calculate: (a) the Froude number of the incoming flow, (b) the post-jump depth, (c) the energy loss in the jump, (d) the efficiency of the jump, and (e) the total power dissipated by the jump.

**Given:** $y_1 = 0.6$ m, $V_1 = 10$ m/s, $b = 6$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

**(a) Froude number of incoming flow:**

$$
Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.6}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5.886}} = \frac{10}{2.4261} = 4.122
$$

Since $2.5 \leq Fr_1 < 4.5$, this is an **oscillating jump**.

**(b) Post-jump depth (Bélanger equation):**

$$
\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8(4.122)^2} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
8 Fr_1^2 = 8 \times 16.991 = 135.93
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 + 135.93} = \sqrt{136.93} = 11.701
$$

$$
\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{11.701 - 1}{2} = \frac{10.701}{2} = 5.351
$$

$$
y_2 = 5.351 \times 0.6 = 3.210 \text{ m}
$$

**(c) Energy loss:**

$$
\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \frac{(3.210 - 0.600)^3}{4 \times 0.600 \times 3.210} = \frac{(2.610)^3}{7.704}
$$

$$
(2.610)^3 = 17.778
$$

$$
\Delta E = \frac{17.778}{7.704} = 2.308 \text{ m}
$$

**(d) Efficiency:**

$$
E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.6 + \frac{(10)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.6 + 5.097 = 5.697 \text{ m}
$$

$$
E_2 = E_1 - \Delta E = 5.697 - 2.308 = 3.389 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{3.389}{5.697} = 0.595 = 59.5\%
$$

**(e) Total power dissipated:**

$$
q = V_1 y_1 = 10 \times 0.6 = 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q = q \times b = 6 \times 6 = 36 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
P = \rho g Q \Delta E = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 36 \times 2.308
$$

$$
P = 815{,}037 \text{ W} \approx 815.04 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $\Delta E / E_1 = 2.308 / 5.697 = 0.405 = 40.5\%$, which lies in the expected range (15% – 45%) for an oscillating jump. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — USBR Classification of Hydraulic Jumps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Incoming Flow in Channel] --> nodeB[Compute Froude Number Fr1]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Fr1 Classification}
    nodeC -- Fr1 = 1.0 --> nodeD[NO JUMP - Critical Flow]
    nodeC -- 1.0 to 1.7 --> nodeE[UNDULAR JUMP]
    nodeC -- 1.7 to 2.5 --> nodeF[WEAK JUMP]
    nodeC -- 2.5 to 4.5 --> nodeG[OSCILLATING JUMP]
    nodeC -- 4.5 to 9.0 --> nodeH[STEADY JUMP]
    nodeC -- Fr1 greater than 9.0 --> nodeI[STRONG CHOPPY JUMP]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Energy Loss 0 percent]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Energy Loss 0 to 5 percent]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Energy Loss 5 to 15 percent]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Energy Loss 15 to 45 percent]
    nodeH --> nodeN[Energy Loss 45 to 70 percent]
    nodeI --> nodeO[Energy Loss greater than 70 percent]
    nodeK --> nodeP[Stilling Basin Design Selection]
    nodeL --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Final Hydraulic Structure]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Energy Flow — Hydraulic Jump Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Upstream Section 1] -- V1 y1 supercritical --> nodeCV[Control Volume of Jump]
    nodeIn -- Pressure P1 hydrostatic --> nodeCV
    nodeCV -- Turbulent rollers and eddies --> nodeMix[Intense Internal Mixing]
    nodeMix -- Air entrainment --> nodeOut[Downstream Section 2]
    nodeCV -- V2 y2 subcritical --> nodeOut
    nodeOut -- Pressure P2 hydrostatic --> nodeCheck{Energy Balance}
    nodeCheck --> nodeE1[Specific Energy E1]
    nodeCheck --> nodeE2[Specific Energy E2]
    nodeE1 -- Delta E greater than 0 --> nodeLoss[Power Dissipated as Heat and Sound]
    nodeE2 --> nodeEff[Efficiency E2 by E1]
    nodeLoss --> nodeFinal[Downstream Riverbed Protected]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Typical Stilling Basin (Practical Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSp[Spillway Crest] --> nodeSup[Supercritical Fast Flow]
    nodeSup -- High velocity shallow depth --> nodeApr[Horizontal Apron of Stilling Basin]
    nodeApr --> nodeJump[Hydraulic Jump Location]
    nodeJump -- Fr1 4.5 to 9.0 ideal --> nodeRoll[Surface Rollers Dissipate Energy]
    nodeRoll --> nodeSub[Subcritical Calm Flow]
    nodeSub --> nodeDS[Downstream Channel or River]
    nodeApr -. Baffle Blocks .-> nodeJump
    nodeApr -. Dentated End Sill .-> nodeSub
    nodeJump -. Chute Blocks .-> nodeApr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - Diagram 4.1 is a **decision-tree classification** of jumps by Froude number, used by KTU examiners when they ask "Classify the jump."
> - Diagram 4.2 is a **conservation-of-energy block flow** showing where $\Delta E$ is lost.
> - Diagram 4.3 is a **practical schematic of a USBR Type-III stilling basin**, the most common energy dissipator below Indian spillways.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define a hydraulic jump. Mention any two conditions necessary for its formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hydraulic jump is a rapidly varied open-channel flow phenomenon in which a supercritical flow (Fr > 1) abruptly transitions to a subcritical flow (Fr < 1) with a sudden rise in water surface, intense turbulence, and significant energy dissipation. **Two necessary conditions:**

1. The upstream Froude number must be greater than **1.0** ($Fr_1 > 1$).
2. The downstream (tailwater) depth must be approximately equal to or greater than the **sequent depth** $y_2$ computed from the Bélanger equation.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two conditions.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List the USBR classification of hydraulic jumps and state which type has the maximum energy dissipation efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The USBR classification is based on the upstream Froude number $Fr_1$:

1. No jump ($Fr_1 = 1.0$)
2. Undular jump ($1.0 < Fr_1 < 1.7$)
3. Weak jump ($1.7 \leq Fr_1 < 2.5$)
4. Oscillating jump ($2.5 \leq Fr_1 < 4.5$)
5. **Steady jump ($4.5 \leq Fr_1 < 9.0$)** — maximum dissipation efficiency
6. Strong / Choppy jump ($Fr_1 \geq 9.0$)

The **steady jump** is preferred in design because it is stable, well-defined, and dissipates **45% to 70%** of the incoming specific energy without erratic pulsations. *(3 marks: 2 for list, 1 for identifying the steady jump.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Bélanger equation for the sequent depth ratio in a hydraulic jump occurring in a horizontal rectangular channel. **State all assumptions clearly.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Water flows in a horizontal rectangular channel of width 8 m at a depth of 1.2 m with a velocity of 12 m/s. A hydraulic jump forms. Determine: *(i)* the Froude number of the incoming flow, *(ii)* the conjugate depth, *(iii)* the energy loss in the jump, *(iv)* the efficiency, and *(v)* the total power dissipated. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
*(Present the same step-by-step derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key points examiners look for:)*

1. **Assumptions** (1 mark): Horizontal, rectangular, prismatic channel; hydrostatic pressure; no friction; steady flow.
2. **Continuity equation** (1 mark): $q = V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2$.
3. **Momentum equation** in the x-direction (2 marks): $\frac{\rho g y_1^2}{2} - \frac{\rho g y_2^2}{2} = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)$.
4. **Algebraic simplification** to obtain $2 Fr_1^2 = r + r^2$ (2 marks).
5. **Final quadratic solution** and writing the final boxed answer (1 mark):

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right]}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $y_1 = 1.2$ m, $V_1 = 12$ m/s, $b = 8$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

*(i) Froude number (1 mark):*

$$
Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.2}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{11.772}} = \frac{12}{3.431} = 3.498
$$

This is an **oscillating jump** ($2.5 \leq Fr_1 < 4.5$).

*(ii) Conjugate depth (2 marks):*

$$
\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \times (3.498)^2} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \times 12.236} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 97.886} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{98.886} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 9.944 - 1 \right] = 4.472
$$

$$
y_2 = 4.472 \times 1.2 = 5.366 \text{ m}
$$

*(iii) Energy loss (1.5 marks):*

$$
\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \frac{(5.366 - 1.2)^3}{4 \times 1.2 \times 5.366} = \frac{(4.166)^3}{25.757} = \frac{72.29}{25.757} = 2.806 \text{ m}
$$

*(iv) Efficiency (1 mark):*

$$
E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 1.2 + \frac{144}{19.62} = 1.2 + 7.339 = 8.539 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{\Delta E}{E_1} = 1 - \frac{2.806}{8.539} = 1 - 0.3286 = 0.6714 = 67.14\%
$$

*(v) Total power dissipated (1.5 marks):*

$$
Q = V_1 y_1 b = 12 \times 1.2 \times 8 = 115.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
P = \rho g Q \Delta E = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 115.2 \times 2.806 = 3{,}170{,}618 \text{ W} \approx 3170.6 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What are the different types of hydraulic jumps according to USBR classification? Explain the characteristics of each with the help of a sketch. **Discuss the practical use of hydraulic jumps in hydraulic engineering.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hydraulic jump is to be formed below a spillway where the depth at the toe of the spillway is 0.8 m and the velocity is 18 m/s. Find: *(i)* the Froude number, *(ii)* the post-jump depth, *(iii)* the energy loss, and *(iv)* the length of the jump (using the USBR empirical relation $L_j = 6.1 y_2$). *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Classification and Uses (7 marks):**
*(Present a 6-row classification table as in Section 2.4 of these notes, mentioning 1 mark for each type plus 1 mark for the sketch. Add the following 6 uses for 1 mark: energy dissipation, raising water levels, chemical mixing, uplift reduction, aeration, flow measurement.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $y_1 = 0.8$ m, $V_1 = 18$ m/s.

*(i) Froude number (1 mark):*

$$
Fr_1 = \frac{18}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.8}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{7.848}} = \frac{18}{2.801} = 6.426
$$

This is a **steady jump** ($4.5 \leq Fr_1 < 9.0$).

*(ii) Post-jump depth (2.5 marks):*

$$
\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \times (6.426)^2} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \times 41.293} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 330.346} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{331.346} - 1 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 18.203 - 1 \right] = 8.602
$$

$$
y_2 = 8.602 \times 0.8 = 6.881 \text{ m}
$$

*(iii) Energy loss (2 marks):*

$$
\Delta E = \frac{(6.881 - 0.8)^3}{4 \times 0.8 \times 6.881} = \frac{(6.081)^3}{22.020} = \frac{224.84}{22.020} = 10.21 \text{ m}
$$

*(iv) Length of the jump (1.5 marks):*

$$
L_j = 6.1 \times y_2 = 6.1 \times 6.881 = 41.97 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** of $g = 9.81$ m/s² — losing 0.5 mark per question.
> 2. **Confusing $Fr_1$ with $Fr_2$** — the Bélanger equation uses the **upstream** Froude number; writing $Fr_2$ loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Not classifying the jump type** after computing $Fr_1$ — a single-line addition like "Hence, this is a steady jump" earns 0.5 mark and shows complete understanding.
> 4. **Sign error in $\Delta E$** — the energy loss is always a **positive quantity**; the formula gives a positive value only if $y_2 > y_1$ (which it must for a jump).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention assumptions** in the derivation part — KTU examiners award **1 dedicated mark** for the assumption block.
> 6. **In the power dissipation, mixing $q$ and $Q$** — $q$ is per-unit-width discharge (m²/s), $Q$ is total (m³/s); do not forget the channel width $b$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Hydraulic Jump (Module 4):**
> - ☐ A **hydraulic jump** is a sudden, turbulent transition from **supercritical** ($Fr_1 > 1$) to **subcritical** ($Fr_1 < 1$) flow.
> - ☐ It is **rapidly varied flow (RVF)** with intense energy loss, air entrainment, and surface rollers.
> - ☐ Governing assumptions: **rectangular, horizontal, hydrostatic pressure, no friction, steady**.
> - ☐ **Bélanger Equation** is the heart of the topic: $\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right]$.
> - ☐ **Energy loss** is given by $\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$ — always positive.
> - ☐ **Power dissipated** per unit width: $P = \rho g q \Delta E$; total: $P = \rho g Q \Delta E$.
> - ☐ **Efficiency** $\eta = E_2 / E_1 = 1 - \Delta E / E_1$.
> - ☐ **USBR Classification** by $Fr_1$: No jump, Undular, Weak, Oscillating, **Steady (best for design)**, Strong.
> - ☐ **Steady jump** ($4.5 \leq Fr_1 < 9.0$) is the most efficient and is used in stilling basin design.
> - ☐ **Oscillating jump** must be **avoided** in design due to pulsations and damage potential.
> - ☐ **Length of jump** (USBR steady type): $L_j \approx 6.1 \, y_2$.
> - ☐ Practical applications: **energy dissipation, aeration, chemical mixing, uplift reduction, raising water levels**.
> - ☐ **Heights of jump** $h_j = y_2 - y_1$ — useful for designing the apron elevation of a stilling basin.
> - ☐ The **conjugate depth relation** $r^2 + r - 2 Fr_1^2 = 0$ is the quadratic from which the Bélanger equation is solved.
> - ☐ **Siamese-twin equation**: $2 Fr_1^2 = r (1 + r)$ — a compact momentum form worth memorizing.
> - ☐ Always state the **jump type** (oscillating, steady, etc.) at the end of every numerical — 0.5 mark and a "complete answer" signal.
